☸ Kubernetes CLI (kubectl) Complete Documentation

Complete guide for deploying and managing Django applications on Kubernetes - anihpj project with jobpost app

📦 kubectl v1.29+ 🐍 Django 5.2+ 🎯 Python 3.11+ ☸ Kubernetes 🔧 Helm Charts

📋 Table of Contents

🚀 Getting Started

create, get, run, expose, delete - Essential commands for running workloads

📱 App Management

apply, annotate, autoscale, debug, diff, edit, kustomize, label, patch, replace, rollout, scale, set, wait

🔍 Working with Apps

attach, auth, cp, describe, events, exec, logs, port-forward, proxy, top

🏗️ Cluster Management

api-versions, api-resources, certificate, cluster-info, cordon, drain, taint, uncordon

⚙️ kubectl Settings & Usage

alpha, completion, config, explain, options, plugin, version

📊 Document Summary & Quick Reference

This comprehensive documentation covers the Kubernetes CLI (kubectl) for deploying and managing the anihpj Django project with the jobpost app. It includes practical examples using the 5 Django models: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost.

📈 Documentation Statistics

Main Commands: 45+ kubectl commands
Subcommands: 60+ specialized operations
Examples: 5+ per command section
Django Models: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
Context Files: Dockerfile, K8s manifests, Helm charts
Output Formats: YAML, JSON, table, wide

🎯 Quick Start for anihpj Django Project

Namespace: anihpj-production
Deployment: jobpost-web, jobpost-worker
Services: ClusterIP, LoadBalancer
ConfigMaps: django-config, nginx-config
Secrets: django-secrets, db-credentials
Database: PostgreSQL StatefulSet

🚀 GETTING STARTED

This section contains the most basic commands for getting a workload running on your cluster. run will start running 1 or more instances of a container image on your cluster. expose will load balance traffic across the running instances, and can create a HA proxy for accessing the containers from outside the cluster.

kubectl create

CREATE Resource Creation Declarative Imperative

Create a resource from a file or from stdin

The kubectl create command creates one or more Kubernetes resources from a file, URL, or stdin. This is an imperative command that creates resources directly. Unlike kubectl apply, it will fail if the resource already exists.

For the anihpj Django project, this command is essential for initial cluster setup, creating namespaces, deployments, services, configmaps, and secrets needed to run the jobpost application.

Syntax

# Create resource from file
kubectl create -f FILENAME [options]

# Create resource from stdin
cat resource.yaml | kubectl create -f -

# Create resource from URL
kubectl create -f https://example.com/resource.yaml

# Create with specific resource type
kubectl create <resource-type> NAME [options]

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filenamestring[]Filename, directory, or URL to files to use to create the resource
-k, --kustomizestringProcess a kustomization directory
--dry-runstringMust be "none", "server", or "client". (default "none")
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|name|go-template|go-template-file|template|templatefile|jsonpath|jsonpath-as-json|jsonpath-file
--save-configboolSave the configuration in resource annotations
-R, --recursiveboolProcess the directory used in -f recursively
--validatestringValidation mode: strict|warn|ignore (default "strict")
-l, --selectorstringSelector (label query) to filter on
--field-managerstringName of the manager used to track field ownership
--windows-line-endingsboolUse Windows line-endings (\r\n) for all text output

Available Subcommands

SubcommandDescription
clusterroleCreate a cluster role
clusterrolebindingCreate a cluster role binding
configmapCreate a config map from a local file, directory or literal value
cronjobCreate a cron job with the specified name
deploymentCreate a deployment with the specified name
ingressCreate an ingress with the specified name
jobCreate a job with the specified name
namespaceCreate a namespace with the specified name
poddisruptionbudgetCreate a pod disruption budget
priorityclassCreate a priority class
quotaCreate a quota with the specified name
roleCreate a role with single rule
rolebindingCreate a role binding
secretCreate a secret (generic, docker-registry, tls)
serviceCreate a service (clusterip, externalname, loadbalancer, nodeport)
serviceaccountCreate a service account
tokenRequest a service account token

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Resource created successfully
1Error occurred (resource exists, validation failed, etc.)

📁 Context Files for anihpj Django Project

Project Structure: The following files provide context for the kubectl create examples below.
Dockerfile (anihpj/Dockerfile)
# Dockerfile for anihpj Django project with jobpost app
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1
ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings

# Set work directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    postgresql-client \
    gcc \
    python3-dev \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy project files
COPY . .

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Create non-root user
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' appuser && \
    chown -R appuser:appuser /app
USER appuser

# Expose port
EXPOSE 8000

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8000/health/')"

# Run gunicorn
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "3", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
Base Kubernetes Manifest (k8s/base/deployment.yaml)
# Base deployment for anihpj Django jobpost application
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: web
        version: v1.0.0
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: jobpost-sa
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: database-url
        - name: SECRET_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: secret-key
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: regcred
Service Configuration (k8s/base/service.yaml)
# Service for anihpj Django jobpost application
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP

Examples

Example 1: Create Resources from Single YAML File

Scenario: Deploy the complete anihpj Django application stack from a single manifest file containing namespace, configmap, secret, deployment, and service.

📄 Setup - Combined Manifest (k8s/complete-stack.yaml):

# Complete stack for anihpj Django jobpost application
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    environment: production
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "jobpost.example.com,localhost"
  CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS: "https://jobpost.example.com"
  DB_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DB_NAME: "jobpost_db"
  DB_HOST: "postgres-service"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-secrets
  namespace: anihpj-production
type: Opaque
stringData:
  secret-key: "django-insecure-change-in-production-abc123xyz"
  database-url: "postgres://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db"
  db-password: "secretpass"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  selector:
    app: jobpost
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  type: ClusterIP

Command:

# Create all resources from the complete stack file
kubectl create -f k8s/complete-stack.yaml

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production created
configmap/django-config created
secret/django-secrets created
deployment.apps/jobpost-web created
service/jobpost-service created

✅ Verification:

# Verify all resources are created
kubectl get all -n anihpj-production

# Check namespace labels
kubectl get namespace anihpj-production --show-labels
NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/jobpost-web-6d4f5b8c9d-2xk7m   1/1     Running   0          45s
pod/jobpost-web-6d4f5b8c9d-9rt3n   1/1     Running   0          45s

NAME                      TYPE        CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
service/jobpost-service   ClusterIP   10.96.142.89    <none>        80/TCP    45s

NAME                          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/jobpost-web   2/2     2            2           45s

NAME                                     DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
replicaset.apps/jobpost-web-6d4f5b8c9d   2         2         2       45s

NAME                   STATUS   AGE   LABELS
anihpj-production      Active   45s   app=jobpost,environment=production
Example 2: Create Resources from Directory with Recursive Flag

Scenario: Deploy the anihpj application using a structured directory containing separate YAML files for each resource type, including nested directories for different environments.

📄 Setup - Directory Structure:

k8s/
├── namespace.yaml
├── configmaps/
│   ├── django-config.yaml
│   └── nginx-config.yaml
├── secrets/
│   ├── django-secrets.yaml
│   └── db-credentials.yaml
├── deployments/
│   ├── jobpost-web.yaml
│   └── jobpost-celery.yaml
└── services/
    ├── jobpost-service.yaml
    └── postgres-service.yaml

📄 k8s/namespace.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    managed-by: kubectl

📄 k8s/configmaps/django-config.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "jobpost.example.com"
  TIME_ZONE: "UTC"
  LANGUAGE_CODE: "en-us"
  USE_I18N: "true"
  USE_TZ: "true"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
  MEDIA_URL: "/media/"

📄 k8s/deployments/jobpost-celery.yaml:

# Celery worker for async tasks (processing JobPost model operations)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-celery
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: worker
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "info"]
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "100m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "300m"

Command:

# Create all resources from directory recursively
kubectl create -f k8s/ --recursive

# Or using the short flag
kubectl create -f k8s/ -R

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production created
configmap/django-config created
configmap/nginx-config created
secret/django-secrets created
secret/db-credentials created
deployment.apps/jobpost-web created
deployment.apps/jobpost-celery created
service/jobpost-service created
service/postgres-service created

✅ Verification:

# List all created resources
kubectl get all,configmap,secret -n anihpj-production

# Check deployments status
kubectl get deployments -n anihpj-production -o wide
NAME                      READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE   CONTAINERS   IMAGES                    SELECTOR
jobpost-web               3/3     3            3           60s   django       anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0    app=jobpost,tier=web
jobpost-celery            2/2     2            2           60s   celery       anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0    app=jobpost,tier=worker
Example 3: Create Resources with Dry-Run and Output

Scenario: Validate the anihpj deployment configuration before actual creation, generate the processed YAML for review, and save it for GitOps workflow.

📄 Setup - Deployment Template (k8s/templates/deployment.yaml):

# Template for jobpost deployment with Skills model caching
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    component: api
    version: "${VERSION}"
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "Deploy version ${VERSION}"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: api
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "8000"
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
      containers:
      - name: django-api
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: CACHE_BACKEND
          value: "django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache"
        - name: CACHE_LOCATION
          value: "redis://redis-service:6379/1"
        - name: POD_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: POD_NAMESPACE
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.namespace
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /api/v1/health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /api/v1/health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 20
          failureThreshold: 3

Command:

# Validate with client-side dry-run (no API server contact)
kubectl create -f k8s/templates/deployment.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Validate with server-side dry-run (validates against API server)
kubectl create -f k8s/templates/deployment.yaml --dry-run=server -o yaml

# Save validated output to file for GitOps
kubectl create -f k8s/templates/deployment.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml > k8s/rendered/deployment-validated.yaml

Output (dry-run=client):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: null
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    component: api
    version: ${VERSION}
  name: jobpost-api
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: api
  strategy:
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
    type: RollingUpdate
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/port: "8000"
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: api
    spec:
      containers:
      - env:
        - name: CACHE_BACKEND
          value: django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache
        - name: CACHE_LOCATION
          value: redis://redis-service:6379/1
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        name: django-api
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        resources:
          limits:
            cpu: "1"
            memory: 1Gi
          requests:
            cpu: 250m
            memory: 512Mi
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
status: {}

✅ Verification:

# Verify no resources were actually created
kubectl get deployment jobpost-api -n anihpj-production

# Check the saved file
cat k8s/rendered/deployment-validated.yaml | head -20
Error from server (NotFound): deployments.apps "jobpost-api" not found

# File saved successfully for GitOps review
Example 4: Create Resources from URL (Remote Repository)

Scenario: Deploy the anihpj application using manifests hosted in a Git repository or remote storage, useful for CI/CD pipelines and shared configurations.

📄 Setup - Remote Repository Structure (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/main/):

k8s-configs/
├── production/
│   ├── namespace.yaml
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── ingress.yaml
├── staging/
│   ├── namespace.yaml
│   └── deployment.yaml
└── common/
    ├── rbac.yaml
    └── networkpolicy.yaml

📄 Remote File Content (production/deployment.yaml):

# Production deployment for jobpost - manages User, Author, Location, JobPost models
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-production
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    environment: production
    maintainer: devops-team
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      environment: production
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        environment: production
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: jobpost
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.0.0-stable
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        volumeMounts:
        - name: static-files
          mountPath: /app/staticfiles
        - name: media-files
          mountPath: /app/media
      volumes:
      - name: static-files
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: static-pvc
      - name: media-files
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: media-pvc

Command:

# Create from single remote file
kubectl create -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/main/production/deployment.yaml

# Create multiple resources from remote URLs
kubectl create \
  -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/main/production/namespace.yaml \
  -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/main/production/deployment.yaml \
  -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/main/production/service.yaml

# Create from remote directory (using kustomize)
kubectl create -k https://github.com/anihpj/k8s-configs/production

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-production created

# Or for multiple files:
namespace/anihpj-production created
deployment.apps/jobpost-production created
service/jobpost-service created

✅ Verification:

# Verify deployment created from remote
kubectl get deployment jobpost-production -n anihpj-production -o wide

# Check pod distribution across nodes (anti-affinity)
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o wide --show-labels
NAME                 READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE   CONTAINERS   IMAGES                          SELECTOR
jobpost-production   5/5     5            5           30s   django       anihpj/jobpost:v2.0.0-stable   app=jobpost,environment=production

NAME                                  READY   STATUS    NODE           LABELS
jobpost-production-7d8f9c6b5d-2kx8m   1/1     Running   node-pool-1    app=jobpost,environment=production
jobpost-production-7d8f9c6b5d-4nj7p   1/1     Running   node-pool-2    app=jobpost,environment=production
jobpost-production-7d8f9c6b5d-6rt5q   1/1     Running   node-pool-3    app=jobpost,environment=production
jobpost-production-7d8f9c6b5d-8wm3s   1/1     Running   node-pool-1    app=jobpost,environment=production
jobpost-production-7d8f9c6b5d-9zy6v   1/1     Running   node-pool-2    app=jobpost,environment=production
Example 5: Create Resources Using Kustomize

Scenario: Deploy the anihpj Django application to multiple environments (dev, staging, production) using Kustomize overlays, allowing environment-specific customizations without duplicating manifests.

📄 Setup - Kustomize Directory Structure:

k8s/
├── base/
│   ├── kustomization.yaml
│   ├── namespace.yaml
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── configmap.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── development/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   ├── replica-patch.yaml
    │   └── env-config.yaml
    ├── staging/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   └── replica-patch.yaml
    └── production/
        ├── kustomization.yaml
        ├── replica-patch.yaml
        ├── resource-patch.yaml
        └── hpa.yaml

📄 k8s/base/kustomization.yaml:

apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

metadata:
  name: jobpost-base

resources:
  - namespace.yaml
  - deployment.yaml
  - service.yaml
  - configmap.yaml

commonLabels:
  app: jobpost
  managed-by: kustomize

commonAnnotations:
  project: anihpj
  team: backend

📄 k8s/base/deployment.yaml:

# Base deployment - handles Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "100m"

📄 k8s/overlays/production/kustomization.yaml:

apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: anihpj-production

resources:
  - ../../base
  - hpa.yaml

namePrefix: prod-

commonLabels:
  environment: production

patches:
  - path: replica-patch.yaml
  - path: resource-patch.yaml

images:
  - name: anihpj/jobpost
    newTag: v2.1.0-stable

configMapGenerator:
  - name: django-config
    behavior: merge
    literals:
      - DEBUG=false
      - LOG_LEVEL=WARNING
      - CACHE_ENABLED=true

📄 k8s/overlays/production/replica-patch.yaml:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
spec:
  replicas: 5

📄 k8s/overlays/production/resource-patch.yaml:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"

📄 k8s/overlays/production/hpa.yaml:

apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: prod-jobpost-web
  minReplicas: 5
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80

Command:

# Create production environment using kustomize
kubectl create -k k8s/overlays/production

# Preview what will be created (dry-run)
kubectl create -k k8s/overlays/production --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Create development environment
kubectl create -k k8s/overlays/development

# Create staging environment
kubectl create -k k8s/overlays/staging

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production created
configmap/prod-django-config-7c8d9f2b5m created
deployment.apps/prod-jobpost-web created
service/prod-jobpost-service created
horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-web-hpa created

✅ Verification:

# Verify production deployment with correct image tag
kubectl get deployment prod-jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-production

# Verify resource limits were applied
kubectl get deployment prod-jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources}' | jq
anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0-stable

NAME              REFERENCE                     TARGETS         MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
jobpost-web-hpa   Deployment/prod-jobpost-web   12%/70%, 45%/80%   5         20        5          45s

{
  "limits": {
    "cpu": "1",
    "memory": "1Gi"
  },
  "requests": {
    "cpu": "500m",
    "memory": "512Mi"
  }
}
Example 6: Create Resources with Save Config and Field Manager

Scenario: Create the anihpj resources while saving configuration for future kubectl apply operations, and specify a field manager for server-side apply tracking - useful for GitOps tools like ArgoCD or Flux.

📄 Setup - GitOps Manifest (gitops/jobpost-deployment.yaml):

# GitOps managed deployment for anihpj
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-gitops
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: argocd
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: gitops
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: gitops
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.5.0
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: GIT_COMMIT
          value: "abc123def456"
        - name: DEPLOYED_BY
          value: "argocd"
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "200m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"

Command:

# Create with save-config for future apply operations
kubectl create -f gitops/jobpost-deployment.yaml --save-config

# Create with specific field manager (for GitOps tools)
kubectl create -f gitops/jobpost-deployment.yaml \
  --field-manager="argocd-controller" \
  --save-config

# Create with server-side apply field manager
kubectl create -f gitops/jobpost-deployment.yaml \
  --field-manager="flux-system" \
  --dry-run=server \
  -o yaml

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-gitops created

# With field-manager specification:
deployment.apps/jobpost-gitops created

✅ Verification:

# Check saved configuration annotation
kubectl get deployment jobpost-gitops -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.kubectl\.kubernetes\.io/last-applied-configuration}' | jq

# Check managed fields and field managers
kubectl get deployment jobpost-gitops -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.managedFields[*].manager}'

# View full managed fields detail
kubectl get deployment jobpost-gitops -n anihpj-production -o yaml | grep -A 20 "managedFields:"
# Last applied configuration:
{
  "apiVersion": "apps/v1",
  "kind": "Deployment",
  "metadata": {
    "name": "jobpost-gitops",
    "namespace": "anihpj-production",
    "labels": {
      "app": "jobpost",
      "app.kubernetes.io/managed-by": "argocd"
    }
  },
  "spec": {
    "replicas": 3,
    ...
  }
}

# Field managers:
argocd-controller kubectl-create

# Managed fields detail:
managedFields:
- apiVersion: apps/v1
  fieldsType: FieldsV1
  fieldsV1:
    f:metadata:
      f:labels:
        f:app: {}
        f:app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: {}
    f:spec:
      f:replicas: {}
      f:selector: {}
  manager: argocd-controller
  operation: Update
  time: "2026-03-01T10:30:00Z"

kubectl create clusterrole

CREATE RBAC Security Cluster-wide

Create a cluster role with the specified rules

The kubectl create clusterrole command creates a ClusterRole resource which defines permissions at the cluster level. Unlike namespaced Roles, ClusterRoles can grant access to cluster-scoped resources (like nodes, persistent volumes) and non-resource endpoints.

For the anihpj Django project, ClusterRoles are essential for:

  • Granting admin access to manage JobPost deployments across all namespaces
  • Allowing monitoring tools to read metrics from all namespaces
  • Enabling CI/CD pipelines to deploy to multiple environments
  • Configuring backup operators to access persistent volumes

Syntax

# Create a cluster role with specific verbs and resources
kubectl create clusterrole NAME --verb=verb --resource=resource.group [options]

# Create with aggregation labels
kubectl create clusterrole NAME --aggregation-rule="rbac.example.com/aggregate=true"

# Create with non-resource URLs
kubectl create clusterrole NAME --verb=verb --non-resource-url=/path

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--verbstring[]Verbs to grant: get, list, watch, create, update, patch, delete, deletecollection, * (all)
--resourcestring[]Resources to apply verbs to (e.g., pods, deployments.apps, secrets)
--resource-namestring[]Specific resource names to restrict access (whitelist)
--non-resource-urlstring[]Non-resource URLs to grant access to (e.g., /healthz, /metrics)
--aggregation-rulestringAggregation label selector for combining ClusterRoles
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client" (default "none")
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|name|go-template
--save-configboolSave configuration in annotation
--validatestringValidation mode: strict|warn|ignore

Available Verbs

VerbHTTP MethodDescription
getGETRead a specific resource by name
listGETList all resources of a type
watchGET (streaming)Watch for changes to resources
createPOSTCreate new resources
updatePUTReplace entire resource
patchPATCHPartially update resource
deleteDELETEDelete a specific resource
deletecollectionDELETEDelete multiple resources
*ALLAll verbs (wildcard)

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0ClusterRole created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid syntax, permission denied)

📁 Context: RBAC Architecture for anihpj

RBAC Setup: The anihpj project uses a layered RBAC approach with ClusterRoles for cross-namespace operations and namespace-scoped Roles for environment-specific access.
RBAC Architecture Diagram
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          anihpj RBAC Architecture                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐       │
│  │  ClusterRole:   │     │  ClusterRole:   │     │  ClusterRole:   │       │
│  │  jobpost-admin  │     │ jobpost-viewer  │     │ jobpost-deploy  │       │
│  │  ─────────────  │     │  ─────────────  │     │  ─────────────  │       │
│  │  Full CRUD on:  │     │  Read-only on:  │     │  Deploy perms:  │       │
│  │  - deployments  │     │  - pods         │     │  - deployments  │       │
│  │  - services     │     │  - services     │     │  - configmaps   │       │
│  │  - configmaps   │     │  - configmaps   │     │  - secrets      │       │
│  │  - secrets      │     │  - events       │     │  - services     │       │
│  │  - pods         │     │  - logs         │     │                 │       │
│  └────────┬────────┘     └────────┬────────┘     └────────┬────────┘       │
│           │                       │                       │                 │
│           ▼                       ▼                       ▼                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │                   ClusterRoleBindings                            │       │
│  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐      │       │
│  │  │ admin-team  │  │ dev-team    │  │ ci-cd-serviceaccount│      │       │
│  │  │ → admin     │  │ → viewer    │  │ → deploy            │      │       │
│  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘      │       │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Namespaces: anihpj-dev │ anihpj-staging │ anihpj-production              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Admin ClusterRole for Full Deployment Management

Scenario: Create a ClusterRole that grants DevOps team full control over jobpost deployments, services, configmaps, and secrets across all namespaces (dev, staging, production).

📄 Context - Team Access Requirements:

# Team access matrix for anihpj project
# 
# Team          | Namespace              | Access Level
# --------------|------------------------|------------------
# DevOps Admin  | all (cluster-wide)     | Full CRUD
# Developers    | anihpj-dev             | Read + Deploy
# QA Team       | anihpj-staging         | Read-only
# Monitoring    | all                    | Read pods/metrics
#
# Models managed: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost

Command:

# Create admin clusterrole with full permissions
kubectl create clusterrole jobpost-admin \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete \
  --resource=deployments.apps,services,configmaps,secrets,pods,pods/log,pods/exec

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-admin created

✅ Verification:

# View the created clusterrole
kubectl get clusterrole jobpost-admin -o yaml

# Check the rules
kubectl describe clusterrole jobpost-admin
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: jobpost-admin
  creationTimestamp: "2026-03-01T10:00:00Z"
rules:
- apiGroups:
  - apps
  resources:
  - deployments
  verbs:
  - get
  - list
  - watch
  - create
  - update
  - patch
  - delete
- apiGroups:
  - ""
  resources:
  - services
  - configmaps
  - secrets
  - pods
  - pods/log
  - pods/exec
  verbs:
  - get
  - list
  - watch
  - create
  - update
  - patch
  - delete
Example 2: Create Read-Only Viewer ClusterRole

Scenario: Create a ClusterRole for monitoring systems and QA team to view jobpost resources and logs without modification permissions.

📄 Context - Monitoring Stack Configuration (monitoring/prometheus-rbac.yaml):

# Prometheus monitoring needs to scrape metrics from all namespaces
# This requires read access to pods, services, and endpoints
# 
# Targets:
# - anihpj-dev/jobpost-web:8000/metrics
# - anihpj-staging/jobpost-web:8000/metrics  
# - anihpj-production/jobpost-web:8000/metrics
#
# Django metrics exposed:
# - django_http_requests_total (by method, path, status)
# - django_model_operations_total (by model: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost)
# - django_db_query_duration_seconds

Command:

# Create viewer clusterrole for monitoring
kubectl create clusterrole jobpost-viewer \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,pods/log,services,endpoints,configmaps,events,deployments.apps,replicasets.apps

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-viewer created

✅ Verification:

# Verify read-only permissions (no create/update/delete)
kubectl describe clusterrole jobpost-viewer | grep -A 5 "Rules:"

# Test with auth can-i
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:prometheus-sa \
  --namespace=anihpj-production
Rules:
  Resources                         Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------                         -----------------  --------------  -----
  pods, pods/log                    []                 []              [get list watch]
  services, endpoints, configmaps   []                 []              [get list watch]
  events                            []                 []              [get list watch]
  deployments.apps, replicasets.apps []               []              [get list watch]

no - RBAC: clusterrole "jobpost-viewer" does not grant delete on pods
Example 3: Create CI/CD Deployment ClusterRole

Scenario: Create a ClusterRole for GitHub Actions or Jenkins CI/CD pipeline to deploy jobpost application updates, roll back deployments, and manage related resources.

📄 Context - CI/CD Pipeline Configuration (.github/workflows/deploy.yaml):

# GitHub Actions workflow for anihpj deployment
name: Deploy JobPost to Kubernetes

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'anihpj/**'
      - 'jobpost/**'
      - 'k8s/**'

env:
  CLUSTER_NAME: anihpj-cluster
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io/anihpj
  
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBE_CONFIG }}
      
      # ServiceAccount: github-actions-deployer
      # Needs: deployments, services, configmaps, secrets, pods
      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/jobpost-web \
            django=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/jobpost:${{ github.sha }} \
            -n anihpj-staging
          kubectl rollout status deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-staging
      
      - name: Run migrations
        run: |
          kubectl exec -n anihpj-staging deployment/jobpost-web -- \
            python manage.py migrate --noinput

Command:

# Create deployment clusterrole for CI/CD
kubectl create clusterrole jobpost-deployer \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch \
  --resource=deployments.apps,replicasets.apps,services,configmaps,secrets,pods,pods/exec,pods/log

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-deployer created

✅ Verification:

# Verify deployment permissions
kubectl auth can-i update deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:ci-cd:github-actions \
  --namespace=anihpj-staging

# Check can create but not delete (safety)
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:ci-cd:github-actions

# List all permissions
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:ci-cd:github-actions
yes

no

Resources                                       Non-Resource URLs   Resource Names   Verbs
deployments.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
replicasets.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
services                                        []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
configmaps                                      []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
secrets                                         []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
pods                                            []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
pods/exec                                       []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
pods/log                                        []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
Example 4: Create ClusterRole for Specific Resource Names

Scenario: Create a restricted ClusterRole that only allows access to specific named resources (jobpost-web deployment, jobpost-service) for external contractors with limited scope.

📄 Context - Contractor Access Policy:

# External contractor access policy for anihpj project
# 
# Contractor: frontend-agency
# Project: UI updates for JobPost listing pages
# Duration: 2026-03-01 to 2026-04-30
# 
# Allowed resources (explicit whitelist):
# - Deployment: jobpost-web (only)
# - Service: jobpost-service (only)
# - ConfigMap: django-config (read-only)
# - Pod logs: jobpost-web-* pods only
#
# Denied:
# - database deployments
# - secrets
# - other services
# - production namespace

Command:

# Create restricted clusterrole for specific resources only
kubectl create clusterrole contractor-limited \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=deployments.apps \
  --resource-name=jobpost-web

# Add service access (separate command)
kubectl create clusterrole contractor-service-view \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=services \
  --resource-name=jobpost-service

# Combined approach using dry-run and manual edit
kubectl create clusterrole contractor-jobpost-access \
  --verb=get,list,watch,update \
  --resource=deployments.apps \
  --resource-name=jobpost-web \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > contractor-role.yaml

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/contractor-limited created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/contractor-service-view created

✅ Verification:

# Verify access is limited to specific resource names
kubectl describe clusterrole contractor-limited

# Test access to allowed resource
kubectl auth can-i get deployment/jobpost-web \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:contractors:frontend-agency -n anihpj-staging

# Test access to disallowed resource
kubectl auth can-i get deployment/postgres \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:contractors:frontend-agency -n anihpj-staging
Name:         contractor-limited
Labels:       <none>
Annotations:  <none>
PolicyRule:
  Resources         Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------         -----------------  --------------  -----
  deployments.apps  []                 [jobpost-web]   [get list watch]

yes

no - RBAC: access to deployment "postgres" denied by resource name restriction
Example 5: Create ClusterRole with Non-Resource URL Access

Scenario: Create a ClusterRole for health monitoring systems that need to access cluster health endpoints (/healthz, /readyz) and metrics endpoints without accessing any Kubernetes resources.

📄 Context - Health Check System (monitoring/health-check-job.yaml):

# External health monitoring CronJob for anihpj cluster
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: cluster-health-check
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"  # Every 5 minutes
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: health-checker
          containers:
          - name: health-check
            image: curlimages/curl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              # Check API server health
              curl -sk https://kubernetes.default.svc/healthz
              
              # Check readiness
              curl -sk https://kubernetes.default.svc/readyz
              
              # Check livez
              curl -sk https://kubernetes.default.svc/livez
              
              # Fetch metrics (if prometheus adapter installed)
              curl -sk https://kubernetes.default.svc/metrics
              
              # Report to external monitoring
              curl -X POST https://statuspage.anihpj.com/api/health \
                -d '{"cluster": "anihpj-production", "status": "healthy"}'
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

Command:

# Create clusterrole for health endpoint access
kubectl create clusterrole health-check-endpoints \
  --verb=get \
  --non-resource-url=/healthz,/healthz/*,/readyz,/readyz/*,/livez,/livez/*

# Create clusterrole for metrics access
kubectl create clusterrole metrics-reader \
  --verb=get \
  --non-resource-url=/metrics,/metrics/*

# Combined health and metrics access
kubectl create clusterrole cluster-health-reader \
  --verb=get \
  --non-resource-url=/healthz,/healthz/*,/readyz,/readyz/*,/livez,/livez/*,/metrics

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/health-check-endpoints created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/metrics-reader created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/cluster-health-reader created

✅ Verification:

# Verify non-resource URL access
kubectl describe clusterrole cluster-health-reader

# Test access to health endpoints
kubectl auth can-i get /healthz \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:health-checker

# Verify no resource access
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:health-checker
Name:         cluster-health-reader
Labels:       <none>
Annotations:  <none>
PolicyRule:
  Resources  Non-Resource URLs                                    Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------  -----------------                                    --------------  -----
             [/healthz /healthz/* /readyz /readyz/* /livez /livez/* /metrics]  []              [get]

yes

no - no RBAC policy matched
Example 6: Create Aggregated ClusterRole for Extensibility

Scenario: Create aggregated ClusterRoles that automatically combine permissions from multiple ClusterRoles, allowing modular RBAC management for the anihpj project as it grows.

📄 Context - Modular RBAC Strategy:

# anihpj RBAC module architecture
#
# Base roles (labeled for aggregation):
# ├── jobpost-pods-reader      (rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true")
# ├── jobpost-deployments-reader (rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true")
# ├── jobpost-services-reader  (rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true")
# └── jobpost-logs-reader      (rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true")
#
# Aggregated role:
# └── jobpost-viewer-aggregate (aggregates all above)
#
# Benefits:
# - Add new permissions without modifying existing bindings
# - Module-based permission grants
# - Easy to audit and extend

📄 Setup - Component ClusterRoles (rbac/components/):

# rbac/components/pods-reader.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: jobpost-pods-reader
  labels:
    rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true"
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/status"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
# rbac/components/deployments-reader.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: jobpost-deployments-reader
  labels:
    rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true"
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
# rbac/components/logs-reader.yaml  
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: jobpost-logs-reader
  labels:
    rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer: "true"
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]

Command:

# First, create the component roles
kubectl create -f rbac/components/

# Create aggregated clusterrole that combines all component roles
kubectl create clusterrole jobpost-viewer-aggregate \
  --aggregation-rule="rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer=true"

# View the aggregated rules (empty initially, populated by controller)
kubectl get clusterrole jobpost-viewer-aggregate -o yaml

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-pods-reader created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-deployments-reader created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-logs-reader created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-viewer-aggregate created

✅ Verification:

# Check aggregation rule
kubectl get clusterrole jobpost-viewer-aggregate -o jsonpath='{.aggregationRule}'

# Wait for aggregation and verify combined rules
kubectl describe clusterrole jobpost-viewer-aggregate

# Test that permissions from all components are aggregated
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:default:viewer-sa
kubectl auth can-i get deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:default:viewer-sa
kubectl auth can-i get pods/log --as=system:serviceaccount:default:viewer-sa
# Aggregation rule:
{"clusterRoleSelectors":[{"matchLabels":{"rbac.anihpj.io/aggregate-to-viewer":"true"}}]}

# Aggregated ClusterRole with combined rules:
Name:         jobpost-viewer-aggregate
Labels:       <none>
Annotations:  <none>
PolicyRule:
  Resources                      Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------                      -----------------  --------------  -----
  pods, pods/status              []                 []              [get list watch]
  pods/log                       []                 []              [get list]
  deployments.apps               []                 []              [get list watch]
  replicasets.apps               []                 []              [get list watch]

# All component permissions available:
yes
yes
yes

kubectl create clusterrolebinding

CREATE RBAC Security Binding

Create a cluster role binding for a particular cluster role

The kubectl create clusterrolebinding command creates a ClusterRoleBinding that grants the permissions defined in a ClusterRole to a user, group, or service account across all namespaces in the cluster.

For the anihpj Django project, ClusterRoleBindings are used to:

  • Grant DevOps team admin access across all environments (dev, staging, production)
  • Allow CI/CD service accounts to deploy to multiple namespaces
  • Enable monitoring systems to scrape metrics cluster-wide
  • Bind external identity providers (OIDC) to Kubernetes roles

Syntax

# Bind clusterrole to a user
kubectl create clusterrolebinding NAME --clusterrole=ROLE --user=USER

# Bind clusterrole to a group
kubectl create clusterrolebinding NAME --clusterrole=ROLE --group=GROUP

# Bind clusterrole to a service account
kubectl create clusterrolebinding NAME --clusterrole=ROLE --serviceaccount=NAMESPACE:NAME

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--clusterrolestringClusterRole to bind (required)
--userstring[]Usernames to bind (can specify multiple)
--groupstring[]Groups to bind (can specify multiple)
--serviceaccountstring[]Service accounts in namespace:name format
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|name
--save-configboolSave configuration in annotation
--validatestringValidation mode: strict|warn|ignore

Subject Types

SubjectKindDescription
--userUserHuman user authenticated via certificates, tokens, or OIDC
--groupGroupCollection of users (e.g., system:masters, developers)
--serviceaccountServiceAccountIn-cluster identity for pods and workloads

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0ClusterRoleBinding created successfully
1Error (already exists, clusterrole not found, permission denied)

📁 Context: Service Account Architecture for anihpj

Identity Management: The anihpj project uses dedicated service accounts for different workloads, each bound to appropriate ClusterRoles for least-privilege access.
Service Account Mapping
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Service Account → ClusterRole Bindings                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  ServiceAccount                    ClusterRole              Namespaces           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│  ci-cd:github-actions         →    jobpost-deployer         all namespaces      │
│  monitoring:prometheus        →    jobpost-viewer           all namespaces      │
│  monitoring:grafana           →    jobpost-viewer           all namespaces      │
│  backup:velero               →    jobpost-backup-admin     all namespaces      │
│  logging:fluentd             →    jobpost-log-reader       all namespaces      │
│                                                                                  │
│  User/Group Bindings:                                                            │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│  user: admin@anihpj.io       →    cluster-admin            all namespaces      │
│  group: devops-team          →    jobpost-admin            all namespaces      │
│  group: developers           →    jobpost-viewer           dev only (Role)     │
│  oidc: github:anihpj-org     →    jobpost-deployer         staging, prod       │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Bind ClusterRole to Service Account for CI/CD

Scenario: Grant the GitHub Actions service account deployment permissions across all namespaces to enable automated deployments of the jobpost application.

📄 Context - CI/CD ServiceAccount (ci-cd/serviceaccount.yaml):

# Service account for GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: ci-cd
  labels:
    purpose: automation
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: github-actions
  namespace: ci-cd
  labels:
    app: github-actions
    purpose: ci-cd-deployment
  annotations:
    description: "Service account for GitHub Actions to deploy jobpost"
    github.com/repository: "anihpj/jobpost"
---
# Secret for ServiceAccount token (for external access)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: github-actions-token
  namespace: ci-cd
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/service-account.name: github-actions
type: kubernetes.io/service-account-token

Command:

# First create the namespace and service account
kubectl create -f ci-cd/serviceaccount.yaml

# Create the clusterrolebinding for CI/CD
kubectl create clusterrolebinding github-actions-deployer \
  --clusterrole=jobpost-deployer \
  --serviceaccount=ci-cd:github-actions

Output:

namespace/ci-cd created
serviceaccount/github-actions created
secret/github-actions-token created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/github-actions-deployer created

✅ Verification:

# Verify the binding
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding github-actions-deployer

# Test permissions in different namespaces
kubectl auth can-i update deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:ci-cd:github-actions \
  -n anihpj-production

kubectl auth can-i update deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:ci-cd:github-actions \
  -n anihpj-staging

# Export kubeconfig for GitHub Actions
kubectl config set-credentials github-actions \
  --token=$(kubectl get secret github-actions-token -n ci-cd -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d)
Name:         github-actions-deployer
Labels:       <none>
Annotations:  <none>
Role:
  Kind:  ClusterRole
  Name:  jobpost-deployer
Subjects:
  Kind            Name            Namespace
  ----            ----            ---------
  ServiceAccount  github-actions  ci-cd

yes
yes
Example 2: Bind ClusterRole to User Group for DevOps Team

Scenario: Grant the DevOps team full admin access to all jobpost resources across all namespaces using group-based binding with OIDC integration.

📄 Context - Azure AD/Entra ID OIDC Configuration:

# kube-apiserver OIDC configuration for Azure AD
# Located in: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
#
# --oidc-issuer-url=https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0
# --oidc-client-id=<app-registration-client-id>
# --oidc-username-claim=email
# --oidc-groups-claim=groups
# --oidc-username-prefix=oidc:
# --oidc-groups-prefix=oidc:
#
# Azure AD Group Mapping:
# ├── oidc:devops-team      → DevOps engineers (full admin)
# ├── oidc:developers       → Development team (read + deploy to dev)
# ├── oidc:qa-team          → QA engineers (read-only staging)
# └── oidc:security-team    → Security auditors (read all + audit)
#
# Team members (example):
# devops-team:
#   - alice@anihpj.io (Lead DevOps)
#   - bob@anihpj.io (Senior SRE)
#   - carol@anihpj.io (Platform Engineer)

Command:

# Create clusterrolebinding for DevOps team group
kubectl create clusterrolebinding devops-admin \
  --clusterrole=jobpost-admin \
  --group=oidc:devops-team

# Also bind to the cluster-admin role for full cluster access
kubectl create clusterrolebinding devops-cluster-admin \
  --clusterrole=cluster-admin \
  --group=oidc:devops-team

Output:

clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/devops-admin created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/devops-cluster-admin created

✅ Verification:

# Verify group binding
kubectl get clusterrolebinding devops-admin -o yaml

# Test permissions for a user in the group
kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --as=oidc:alice@anihpj.io \
  --as-group=oidc:devops-team \
  -n anihpj-production

# List all bindings for the group
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name == "oidc:devops-team") | .metadata.name'
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: devops-admin
  creationTimestamp: "2026-03-01T11:00:00Z"
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: jobpost-admin
subjects:
- apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Group
  name: oidc:devops-team

yes

"devops-admin"
"devops-cluster-admin"
Example 3: Bind ClusterRole to Multiple Service Accounts

Scenario: Grant monitoring permissions to both Prometheus and Grafana service accounts in a single binding for efficient RBAC management.

📄 Context - Monitoring Stack (monitoring/stack.yaml):

# Monitoring namespace and service accounts
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: monitoring
  labels:
    purpose: observability
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: prometheus
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: grafana
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: grafana
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alertmanager
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: alertmanager
---
# Prometheus deployment that scrapes jobpost metrics
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: prometheus
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: prometheus
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: prometheus
      containers:
      - name: prometheus
        image: prom/prometheus:v2.48.0
        args:
        - '--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml'
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /etc/prometheus
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: prometheus-config

Command:

# Create monitoring resources first
kubectl create -f monitoring/stack.yaml

# Bind viewer role to multiple service accounts
kubectl create clusterrolebinding monitoring-viewers \
  --clusterrole=jobpost-viewer \
  --serviceaccount=monitoring:prometheus \
  --serviceaccount=monitoring:grafana \
  --serviceaccount=monitoring:alertmanager

Output:

namespace/monitoring created
serviceaccount/prometheus created
serviceaccount/grafana created
serviceaccount/alertmanager created
deployment.apps/prometheus created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/monitoring-viewers created

✅ Verification:

# Verify all service accounts are in the binding
kubectl get clusterrolebinding monitoring-viewers -o jsonpath='{.subjects[*].name}'

# Test each service account's permissions
for sa in prometheus grafana alertmanager; do
  echo "Testing $sa:"
  kubectl auth can-i list pods \
    --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:$sa \
    -n anihpj-production
done

# Verify Prometheus can discover jobpost services
kubectl auth can-i list services \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:prometheus \
  --all-namespaces
prometheus grafana alertmanager

Testing prometheus:
yes
Testing grafana:
yes
Testing alertmanager:
yes

yes
Example 4: Bind ClusterRole to Individual User

Scenario: Grant temporary admin access to an external consultant for auditing the jobpost Kubernetes deployment.

📄 Context - Consultant Access Request:

# Consultant Access Ticket: SEC-2026-0301
#
# Requester: security-team
# Consultant: audit@securityfirm.com
# Duration: 2026-03-01 to 2026-03-15
# Purpose: Security audit of anihpj Kubernetes deployment
#
# Access Required:
# - Read all resources in all namespaces
# - View secrets (for audit purposes)
# - Access pod logs
# - View RBAC configurations
#
# Restrictions:
# - No write access
# - No delete access
# - Access will be revoked on 2026-03-16
#
# Audit Scope:
# - Review RBAC configurations
# - Check secrets management
# - Verify network policies
# - Assess pod security standards
# - Review JobPost data handling (Skills, User, Author, Location models)

Command:

# Create a read-only clusterrole for auditing (includes secrets read)
kubectl create clusterrole security-auditor \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,pods/log,services,deployments.apps,secrets,configmaps,\
networkpolicies.networking.k8s.io,clusterroles.rbac.authorization.k8s.io,\
clusterrolebindings.rbac.authorization.k8s.io,roles.rbac.authorization.k8s.io,\
rolebindings.rbac.authorization.k8s.io,serviceaccounts

# Bind to the consultant user
kubectl create clusterrolebinding consultant-audit-access \
  --clusterrole=security-auditor \
  --user=audit@securityfirm.com

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/security-auditor created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/consultant-audit-access created

✅ Verification:

# Verify user binding
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding consultant-audit-access

# Test read permissions
kubectl auth can-i get secrets \
  --as=audit@securityfirm.com \
  -n anihpj-production

# Verify no write permissions
kubectl auth can-i create pods \
  --as=audit@securityfirm.com \
  -n anihpj-production

# Schedule automatic deletion (using a CronJob or reminder)
echo "REMINDER: Delete clusterrolebinding consultant-audit-access on 2026-03-16"
# kubectl delete clusterrolebinding consultant-audit-access  # Run on 2026-03-16
Name:         consultant-audit-access
Labels:       <none>
Annotations:  <none>
Role:
  Kind:  ClusterRole
  Name:  security-auditor
Subjects:
  Kind  Name                     Namespace
  ----  ----                     ---------
  User  audit@securityfirm.com

yes

no
Example 5: Bind Built-in ClusterRole for View Access

Scenario: Grant developers read-only access to cluster resources using the built-in 'view' ClusterRole, allowing them to inspect jobpost deployments without modification rights.

📄 Context - Developer Onboarding Process:

# Developer Onboarding Checklist for anihpj project
#
# Step 1: Add developer to GitHub team "anihpj-developers"
# Step 2: Configure OIDC group membership in Azure AD
# Step 3: Create ClusterRoleBinding for cluster-wide view access
# Step 4: Create RoleBinding for write access in dev namespace only
#
# Built-in ClusterRoles available:
# ├── cluster-admin  → Full control (DANGEROUS - don't use)
# ├── admin          → Full control within namespace
# ├── edit           → Read/write most resources (no RBAC)
# └── view           → Read-only access (recommended for new devs)
#
# New developers get:
# - cluster-wide: view (read-only everywhere)
# - anihpj-dev: edit (full dev namespace access)
#
# After 30 days probation:
# - anihpj-staging: edit (staging access)

Command:

# Bind built-in 'view' clusterrole to developers group
kubectl create clusterrolebinding developers-view \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --group=oidc:developers

# For a specific new developer
kubectl create clusterrolebinding new-dev-alice-view \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --user=alice.newdev@anihpj.io

Output:

clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developers-view created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/new-dev-alice-view created

✅ Verification:

# Check what the built-in 'view' role allows
kubectl describe clusterrole view | head -40

# Test developer permissions
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=alice.newdev@anihpj.io -n anihpj-production
kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=alice.newdev@anihpj.io -n anihpj-production
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=alice.newdev@anihpj.io -n anihpj-production

# Compare with edit role (what they'll get in dev namespace later)
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=alice.newdev@anihpj.io -n anihpj-dev
Name:         view
Labels:       kubernetes.io/bootstrapping=rbac-defaults
              rbac.authorization.k8s.io/aggregate-to-edit=true
Annotations:  rbac.authorization.kubernetes.io/autoupdate: true
PolicyRule:
  Resources                                    Non-Resource URLs  Verbs
  ---------                                    -----------------  -----
  bindings                                     []                 [get list watch]
  configmaps                                   []                 [get list watch]
  endpoints                                    []                 [get list watch]
  events                                       []                 [get list watch]
  limitranges                                  []                 [get list watch]
  namespaces                                   []                 [get list watch]
  persistentvolumeclaims                       []                 [get list watch]
  pods                                         []                 [get list watch]
  replicationcontrollers                       []                 [get list watch]
  resourcequotas                               []                 [get list watch]
  serviceaccounts                              []                 [get list watch]
  services                                     []                 [get list watch]
  ...

yes
no  - secrets are excluded from view role
no

no - need edit role in anihpj-dev namespace
Example 6: Create Binding with Dry-Run for GitOps Review

Scenario: Generate ClusterRoleBinding YAML for review and Git commit before applying, following GitOps practices for the anihpj infrastructure repository.

📄 Context - GitOps Repository Structure:

# anihpj-infrastructure repository structure
anihpj-infrastructure/
├── README.md
├── clusters/
│   └── production/
│       ├── flux-system/
│       └── kustomization.yaml
├── infrastructure/
│   ├── rbac/
│   │   ├── kustomization.yaml
│   │   ├── clusterroles/
│   │   │   ├── jobpost-admin.yaml
│   │   │   ├── jobpost-viewer.yaml
│   │   │   └── jobpost-deployer.yaml
│   │   └── clusterrolebindings/
│   │       ├── devops-admin.yaml           # ← Generate this
│   │       ├── monitoring-viewers.yaml
│   │       └── ci-cd-deployer.yaml
│   ├── monitoring/
│   └── namespaces/
└── apps/
    ├── anihpj-dev/
    ├── anihpj-staging/
    └── anihpj-production/

Command:

# Generate YAML for GitOps without creating the resource
kubectl create clusterrolebinding backup-operator-binding \
  --clusterrole=jobpost-admin \
  --serviceaccount=backup:velero \
  --dry-run=client \
  -o yaml > infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml

# Add metadata for GitOps tracking
cat <<EOF >> infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: flux
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
  annotations:
    description: "Velero backup operator access for disaster recovery"
    owner: devops-team
    created-by: kubectl-generate
EOF

# View the generated file
cat infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml

Output:

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: null
  name: backup-operator-binding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: jobpost-admin
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: velero
  namespace: backup
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: flux
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
  annotations:
    description: "Velero backup operator access for disaster recovery"
    owner: devops-team
    created-by: kubectl-generate

✅ Verification:

# Validate the generated YAML
kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml

# Verify with kubeval or kubeconform
kubeconform -strict infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml

# Commit to Git for GitOps
cd anihpj-infrastructure
git add infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml
git commit -m "Add ClusterRoleBinding for Velero backup operator"
git push origin main

# Flux will automatically apply the changes
flux reconcile kustomization infrastructure --with-source
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/backup-operator-binding created (dry run)

infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml - ClusterRoleBinding backup-operator-binding is valid

[main abc1234] Add ClusterRoleBinding for Velero backup operator
 1 file changed, 18 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 infrastructure/rbac/clusterrolebindings/backup-operator.yaml

► annotating GitRepository flux-system in flux-system namespace
✔ GitRepository annotated
◎ waiting for Kustomization reconciliation
✔ Kustomization reconciliation completed
✔ applied revision main/abc1234

kubectl create configmap

CREATE Configuration Settings Environment

Create a config map from a local file, directory, or literal value

The kubectl create configmap command creates a ConfigMap resource that stores non-confidential configuration data as key-value pairs. Pods can consume ConfigMaps as environment variables, command-line arguments, or as configuration files in a volume.

For the anihpj Django project, ConfigMaps are essential for managing:

  • Django settings (DEBUG, ALLOWED_HOSTS, TIME_ZONE, LANGUAGE_CODE)
  • Database connection parameters (excluding passwords - use Secrets)
  • Nginx configuration for serving static files and proxying to Gunicorn
  • Feature flags for Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models
  • Logging configuration and log levels

Syntax

# Create from literal values
kubectl create configmap NAME --from-literal=key1=value1 --from-literal=key2=value2

# Create from file (key name = filename)
kubectl create configmap NAME --from-file=path/to/file

# Create from file with custom key name
kubectl create configmap NAME --from-file=KEY_NAME=path/to/file

# Create from directory (each file becomes a key)
kubectl create configmap NAME --from-file=path/to/directory

# Create from env file
kubectl create configmap NAME --from-env-file=path/to/.env

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--from-literalstring[]Key=value pairs to store in the ConfigMap
--from-filestring[]File or directory path (key=filename or key=filepath)
--from-env-filestringPath to .env file with KEY=VALUE pairs
--append-hashboolAppend hash to ConfigMap name for immutable deployments
-n, --namespacestringTarget namespace (default: current context)
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|name
--save-configboolSave configuration in annotation
--validatestringValidation mode: strict|warn|ignore

ConfigMap Usage Methods

MethodUsageBest For
envFromLoad all keys as environment variablesDjango settings, feature flags
env.valueFromLoad specific key as environment variableIndividual settings
volumeMount as files in containerNginx config, Django settings.py
command/argsUse in container commandStartup scripts

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0ConfigMap created successfully
1Error (already exists, file not found, invalid format)

📁 Context: Django Configuration Files for anihpj

Configuration Strategy: The anihpj project uses ConfigMaps for environment-specific settings and file-based configurations.
Configuration File Structure
anihpj/
├── config/
│   ├── django/
│   │   ├── settings-base.env       # Base Django settings
│   │   ├── settings-dev.env        # Development overrides
│   │   ├── settings-staging.env    # Staging overrides
│   │   └── settings-production.env # Production overrides
│   ├── nginx/
│   │   ├── nginx.conf              # Main Nginx config
│   │   └── django.conf             # Django proxy config
│   └── gunicorn/
│       └── gunicorn.conf.py        # Gunicorn configuration
├── jobpost/
│   └── models.py                   # Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
└── anihpj/
    └── settings.py                 # Django settings (reads from env)

Examples

Example 1: Create ConfigMap from Literal Values for Django Settings

Scenario: Create a ConfigMap containing basic Django configuration settings for the jobpost application, including debug mode, allowed hosts, and database settings.

📄 Context - Django Settings Design (anihpj/settings.py):

# anihpj/settings.py - Kubernetes-aware Django settings
import os
from pathlib import Path

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent

# Security settings from environment (via ConfigMap/Secret)
SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY', 'django-insecure-default')
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'false').lower() == 'true'
ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_HOSTS', 'localhost').split(',')

# CSRF settings for Kubernetes ingress
CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS = os.environ.get('CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS', '').split(',')

# Database configuration (password from Secret, rest from ConfigMap)
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': os.environ.get('DB_ENGINE', 'django.db.backends.postgresql'),
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DB_NAME', 'jobpost_db'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DB_USER', 'jobpost_user'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DB_PASSWORD', ''),  # From Secret
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DB_HOST', 'postgres-service'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DB_PORT', '5432'),
    }
}

# Internationalization (from ConfigMap)
LANGUAGE_CODE = os.environ.get('LANGUAGE_CODE', 'en-us')
TIME_ZONE = os.environ.get('TIME_ZONE', 'UTC')
USE_I18N = os.environ.get('USE_I18N', 'true').lower() == 'true'
USE_TZ = os.environ.get('USE_TZ', 'true').lower() == 'true'

# Static and media files
STATIC_URL = os.environ.get('STATIC_URL', '/static/')
STATIC_ROOT = os.environ.get('STATIC_ROOT', BASE_DIR / 'staticfiles')
MEDIA_URL = os.environ.get('MEDIA_URL', '/media/')
MEDIA_ROOT = os.environ.get('MEDIA_ROOT', BASE_DIR / 'media')

# Feature flags for JobPost models
ENABLE_SKILLS_AUTOCOMPLETE = os.environ.get('ENABLE_SKILLS_AUTOCOMPLETE', 'true').lower() == 'true'
ENABLE_LOCATION_SEARCH = os.environ.get('ENABLE_LOCATION_SEARCH', 'true').lower() == 'true'
MAX_JOBPOST_PER_AUTHOR = int(os.environ.get('MAX_JOBPOST_PER_AUTHOR', '50'))

Command:

# Create ConfigMap for production Django settings
kubectl create configmap django-config \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DEBUG=false \
  --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS=jobpost.example.com,www.jobpost.example.com \
  --from-literal=CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS=https://jobpost.example.com \
  --from-literal=DB_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql \
  --from-literal=DB_NAME=jobpost_db \
  --from-literal=DB_USER=jobpost_user \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres-service \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 \
  --from-literal=LANGUAGE_CODE=en-us \
  --from-literal=TIME_ZONE=UTC \
  --from-literal=STATIC_URL=/static/ \
  --from-literal=MEDIA_URL=/media/ \
  --from-literal=ENABLE_SKILLS_AUTOCOMPLETE=true \
  --from-literal=ENABLE_LOCATION_SEARCH=true \
  --from-literal=MAX_JOBPOST_PER_AUTHOR=50

Output:

configmap/django-config created

✅ Verification:

# View the created ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap django-config -n anihpj-production -o yaml

# Describe for detailed info
kubectl describe configmap django-config -n anihpj-production

# View specific keys
kubectl get configmap django-config -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.data.DEBUG}'
kubectl get configmap django-config -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.data.ALLOWED_HOSTS}'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: jobpost.example.com,www.jobpost.example.com
  CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS: https://jobpost.example.com
  DB_ENGINE: django.db.backends.postgresql
  DB_HOST: postgres-service
  DB_NAME: jobpost_db
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_USER: jobpost_user
  DEBUG: "false"
  ENABLE_LOCATION_SEARCH: "true"
  ENABLE_SKILLS_AUTOCOMPLETE: "true"
  LANGUAGE_CODE: en-us
  MAX_JOBPOST_PER_AUTHOR: "50"
  MEDIA_URL: /media/
  STATIC_URL: /static/
  TIME_ZONE: UTC
Example 2: Create ConfigMap from Environment File

Scenario: Create a ConfigMap from a .env file for the development environment, making it easy to maintain configuration as code.

📄 Context - Environment File (config/django/settings-dev.env):

# config/django/settings-dev.env
# Development environment settings for anihpj jobpost app
# 
# Usage: kubectl create configmap django-config --from-env-file=settings-dev.env

# Django core settings
DEBUG=true
ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost,127.0.0.1,jobpost-dev.local
CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS=http://localhost:8000,http://jobpost-dev.local

# Database settings (dev uses local PostgreSQL)
DB_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql
DB_NAME=jobpost_dev
DB_USER=postgres
DB_HOST=postgres-dev-service
DB_PORT=5432

# Logging (verbose for development)
LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
DJANGO_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
SQL_DEBUG=true

# Cache settings (disable in dev for easier debugging)
CACHE_BACKEND=django.core.cache.backends.dummy.DummyCache

# Feature flags (enable all features in dev)
ENABLE_SKILLS_AUTOCOMPLETE=true
ENABLE_LOCATION_SEARCH=true
ENABLE_USER_ANALYTICS=true
ENABLE_JOBPOST_RECOMMENDATIONS=true
MAX_JOBPOST_PER_AUTHOR=100

# Email (use console backend in dev)
EMAIL_BACKEND=django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend

# Static files
STATIC_URL=/static/
MEDIA_URL=/media/

# Development-specific
INTERNAL_IPS=127.0.0.1
SHOW_DEBUG_TOOLBAR=true

Command:

# Create ConfigMap from env file for development
kubectl create configmap django-dev-config \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --from-env-file=config/django/settings-dev.env

# Create with namespace if it doesn't exist
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create configmap django-dev-config \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --from-env-file=config/django/settings-dev.env

Output:

configmap/django-dev-config created

✅ Verification:

# Count the keys in the ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap django-dev-config -n anihpj-dev -o json | jq '.data | keys | length'

# Verify specific development settings
kubectl get configmap django-dev-config -n anihpj-dev -o jsonpath='{.data.DEBUG}'
kubectl get configmap django-dev-config -n anihpj-dev -o jsonpath='{.data.LOG_LEVEL}'

# Export back to file for comparison
kubectl get configmap django-dev-config -n anihpj-dev -o go-template='{{range $k,$v := .data}}{{$k}}={{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}' > exported-dev.env
diff config/django/settings-dev.env exported-dev.env
24

true
DEBUG

# Files match (accounting for comment removal)
Example 3: Create ConfigMap from Configuration File

Scenario: Create a ConfigMap from an Nginx configuration file for proxying requests to the Django Gunicorn server and serving static files.

📄 Context - Nginx Configuration (config/nginx/django.conf):

# config/nginx/django.conf
# Nginx configuration for anihpj Django jobpost application
# Handles: static files, media files, proxy to Gunicorn

upstream django_backend {
    # Gunicorn workers - will be resolved to pod IPs by Kubernetes DNS
    server 127.0.0.1:8000;
    keepalive 32;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name jobpost.example.com www.jobpost.example.com;
