kubectl get namespaces -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj -o wide

# Show namespace labels
kubectl get namespaces -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj --show-labels

# Count resources in each namespace
for NS in anihpj-dev anihpj-staging anihpj-production; do
  echo "=== $NS ==="
  kubectl get all -n $NS --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l || echo "0"
done
NAME                 STATUS   AGE
anihpj-dev           Active   30s
anihpj-staging       Active   25s
anihpj-production    Active   20s

NAME                 STATUS   AGE   LABELS
anihpj-dev           Active   30s   app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj,environment=dev
anihpj-staging       Active   25s   app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj,environment=staging
anihpj-production    Active   20s   app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj,environment=production

=== anihpj-dev ===
0
=== anihpj-staging ===
0
=== anihpj-production ===
0
Example 3: Create Namespace with Resource Quota

Scenario: Create a development namespace with resource quotas to prevent excessive resource usage.

📄 Context - Namespace with Resource Quota Definition:

# k8s/namespaces/dev-with-quota.yaml
# Development namespace with resource limits
# Prevents runaway pods from consuming cluster resources
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    environment: dev
    team: jobpost
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-quota
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"           # Total CPU requests
    requests.memory: "8Gi"      # Total memory requests
    limits.cpu: "8"             # Total CPU limits
    limits.memory: "16Gi"       # Total memory limits
    pods: "20"                  # Max pods
    services: "10"              # Max services
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5" # Max PVCs
    secrets: "20"               # Max secrets
    configmaps: "20"            # Max configmaps
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: dev-limits
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    type: Container

Command:

# Create namespace first
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# Apply resource quota
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-quota
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
EOF

Output:

namespace/anihpj-dev created
resourcequota/dev-quota created

✅ Verification:

# Check resource quota
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-dev

# Describe quota with usage
kubectl describe resourcequota dev-quota -n anihpj-dev

# Check LimitRange
kubectl get limitrange -n anihpj-dev

# Test quota by deploying (see quota enforcement)
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx -n anihpj-dev
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev
kubectl describe resourcequota dev-quota -n anihpj-dev
NAME        AGE   REQUEST                                           LIMIT
dev-quota   10s   pods: 0/20, requests.cpu: 0/4, requests.memory: 0/8Gi

Name:                   dev-quota
Namespace:              anihpj-dev
Resource                Used  Hard
--------                ----  ----
pods                    0     20
requests.cpu            0     4
requests.memory         0     8Gi
services                0     10

NAME         CREATED AT
dev-limits   2024-01-15T10:00:00Z

pod/test-pod created

NAME       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
test-pod   1/1     Running   0          10s

Resource                Used    Hard
--------                ----    ----
pods                    1       20
requests.cpu            100m    4
requests.memory         128Mi   8Gi
Example 4: Create Namespace with Network Policy

Scenario: Create a production namespace with default network policies to isolate workloads.

📄 Context - Namespace with Network Isolation:

# k8s/namespaces/production-isolated.yaml
# Production namespace with network isolation
# Default deny all ingress/egress, then allow specific traffic
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  labels:
    environment: production
    team: jobpost
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
---
# Default deny all ingress traffic
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  podSelector: {}  # Applies to all pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
---
# Default deny all egress traffic
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-egress
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
---
# Allow DNS resolution for all pods
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
---
# Allow traffic from ingress controller to web pods
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-to-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-web
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8000

Command:

# Create namespace
kubectl create namespace anihpj-production

# Apply network policies
kubectl apply -f k8s/namespaces/production-isolated.yaml

# Or create inline default deny
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
EOF

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production created
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/default-deny-ingress created
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/default-deny-egress created
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/allow-dns created
networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/allow-ingress-to-web created

✅ Verification:

# List network policies
kubectl get networkpolicies -n anihpj-production

# Describe policies
kubectl describe networkpolicy default-deny-ingress -n anihpj-production

# Test network isolation
kubectl run test-curl --image=curlimages/curl -n anihpj-production --rm -it -- \
  curl -v --connect-timeout 5 http://jobpost-web.anihpj-production:8000
NAME                   POD-SELECTOR        AGE
default-deny-ingress   <none>              10s
default-deny-egress    <none>              10s
allow-dns              <none>              10s
allow-ingress-to-web   app=jobpost-web     10s

Name:         default-deny-ingress
Namespace:    anihpj-production
Spec:
  PodSelector:     <none> (Coverage: all pods in the namespace)
  Allowing ingress traffic:
    <none> (Selected pods are isolated for ingress connectivity)
  Not affecting egress traffic
  Policy Types: Ingress
Example 5: Create Namespace with RBAC Bindings

Scenario: Create a namespace with pre-configured RBAC allowing developers to deploy but not delete resources.

📄 Context - Namespace with RBAC Setup:

# k8s/namespaces/staging-with-rbac.yaml
# Staging namespace with RBAC for different roles
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-staging
  labels:
    environment: staging
    team: jobpost
---
# Role for developers - can deploy and view, but not delete
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer
  namespace: anihpj-staging
rules:
- apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
  resources: ["deployments", "pods", "services", "configmaps", "jobs"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/log", "pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["get", "create"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]  # Read-only for secrets
---
# Role for QA - can view everything, run jobs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: qa-tester
  namespace: anihpj-staging
rules:
- apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["batch"]
  resources: ["jobs"]
  verbs: ["create", "delete"]
---
# Bind developer role to dev team
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: developer-binding
  namespace: anihpj-staging
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: jobpost-developers
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# Bind qa role to QA team
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: qa-binding
  namespace: anihpj-staging
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: jobpost-qa
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: qa-tester
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command:

# Create namespace with RBAC
kubectl create namespace anihpj-staging
kubectl apply -f k8s/namespaces/staging-with-rbac.yaml

# Or create individual components
kubectl create namespace anihpj-staging
kubectl create role developer -n anihpj-staging \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch \
  --resource=deployments,pods,services,configmaps
kubectl create rolebinding developer-binding -n anihpj-staging \
  --role=developer \
  --group=jobpost-developers

Output:

namespace/anihpj-staging created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/qa-tester created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer-binding created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/qa-binding created

✅ Verification:

# List RBAC in namespace
kubectl get roles,rolebindings -n anihpj-staging

# Test developer permissions (using impersonation)
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n anihpj-staging --as-group=jobpost-developers
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments -n anihpj-staging --as-group=jobpost-developers
kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n anihpj-staging --as-group=jobpost-developers

# Test QA permissions
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-staging --as-group=jobpost-qa
kubectl auth can-i create jobs -n anihpj-staging --as-group=jobpost-qa
NAME                          CREATED AT
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer  2024-01-15T10:00:00Z
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/qa-tester  2024-01-15T10:00:00Z

NAME                                        ROLE            AGE
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer-binding  Role/developer  10s
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/qa-binding         Role/qa-tester  10s

yes
no
yes

yes
yes
Example 6: Create Namespace for Monitoring Stack

Scenario: Create a dedicated namespace for monitoring infrastructure with Prometheus and Grafana.

📄 Context - Monitoring Namespace Setup:

# k8s/namespaces/monitoring.yaml
# Monitoring namespace with cross-namespace access
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: monitoring
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: monitoring
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: baseline
  annotations:
    description: "Monitoring infrastructure - Prometheus, Grafana, Alertmanager"
---
# ServiceAccount for Prometheus to scrape all namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
---
# ClusterRole for cluster-wide scraping
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: prometheus-scraper
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes", "nodes/proxy", "services", "endpoints", "pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["extensions", "networking.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["ingresses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- nonResourceURLs: ["/metrics"]
  verbs: ["get"]
---
# Bind to prometheus service account
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: prometheus-scraper-binding
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: prometheus-scraper
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# ServiceMonitor to scrape anihpj pods (requires prometheus-operator)
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    release: prometheus
spec:
  namespaceSelector:
    matchNames:
    - anihpj-production
    - anihpj-staging
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-web
  endpoints:
  - port: metrics
    interval: 30s
    path: /metrics

Command:

# Create monitoring namespace
kubectl create namespace monitoring

# Label for pod security
kubectl label namespace monitoring \
  app.kubernetes.io/name=monitoring \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline

# Create Prometheus service account with cluster access
kubectl apply -f k8s/namespaces/monitoring.yaml

# Or use Helm for full monitoring stack
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
  --namespace monitoring \
  --set prometheus.prometheusSpec.serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues=false

Output:

namespace/monitoring created
namespace/monitoring labeled
serviceaccount/prometheus created
clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/prometheus-scraper created
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/prometheus-scraper-binding created
servicemonitor.monitoring.coreos.com/anihpj-web created

✅ Verification:

# Check monitoring namespace
kubectl get namespace monitoring --show-labels

# List monitoring resources
kubectl get all -n monitoring

# Verify Prometheus can access other namespaces
kubectl auth can-i list pods --all-namespaces \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:prometheus

# Check ServiceMonitor targets (if prometheus-operator is installed)
kubectl get servicemonitors -n monitoring

# Test metrics endpoint from anihpj
kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
  curl -s localhost:8000/metrics | head -10
NAME        STATUS   AGE   LABELS
monitoring  Active   30s   app.kubernetes.io/name=monitoring,pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline

NAME                                       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/prometheus-kube-prometheus-operato-0   1/1     Running   0          5m
pod/prometheus-grafana-5f4c8b7d4-abc12     1/1     Running   0          5m
pod/alertmanager-prometheus-kube-0         1/1     Running   0          5m

yes

NAME          AGE
anihpj-web    30s

# HELP django_http_requests_total Total count of HTTP requests
# TYPE django_http_requests_total counter
django_http_requests_total{method="GET",path="/api/jobs/"} 1523
django_http_requests_total{method="POST",path="/api/jobs/"} 234
# HELP django_db_query_duration Database query duration in seconds
# TYPE django_db_query_duration histogram

kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

CREATE High Availability Reliability Maintenance

Create a pod disruption budget with the specified name

The kubectl create poddisruptionbudget command creates a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) resource that limits the number of pods that can be simultaneously unavailable during voluntary disruptions like node drains, cluster upgrades, or rolling deployments.

For the anihpj Django project, PDBs ensure:

  • High availability during cluster maintenance
  • Minimum viable service during node upgrades
  • Protection against accidental mass pod termination
  • Zero-downtime deployments for Django web pods
  • Database connection stability during rolling updates
  • Celery worker availability for background job processing

Syntax

# Create PDB with minimum available pods
kubectl create pdb NAME --selector=SELECTOR --min-available=N

# Create PDB with maximum unavailable pods
kubectl create pdb NAME --selector=SELECTOR --max-unavailable=N

# Use percentage instead of absolute number
kubectl create pdb NAME --selector=SELECTOR --min-available=80%

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--selectorstringLabel selector to identify pods (key=value)
--min-availablestringMinimum pods that must be available (number or %)
--max-unavailablestringMaximum pods that can be unavailable (number or %)
-n, --namespacestringTarget namespace
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

PDB Strategy Guidelines

ScenarioReplicasRecommended PDBReasoning
Web tier3+minAvailable: 2Keep capacity during updates
Stateful3maxUnavailable: 1Quorum-based systems
Workers5+minAvailable: 50%Scale-based protection
Singleton1maxUnavailable: 0Never disrupt

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0PDB created successfully
1Error (invalid selector, both min/max specified)

📁 Context: High Availability Architecture for anihpj

Disruption Types: PDBs protect against voluntary disruptions (drains, updates) but NOT involuntary ones (node failures, OOM kills).
PDB Architecture Overview
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Pod Disruption Budget Architecture                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Voluntary Disruptions (PDB Protected)                                       │
│  ├── kubectl drain node                                                      │
│  ├── Cluster autoscaler scale-down                                          │
│  ├── Node pool upgrades                                                      │
│  ├── kubectl delete pod (respects PDB)                                      │
│  └── Rolling deployments                                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  Involuntary Disruptions (NOT PDB Protected)                                │
│  ├── Node crashes / hardware failure                                        │
│  ├── Kernel panic                                                            │
│  ├── Out of memory (OOM) kills                                              │
│  └── Force delete: kubectl delete pod --force                               │
│                                                                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-production namespace                                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                                       │
│  │ jobpost-web (3 replicas)         │◄── PDB: minAvailable=2                │
│  │   ├── pod-1 (node-a)             │    "Always keep 2 pods running"       │
│  │   ├── pod-2 (node-b)             │                                       │
│  │   └── pod-3 (node-c)             │                                       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                                       │
│  │ celery-worker (5 replicas)       │◄── PDB: maxUnavailable=2              │
│  │   ├── pod-1 through pod-5        │    "Allow up to 2 pods down"          │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                                       │
│  │ redis (3 replicas - StatefulSet) │◄── PDB: maxUnavailable=1              │
│  │   ├── redis-0 (master)           │    "Maintain quorum"                  │
│  │   ├── redis-1 (replica)          │                                       │
│  │   └── redis-2 (replica)          │                                       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                                       │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create PDB for Django Web Pods

Scenario: Create a PodDisruptionBudget to ensure at least 2 Django web pods are always available during cluster maintenance.

📄 Context - Django Deployment with PDB:

# k8s/production/deployment.yaml
# Django web deployment with 3 replicas
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost-web
    tier: frontend
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost-web
        tier: frontend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1Gi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5

Command:

# Create PDB for Django web pods
kubectl create pdb jobpost-web-pdb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --selector=app=jobpost-web \
  --min-available=2

Output:

poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-web-pdb created

✅ Verification:

# Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production

# Describe PDB details
kubectl describe pdb jobpost-web-pdb -n anihpj-production

# Test drain (will respect PDB)
kubectl drain node-1 --ignore-daemonsets --dry-run=client

# Verify pods are distributed across nodes
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost-web -o wide
NAME              MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
jobpost-web-pdb   2               N/A               1                     30s

Name:           jobpost-web-pdb
Namespace:      anihpj-production
Min available:  2
Selector:       app=jobpost-web
Status:
    Allowed disruptions:  1
    Current:              3
    Desired:              2
    Total:                3
Events:         

node/node-1 cordoned (dry-run)
evicting pod anihpj-production/jobpost-web-abc12 (dry-run)

NAME                           READY   STATUS    NODE     AGE
jobpost-web-abc12              1/1     Running   node-1   1h
jobpost-web-def34              1/1     Running   node-2   1h
jobpost-web-ghi56              1/1     Running   node-3   1h
Example 2: Create PDB with Percentage-Based Availability

Scenario: Create a PDB for Celery workers that scales with replica count, maintaining at least 60% availability.

📄 Context - Celery Worker Deployment:

# k8s/production/celery-worker.yaml
# Celery workers for background job processing
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: celery-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: celery-worker
    tier: worker
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: celery-worker
        tier: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "info"]
        env:
        - name: CELERY_BROKER_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: celery-secrets
              key: broker-url
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"

Command:

# Create PDB with percentage
kubectl create pdb celery-worker-pdb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --selector=app=celery-worker \
  --min-available=60%

Output:

poddisruptionbudget.policy/celery-worker-pdb created

✅ Verification:

# Check PDB with percentage
kubectl get pdb celery-worker-pdb -n anihpj-production -o yaml

# Calculate allowed disruptions
# 5 replicas * 60% = 3 must be available, so 2 can be unavailable
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production

# Scale up and verify PDB adjusts
kubectl scale deployment celery-worker -n anihpj-production --replicas=10
kubectl get pdb celery-worker-pdb -n anihpj-production
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: celery-worker-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  minAvailable: 60%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-worker
status:
  currentHealthy: 5
  desiredHealthy: 3
  disruptionsAllowed: 2
  expectedPods: 5

NAME                MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
celery-worker-pdb   60%             N/A               2                     30s
jobpost-web-pdb     2               N/A               1                     5m

NAME                MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
celery-worker-pdb   60%             N/A               4                     1m
# Now: 10 replicas * 60% = 6 must be available, 4 can be disrupted
Example 3: Create PDB for StatefulSet Database

Scenario: Create a PDB for Redis StatefulSet to maintain quorum during maintenance.

📄 Context - Redis StatefulSet Configuration:

# k8s/production/redis-statefulset.yaml
# Redis cluster with 3 nodes for session and cache storage
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: redis
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: redis
    tier: cache
spec:
  serviceName: redis-headless
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: redis
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: redis
        tier: cache
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: redis
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379
          name: redis
        volumeMounts:
        - name: redis-data
          mountPath: /data
        command:
        - redis-server
        - --appendonly
        - "yes"
        - --cluster-enabled
        - "yes"
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: redis-data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: fast-ssd
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi

Command:

# Create PDB with maxUnavailable for quorum protection
kubectl create pdb redis-pdb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --selector=app=redis \
  --max-unavailable=1

Output:

poddisruptionbudget.policy/redis-pdb created

✅ Verification:

# Verify PDB for StatefulSet
kubectl get pdb redis-pdb -n anihpj-production

# Check StatefulSet pods
kubectl get pods -l app=redis -n anihpj-production -o wide

# Test drain behavior - should allow only 1 eviction at a time
kubectl drain node-a --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Verify Redis cluster health
kubectl exec redis-0 -n anihpj-production -- redis-cli cluster nodes
NAME        MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
redis-pdb   N/A             1                 1                     30s

NAME      READY   STATUS    NODE     AGE
redis-0   1/1     Running   node-a   2h
redis-1   1/1     Running   node-b   2h
redis-2   1/1     Running   node-c   2h

node/node-a cordoned
evicting pod anihpj-production/redis-0
# Waits until redis-0 is rescheduled before continuing

abc123 192.168.1.10:6379@16379 master - 0 1705312345000 1 connected 0-5460
def456 192.168.1.11:6379@16379 master - 0 1705312345000 2 connected 5461-10922
ghi789 192.168.1.12:6379@16379 master - 0 1705312345000 3 connected 10923-16383
Example 4: Create Multiple PDBs via Manifest

Scenario: Create comprehensive PDBs for all anihpj components in a single manifest for GitOps deployment.

📄 Context - Complete PDB Configuration:

# k8s/production/pdb-all.yaml
# Pod Disruption Budgets for all anihpj components
# Apply with: kubectl apply -f k8s/production/pdb-all.yaml
---
# Web tier - keep at least 2 pods available
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-web
---
# API tier - percentage based for scalability
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: jobpost-api-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  minAvailable: 75%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-api
---
# Celery workers - allow more disruption for batch workloads
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: celery-worker-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 40%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-worker
---
# Celery beat - singleton, never disrupt
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: celery-beat-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-beat
---
# Redis cache - maintain quorum
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: redis-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: redis

Command:

# Apply all PDBs at once
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/pdb-all.yaml

# Or generate from CLI and apply
kubectl create pdb jobpost-web-pdb --selector=app=jobpost-web --min-available=2 \
  --namespace=anihpj-production --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

Output:

poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-web-pdb created
poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-api-pdb created
poddisruptionbudget.policy/celery-worker-pdb created
poddisruptionbudget.policy/celery-beat-pdb created
poddisruptionbudget.policy/redis-pdb created

✅ Verification:

# List all PDBs in namespace
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production

# Export current PDB configuration
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj -o yaml > pdb-backup.yaml

# Check which pods are protected
for PDB in $(kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production -o name); do
  echo "=== $PDB ==="
  kubectl get $PDB -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector.matchLabels}'
  echo ""
done
NAME                MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
celery-beat-pdb     N/A             0                 0                     30s
celery-worker-pdb   N/A             40%               2                     30s
jobpost-api-pdb     75%             N/A               1                     30s
jobpost-web-pdb     2               N/A               1                     30s
redis-pdb           N/A             1                 1                     30s

=== poddisruptionbudget.policy/celery-beat-pdb ===
{"app":"celery-beat"}
=== poddisruptionbudget.policy/celery-worker-pdb ===
{"app":"celery-worker"}
=== poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-api-pdb ===
{"app":"jobpost-api"}
=== poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-web-pdb ===
{"app":"jobpost-web"}
=== poddisruptionbudget.policy/redis-pdb ===
{"app":"redis"}
Example 5: Create PDB for Zero-Downtime Node Maintenance

Scenario: Configure PDBs to enable rolling node maintenance without service interruption.

📄 Context - Node Maintenance Procedure:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/node-maintenance.sh
# Safe node maintenance procedure with PDB respect
#
# Usage: ./node-maintenance.sh node-name
#
# Requirements:
# - PDBs configured for all critical workloads
# - Pods spread across multiple nodes (podAntiAffinity)
# - Sufficient capacity on remaining nodes

NODE=$1

echo "=== Pre-maintenance checks ==="
# Check PDB status before starting
kubectl get pdb -A --no-headers | while read NS NAME MIN MAX ALLOWED AGE; do
  if [ "$ALLOWED" == "0" ]; then
    echo "WARNING: PDB $NS/$NAME has 0 allowed disruptions!"
    exit 1
  fi
done

echo "=== Pods on $NODE ==="
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o wide --field-selector spec.nodeName=$NODE

echo "=== Cordoning node ==="
kubectl cordon $NODE

echo "=== Draining node (respecting PDBs) ==="
kubectl drain $NODE \
  --ignore-daemonsets \
  --delete-emptydir-data \
  --grace-period=120 \
  --timeout=600s

echo "=== Perform maintenance ==="
echo "Node $NODE is ready for maintenance"
echo "Run: ./node-maintenance.sh --uncordon $NODE when done"

📄 Pod Anti-Affinity for Spread:

# k8s/production/deployment-with-spread.yaml
# Deployment with pod anti-affinity for PDB effectiveness
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost-web
    spec:
      affinity:
        # Spread pods across nodes
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: jobpost-web
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
        # Spread across availability zones
        topologySpreadConstraints:
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
          whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: jobpost-web
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest

Command:

# Create PDB before maintenance
kubectl create pdb jobpost-web-pdb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --selector=app=jobpost-web \
  --min-available=2

# Verify pods are spread
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost-web -o wide

# Start maintenance (will respect PDB)
kubectl drain node-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

Output:

poddisruptionbudget.policy/jobpost-web-pdb created

NAME                           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   NODE
jobpost-web-abc12              1/1     Running   0          1h    node-1
jobpost-web-def34              1/1     Running   0          1h    node-2
jobpost-web-ghi56              1/1     Running   0          1h    node-3

node/node-1 cordoned
evicting pod anihpj-production/jobpost-web-abc12
pod/jobpost-web-abc12 evicted
node/node-1 drained

✅ Verification:

# Verify service continuity during drain
for i in {1..10}; do
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://anihpj.example.com/health/
  echo ""
  sleep 1
done

# Check pod redistribution
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost-web -o wide

# Verify remaining nodes have capacity
kubectl top nodes

# Uncordon after maintenance
kubectl uncordon node-1
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200

NAME                           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   NODE
jobpost-web-abc12              1/1     Running   0          30s   node-2  # Rescheduled
jobpost-web-def34              1/1     Running   0          1h    node-2
jobpost-web-ghi56              1/1     Running   0          1h    node-3

NAME     CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)   
node-1   100m         500Mi           # Drained
node-2   800m         2Gi             # More load
node-3   600m         1.5Gi           

node/node-1 uncordoned
Example 6: Troubleshoot PDB Blocking Drain

Scenario: Debug and resolve a situation where PDBs are preventing node drain operations.

📄 Context - PDB Troubleshooting Guide:

# Common PDB issues and solutions
#
# Issue 1: "Cannot evict pod, would violate PDB"
# Cause: Not enough healthy pods to satisfy PDB
# Solution: Scale up, fix unhealthy pods, or adjust PDB
#
# Issue 2: "Drain timeout - eviction stuck"
# Cause: Pod is terminating slowly or PDB too restrictive
# Solution: Check terminationGracePeriodSeconds, review PDB values
#
# Issue 3: "AllowedDisruptions: 0"
# Cause: Running at minimum required pods
# Solution: Scale up replicas or reduce minAvailable
#
# Diagnostic commands:
# 1. Check PDB status
# 2. Check pod health
# 3. Check deployment replicas
# 4. Review events

Command:

# Diagnose PDB blocking issue
# Step 1: Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production

# Step 2: Find problem PDB
kubectl get pdb -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,MIN:.spec.minAvailable,CURRENT:.status.currentHealthy,DISRUPTIONS:.status.disruptionsAllowed'

# Step 3: Check related pods
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost-web

# Step 4: Check events
kubectl get events -n anihpj-production --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | grep -i evict

Output:

NAME                MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
jobpost-web-pdb     2               N/A               0                     1h
celery-worker-pdb   3               N/A               0                     1h

NAME              MIN   CURRENT   DISRUPTIONS
jobpost-web-pdb   2     2         0
celery-worker-pdb 3     3         0

NAME                           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-abc12              1/1     Running   0          1h
jobpost-web-def34              0/1     CrashLoop 5          1h   # Problem!

warning  FailedEvict  Cannot evict pod as it would violate pod disruption budget

✅ Resolution Steps:

# Fix 1: Resolve unhealthy pod
kubectl logs jobpost-web-def34 -n anihpj-production --previous
kubectl describe pod jobpost-web-def34 -n anihpj-production

# Fix 2: Scale up to allow disruption
kubectl scale deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production --replicas=4

# Fix 3: Wait for pods to be healthy
kubectl rollout status deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Fix 4: Retry drain
kubectl drain node-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Alternative: Temporarily adjust PDB (use with caution!)
kubectl patch pdb jobpost-web-pdb -n anihpj-production \
  --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"minAvailable":1}}'

# Or delete PDB temporarily (last resort)
kubectl delete pdb jobpost-web-pdb -n anihpj-production
kubectl drain node-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# Recreate PDB after drain
kubectl create pdb jobpost-web-pdb --selector=app=jobpost-web --min-available=2 \
  -n anihpj-production
# After fixing:
NAME                MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
jobpost-web-pdb     2               N/A               2                     1h

NAME                           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-abc12              1/1     Running   0          1h
jobpost-web-def34              1/1     Running   0          5m   # Fixed
jobpost-web-ghi56              1/1     Running   0          1h
jobpost-web-jkl78              1/1     Running   0          2m   # Scaled up

node/node-1 cordoned
evicting pod anihpj-production/jobpost-web-abc12
pod/jobpost-web-abc12 evicted
node/node-1 drained

kubectl create priorityclass

CREATE Scheduling Priority Preemption

Create a priority class with the specified name

The kubectl create priorityclass command creates a PriorityClass resource that defines scheduling priority and preemption behavior for pods. Higher priority pods are scheduled before lower priority ones, and can preempt (evict) lower priority pods when resources are scarce.

For the anihpj Django project, priority classes ensure:

  • Critical services (Django web) get resources first
  • Background jobs (Celery workers) don't starve production pods
  • Batch imports run only when cluster has spare capacity
  • Database pods are never preempted
  • Monitoring stays available during resource pressure
  • Development workloads yield to production

Syntax

# Create priority class
kubectl create priorityclass NAME --value=VALUE [--preemption-policy=POLICY] [--description=DESC]

# With global default
kubectl create priorityclass NAME --value=VALUE --global-default=true

# Dry-run for manifest generation
kubectl create priorityclass NAME --value=1000 --dry-run=client -o yaml

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--valueint32Priority value (higher = more priority, max ~1B)
--preemption-policystring"PreemptLowerPriority" or "Never"
--global-defaultboolUse as default for pods without priorityClassName
--descriptionstringHuman-readable description
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Priority Value Guidelines

RangeUse CaseExample
1,000,000,000+System critical (reserved)system-cluster-critical
100,000 - 999,999Production criticaldatabase, monitoring
10,000 - 99,999Production standardweb servers, APIs
1,000 - 9,999Background/batchworkers, jobs
1 - 999Best effortdevelopment, testing

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0PriorityClass created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid value)

📁 Context: Priority Hierarchy for anihpj Django Project

Preemption: When enabled, higher priority pods can evict lower priority pods to get scheduled. Use with caution in production.
Priority Class Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Priority Class Hierarchy                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 1,000,000,000 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (System Reserved)    │ system-cluster-critical                     │       │
│                       │ kube-system pods, CNI, DNS                   │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 100,000       ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (Critical)           │ production-critical                          │       │
│                       │ PostgreSQL, Redis master, Monitoring         │       │
│                       │ PreemptLowerPriority: true                   │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 50,000        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (High)               │ production-high                              │       │
│                       │ Django web pods, API servers                 │       │
│                       │ PreemptLowerPriority: true                   │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 10,000        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (Standard)           │ production-standard (default)                │       │
│                       │ Celery workers, background processors        │       │
│                       │ PreemptLowerPriority: false                  │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 1,000         ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (Batch)              │ batch-processing                             │       │
│                       │ Data imports, reports, migrations            │       │
│                       │ PreemptLowerPriority: false                  │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  Value: 100           ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  (Development)        │ development                                  │       │
│                       │ Dev/test pods, experiments                   │       │
│                       │ Can be preempted by anything                 │       │
│                       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Production Critical Priority Class

Scenario: Create a high-priority class for database and critical infrastructure that should never be preempted.

📄 Context - Critical Services for anihpj:

# Services that need production-critical priority:
# - PostgreSQL database pods
# - Redis primary/master
# - Prometheus and Alertmanager
# - Ingress controller
#
# These services should:
# 1. Be scheduled before everything else
# 2. Be able to preempt lower priority pods
# 3. Never be preempted themselves (except by system pods)

Command:

# Create production-critical priority class
kubectl create priorityclass production-critical \
  --value=100000 \
  --preemption-policy=PreemptLowerPriority \
  --description="Critical infrastructure - databases, monitoring, ingress"

Output:

priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/production-critical created

✅ Verification:

# Check priority class
kubectl get priorityclass production-critical

# View full configuration
kubectl describe priorityclass production-critical

# Use in a pod/deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: postgres-critical
spec:
  priorityClassName: production-critical
  containers:
  - name: postgres
    image: postgres:15
EOF
NAME                  VALUE      GLOBAL-DEFAULT   AGE
production-critical   100000     false            30s

Name:              production-critical
Value:             100000
GlobalDefault:     false
PreemptionPolicy:  PreemptLowerPriority
Description:       Critical infrastructure - databases, monitoring, ingress
Events:            

pod/postgres-critical created
Example 2: Create Default Priority Class

Scenario: Create a global default priority class for all pods that don't specify one.

📄 Context - Default Priority Strategy:

# Default priority class strategy:
#
# Issue: Pods without priorityClassName get priority 0
# Solution: Set a reasonable default for all workloads
#
# Benefits:
# - All pods have defined priority
# - Prevents batch jobs from being equal to web servers
# - Enables predictable scheduling behavior
#
# Note: Only ONE priority class can be global-default

Command:

# Create global default priority class
kubectl create priorityclass production-standard \
  --value=10000 \
  --global-default=true \
  --preemption-policy=Never \
  --description="Default priority for production pods"

Output:

priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/production-standard created

✅ Verification:

# List all priority classes
kubectl get priorityclass

# Verify global default
kubectl get priorityclass -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,VALUE:.value,DEFAULT:.globalDefault'

# Test - create pod without priorityClassName
kubectl run test-default --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  grep -A2 priorityClassName || echo "No priorityClassName needed"

# Check assigned priority
kubectl run test-default --image=nginx
kubectl get pod test-default -o jsonpath='{.spec.priorityClassName}'
NAME                      VALUE        GLOBAL-DEFAULT   AGE
production-critical       100000       false            5m
production-standard       10000        true             30s
system-cluster-critical   2000000000   false            30d
system-node-critical      2000001000   false            30d

NAME                      VALUE        DEFAULT
production-critical       100000       false
production-standard       10000        true
system-cluster-critical   2000000000   false
system-node-critical      2000001000   false

No priorityClassName needed

production-standard
Example 3: Create Complete Priority Class Hierarchy

Scenario: Create all priority classes needed for the anihpj Django project deployment.

📄 Context - Complete Priority Configuration:

# k8s/cluster/priority-classes.yaml
# Complete priority class hierarchy for anihpj
---
# Critical infrastructure - databases, monitoring
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: production-critical
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
value: 100000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "Critical infrastructure - databases, caches, monitoring. Can preempt lower priority."
---
# High priority - web servers, APIs
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: production-high
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
value: 50000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "Production web servers and APIs. Can preempt batch and dev workloads."
---
# Standard - default for production
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: production-standard
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
value: 10000
globalDefault: true
preemptionPolicy: Never
description: "Default production priority. Cannot preempt other pods."
---
# Batch processing - jobs, imports
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: batch-processing
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
value: 1000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never
description: "Batch jobs, data imports, reports. Runs when capacity available."
---
# Development - lowest priority
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: development
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
value: 100
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never
description: "Development and test workloads. Can be preempted by production."

Command:

# Apply all priority classes
kubectl apply -f k8s/cluster/priority-classes.yaml

# Or create via CLI
kubectl create priorityclass production-critical --value=100000 \
  --preemption-policy=PreemptLowerPriority \
  --description="Critical infrastructure"
kubectl create priorityclass production-high --value=50000 \
  --preemption-policy=PreemptLowerPriority \
  --description="Production web/API"
kubectl create priorityclass production-standard --value=10000 \
  --global-default=true --preemption-policy=Never \
  --description="Default production"
kubectl create priorityclass batch-processing --value=1000 \
  --preemption-policy=Never \
  --description="Batch jobs"
kubectl create priorityclass development --value=100 \
  --preemption-policy=Never \
  --description="Development"

Output:

priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/production-critical created
priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/production-high created
priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/production-standard created
priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/batch-processing created
priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/development created

✅ Verification:

# List all priority classes sorted by value
kubectl get priorityclass --sort-by='.value'

# Get custom overview
kubectl get priorityclass -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,VALUE:.value,PREEMPTION:.preemptionPolicy,DEFAULT:.globalDefault'

# Export for GitOps
kubectl get priorityclass -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj-platform -o yaml > priority-classes-backup.yaml
NAME                      VALUE        GLOBAL-DEFAULT   AGE
development               100          false            30s
batch-processing          1000         false            30s
production-standard       10000        true             30s
production-high           50000        false            30s
production-critical       100000       false            30s
system-cluster-critical   2000000000   false            30d
system-node-critical      2000001000   false            30d

NAME                      VALUE        PREEMPTION            DEFAULT
development               100          Never                 false
batch-processing          1000         Never                 false
production-standard       10000        Never                 true
production-high           50000        PreemptLowerPriority  false
production-critical       100000       PreemptLowerPriority  false
system-cluster-critical   2000000000   PreemptLowerPriority  false
system-node-critical      2000001000   PreemptLowerPriority  false
Example 4: Assign Priority Classes to Deployments

Scenario: Configure all anihpj deployments with appropriate priority classes.

📄 Context - Deployment Priority Assignment:

# k8s/production/deployments-with-priority.yaml
# All anihpj deployments with priority classes assigned
---
# PostgreSQL - Critical
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: production-critical  # Value: 100000
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
---
# Django Web - High  
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: production-high  # Value: 50000
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        command: ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
---
# Celery Workers - Standard (default)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: celery-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: production-standard  # Value: 10000
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker"]
---
# Data Import Job - Batch
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: import-jobs
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: batch-processing  # Value: 1000
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: import
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "import_jobs"]

Command:

# Patch existing deployment to add priority class
kubectl patch deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  --type='strategic' \
  --patch='{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"priorityClassName":"production-high"}}}}'

# Or use kubectl set (not available, use patch instead)
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/deployments-with-priority.yaml

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web patched

✅ Verification:

# Check priority class assignment
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,PRIORITY-CLASS:.spec.priorityClassName,PRIORITY:.spec.priority'

# Verify rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Check pod scheduling priority
kubectl describe pod -l app=jobpost-web -n anihpj-production | grep -A2 "Priority"
NAME                           PRIORITY-CLASS        PRIORITY
postgres-0                     production-critical   100000
postgres-1                     production-critical   100000
jobpost-web-abc12              production-high       50000
jobpost-web-def34              production-high       50000
celery-worker-ghi56            production-standard   10000
celery-worker-jkl78            production-standard   10000
import-jobs-mno90              batch-processing      1000

deployment "jobpost-web" successfully rolled out

Priority:             50000
Priority Class Name:  production-high
Example 5: Test Preemption Behavior

Scenario: Demonstrate how higher priority pods preempt lower priority pods when resources are constrained.

📄 Context - Preemption Test Setup:

# Preemption testing procedure
#
# 1. Fill cluster with low-priority pods
# 2. Create high-priority pod that needs resources
# 3. Observe preemption of low-priority pod
#
# Note: Preemption only happens when:
# - Higher priority pod is pending (can't be scheduled)
# - Lower priority pod is using resources it needs
# - preemptionPolicy is PreemptLowerPriority

Command:

# Step 1: Create low-priority pods that consume resources
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: low-priority-filler
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: filler
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: filler
    spec:
      priorityClassName: development
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
EOF

# Wait for pods to be running
kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=filler -n anihpj-dev --timeout=60s

# Step 2: Create high-priority pod
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: high-priority-web
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  priorityClassName: production-high
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "1000m"
        memory: "1Gi"
EOF

Output:

deployment.apps/low-priority-filler created
pod/low-priority-filler-xyz condition met (10 pods)
pod/high-priority-web created

✅ Verification:

# Watch preemption in action
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev -w

# Check events for preemption
kubectl get events -n anihpj-dev --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | grep -i preempt

# Verify high-priority pod is running
kubectl get pod high-priority-web -n anihpj-dev

# Check which low-priority pod was preempted
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev -l app=filler --field-selector=status.phase!=Running
NAME                                  READY   STATUS    AGE
low-priority-filler-abc12             1/1     Running   1m
low-priority-filler-def34             1/1     Running   1m
...
high-priority-web                     0/1     Pending   5s
low-priority-filler-abc12             1/1     Terminating   1m   # Preempted!
high-priority-web                     0/1     Pending   10s
high-priority-web                     1/1     Running   15s

Events:
  LAST SEEN   TYPE     REASON      MESSAGE
  15s         Normal   Preempted   Preempted by anihpj-dev/high-priority-web
  10s         Normal   Scheduled   Successfully assigned high-priority-web to node-1

NAME              READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
high-priority-web 1/1     Running   0          20s
Example 6: Non-Preempting Priority for Batch Jobs

Scenario: Create a priority class where jobs have scheduling priority but cannot preempt other workloads.

📄 Context - Non-Preempting Priority Use Case:

# Non-preempting priority classes are useful for:
#
# 1. Batch jobs that should run when resources are free
#    - Data imports, report generation
#    - Don't preempt production, but schedule before dev
#
# 2. Background workers that are important but shouldn't disrupt
#    - Celery workers processing queues
#    - Higher priority than dev, won't preempt web servers
#
# 3. Development/staging environments
#    - Want some priority ordering within environment
#    - Should never affect production
#
# How it works:
# - Pod gets scheduled based on priority order
# - But cannot evict running pods to get resources
# - Will wait until resources become naturally available

Command:

# Create non-preempting batch priority
kubectl create priorityclass batch-non-preempting \
  --value=5000 \
  --preemption-policy=Never \
  --description="Batch jobs - schedules before dev but won't preempt anything"

# Create deployment using this priority
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-export
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: batch-non-preempting
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: export
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
        command:
        - python
        - manage.py
        - export_data
        - --format=csv
        - --output=s3://anihpj-reports/exports/
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"
EOF

Output:

priorityclass.scheduling.k8s.io/batch-non-preempting created
job.batch/data-export created

✅ Verification:

# Check priority class
kubectl get priorityclass batch-non-preempting -o yaml

# Verify job pod uses correct priority
kubectl get pod -l job-name=data-export -n anihpj-production \
  -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,PRIORITY:.spec.priority,PREEMPTION:.spec.preemptionPolicy'

# Compare scheduling behavior
# High priority + Never preempt = scheduled in order, waits for resources
kubectl describe pod -l job-name=data-export -n anihpj-production | grep -A5 "Events:"
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: batch-non-preempting
value: 5000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never
description: Batch jobs - schedules before dev but won't preempt anything

NAME                 PRIORITY   PREEMPTION
data-export-abc12    5000       Never

Events:
  Type    Reason     Age   From               Message
  ----    ------     ----  ----               -------
  Normal  Scheduled  30s   default-scheduler  Successfully assigned pod to node-2
  Normal  Pulling    29s   kubelet            Pulling image "registry.example.com/anihpj:latest"
  Normal  Pulled     20s   kubelet            Successfully pulled image
  Normal  Created    20s   kubelet            Created container export
  Normal  Started    19s   kubelet            Started container export

kubectl create quota

CREATE Limits Multi-tenancy Governance

Create a resource quota with the specified name

The kubectl create quota command creates a ResourceQuota that limits the aggregate resource consumption within a namespace. This ensures fair resource distribution among teams and prevents any single namespace from monopolizing cluster resources.

For the anihpj Django project, quotas are used for:

  • Environment limits - different quotas for dev/staging/production
  • Team budgets - allocating resources to the jobpost team
  • Cost control - preventing runaway resource usage
  • Capacity planning - reserving resources for critical services
  • Object counts - limiting number of pods, secrets, configmaps
  • Storage limits - controlling persistent volume usage

Syntax

# Create basic resource quota
kubectl create quota NAME --hard=RESOURCE=VALUE

# Multiple resources
kubectl create quota NAME --hard=cpu=4,memory=8Gi,pods=10

# With scope selector
kubectl create quota NAME --hard=pods=5 --scopes=PriorityClass=batch

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--hardstringResource limits (key=value,key=value)
--scopesstringQuota scopes: Terminating, NotTerminating, BestEffort, NotBestEffort, PriorityClass
-n, --namespacestringTarget namespace
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Common Resource Types

ResourceDescriptionExample
requests.cpuSum of CPU requests4 (4 cores)
requests.memorySum of memory requests8Gi
limits.cpuSum of CPU limits8
limits.memorySum of memory limits16Gi
podsNumber of pods20
servicesNumber of services10
secretsNumber of secrets50
configmapsNumber of configmaps50
persistentvolumeclaimsNumber of PVCs10
requests.storageTotal PVC storage100Gi

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0ResourceQuota created successfully
1Error (invalid resource, already exists)

📁 Context: Resource Quota Strategy for anihpj Environments

Note: ResourceQuotas require LimitRanges to set defaults, otherwise pods without resource requests/limits will be rejected.
Quota Architecture by Environment
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Resource Quota by Environment                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-dev                    anihpj-staging             anihpj-production  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐│
│  │ requests.cpu: 4 │          │ requests.cpu: 8 │        │ requests.cpu:20 ││
│  │ requests.mem:8Gi│          │ requests.mem:16Gi        │ requests.mem:64Gi│
│  │ limits.cpu: 8   │          │ limits.cpu: 16  │        │ limits.cpu: 40  ││
│  │ limits.mem: 16Gi│          │ limits.mem: 32Gi│        │ limits.mem:128Gi││
│  │ pods: 20        │          │ pods: 50        │        │ pods: 200       ││
│  │ services: 10    │          │ services: 20    │        │ services: 50    ││
│  │ secrets: 20     │          │ secrets: 50     │        │ secrets: 200    ││
│  │ pvc: 5          │          │ pvc: 20         │        │ pvc: 100        ││
│  │ storage: 50Gi   │          │ storage: 200Gi  │        │ storage: 2Ti    ││
│  └─────────────────┘          └─────────────────┘        └─────────────────┘│
│                                                                              │
│  Purpose:                     Purpose:                   Purpose:            │
│  - Developer testing          - Integration tests        - Full capacity     │
│  - Low resource usage         - Performance tests        - High availability │
│  - Quick iteration            - UAT environment          - Production SLAs   │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Basic Resource Quota for Production

Scenario: Create a resource quota for the anihpj production namespace to limit total resource consumption.

📄 Context - Production Capacity Planning:

# Production capacity requirements for anihpj:
#
# Django Web:     3 pods × (500m CPU, 1Gi memory) = 1.5 CPU, 3Gi
# Celery Workers: 5 pods × (250m CPU, 512Mi memory) = 1.25 CPU, 2.5Gi
# Celery Beat:    1 pod × (100m CPU, 256Mi memory) = 100m CPU, 256Mi
# Redis:          3 pods × (250m CPU, 512Mi memory) = 750m CPU, 1.5Gi
# PostgreSQL:     2 pods × (500m CPU, 2Gi memory) = 1 CPU, 4Gi
# Jobs (peak):    3 pods × (500m CPU, 1Gi memory) = 1.5 CPU, 3Gi
#
# Total Requests: ~6.1 CPU, ~14.25Gi memory
# Buffer (50%):   ~9 CPU, ~21Gi memory
# Limits (2x):    ~18 CPU, ~42Gi memory
#
# Round up for production quota: 20 CPU requests, 64Gi memory

Command:

# Create production resource quota
kubectl create quota anihpj-production-quota \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --hard=requests.cpu=20,requests.memory=64Gi,limits.cpu=40,limits.memory=128Gi,pods=200

Output:

resourcequota/anihpj-production-quota created

✅ Verification:

# Check quota
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production

# View detailed usage
kubectl describe resourcequota anihpj-production-quota -n anihpj-production

# Check quota status after deployments
kubectl get quota anihpj-production-quota -n anihpj-production -o yaml
NAME                       AGE   REQUEST                                                    LIMIT
anihpj-production-quota    30s   pods: 0/200, requests.cpu: 0/20, requests.memory: 0/64Gi   limits.cpu: 0/40, limits.memory: 0/128Gi

Name:            anihpj-production-quota
Namespace:       anihpj-production
Resource         Used   Hard
--------         ----   ----
limits.cpu       0      40
limits.memory    0      128Gi
pods             0      200
requests.cpu     0      20
requests.memory  0      64Gi
Example 2: Create Development Quota with Object Limits

Scenario: Create a quota for the development namespace with both resource and object count limits.

📄 Context - Development Environment Controls:

# Development namespace restrictions:
#
# Goals:
# 1. Prevent developers from consuming too many cluster resources
# 2. Limit object counts to avoid etcd bloat
# 3. Restrict storage to shared dev storage quota
# 4. Allow enough for local testing but not full app
#
# Limits applied:
# - Compute: Enough for 2-3 service replicas
# - Storage: Limited PVC for database testing
# - Objects: Prevent secret/configmap sprawl

Command:

# Create development quota with all limits
kubectl create quota dev-quota \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --hard=requests.cpu=4,requests.memory=8Gi,limits.cpu=8,limits.memory=16Gi,\
pods=20,services=10,secrets=30,configmaps=30,persistentvolumeclaims=5,requests.storage=50Gi

Output:

resourcequota/dev-quota created

✅ Verification:

# Check all resources in quota
kubectl describe resourcequota dev-quota -n anihpj-dev

# Test quota enforcement - try to exceed
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx -n anihpj-dev \
  --requests='cpu=5,memory=10Gi' --dry-run=server

# Check current usage vs limits
kubectl get quota dev-quota -n anihpj-dev -o custom-columns=\
'RESOURCE:.status.hard.*,USED:.status.used.*'
Name:                   dev-quota
Namespace:              anihpj-dev
Resource                Used  Hard
--------                ----  ----
configmaps              1     30
limits.cpu              0     8
limits.memory           0     16Gi
persistentvolumeclaims  0     5
pods                    0     20
requests.cpu            0     4
requests.memory         0     8Gi
requests.storage        0     50Gi
secrets                 2     30
services                0     10

Error from server (Forbidden): pods "test-pod" is forbidden: 
exceeded quota: dev-quota, requested: requests.cpu=5,requests.memory=10Gi, 
used: requests.cpu=0,requests.memory=0, limited: requests.cpu=4,requests.memory=8Gi
Example 3: Create Scoped Quota for Batch Jobs

Scenario: Create a quota that only applies to batch jobs (pods with deadlines) to limit their resource consumption.

📄 Context - Batch Job Resource Control:

# Scoped quotas allow different limits based on workload type:
#
# Terminating scope: Pods with activeDeadlineSeconds (Jobs, CronJobs)
# NotTerminating scope: Long-running pods (Deployments, StatefulSets)
#
# Strategy for anihpj:
# - Production Deployments: High quota for always-on services
# - Batch Jobs: Limited quota, prevents batch from starving services
#
# Scopes:
# - Terminating: spec.activeDeadlineSeconds is set
# - NotTerminating: spec.activeDeadlineSeconds is not set
# - BestEffort: Pods without resource requests/limits
# - NotBestEffort: Pods with resource requests/limits
# - PriorityClass: Pods with specific priority class

Command:

# Create quota for batch/terminating workloads only
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: batch-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    pods: "10"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - scopeName: Terminating
      operator: Exists
EOF

# Create separate quota for long-running services
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: service-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "16"
    requests.memory: "56Gi"
    pods: "190"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - scopeName: NotTerminating
      operator: Exists
EOF

Output:

resourcequota/batch-quota created
resourcequota/service-quota created

✅ Verification:

# View scoped quotas
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production

# Check scope selectors
kubectl get resourcequota batch-quota -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.scopeSelector}'

# Create a Job (will use batch-quota)
kubectl create job test-job --image=busybox -n anihpj-production -- sleep 60
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production

# Create a Deployment (will use service-quota)
kubectl create deployment test-deploy --image=nginx -n anihpj-production
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production
NAME            AGE   REQUEST                                               LIMIT
batch-quota     30s   pods: 0/10, requests.cpu: 0/4, requests.memory: 0/8Gi
service-quota   30s   pods: 0/190, requests.cpu: 0/16, requests.memory: 0/56Gi

{"matchExpressions":[{"scopeName":"Terminating","operator":"Exists"}]}

job.batch/test-job created

NAME            AGE   REQUEST                                               LIMIT
batch-quota     1m    pods: 1/10, requests.cpu: 100m/4, requests.memory: 128Mi/8Gi
service-quota   1m    pods: 0/190, requests.cpu: 0/16, requests.memory: 0/56Gi

NAME            AGE   REQUEST                                               LIMIT  
batch-quota     2m    pods: 1/10, requests.cpu: 100m/4, requests.memory: 128Mi/8Gi
service-quota   2m    pods: 1/190, requests.cpu: 100m/16, requests.memory: 128Mi/56Gi
Example 4: Create Priority-Based Quotas

Scenario: Create quotas based on priority class to limit resources for different workload priorities.

📄 Context - Priority-Based Resource Allocation:

# k8s/cluster/priority-quotas.yaml
# Resource quotas per priority class
#
# This ensures:
# 1. Critical workloads always have guaranteed resources
# 2. Batch jobs can't consume all cluster capacity
# 3. Development workloads have strict limits
---
# Quota for production-critical priority
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: critical-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: "32Gi"
    pods: "50"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - scopeName: PriorityClass
      operator: In
      values:
      - production-critical
---
# Quota for production-high priority
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: high-priority-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: "24Gi"
    pods: "100"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - scopeName: PriorityClass
      operator: In
      values:
      - production-high
---
# Quota for batch-processing priority
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: batch-priority-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    pods: "50"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - scopeName: PriorityClass
      operator: In
      values:
      - batch-processing

Command:

# Apply priority-based quotas
kubectl apply -f k8s/cluster/priority-quotas.yaml

# Or create via CLI
kubectl create quota critical-quota -n anihpj-production \
  --hard=requests.cpu=8,requests.memory=32Gi,pods=50 \
  --scopes=PriorityClass=production-critical

Output:

resourcequota/critical-quota created
resourcequota/high-priority-quota created
resourcequota/batch-priority-quota created

✅ Verification:

# List all quotas
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production

# Check current allocation by priority
for QUOTA in critical-quota high-priority-quota batch-priority-quota; do
  echo "=== $QUOTA ==="
  kubectl get resourcequota $QUOTA -n anihpj-production \
    -o custom-columns='CPU-USED:.status.used.requests\.cpu,CPU-HARD:.status.hard.requests\.cpu'
done

# Test: Deploy with specific priority class
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-critical
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  priorityClassName: production-critical
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
EOF

# Check which quota was consumed
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-production
NAME                   AGE   REQUEST                                               LIMIT
critical-quota         30s   pods: 0/50, requests.cpu: 0/8, requests.memory: 0/32Gi
high-priority-quota    30s   pods: 0/100, requests.cpu: 0/8, requests.memory: 0/24Gi
batch-priority-quota   30s   pods: 0/50, requests.cpu: 0/4, requests.memory: 0/8Gi

=== critical-quota ===
CPU-USED   CPU-HARD
0          8
=== high-priority-quota ===
CPU-USED   CPU-HARD
0          8
=== batch-priority-quota ===
CPU-USED   CPU-HARD
0          4

NAME                   AGE   REQUEST                                               LIMIT
critical-quota         1m    pods: 1/50, requests.cpu: 500m/8, requests.memory: 512Mi/32Gi  # Used!
high-priority-quota    1m    pods: 0/100, requests.cpu: 0/8, requests.memory: 0/24Gi
batch-priority-quota   1m    pods: 0/50, requests.cpu: 0/4, requests.memory: 0/8Gi
Example 5: Create Storage-Specific Quotas

Scenario: Create quotas for storage resources to control persistent volume usage per storage class.

📄 Context - Storage Quota Strategy:

# Storage quota configuration for anihpj:
#
# Storage classes in cluster:
# - fast-ssd: High-performance SSD storage (expensive)
# - standard-hdd: Standard HDD storage (economical)
# - shared-nfs: Shared NFS for static files (ReadWriteMany)
#
# Quota strategy:
# - Limit fast SSD usage to databases only
# - Allow more standard storage for logs, backups
# - Limit number of PVCs to prevent resource sprawl
#
# Storage quota resources:
# - requests.storage: Total storage across all PVCs
# - persistentvolumeclaims: Count of PVCs
# - <storage-class>.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage: Per-class storage
# - <storage-class>.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims: Per-class PVC count

Command:

# Create storage quota with per-class limits
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: storage-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  hard:
    # Overall limits
    requests.storage: "500Gi"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "30"
    # Fast SSD - limited for databases
    fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage: "200Gi"
    fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims: "10"
    # Standard HDD - more allowance
    standard-hdd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage: "300Gi"
    standard-hdd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims: "20"
EOF

Output:

resourcequota/storage-quota created

✅ Verification:

# Check storage quota
kubectl describe resourcequota storage-quota -n anihpj-production

# Test creating PVC
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-data
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: fast-ssd
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 50Gi
EOF

# Check quota after PVC creation
kubectl get resourcequota storage-quota -n anihpj-production
Name:                                                         storage-quota
Namespace:                                                    anihpj-production
Resource                                                      Used   Hard
--------                                                      ----   ----
fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims  0      10
fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage        0      200Gi
persistentvolumeclaims                                        0      30
requests.storage                                              0      500Gi
standard-hdd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims  0   20
standard-hdd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage    0      300Gi

persistentvolumeclaim/postgres-data created

Resource                                                      Used   Hard
fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/persistentvolumeclaims  1      10
fast-ssd.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage        50Gi   200Gi
persistentvolumeclaims                                        1      30
requests.storage                                              50Gi   500Gi
Example 6: Complete Multi-Environment Quota Setup

Scenario: Create comprehensive quotas for all anihpj environments with appropriate limits.

📄 Context - Full Quota Configuration:

# k8s/cluster/all-quotas.yaml
# Complete resource quota configuration for all environments
---
# Development - most restrictive
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: resource-quota
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    environment: dev
spec:
  hard:
    # Compute
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: "16Gi"
    # Objects
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
    secrets: "30"
    configmaps: "30"
    replicationcontrollers: "0"  # Don't use deprecated RC
    # Storage
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"
    requests.storage: "50Gi"
---
# Staging - moderate limits
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: resource-quota
  namespace: anihpj-staging
  labels:
    environment: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: "32Gi"
    limits.cpu: "16"
    limits.memory: "64Gi"
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
    secrets: "50"
    configmaps: "50"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "15"
    requests.storage: "200Gi"
---
# Production - highest allowance
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: resource-quota
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    environment: production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "32"
    requests.memory: "128Gi"
    limits.cpu: "64"
    limits.memory: "256Gi"
    pods: "200"
    services: "50"
    secrets: "200"
    configmaps: "200"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "50"
    requests.storage: "1Ti"
