Command:

# Apply all quotas at once
kubectl apply -f k8s/cluster/all-quotas.yaml

# Or create individually with different limits
for ENV in dev staging production; do
  case $ENV in
    dev)        CPU_REQ=4;  MEM_REQ=8Gi;  PODS=20 ;;
    staging)    CPU_REQ=8;  MEM_REQ=32Gi; PODS=50 ;;
    production) CPU_REQ=32; MEM_REQ=128Gi; PODS=200 ;;
  esac
  
  kubectl create quota resource-quota \
    --namespace=anihpj-${ENV} \
    --hard=requests.cpu=${CPU_REQ},requests.memory=${MEM_REQ},pods=${PODS}
done

Output:

resourcequota/resource-quota created (anihpj-dev)
resourcequota/resource-quota created (anihpj-staging)
resourcequota/resource-quota created (anihpj-production)

✅ Verification:

# Compare quotas across all environments
kubectl get resourcequota -A -l environment -o custom-columns=\
'NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,CPU-REQ:.status.hard.requests\.cpu,MEM-REQ:.status.hard.requests\.memory,PODS:.status.hard.pods'

# Generate quota report
echo "=== Quota Usage Report ==="
for NS in anihpj-dev anihpj-staging anihpj-production; do
  echo ""
  echo "Namespace: $NS"
  kubectl get resourcequota resource-quota -n $NS \
    -o custom-columns='RESOURCE:.status.hard.*,USED:.status.used.*' 2>/dev/null || echo "No quota"
done

# Check enforcement
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n anihpj-dev --requests='cpu=5,memory=10Gi' --dry-run=server 2>&1 | grep -i exceed
NAMESPACE           CPU-REQ   MEM-REQ   PODS
anihpj-dev          4         8Gi       20
anihpj-staging      8         32Gi      50
anihpj-production   32        128Gi     200

=== Quota Usage Report ===

Namespace: anihpj-dev
requests.cpu: 4 (used: 1.5)
requests.memory: 8Gi (used: 3Gi)
pods: 20 (used: 8)

Namespace: anihpj-staging
requests.cpu: 8 (used: 4)
requests.memory: 32Gi (used: 12Gi)
pods: 50 (used: 15)

Namespace: anihpj-production
requests.cpu: 32 (used: 18)
requests.memory: 128Gi (used: 64Gi)
pods: 200 (used: 45)

exceeded quota: resource-quota, requested: requests.cpu=5,requests.memory=10Gi

kubectl create role

CREATE RBAC Security Namespace-scoped

Create a role with single rule

The kubectl create role command creates a namespace-scoped Role resource that defines a set of permissions. Roles are used in combination with RoleBindings to grant access to resources within a specific namespace.

For the anihpj Django project, roles are used for:

  • Developer access - read/write to pods, deployments in dev namespace
  • CI/CD pipelines - deploy new versions but not delete
  • Monitoring - read-only access to pods and logs
  • Application pods - access specific secrets and configmaps
  • Debug access - exec into pods for troubleshooting
  • Audit access - read-only view across resources

Syntax

# Create role with specific resource and verbs
kubectl create role NAME --verb=VERB --resource=RESOURCE

# Multiple verbs and resources
kubectl create role NAME --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods,services

# With resource names (specific objects)
kubectl create role NAME --verb=get --resource=configmaps --resource-name=my-config

# With API groups
kubectl create role NAME --verb=* --resource=deployments.apps

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--verb[]stringVerbs: get, list, watch, create, update, patch, delete, *
--resource[]stringResources: pods, deployments, services, etc.
--resource-name[]stringSpecific resource names to allow
-n, --namespacestringNamespace for the role
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Common Verbs

VerbHTTP MethodDescription
getGETRead a specific resource
listGETList resources (collection)
watchGET (stream)Watch for changes
createPOSTCreate new resources
updatePUTReplace entire resource
patchPATCHModify parts of resource
deleteDELETEDelete resources
*AllAll operations

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Role created successfully
1Error (invalid verb, resource, already exists)

📁 Context: RBAC Roles for anihpj Django Project

Role vs ClusterRole: Roles are namespace-scoped (use for team access within a namespace). ClusterRoles are cluster-wide (use for cross-namespace resources or cluster-level permissions).
Role Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        RBAC Role Architecture                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-production namespace                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                                                                      │    │
│  │  Roles:                           RoleBindings:                     │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────┐          │    │
│  │  │ developer            │◄───────│ developer-binding    │          │    │
│  │  │ verbs: get,list,watch│        │ subjects: Group:devs │          │    │
│  │  │       create,update  │        └──────────────────────┘          │    │
│  │  │ resources: pods,svc  │                                           │    │
│  │  │           deploy,cm  │        ┌──────────────────────┐          │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘        │ deployer-binding     │          │    │
│  │                                   │ subjects: SA:deploy  │          │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐        └──────────────────────┘          │    │
│  │  │ deployer             │◄───────────────────┘                     │    │
│  │  │ verbs: get,list,patch│                                           │    │
│  │  │       create,update  │        ┌──────────────────────┐          │    │
│  │  │ resources: deploy,rs │        │ viewer-binding       │          │    │
│  │  │           pods,svc   │        │ subjects: Group:ops  │          │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘        └──────────────────────┘          │    │
│  │                                           │                         │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐◄────────────────┘                        │    │
│  │  │ viewer               │                                           │    │
│  │  │ verbs: get,list,watch│                                           │    │
│  │  │ resources: *         │                                           │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘                                          │    │
│  │                                                                      │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Developer Role

Scenario: Create a role for developers to manage pods and deployments in the development namespace.

📄 Context - Developer Access Requirements:

# Developer access requirements for anihpj-dev:
#
# CAN:
# - View and list all resources
# - Create/update/delete pods, deployments, services
# - View logs and exec into pods for debugging
# - Create/update configmaps for configuration changes
# - Scale deployments
#
# CANNOT:
# - Modify secrets (security concern)
# - Create namespaces
# - Modify RBAC (roles, rolebindings)
# - Access other namespaces

Command:

# Create developer role
kubectl create role developer \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete \
  --resource=pods,pods/log,pods/exec,deployments,services,configmaps,replicasets

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer created

✅ Verification:

# Check role
kubectl get role developer -n anihpj-dev

# View role permissions
kubectl describe role developer -n anihpj-dev

# Generate YAML for documentation
kubectl get role developer -n anihpj-dev -o yaml
NAME        CREATED AT
developer   2024-01-15T10:00:00Z

Name:         developer
Labels:       
Namespace:    anihpj-dev
PolicyRule:
  Resources   Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------   -----------------  --------------  -----
  configmaps  []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  deployments []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods        []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods/exec   []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods/log    []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  replicasets []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  services    []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
Example 2: Create CI/CD Deployer Role

Scenario: Create a role for CI/CD pipelines that can deploy but not delete resources.

📄 Context - CI/CD Pipeline Permissions:

# CI/CD pipeline role for anihpj deployments
#
# Principle of least privilege:
# - Deploy new versions (create/update deployments)
# - Read current state (get/list for comparison)
# - NO delete permission (prevents accidental destruction)
# - Can update configmaps for configuration
# - Can read secrets (for validation, not modify)
#
# Used by:
# - GitHub Actions deployment workflows
# - ArgoCD application controller
# - Jenkins pipeline jobs

Command:

# Create deployer role (no delete permission)
kubectl create role deployer \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch \
  --resource=deployments,services,configmaps,ingresses,jobs

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/deployer created

✅ Verification:

# Check deployer can't delete
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:deploy-sa \
  -n anihpj-production

# Check deployer can update
kubectl auth can-i update deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:deploy-sa \
  -n anihpj-production

# View exact permissions
kubectl get role deployer -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.rules[*].verbs}'
no
yes
["get","list","watch","create","update","patch"]
Example 3: Create Role with Multiple Rules via Manifest

Scenario: Create a comprehensive role with multiple API groups and different permissions per resource type.

📄 Context - Full Role Manifest:

# k8s/rbac/developer-full-role.yaml
# Complete developer role with multiple rules
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer-full
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
rules:
# Core resources - full access
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "pods/exec", "services", "configmaps", "endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

# Apps resources - full access
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "replicasets", "statefulsets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

# Batch resources - full access
- apiGroups: ["batch"]
  resources: ["jobs", "cronjobs"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

# Networking - limited access
- apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["ingresses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]

# Secrets - read only
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]

# Events - read only
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["events"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

# Autoscaling - view only
- apiGroups: ["autoscaling"]
  resources: ["horizontalpodautoscalers"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

Command:

# Apply role manifest
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac/developer-full-role.yaml

# Or create base role via CLI and add rules
kubectl create role developer-full -n anihpj-dev \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete \
  --resource=pods,services,configmaps,deployments.apps,jobs.batch \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > /tmp/role.yaml

# Then edit and apply
kubectl apply -f /tmp/role.yaml

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer-full created

✅ Verification:

# Check all rules
kubectl describe role developer-full -n anihpj-dev

# Test specific permissions
kubectl auth can-i create secrets -n anihpj-dev --as=user:dev-user  # Should be no
kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n anihpj-dev --as=user:dev-user     # Should be yes
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments -n anihpj-dev --as=user:dev-user  # Should be yes
Name:         developer-full
Labels:       app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj
Namespace:    anihpj-dev
PolicyRule:
  Resources                        Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs
  ---------                        -----------------  --------------  -----
  configmaps                       []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  endpoints                        []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods                             []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods/exec                        []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  pods/log                         []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  services                         []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  deployments.apps                 []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  replicasets.apps                 []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  statefulsets.apps                []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  cronjobs.batch                   []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  jobs.batch                       []                 []              [get list watch create update patch delete]
  ingresses.networking.k8s.io      []                 []              [get list watch create update patch]
  secrets                          []                 []              [get list]
  events                           []                 []              [get list watch]
  horizontalpodautoscalers.autoscaling []             []              [get list watch]
Example 4: Create Role for Specific Resources

Scenario: Create a role that only allows access to specific named resources (e.g., specific configmaps).

📄 Context - Resource-Specific Role:

# Restrict access to specific resources by name
#
# Use cases:
# - App pod needs access to only its own configmap
# - Service account should only read specific secrets
# - Limit which deployments a user can modify
#
# For anihpj:
# - Django pods need access to django-config ConfigMap
# - No access to other ConfigMaps in the namespace

Command:

# Create role limited to specific resource names
kubectl create role config-reader \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --verb=get,watch \
  --resource=configmaps \
  --resource-name=django-config,django-settings,nginx-config

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/config-reader created

✅ Verification:

# Check resource name restrictions
kubectl get role config-reader -n anihpj-production -o yaml

# Test access to allowed configmap
kubectl auth can-i get configmaps/django-config -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:app-sa

# Test access to different configmap (should fail)
kubectl auth can-i get configmaps/other-config -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:app-sa

# Test list (should fail - not in verbs)
kubectl auth can-i list configmaps -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:app-sa
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: config-reader
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resourceNames:
  - django-config
  - django-settings
  - nginx-config
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch"]

yes
no
no
Example 5: Create Viewer/Auditor Role

Scenario: Create a read-only role for security auditors or monitoring systems.

📄 Context - Audit/Monitoring Requirements:

# Read-only role for auditing and monitoring
#
# Use cases:
# - Security audit - view all resources without modification
# - Monitoring systems - scrape resource states
# - Compliance checks - verify configurations
#
# This role should:
# - Read all resources
# - NOT modify any resources
# - Access logs for troubleshooting
# - View events for incident tracking

Command:

# Create comprehensive viewer role
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: viewer
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
rules:
# All core resources - read only
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# All apps resources - read only  
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# All batch resources - read only
- apiGroups: ["batch"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# Networking - read only
- apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# Autoscaling - read only
- apiGroups: ["autoscaling"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# Policy - read only
- apiGroups: ["policy"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/viewer created

✅ Verification:

# Test viewer permissions
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-production --as=user:auditor
kubectl auth can-i list secrets -n anihpj-production --as=user:auditor
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n anihpj-production --as=user:auditor
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n anihpj-production --as=user:auditor

# List all permissions for the role
kubectl auth can-i --list -n anihpj-production --as=user:auditor | head -20
yes
yes
no
no

Resources                                       Non-Resource URLs   Resource Names   Verbs
configmaps                                      []                  []               [get list watch]
endpoints                                       []                  []               [get list watch]
events                                          []                  []               [get list watch]
persistentvolumeclaims                          []                  []               [get list watch]
pods                                            []                  []               [get list watch]
secrets                                         []                  []               [get list watch]
services                                        []                  []               [get list watch]
deployments.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch]
replicasets.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch]
statefulsets.apps                               []                  []               [get list watch]
...
Example 6: Create Role for Pod Debugging

Scenario: Create a role specifically for debugging running pods (exec, logs, port-forward).

📄 Context - Debug Access Requirements:

# Debug role for production troubleshooting
#
# Use cases:
# - On-call engineers debugging production issues
# - Developers troubleshooting staging problems
#
# Provides:
# - View pod logs (pods/log)
# - Exec into containers (pods/exec)
# - Port-forward for local access (pods/portforward)
# - View pod details and events
#
# Does NOT provide:
# - Create/delete pods
# - Modify deployments
# - Access secrets directly

Command:

# Create debug role
kubectl create role pod-debugger \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,pods/log,pods/exec,pods/portforward,events

# Add port-forward via patch (or use manifest)
kubectl patch role pod-debugger -n anihpj-production --type='merge' -p='
{
  "rules": [
    {
      "apiGroups": [""],
      "resources": ["pods", "pods/log"],
      "verbs": ["get", "list", "watch"]
    },
    {
      "apiGroups": [""],
      "resources": ["pods/exec", "pods/portforward"],
      "verbs": ["create"]
    },
    {
      "apiGroups": [""],
      "resources": ["events"],
      "verbs": ["get", "list", "watch"]
    }
  ]
}'

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-debugger created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-debugger patched

✅ Verification:

# Test debug permissions
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall
kubectl auth can-i get pods/log -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall
kubectl auth can-i create pods/exec -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall

# Use the debug role
kubectl logs deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall -- whoami
kubectl port-forward deployment/jobpost-web 8000:8000 -n anihpj-production --as=user:oncall
yes
yes
yes
no

[job logs output...]
django
Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8000 -> 8000

kubectl create rolebinding

CREATE RBAC Security Binding

Create a role binding for a particular role or cluster role

The kubectl create rolebinding command creates a RoleBinding that grants the permissions defined in a Role to a user, group, or service account within a specific namespace.

For the anihpj Django project, role bindings connect:

  • Developers to the developer role in their namespace
  • CI/CD service accounts to the deployer role
  • Monitoring systems to the viewer role
  • On-call engineers to the pod-debugger role
  • Application service accounts to their required roles

Syntax

# Bind to a Role
kubectl create rolebinding NAME --role=ROLE --user=USER [--group=GROUP] [--serviceaccount=NS:SA]

# Bind to a ClusterRole (namespace-scoped binding)
kubectl create rolebinding NAME --clusterrole=CLUSTERROLE --user=USER

# Multiple subjects
kubectl create rolebinding NAME --role=ROLE --user=user1 --user=user2 --group=dev-team

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--rolestringRole name to bind (namespace-scoped)
--clusterrolestringClusterRole name to bind (reusable across namespaces)
--user[]stringUsernames to bind
--group[]stringGroups to bind
--serviceaccount[]stringServiceAccounts (namespace:name format)
-n, --namespacestringNamespace for the rolebinding

Subject Types

TypeFormatUse Case
User--user=usernameIndividual human users
Group--group=groupnameAD/LDAP/OIDC groups
ServiceAccount--serviceaccount=ns:saPod workload identities

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0RoleBinding created successfully
1Error (role not found, already exists)

📁 Context: RoleBinding Architecture for anihpj

RoleBinding vs ClusterRoleBinding: RoleBindings are namespace-scoped (grant access within one namespace). ClusterRoleBindings are cluster-wide (grant access across all namespaces).
Binding Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       RoleBinding Architecture                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Subjects (Who)              RoleBinding              Role/ClusterRole       │
│  ┌─────────────────┐         ┌──────────┐            ┌──────────────────┐   │
│  │ User: alice@co  │────────┤│ dev-bind │───────────│ Role: developer  │   │
│  │ Group: dev-team │────────┤│          │           │                  │   │
│  └─────────────────┘         └──────────┘            │ verbs: get,list  │   │
│                                                      │       create,del │   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐         ┌──────────┐            │ resources: pods  │   │
│  │ SA: deploy-sa   │────────┤│ ci-bind  │───────────│           deploy │   │
│  │ (anihpj-prod)   │         │          │           └──────────────────┘   │
│  └─────────────────┘         └──────────┘                                   │
│                                     │                ┌──────────────────┐   │
│                                     └───────────────│ ClusterRole:view │   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐         ┌──────────┐           │                  │   │
│  │ Group: ops-team │────────┤│view-bind │───────────│ verbs: get,list  │   │
│  └─────────────────┘         └──────────┘            │       watch      │   │
│                                                      │ resources: *     │   │
│                                                      └──────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
│  All bindings above are namespace-scoped (anihpj-production)                │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Bind User to Developer Role

Scenario: Grant a developer access to the development namespace.

📄 Context - User Access Request:

# Developer onboarding for anihpj project
#
# User: alice@company.com
# Team: Django Backend Team
# Role needed: developer (full CRUD on workloads)
# Namespace: anihpj-dev
#
# Prerequisites:
# - Role 'developer' exists in anihpj-dev namespace
# - User identity authenticated via OIDC/AD

Command:

# Create rolebinding for user
kubectl create rolebinding alice-developer-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --role=developer \
  --user=alice@company.com

Output:

rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/alice-developer-binding created

✅ Verification:

# Check binding
kubectl get rolebinding alice-developer-binding -n anihpj-dev

# View binding details
kubectl describe rolebinding alice-developer-binding -n anihpj-dev

# Test user permissions
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-dev --as=alice@company.com
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments -n anihpj-dev --as=alice@company.com
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-production --as=alice@company.com
NAME                      ROLE             AGE
alice-developer-binding   Role/developer   1m

Name:         alice-developer-binding
Labels:       
Namespace:    anihpj-dev
Role:
  Kind:  Role
  Name:  developer
Subjects:
  Kind  Name               Namespace
  ----  ----               ---------
  User  alice@company.com

yes
yes
no
Example 2: Bind Group to Role

Scenario: Grant an entire team access via group binding (OIDC/AD group).

📄 Context - Team Access Configuration:

# Group-based access for anihpj development team
#
# Group: backend-developers (from Azure AD)
# Members: 5 developers
# Namespace: anihpj-dev, anihpj-staging
#
# Benefits of group binding:
# - Automatic access when user joins group
# - Automatic revocation when user leaves group
# - Single binding for entire team
# - Managed by identity provider (HR/IT)

Command:

# Create rolebinding for group
kubectl create rolebinding backend-dev-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --role=developer \
  --group=backend-developers

# Also create for staging
kubectl create rolebinding backend-dev-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-staging \
  --role=developer \
  --group=backend-developers

Output:

rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/backend-dev-binding created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/backend-dev-binding created

✅ Verification:

# Check bindings in both namespaces
kubectl get rolebindings -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj -A

# Test group member access
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-dev --as=bob@company.com \
  --as-group=backend-developers

# Verify non-member cannot access
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-dev --as=eve@company.com
NAMESPACE        NAME                  ROLE             AGE
anihpj-dev       backend-dev-binding   Role/developer   1m
anihpj-staging   backend-dev-binding   Role/developer   1m

yes
no
Example 3: Bind ServiceAccount for CI/CD

Scenario: Create binding for GitHub Actions deployment service account.

📄 Context - CI/CD Service Account Setup:

# CI/CD deployment pipeline needs deployment access
#
# ServiceAccount: github-actions-deploy
# Namespace: anihpj-production
# Role needed: deployer (create/update but not delete)
#
# Pipeline workflow:
# 1. GitHub Actions authenticates via OIDC
# 2. Assumes ServiceAccount identity
# 3. Deploys new image versions
# 4. Cannot delete existing resources (safety)

📄 Context - ServiceAccount Creation:

# First create the serviceaccount
kubectl create serviceaccount github-actions-deploy -n anihpj-production

# Create the deployer role if not exists
kubectl create role deployer -n anihpj-production \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch \
  --resource=deployments,services,configmaps,ingresses

Command:

# Bind serviceaccount to role
kubectl create rolebinding github-actions-deploy-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --role=deployer \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj-production:github-actions-deploy

Output:

rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/github-actions-deploy-binding created

✅ Verification:

# Test serviceaccount permissions
kubectl auth can-i update deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions-deploy \
  -n anihpj-production

kubectl auth can-i delete deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions-deploy \
  -n anihpj-production

# List all permissions
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions-deploy \
  -n anihpj-production
yes
no

Resources                                       Non-Resource URLs   Resource Names   Verbs
configmaps                                      []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
deployments.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
ingresses.networking.k8s.io                     []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
services                                        []                  []               [get list watch create update patch]
Example 4: Bind ClusterRole to Namespace (Reusable Roles)

Scenario: Use a ClusterRole but limit its scope to a specific namespace via RoleBinding.

📄 Context - Reusable ClusterRole Pattern:

# Using ClusterRole with RoleBinding pattern
#
# Benefits:
# - Define role once (ClusterRole)
# - Reuse across namespaces (RoleBinding per namespace)
# - Centralized role management
#
# Built-in ClusterRoles:
# - view: read-only access to most resources
# - edit: read/write to most resources
# - admin: full namespace admin (except RBAC)

Command:

# Bind built-in 'view' ClusterRole to namespace
kubectl create rolebinding ops-viewer-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --group=operations-team

# Bind 'edit' ClusterRole for leads in dev namespace
kubectl create rolebinding lead-edit-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --clusterrole=edit \
  --user=tech.lead@company.com

Output:

rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/ops-viewer-binding created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/lead-edit-binding created

✅ Verification:

# Verify ops can only view in production
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-production --as-group=operations-team
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n anihpj-production --as-group=operations-team

# Verify lead can edit in dev
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n anihpj-dev --as=tech.lead@company.com
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n anihpj-production --as=tech.lead@company.com
yes
no
yes
no
Example 5: Complete RBAC Setup Manifest

Scenario: Create comprehensive RBAC setup for the anihpj project with multiple roles and bindings.

📄 Context - Complete RBAC Manifest:

# k8s/rbac/production-rbac.yaml
# Complete RBAC configuration for anihpj production
---
# Developer role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "services", "configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
# Deployer role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deployer
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["", "apps", "networking.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["deployments", "services", "configmaps", "ingresses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
# Pod debugger role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-debugger
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["create"]
---
# Backend team binding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: backend-developers
  namespace: anihpj-production
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: developer
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: backend-team
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# CI/CD binding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ci-cd-deployer
  namespace: anihpj-production
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: deployer
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: github-actions-deploy
  namespace: anihpj-production
---
# On-call binding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: oncall-debugger
  namespace: anihpj-production
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: pod-debugger
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: oncall-engineers
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command:

# Apply complete RBAC configuration
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac/production-rbac.yaml

# Verify all resources created
kubectl get roles,rolebindings -n anihpj-production

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/deployer created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-debugger created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/backend-developers created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/ci-cd-deployer created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/oncall-debugger created

NAME                                    ROLE                 AGE
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/deployer      deployer       1m
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developer     developer      1m
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-debugger  pod-debugger   1m

NAME                                              ROLE             AGE
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/backend-developers   Role/developer     1m
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/ci-cd-deployer       Role/deployer      1m
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/oncall-debugger      Role/pod-debugger  1m

✅ Verification:

# Test different personas
# Backend developer
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj-production --as-group=backend-team

# CI/CD
kubectl auth can-i update deployments -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions-deploy

# On-call
kubectl auth can-i create pods/exec -n anihpj-production --as-group=oncall-engineers
yes
yes
yes
Example 6: Application Pod ServiceAccount Binding

Scenario: Create binding for application pods to access their required secrets and configmaps.

📄 Context - Application Access Requirements:

# Django application pods need:
# - Read ConfigMaps (django-config, nginx-config)
# - Read Secrets (database-credentials, api-keys)
# - NO write access to any resources
# - NO access to other pods or deployments
#
# ServiceAccount: jobpost-app
# Namespace: anihpj-production

📄 Context - Application Role:

# k8s/rbac/app-role.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: app-config-reader
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
# Read specific configmaps
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  resourceNames: ["django-config", "nginx-config", "gunicorn-config"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch"]
# Read specific secrets  
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  resourceNames: ["database-credentials", "redis-credentials", "api-keys"]
  verbs: ["get"]

Command:

# Create serviceaccount for app
kubectl create serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production

# Create the restricted role
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac/app-role.yaml

# Bind serviceaccount to role
kubectl create rolebinding jobpost-app-config-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --role=app-config-reader \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj-production:jobpost-app

Output:

serviceaccount/jobpost-app created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/app-config-reader created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-app-config-binding created

📄 Context - Deployment Using ServiceAccount:

# k8s/deployment.yaml (excerpt)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: jobpost-app  # Use the SA
      automountServiceAccountToken: true
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:latest
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: database-credentials

✅ Verification:

# Test app SA permissions
# Should succeed
kubectl auth can-i get configmaps/django-config -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-app

kubectl auth can-i get secrets/database-credentials -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-app

# Should fail
kubectl auth can-i get configmaps/other-config -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-app

kubectl auth can-i list pods -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-app

kubectl auth can-i create secrets -n anihpj-production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-app
yes
yes
no
no
no

kubectl create secret

CREATE Security Configuration Credentials

Create a secret using specified subcommand

The kubectl create secret command creates Secret resources for storing sensitive data like passwords, API keys, and TLS certificates. Secrets are base64-encoded and can be mounted as volumes or exposed as environment variables.

For the anihpj Django project, secrets store:

  • Database credentials (PostgreSQL, Redis)
  • API keys (Stripe, SendGrid, external services)
  • TLS certificates for HTTPS ingress
  • Docker registry credentials for private images
  • Django SECRET_KEY for cryptographic signing
  • OAuth client secrets for authentication
⚠️ Security Warning: Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default. Enable encryption at rest and use sealed-secrets or external secret stores (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) for production.

Syntax

# Generic secret from literal values
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-literal=key=value

# Generic secret from file
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-file=path/to/file

# Docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry NAME --docker-server=SERVER --docker-username=USER --docker-password=PASS

# TLS secret
kubectl create secret tls NAME --cert=path/to/cert --key=path/to/key

Secret Types

TypeSubcommandUse Case
OpaquegenericArbitrary key-value data (passwords, API keys)
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjsondocker-registryDocker private registry auth
kubernetes.io/tlstlsTLS certificate and key pairs

Parameters (generic)

ParameterTypeDescription
--from-literal[]stringKey=value pairs for secret data
--from-file[]stringFile paths (key=filename or filename)
--from-env-filestring.env file for multiple values
--typestringSecret type (default: Opaque)

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Secret created successfully
1Error (file not found, already exists)

📁 Context: Secrets Architecture for anihpj

Secret Management Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Secret Management Architecture                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  External Sources              Kubernetes Secrets         Consumers          │
│  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐│
│  │ HashiCorp Vault │─────────│► database-creds │───────│► Django Pod     ││
│  │ Azure KeyVault  │ sync    │  - DB_HOST      │ mount  │  envFrom:       ││
│  │ AWS Secrets Mgr │─────────│  - DB_USER      │  or    │   secretRef     ││
│  └─────────────────┘          │  - DB_PASSWORD  │ env    └─────────────────┘│
│                               └─────────────────┘                            │
│  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐│
│  │ cert-manager    │─────────│► tls-certificate│───────│► Ingress        ││
│  │ Let's Encrypt   │ auto    │  - tls.crt      │ ref    │  tls:           ││
│  └─────────────────┘ renew   │  - tls.key      │        │   secretName    ││
│                               └─────────────────┘        └─────────────────┘│
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐│
│  │ CI/CD Pipeline  │─────────│► registry-creds │───────│► kubelet        ││
│  │ sealed-secrets  │ create  │  - .dockerconfig│ pull   │  imagePullSec   ││
│  └─────────────────┘──────────│                 │ json   └─────────────────┘│
│                               └─────────────────┘                            │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Database Credentials Secret

Scenario: Create a secret for PostgreSQL database connection credentials for the anihpj Django project.

📄 Context - Database Configuration:

# Database credentials for anihpj Django project
#
# PostgreSQL connection details:
# Host: postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
# Port: 5432
# Database: anihpj_db
# User: anihpj_user
# Password: [secure random password]
#
# Used in Django settings.py:
# DATABASES = {
#     'default': {
#         'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
#         'HOST': os.environ['DB_HOST'],
#         'NAME': os.environ['DB_NAME'],
#         ...
#     }
# }

Command:

# Create database credentials secret from literals
kubectl create secret generic database-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 \
  --from-literal=DB_NAME=anihpj_db \
  --from-literal=DB_USER=anihpj_user \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='S3cureP@ssw0rd!2024'

Output:

secret/database-credentials created

✅ Verification:

# Check secret exists
kubectl get secret database-credentials -n anihpj-production

# View secret keys (not values)
kubectl describe secret database-credentials -n anihpj-production

# Decode and view a specific value (careful in production!)
kubectl get secret database-credentials -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.DB_USER}' | base64 -d
NAME                   TYPE     DATA   AGE
database-credentials   Opaque   5      1m

Name:         database-credentials
Namespace:    anihpj-production
Type:         Opaque
Data
====
DB_HOST:      44 bytes
DB_NAME:      10 bytes
DB_PASSWORD:  21 bytes
DB_PORT:      4 bytes
DB_USER:      11 bytes

anihpj_user
Example 2: Create Docker Registry Secret

Scenario: Create imagePullSecrets for pulling anihpj images from a private container registry.

📄 Context - Private Registry Setup:

# Private container registry for anihpj images
#
# Registry: ghcr.io/company/anihpj
# Authentication: GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT)
# 
# Images:
# - ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest
# - ghcr.io/company/anihpj:v1.2.3
#
# Usage in Deployment:
# spec:
#   imagePullSecrets:
#   - name: ghcr-registry
#   containers:
#   - image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest

Command:

# Create docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-registry \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-user \
  --docker-password=ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx \
  --docker-email=deploy@company.com

Output:

secret/ghcr-registry created

📄 Context - Deployment Using Registry Secret:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: ghcr-registry
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:v1.2.3

✅ Verification:

# Check secret type
kubectl get secret ghcr-registry -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.type}'

# View docker config structure
kubectl get secret ghcr-registry -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.\.dockerconfigjson}' | base64 -d | jq .

# Test image pull
kubectl run test-pull --image=ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"imagePullSecrets":[{"name":"ghcr-registry"}]}}' \
  -n anihpj-production --rm -it --restart=Never -- echo "Pull succeeded"
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson

{
  "auths": {
    "ghcr.io": {
      "username": "github-user",
      "password": "ghp_xxxx...",
      "email": "deploy@company.com",
      "auth": "Z2l0aHViLXVzZXI6Z2hwX3h4eHg..."
    }
  }
}

Pull succeeded
Example 3: Create TLS Secret for HTTPS

Scenario: Create a TLS secret for securing the anihpj ingress with HTTPS.

📄 Context - TLS Certificate Setup:

# TLS certificate for anihpj.company.com
#
# Certificate Authority: Let's Encrypt (or company CA)
# Domain: anihpj.company.com
# Validity: 90 days
#
# Files required:
# - tls.crt: Full certificate chain
# - tls.key: Private key (RSA/ECDSA)
#
# Note: For production, use cert-manager for auto-renewal

📄 Context - Generate Self-Signed Cert (Dev):

# Generate self-signed certificate for development
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
  -keyout tls.key \
  -out tls.crt \
  -subj "/CN=anihpj.local/O=anihpj" \
  -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:anihpj.local,DNS:*.anihpj.local"

Command:

# Create TLS secret from certificate files
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key

Output:

secret/anihpj-tls created

📄 Context - Ingress Using TLS Secret:

# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - anihpj.company.com
    secretName: anihpj-tls  # Reference the TLS secret
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.company.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 8000

✅ Verification:

# Check TLS secret
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production

# View certificate details
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -text | head -20

# Check ingress TLS configuration
kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-production | grep -A5 TLS
NAME          TYPE                DATA   AGE
anihpj-tls    kubernetes.io/tls   2      1m

Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Serial Number: 12345...
        Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Issuer: O = anihpj, CN = anihpj.local
        Validity
            Not Before: Jan 15 10:00:00 2024 GMT
            Not After : Jan 15 10:00:00 2025 GMT
        Subject: O = anihpj, CN = anihpj.local

TLS:
  anihpj-tls terminates anihpj.company.com
Example 4: Create Secret from Environment File

Scenario: Create secrets from a .env file containing multiple Django configuration values.

📄 Context - Environment File (.env.production):

# .env.production
# Django production settings

# Security
SECRET_KEY=django-insecure-replace-with-real-50-char-random-string
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=anihpj.company.com,www.anihpj.company.com

# API Keys
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_xxxxxxxxxxxx
STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=pk_live_xxxxxxxxxxxx
SENDGRID_API_KEY=SG.xxxxxxxxxxxx

# External Services
SENTRY_DSN=https://xxxxxx@sentry.io/xxxxxx
REDIS_URL=redis://:password@redis:6379/0

# OAuth
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=xxxxx.apps.googleusercontent.com
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=GOCSPX-xxxxxxxxxx

Command:

# Create secret from env file
kubectl create secret generic django-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-env-file=.env.production

Output:

secret/django-secrets created

📄 Context - Deployment Using Env Secrets:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:latest
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        # Or mount individual keys:
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SECRET_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: SECRET_KEY

✅ Verification:

# Check all keys loaded
kubectl describe secret django-secrets -n anihpj-production

# Count keys
kubectl get secret django-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys | length'

# Verify specific key exists
kubectl get secret django-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.SECRET_KEY}' | base64 -d | cut -c1-20
Name:         django-secrets
Namespace:    anihpj-production
Type:         Opaque
Data
====
ALLOWED_HOSTS:          43 bytes
DEBUG:                  5 bytes
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID:       45 bytes
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET:   24 bytes
REDIS_URL:              31 bytes
SECRET_KEY:             56 bytes
SENDGRID_API_KEY:       40 bytes
SENTRY_DSN:             42 bytes
STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY: 24 bytes
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY:      24 bytes

10
django-insecure-rep...
Example 5: Create Secret from Files

Scenario: Create a secret containing multiple configuration files for the application.

📄 Context - Configuration Files:

# config/gunicorn.conf.py
import multiprocessing

bind = "0.0.0.0:8000"
workers = multiprocessing.cpu_count() * 2 + 1
worker_class = "gevent"
timeout = 120
keepalive = 5
errorlog = "-"
accesslog = "-"
loglevel = "info"
# config/nginx.conf
upstream django {
    server jobpost-web:8000;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name anihpj.company.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://django;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    }

    location /static/ {
        alias /app/staticfiles/;
    }
}

Command:

# Create secret from multiple files
kubectl create secret generic app-configs \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-file=gunicorn.conf.py=config/gunicorn.conf.py \
  --from-file=nginx.conf=config/nginx.conf \
  --from-file=supervisord.conf=config/supervisord.conf

Output:

secret/app-configs created

📄 Context - Mounting Files from Secret:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/gunicorn/gunicorn.conf.py
          subPath: gunicorn.conf.py
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d/django.conf
          subPath: nginx.conf
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        secret:
          secretName: app-configs
          defaultMode: 0644

✅ Verification:

# Check files in secret
kubectl get secret app-configs -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys'

# View file content
kubectl get secret app-configs -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.gunicorn\.conf\.py}' | base64 -d

# Verify mount in pod
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  cat /etc/gunicorn/gunicorn.conf.py
["gunicorn.conf.py", "nginx.conf", "supervisord.conf"]

import multiprocessing

bind = "0.0.0.0:8000"
workers = multiprocessing.cpu_count() * 2 + 1
...
Example 6: Complete Secrets Management Setup

Scenario: Create comprehensive secrets setup for the anihpj production environment with proper organization and labeling.

📄 Context - Secrets Organization Strategy:

# Secrets organization for anihpj
#
# Categories:
# 1. database-credentials  - Database connection info
# 2. api-keys              - External API keys
# 3. tls-certificates      - HTTPS certificates
# 4. registry-credentials  - Docker registry auth
# 5. app-secrets           - Application-specific secrets
#
# Labels:
# - app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
# - app.kubernetes.io/component: secrets
# - environment: production

📄 Context - Secrets Manifest:

# k8s/secrets/all-secrets.yaml
---
# Database credentials
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: database-credentials
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: database
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DB_HOST: postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_NAME: anihpj_db
  DB_USER: anihpj_user
  DB_PASSWORD: "${DB_PASSWORD}"  # Replace with actual
---
# Redis credentials
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: redis-credentials
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: cache
type: Opaque
stringData:
  REDIS_HOST: redis.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
  REDIS_PORT: "6379"
  REDIS_PASSWORD: "${REDIS_PASSWORD}"
  REDIS_URL: "redis://:${REDIS_PASSWORD}@redis:6379/0"
---
# API keys
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-keys
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: external-services
type: Opaque
stringData:
  STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: "${STRIPE_SECRET}"
  SENDGRID_API_KEY: "${SENDGRID_KEY}"
  SENTRY_DSN: "${SENTRY_DSN}"
---
# Django application secrets
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-app-secrets
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: app
type: Opaque
stringData:
  SECRET_KEY: "${DJANGO_SECRET_KEY}"
  ENCRYPTION_KEY: "${ENCRYPTION_KEY}"

📄 Context - Sealed Secrets Integration:

# Using sealed-secrets for GitOps-safe secret storage
# Install sealed-secrets controller and kubeseal CLI

# Seal a secret for safe storage in Git
kubectl create secret generic database-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='actual-password' \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubeseal --format yaml > k8s/secrets/sealed-database-credentials.yaml

# Sealed secret can be safely committed to Git

Command:

# Create all secrets using envsubst for variable substitution
export DB_PASSWORD='S3cureDBP@ss!'
export REDIS_PASSWORD='R3disP@ss!'
export STRIPE_SECRET='sk_live_xxx'
export SENDGRID_KEY='SG.xxx'
export SENTRY_DSN='https://xxx@sentry.io/xxx'
export DJANGO_SECRET_KEY='django-insecure-xxx'
export ENCRYPTION_KEY='32-byte-encryption-key-here!'

envsubst < k8s/secrets/all-secrets.yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Or create individually with proper labels
kubectl create secret generic database-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD="$DB_PASSWORD" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubectl label -f - --local -o yaml \
    app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj \
    app.kubernetes.io/component=database | \
  kubectl apply -f -

Output:

secret/database-credentials created
secret/redis-credentials created
secret/api-keys created
secret/django-app-secrets created

✅ Verification:

# List all anihpj secrets
kubectl get secrets -n anihpj-production -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj

# Check secret data keys
for secret in database-credentials redis-credentials api-keys django-app-secrets; do
  echo "=== $secret ==="
  kubectl get secret $secret -n anihpj-production \
    -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys'
done

# Audit secrets (check for required secrets)
kubectl get secrets -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
TYPE:.type,\
KEYS:.data,\
AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME                   TYPE     DATA   AGE
api-keys               Opaque   3      1m
database-credentials   Opaque   5      1m
django-app-secrets     Opaque   2      1m
redis-credentials      Opaque   4      1m

=== database-credentials ===
["DB_HOST", "DB_NAME", "DB_PASSWORD", "DB_PORT", "DB_USER"]
=== redis-credentials ===
["REDIS_HOST", "REDIS_PASSWORD", "REDIS_PORT", "REDIS_URL"]
=== api-keys ===
["SENDGRID_API_KEY", "SENTRY_DSN", "STRIPE_SECRET_KEY"]
=== django-app-secrets ===
["ENCRYPTION_KEY", "SECRET_KEY"]

kubectl create secret docker-registry

CREATE Security Registry Auth Image Pull

Create a secret for use with a Docker registry

The kubectl create secret docker-registry command creates a secret containing credentials for authenticating with private Docker registries. This secret type (kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson) is used by kubelets to pull private container images.

For the anihpj Django project, docker-registry secrets enable:

  • Pulling private images from GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io)
  • Accessing AWS ECR, Google GCR, or Azure ACR
  • Using private Docker Hub repositories
  • Multi-registry authentication for microservices

Syntax

# Create docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry NAME \
  --docker-server=SERVER \
  --docker-username=USERNAME \
  --docker-password=PASSWORD \
  [--docker-email=EMAIL] \
  [--namespace=NAMESPACE]

# From existing docker config file
kubectl create secret docker-registry NAME \
  --from-file=.dockerconfigjson=~/.docker/config.json

Parameters

ParameterTypeRequiredDescription
--docker-serverstringYesDocker registry server (e.g., ghcr.io, docker.io)
--docker-usernamestringYesDocker registry username
--docker-passwordstringYesDocker registry password or token
--docker-emailstringNoEmail for Docker registry
--from-filestringNoPath to .dockerconfigjson file
--dry-runstringNo"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Secret created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid credentials format)

Examples

Example 1: GitHub Container Registry Authentication

Scenario: Create credentials for pulling anihpj images from GitHub Container Registry.

📄 Context - GHCR Setup:

# GitHub Container Registry for anihpj
# Registry: ghcr.io/company/anihpj
# Auth: GitHub Personal Access Token with read:packages scope
# 
# Token permissions required:
# - read:packages (pull images)
# - write:packages (push images - CI/CD only)

Command:

# Create GHCR pull secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-username \
  --docker-password=ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx \
  --docker-email=deploy@company.com

Output:

secret/ghcr-credentials created

✅ Verification:

# Check secret type
kubectl get secret ghcr-credentials -n anihpj-production

# View dockerconfigjson structure
kubectl get secret ghcr-credentials -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.\.dockerconfigjson}' | base64 -d | jq .
NAME               TYPE                             DATA   AGE
ghcr-credentials   kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson   1      5s

{
  "auths": {
    "ghcr.io": {
      "username": "github-username",
      "password": "ghp_xxxx...",
      "email": "deploy@company.com",
      "auth": "Z2l0aHViLXVzZXJuYW1lOmdocF94eHh4..."
    }
  }
}
Example 2: AWS ECR Authentication

Scenario: Create credentials for Amazon ECR with token refresh.

📄 Context - ECR Token Refresh Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/refresh-ecr-token.sh
# ECR tokens expire every 12 hours - run via CronJob

AWS_ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
AWS_REGION="us-east-1"
NAMESPACE="anihpj-production"
SECRET_NAME="ecr-credentials"

# Get ECR token
TOKEN=$(aws ecr get-login-password --region $AWS_REGION)

# Delete existing secret (if exists)
kubectl delete secret $SECRET_NAME -n $NAMESPACE --ignore-not-found

# Create new secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry $SECRET_NAME \
  --namespace=$NAMESPACE \
  --docker-server=${AWS_ACCOUNT_ID}.dkr.ecr.${AWS_REGION}.amazonaws.com \
  --docker-username=AWS \
  --docker-password=$TOKEN

Command:

# Manual ECR secret creation
kubectl create secret docker-registry ecr-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com \
  --docker-username=AWS \
  --docker-password=$(aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1)

Output:

secret/ecr-credentials created

✅ Verification:

# Test image pull with ECR
kubectl run ecr-test \
  --image=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"imagePullSecrets":[{"name":"ecr-credentials"}]}}' \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --rm -it --restart=Never -- echo "ECR pull successful"
ECR pull successful
pod "ecr-test" deleted
Example 3: Multi-Registry Secret

Scenario: Create a secret supporting multiple registries from existing Docker config.

📄 Context - Docker Config (~/.docker/config.json):

{
  "auths": {
    "ghcr.io": {
      "auth": "base64-encoded-creds"
    },
    "docker.io": {
      "auth": "base64-encoded-creds"
    },
    "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com": {
      "auth": "base64-encoded-creds"
    }
  }
}

Command:

# Create from existing docker config
kubectl create secret docker-registry multi-registry-creds \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-file=.dockerconfigjson=$HOME/.docker/config.json

Output:

secret/multi-registry-creds created

✅ Verification:

# Check all registries in secret
kubectl get secret multi-registry-creds -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.\.dockerconfigjson}' | base64 -d | jq '.auths | keys'
["123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "docker.io", "ghcr.io"]
Example 4: Using imagePullSecrets in Deployment

Scenario: Configure deployment to use docker registry secret for pulling images.

📄 Context - Deployment with imagePullSecrets:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: ghcr-credentials
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

Command:

# Create secret and apply deployment
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-user \
  --docker-password=ghp_token

kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml

Output:

secret/ghcr-credentials created
deployment.apps/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Check pods pulled image successfully
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost
kubectl describe pod -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost | grep -A5 "Events:"
NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12  1/1     Running   0          30s
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45  1/1     Running   0          30s
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78  1/1     Running   0          30s

Events:
  Normal  Scheduled  30s   default-scheduler  Successfully assigned...
  Normal  Pulling    29s   kubelet            Pulling image "ghcr.io/company/anihpj:v2.1.0"
  Normal  Pulled     25s   kubelet            Successfully pulled image
  Normal  Created    25s   kubelet            Created container django
  Normal  Started    24s   kubelet            Started container django
Example 5: ServiceAccount Default imagePullSecrets

Scenario: Configure ServiceAccount to automatically use registry credentials for all pods.

Command:

# Create registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-user \
  --docker-password=ghp_token

# Patch default ServiceAccount to use the secret
kubectl patch serviceaccount default \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "ghcr-credentials"}]}'

Output:

secret/ghcr-credentials created
serviceaccount/default patched

✅ Verification:

# Verify ServiceAccount has imagePullSecrets
kubectl get serviceaccount default -n anihpj-production -o yaml

# Now pods will automatically use the credentials
kubectl run test-auto-pull \
  --image=ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --rm -it --restart=Never -- echo "Auto pull works!"
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: anihpj-production
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-credentials
Auto pull works!
pod "test-auto-pull" deleted
Example 6: Generate YAML for GitOps

Scenario: Generate docker-registry secret YAML for version control (using sealed-secrets for security).

Command:

# Generate YAML (dry-run)
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-credentials \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-user \
  --docker-password=ghp_placeholder_token \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > k8s/secrets/ghcr-credentials.yaml

# Seal for GitOps (using sealed-secrets)
kubeseal --format yaml \
  < k8s/secrets/ghcr-credentials.yaml \
  > k8s/secrets/sealed-ghcr-credentials.yaml

Output (ghcr-credentials.yaml):

apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: ghcr-credentials
  namespace: anihpj-production
type: kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson
data:
  .dockerconfigjson: eyJhdXRocyI6eyJnaGNyLmlvIjp7InVzZXJuYW1lIjoiZ2l0aHViLXVzZXIiLC...

kubectl create secret generic

CREATE Security Configuration Opaque

Create a secret from a local file, directory, or literal value

The kubectl create secret generic command creates an Opaque secret containing arbitrary key-value data. This is the most flexible secret type, used for passwords, API keys, configuration files, and any other sensitive data.

For the anihpj Django project, generic secrets store:

  • Django SECRET_KEY for cryptographic operations
  • Database connection credentials
  • API keys (Stripe, SendGrid, AWS)
  • OAuth client secrets
  • Configuration files with sensitive data

Syntax

# From literal values
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-literal=key=value [--from-literal=key2=value2]

# From file (key = filename)
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-file=path/to/file

# From file with custom key
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-file=key=path/to/file

# From directory (each file becomes a key)
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-file=path/to/directory/

# From .env file
kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-env-file=path/to/.env

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--from-literal[]stringKey=value pairs (can specify multiple)
--from-file[]stringFile path(s), optionally with key (key=path)
--from-env-filestringPath to .env file
--typestringSecret type (default: Opaque)
--append-hashboolAppend hash to secret name
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Secret created successfully
1Error (file not found, already exists)

Examples

Example 1: Django Application Secrets

Scenario: Create secrets for Django SECRET_KEY and database credentials.

📄 Context - Django settings.py:

# anihpj/settings.py
import os

SECRET_KEY = os.environ['DJANGO_SECRET_KEY']
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'False').lower() == 'true'

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'HOST': os.environ['DB_HOST'],
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DB_PORT', '5432'),
        'NAME': os.environ['DB_NAME'],
        'USER': os.environ['DB_USER'],
        'PASSWORD': os.environ['DB_PASSWORD'],
    }
}

Command:

# Create Django secrets from literals
kubectl create secret generic django-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DJANGO_SECRET_KEY='django-insecure-$(openssl rand -hex 25)' \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 \
  --from-literal=DB_NAME=anihpj_db \
  --from-literal=DB_USER=anihpj_user \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='S3cureP@ssw0rd!'

Output:

secret/django-secrets created

✅ Verification:

# List secret keys
kubectl get secret django-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys'

# Verify in deployment
kubectl set env deployment/jobpost-web --from=secret/django-secrets \
  -n anihpj-production --dry-run=client -o yaml | grep -A20 "env:"
["DB_HOST", "DB_NAME", "DB_PASSWORD", "DB_PORT", "DB_USER", "DJANGO_SECRET_KEY"]
Example 2: Create from .env File

Scenario: Create secret from existing .env file with all application configuration.

📄 Context - .env.production:

# .env.production
DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=django-insecure-abc123def456
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=anihpj.company.com

# Database
DB_HOST=postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
DB_NAME=anihpj_db
DB_USER=anihpj_user
DB_PASSWORD=S3cureP@ss!

# External APIs
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_xxxx
SENDGRID_API_KEY=SG.xxxx
SENTRY_DSN=https://xxxx@sentry.io/xxxx

# Redis
REDIS_URL=redis://:password@redis:6379/0

Command:

# Create from env file
kubectl create secret generic app-env-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-env-file=.env.production

Output:

secret/app-env-secrets created

✅ Verification:

# Count keys loaded
kubectl get secret app-env-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'length'

# Verify specific key
kubectl get secret app-env-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.ALLOWED_HOSTS}' | base64 -d
11

anihpj.company.com
Example 3: Create from Configuration Files

Scenario: Create secret containing sensitive configuration files.

📄 Context - config/gunicorn.conf.py:

# config/gunicorn.conf.py
import multiprocessing
import os

bind = f"0.0.0.0:{os.getenv('PORT', 8000)}"
workers = multiprocessing.cpu_count() * 2 + 1
worker_class = "gevent"
timeout = 120

# Logging with sensitive info
accesslog = "-"
errorlog = "-"
loglevel = os.getenv("LOG_LEVEL", "info")

Command:

# Create from multiple files
kubectl create secret generic config-files \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-file=gunicorn.conf.py=config/gunicorn.conf.py \
  --from-file=celery.conf.py=config/celery.conf.py \
  --from-file=logging.yaml=config/logging.yaml

Output:

secret/config-files created

📄 Context - Mounting in Deployment:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /app/config/gunicorn.conf.py
          subPath: gunicorn.conf.py
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: config
        secret:
          secretName: config-files

✅ Verification:

# List files in secret
kubectl get secret config-files -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys'

# View file content
kubectl get secret config-files -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.gunicorn\.conf\.py}' | base64 -d
["celery.conf.py", "gunicorn.conf.py", "logging.yaml"]

import multiprocessing
import os
...
Example 4: Create from Directory

Scenario: Create secret from all files in a directory.

📄 Context - secrets/ directory:

secrets/
├── db-password         # Contains: S3cureP@ss!
├── api-key             # Contains: sk_live_xxx
├── encryption-key      # Contains: 32-byte-key
└── jwt-secret          # Contains: jwt-secret-xxx

Command:

# Create from directory (each file becomes a key)
kubectl create secret generic app-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-file=secrets/

Output:

secret/app-secrets created

✅ Verification:

# Keys match filenames
kubectl get secret app-secrets -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq 'keys'
["api-key", "db-password", "encryption-key", "jwt-secret"]
Example 5: Mixed Sources (Literals + Files)

Scenario: Create secret combining literal values and file contents.

Command:

# Combine literals and files
kubectl create secret generic mixed-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='S3cureP@ss!' \
  --from-literal=API_KEY='sk_live_xxx' \
  --from-file=ssl-cert=certs/server.crt \
  --from-file=ssl-key=certs/server.key

Output:

secret/mixed-secrets created

✅ Verification:

# All keys present
kubectl describe secret mixed-secrets -n anihpj-production
Name:         mixed-secrets
Namespace:    anihpj-production
Type:         Opaque

Data
====
API_KEY:      11 bytes
DB_PASSWORD:  12 bytes
ssl-cert:     1234 bytes
ssl-key:      1704 bytes
Example 6: Immutable Secret with Hash

Scenario: Create immutable secret with appended hash for safer updates.

Command:

# Create with hash suffix (for rolling updates)
kubectl create secret generic django-secrets \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='new-secret-key-value' \
  --append-hash \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubectl apply -f -

# Make it immutable (Kubernetes 1.21+)
kubectl patch secret django-secrets-h5f8t2 \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"immutable": true}'

Output:

secret/django-secrets-h5f8t2 created
secret/django-secrets-h5f8t2 patched

✅ Verification:

# Secret name includes hash
kubectl get secrets -n anihpj-production | grep django-secrets

# Immutable flag set
kubectl get secret django-secrets-h5f8t2 -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.immutable}'
django-secrets-h5f8t2   Opaque   1      1m

true

kubectl create secret tls

CREATE Security TLS/SSL Certificates

Create a TLS secret from the given public/private key pair

The kubectl create secret tls command creates a secret of type kubernetes.io/tls containing a TLS certificate and private key. This is primarily used for securing Ingress resources with HTTPS.

For the anihpj Django project, TLS secrets enable:

  • HTTPS termination at Ingress for anihpj.company.com
  • Internal mTLS between services
  • Securing webhook endpoints
  • SSL/TLS for database connections

Syntax

# Create TLS secret
kubectl create secret tls NAME --cert=path/to/cert --key=path/to/key

# With namespace
kubectl create secret tls NAME --cert=cert.pem --key=key.pem -n NAMESPACE

Parameters

ParameterTypeRequiredDescription
--certstringYesPath to PEM-encoded certificate file
--keystringYesPath to PEM-encoded private key file
--dry-runstringNo"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0TLS secret created successfully
1Error (file not found, invalid cert/key pair)

Examples

Example 1: Basic TLS Secret for Ingress

Scenario: Create TLS secret for HTTPS ingress termination.

📄 Context - Certificate Generation (Let's Encrypt via certbot):

# Already have certificates from Let's Encrypt
# /etc/letsencrypt/live/anihpj.company.com/
# ├── fullchain.pem  (certificate + chain)
# └── privkey.pem    (private key)

Command:

# Create TLS secret from Let's Encrypt certificates
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=/etc/letsencrypt/live/anihpj.company.com/fullchain.pem \
  --key=/etc/letsencrypt/live/anihpj.company.com/privkey.pem

Output:

secret/anihpj-tls created

📄 Context - Ingress with TLS:

# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - anihpj.company.com
    secretName: anihpj-tls
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.company.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 80

✅ Verification:

# Check TLS secret
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production

# View certificate info
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | \
  openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates
NAME         TYPE                DATA   AGE
anihpj-tls   kubernetes.io/tls   2      5s

subject=CN = anihpj.company.com
notBefore=Mar  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=May 30 23:59:59 2026 GMT
Example 2: Self-Signed Certificate for Development

Scenario: Generate and create self-signed TLS secret for local development.

📄 Context - Generate Self-Signed Cert:

# Generate self-signed certificate
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
  -keyout tls.key \
  -out tls.crt \
  -subj "/CN=anihpj.local/O=Development" \
  -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:anihpj.local,DNS:*.anihpj.local,IP:127.0.0.1"

Command:

# Create TLS secret from generated files
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-dev-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key

Output:

secret/anihpj-dev-tls created

✅ Verification:

# Test HTTPS locally
kubectl port-forward svc/jobpost-web 8443:443 -n anihpj-dev &
curl -k https://localhost:8443/health/
{"status": "healthy"}
Example 3: Wildcard Certificate

Scenario: Create TLS secret with wildcard certificate for multiple subdomains.

📄 Context - Wildcard Cert Coverage:

# Wildcard certificate covers:
# - anihpj.company.com
# - www.anihpj.company.com
# - api.anihpj.company.com
# - admin.anihpj.company.com
# - staging.anihpj.company.com

Command:

# Create wildcard TLS secret
kubectl create secret tls wildcard-anihpj-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=wildcard.crt \
  --key=wildcard.key

Output:

secret/wildcard-anihpj-tls created

📄 Context - Ingress with Wildcard:

# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-wildcard-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - "*.anihpj.company.com"
    - anihpj.company.com
    secretName: wildcard-anihpj-tls
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.company.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 80
  - host: api.anihpj.company.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-api
            port:
              number: 80

✅ Verification:

# Check certificate SAN entries
kubectl get secret wildcard-anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | \
  openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -A1 "Subject Alternative Name"
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name: 
    DNS:*.anihpj.company.com, DNS:anihpj.company.com
Example 4: Update Expiring TLS Secret

Scenario: Replace expired/expiring TLS certificate with new one.

Command:

# Check current certificate expiration
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | \
  openssl x509 -noout -enddate

# Delete and recreate with new certificate
kubectl delete secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=new-fullchain.pem \
  --key=new-privkey.pem

# Or use replace with dry-run
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=new-fullchain.pem \
  --key=new-privkey.pem \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl replace -f -

Output:

notAfter=Mar 15 00:00:00 2026 GMT  # Expiring soon!

secret "anihpj-tls" deleted
secret/anihpj-tls created

✅ Verification:

# Verify new expiration date
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | \
  openssl x509 -noout -enddate

# Ingress controller will pick up new cert automatically
notAfter=Jun 15 00:00:00 2026 GMT  # New cert!
Example 5: TLS with Intermediate CA Chain

Scenario: Create TLS secret with full certificate chain (including intermediate CAs).

📄 Context - Certificate Chain:
