# Combine certificates in correct order:
# 1. Server certificate
# 2. Intermediate CA(s)
# 3. Root CA (optional, usually not included)

cat server.crt intermediate.crt > fullchain.crt

Command:

# Create TLS secret with full chain
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls-chain \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cert=fullchain.crt \
  --key=server.key

Output:

secret/anihpj-tls-chain created

✅ Verification:

# Verify chain is complete
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls-chain -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | \
  openssl crl2pkcs7 -nocrl -certfile /dev/stdin | \
  openssl pkcs7 -print_certs -noout
subject=CN = anihpj.company.com
issuer=CN = R3, O = Let's Encrypt

subject=CN = R3, O = Let's Encrypt
issuer=CN = ISRG Root X1, O = Internet Security Research Group
Example 6: cert-manager Integration

Scenario: Let cert-manager automatically create and renew TLS secrets.

📄 Context - cert-manager Certificate Resource:

# k8s/certificate.yaml
# cert-manager will automatically create the TLS secret
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: anihpj-cert
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  secretName: anihpj-tls-auto  # Secret will be created automatically
  issuerRef:
    name: letsencrypt-prod
    kind: ClusterIssuer
  dnsNames:
  - anihpj.company.com
  - www.anihpj.company.com
---
# ClusterIssuer for Let's Encrypt
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: admin@company.com
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-key
    solvers:
    - http01:
        ingress:
          class: nginx

Command:

# Apply Certificate resource (cert-manager creates the secret)
kubectl apply -f k8s/certificate.yaml

# Watch secret creation
kubectl get certificate anihpj-cert -n anihpj-production -w

Output:

certificate.cert-manager.io/anihpj-cert created

NAME          READY   SECRET           AGE
anihpj-cert   False   anihpj-tls-auto  0s
anihpj-cert   True    anihpj-tls-auto  45s

✅ Verification:

# Secret automatically created by cert-manager
kubectl get secret anihpj-tls-auto -n anihpj-production

# Check certificate status
kubectl describe certificate anihpj-cert -n anihpj-production | tail -10
NAME              TYPE                DATA   AGE
anihpj-tls-auto   kubernetes.io/tls   2      1m

Events:
  Type    Reason     Age   From          Message
  ----    ------     ----  ----          -------
  Normal  Issuing    60s   cert-manager  Issuing certificate...
  Normal  Generated  58s   cert-manager  Generated new private key
  Normal  Requested  58s   cert-manager  Created CertificateRequest
  Normal  Issued     45s   cert-manager  Certificate issued successfully

kubectl create service

CREATE Networking Service Discovery Load Balancing

Create a service using a specified subcommand

The kubectl create service command creates Kubernetes Service resources that provide stable network endpoints for pods. Services enable service discovery, load balancing, and network abstraction for applications.

For the anihpj Django project, services expose:

  • Django web application on port 8000
  • PostgreSQL database on port 5432
  • Redis cache on port 6379
  • Celery flower dashboard on port 5555
  • Prometheus metrics endpoint on port 9090

Syntax

# ClusterIP service (internal, default)
kubectl create service clusterip NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGETPORT

# NodePort service (external via node ports)
kubectl create service nodeport NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGETPORT [--node-port=NODEPORT]

# LoadBalancer service (cloud load balancer)
kubectl create service loadbalancer NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGETPORT

# ExternalName service (DNS CNAME)
kubectl create service externalname NAME --external-name=HOSTNAME

Service Types

TypeSubcommandDescription
ClusterIPclusteripInternal cluster IP (default, internal only)
NodePortnodeportExposes on each node's IP at static port
LoadBalancerloadbalancerCloud provider load balancer
ExternalNameexternalnameMaps to external DNS name
Noneclusterip --clusterip=NoneHeadless service (no cluster IP)

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--tcp[]stringPort mappings in PORT:TARGETPORT format
--node-portintStatic node port (30000-32767)
--clusteripstringCluster IP or "None" for headless
--external-namestringExternal DNS hostname

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (invalid port, already exists)

📁 Context: Service Architecture for anihpj

Service Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      Service Architecture for anihpj                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Internet                                                                    │
│     │                                                                        │
│     ▼                                                                        │
│  ┌──────────────┐                                                           │
│  │ LoadBalancer │  jobpost-external (type: LoadBalancer)                    │
│  │ 80, 443      │  Cloud LB → NodePort → ClusterIP → Pods                  │
│  └──────┬───────┘                                                           │
│         │                                                                    │
│         ▼                                                                    │
│  ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐                    │
│  │ jobpost-web  │   │ jobpost-web  │   │ jobpost-web  │  Deployment        │
│  │ :8000        │   │ :8000        │   │ :8000        │  (3 replicas)      │
│  └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘                    │
│         ▲                                                                    │
│         │                                                                    │
│  ┌──────────────┐                                                           │
│  │  ClusterIP   │  jobpost-web (type: ClusterIP)                           │
│  │  10.0.0.100  │  Internal cluster access, DNS: jobpost-web.ns.svc        │
│  └──────────────┘                                                           │
│         │                                                                    │
│         ├─────────────────────────────────────────┐                         │
│         ▼                                         ▼                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐                          ┌──────────────┐                 │
│  │  ClusterIP   │  postgres                │  ClusterIP   │  redis         │
│  │  10.0.0.101  │  (type: ClusterIP)       │  10.0.0.102  │  (ClusterIP)   │
│  │  :5432       │                          │  :6379       │                 │
│  └──────────────┘                          └──────────────┘                 │
│         │                                         │                         │
│         ▼                                         ▼                         │
│  ┌──────────────┐                          ┌──────────────┐                 │
│  │  PostgreSQL  │                          │    Redis     │                 │
│  │  StatefulSet │                          │  Deployment  │                 │
│  └──────────────┘                          └──────────────┘                 │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create ClusterIP Service (Internal)

Scenario: Create an internal ClusterIP service for the Django web application.

📄 Context - Internal Service Requirements:

# Internal ClusterIP service for jobpost-web
#
# Purpose: Internal cluster communication
# Access: Only within cluster
# DNS: jobpost-web.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
#
# Used by:
# - Ingress controller to route traffic
# - Other pods to make API calls
# - Health checks from liveness/readiness probes
# - Prometheus for metrics scraping

Command:

# Create ClusterIP service for Django
kubectl create service clusterip jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=8000:8000

Output:

service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Check service
kubectl get service jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# View service details
kubectl describe service jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Get service endpoints
kubectl get endpoints jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Test DNS resolution from within cluster
kubectl run test-dns --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox \
  -n anihpj-production -- nslookup jobpost-web
NAME          TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
jobpost-web   ClusterIP   10.0.12.150           8000/TCP   1m

Name:              jobpost-web
Namespace:         anihpj-production
Selector:          app=jobpost-web
Type:              ClusterIP
IP:                10.0.12.150
Port:              8000  8000/TCP
TargetPort:        8000/TCP
Endpoints:         10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.8:8000,10.244.3.12:8000

NAME          ENDPOINTS                                              AGE
jobpost-web   10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.8:8000,10.244.3.12:8000      1m

Server:    10.0.0.10
Address 1: 10.0.0.10 kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

Name:      jobpost-web
Address 1: 10.0.12.150 jobpost-web.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Example 2: Create NodePort Service (Development)

Scenario: Create a NodePort service for accessing the application during development without a load balancer.

📄 Context - Development Access:

# NodePort service for development/testing
#
# Purpose: External access without cloud LB
# Access: http://NODE_IP:30080
# Port range: 30000-32767
#
# Use cases:
# - Local development with minikube/kind
# - On-premise clusters without cloud LB
# - Quick testing before setting up Ingress

Command:

# Create NodePort service with specific port
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-nodeport \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=8000:8000 \
  --node-port=30080

Output:

service/jobpost-nodeport created

✅ Verification:

# Check NodePort service
kubectl get service jobpost-nodeport -n anihpj-dev

# Get node IPs
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# Test access (from outside cluster)
curl http://NODE_IP:30080/api/health/

# For minikube
minikube service jobpost-nodeport -n anihpj-dev --url
NAME               TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)          AGE
jobpost-nodeport   NodePort   10.0.15.200           8000:30080/TCP   1m

NAME     STATUS   INTERNAL-IP    EXTERNAL-IP     OS-IMAGE
node-1   Ready    192.168.1.10   34.123.45.67    Ubuntu 22.04
node-2   Ready    192.168.1.11   34.123.45.68    Ubuntu 22.04

{"status": "healthy", "database": "connected"}

http://192.168.49.2:30080
Example 3: Create LoadBalancer Service (Production)

Scenario: Create a LoadBalancer service to expose the application via a cloud load balancer.

📄 Context - Production LoadBalancer:

# LoadBalancer service for production
#
# Purpose: Public internet access via cloud LB
# Providers: AWS ELB/NLB, GCP Load Balancer, Azure LB
#
# Features:
# - Automatic cloud load balancer provisioning
# - Public IP address assignment
# - Health checks and failover
# - SSL termination (with annotations)
#
# Cost: Cloud providers charge for LB resources

📄 Context - LoadBalancer Manifest with Annotations:

# k8s/services/loadbalancer.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-external
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: frontend
  annotations:
    # AWS NLB annotations
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-scheme: "internet-facing"
    # GCP annotations
    cloud.google.com/load-balancer-type: "External"
    # Azure annotations
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/azure-load-balancer-internal: "false"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  - name: https
    port: 443
    targetPort: 8000

Command:

# Create LoadBalancer service
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-external \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000,443:8000

# Or apply manifest for more control
kubectl apply -f k8s/services/loadbalancer.yaml

Output:

service/jobpost-external created

✅ Verification:

# Check service (EXTERNAL-IP may show pending initially)
kubectl get service jobpost-external -n anihpj-production -w

# Wait for external IP
kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}' \
  service/jobpost-external -n anihpj-production --timeout=300s

# Get the external IP
EXTERNAL_IP=$(kubectl get service jobpost-external -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}')

# Test public access
curl http://$EXTERNAL_IP/api/health/
NAME               TYPE           CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)                      AGE
jobpost-external   LoadBalancer   10.0.20.50        80:31234/TCP,443:31235/TCP   10s
jobpost-external   LoadBalancer   10.0.20.50   34.120.56.78  80:31234/TCP,443:31235/TCP   60s

service.v1/jobpost-external condition met

{"status": "healthy"}
Example 4: Create ExternalName Service

Scenario: Create an ExternalName service to access cloud-managed database or external API.

📄 Context - External Service Integration:

# ExternalName service for managed database
#
# Purpose: DNS alias for external services
# Use cases:
# - AWS RDS database
# - Cloud SQL instance
# - External API endpoints
#
# Benefits:
# - Pods use internal DNS name
# - Easy to switch between managed and self-hosted
# - No selector needed (no pods)

Command:

# Create ExternalName service for RDS PostgreSQL
kubectl create service externalname postgres-external \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=anihpj-db.xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com

# Create ExternalName for Redis ElastiCache
kubectl create service externalname redis-external \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=anihpj-redis.xxxx.cache.amazonaws.com

Output:

service/postgres-external created
service/redis-external created

📄 Context - Using ExternalName in Django:

# Django settings.py
# Application uses internal DNS name that maps to external service

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        # Uses ExternalName service DNS
        'HOST': 'postgres-external.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local',
        'PORT': '5432',
        'NAME': os.environ['DB_NAME'],
        'USER': os.environ['DB_USER'],
        'PASSWORD': os.environ['DB_PASSWORD'],
    }
}

✅ Verification:

# Check ExternalName service
kubectl get service postgres-external -n anihpj-production

# Verify DNS resolution
kubectl run test-dns --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox \
  -n anihpj-production -- nslookup postgres-external

# Test from Django pod
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  python -c "import socket; print(socket.gethostbyname('postgres-external'))"
NAME                TYPE           CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP                                      PORT(S)   AGE
postgres-external   ExternalName          anihpj-db.xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com          1m

Server:    10.0.0.10
Address 1: 10.0.0.10 kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

Name:      postgres-external
Address 1: 192.168.100.50 anihpj-db.xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com

192.168.100.50
Example 5: Create Headless Service (StatefulSet)

Scenario: Create a headless service for StatefulSet pods that need direct DNS names.

📄 Context - Headless Service for PostgreSQL Cluster:

# Headless service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet
#
# Purpose: Direct DNS for each pod
# ClusterIP: None (no load balancing)
#
# DNS names created:
# - postgres-0.postgres-headless.ns.svc.cluster.local
# - postgres-1.postgres-headless.ns.svc.cluster.local
#
# Use cases:
# - StatefulSet with stable network identity
# - Database clusters (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB)
# - Kafka, Elasticsearch clusters

📄 Context - StatefulSet Manifest:

# k8s/statefulset/postgres.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  clusterIP: None  # Headless
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    name: postgres
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless  # Links to headless service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432

Command:

# Create headless service via CLI
kubectl create service clusterip postgres-headless \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=5432:5432 \
  --clusterip=None

# Or apply manifest
kubectl apply -f k8s/statefulset/postgres.yaml

Output:

service/postgres-headless created

✅ Verification:

# Check headless service (no ClusterIP)
kubectl get service postgres-headless -n anihpj-production

# Check individual pod DNS names
kubectl run dns-test --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox \
  -n anihpj-production -- sh -c '
    nslookup postgres-headless
    echo "---"
    nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless
    echo "---"
    nslookup postgres-1.postgres-headless
  '

# Test direct pod connection
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  pg_isready -h postgres-0.postgres-headless -p 5432
NAME                TYPE        CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
postgres-headless   ClusterIP   None                 5432/TCP   1m

Server:    10.0.0.10
Address 1: 10.0.0.10 kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

Name:      postgres-headless
Address 1: 10.244.1.20 postgres-0.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Address 2: 10.244.2.21 postgres-1.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Address 3: 10.244.3.22 postgres-2.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
---
Name:      postgres-0.postgres-headless
Address 1: 10.244.1.20 postgres-0.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
---
Name:      postgres-1.postgres-headless
Address 1: 10.244.2.21 postgres-1.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local

postgres-0.postgres-headless:5432 - accepting connections
Example 6: Complete Service Setup for anihpj

Scenario: Create comprehensive service configuration for the entire anihpj application stack.

📄 Context - Complete Services Manifest:

# k8s/services/all-services.yaml
# Complete service configuration for anihpj
---
# Django web application (internal)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: web
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 8000
    targetPort: 8000
  - name: metrics
    port: 9090
    targetPort: 9090
---
# Celery worker service (internal)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-celery
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: worker
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: jobpost-celery
  ports:
  - name: flower
    port: 5555
    targetPort: 5555
---
# PostgreSQL service (internal)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: database
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - name: postgres
    port: 5432
    targetPort: 5432
---
# Redis service (internal)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: redis
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: cache
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: redis
  ports:
  - name: redis
    port: 6379
    targetPort: 6379
---
# Load Balancer for external access
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-lb
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: loadbalancer
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol: http
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-cert: arn:aws:acm:xxx:certificate/xxx
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-ports: "443"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  - name: https
    port: 443
    targetPort: 8000

Command:

# Deploy all services
kubectl apply -f k8s/services/all-services.yaml

# Or create individually
kubectl create service clusterip jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production --tcp=8000:8000,9090:9090

kubectl create service clusterip postgres \
  -n anihpj-production --tcp=5432:5432

kubectl create service clusterip redis \
  -n anihpj-production --tcp=6379:6379

kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-lb \
  -n anihpj-production --tcp=80:8000,443:8000

Output:

service/jobpost-web created
service/jobpost-celery created
service/postgres created
service/redis created
service/jobpost-lb created

✅ Verification:

# List all anihpj services
kubectl get services -n anihpj-production -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj

# Check endpoints for all services
kubectl get endpoints -n anihpj-production

# Test internal service connectivity
kubectl run connectivity-test --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox \
  -n anihpj-production -- sh -c '
    echo "Testing Django..."
    wget -qO- --timeout=5 http://jobpost-web:8000/api/health/ || echo "Django: failed"
    echo "Testing Redis..."
    nc -zv redis 6379 && echo "Redis: connected" || echo "Redis: failed"
    echo "Testing PostgreSQL..."
    nc -zv postgres 5432 && echo "PostgreSQL: connected" || echo "PostgreSQL: failed"
  '

# Get external LB URL
kubectl get service jobpost-lb -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].hostname}'
NAME             TYPE           CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)                      AGE
jobpost-celery   ClusterIP      10.0.12.100               5555/TCP                     1m
jobpost-lb       LoadBalancer   10.0.12.105    34.120.56.78     80:31234/TCP,443:31235/TCP   1m
jobpost-web      ClusterIP      10.0.12.101               8000/TCP,9090/TCP            1m
postgres         ClusterIP      10.0.12.102               5432/TCP                     1m
redis            ClusterIP      10.0.12.103               6379/TCP                     1m

NAME             ENDPOINTS                         AGE
jobpost-celery   10.244.1.30:5555                  1m
jobpost-web      10.244.1.20:8000,10.244.2.21:8000 1m
postgres         10.244.3.10:5432                  1m
redis            10.244.3.11:6379                  1m

Testing Django...
{"status":"healthy"}
Testing Redis...
redis (10.0.12.103:6379) open
Redis: connected
Testing PostgreSQL...
postgres (10.0.12.102:5432) open
PostgreSQL: connected

anihpj-lb-xxxxx.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com

kubectl create service clusterip

CREATE Networking Internal Service Discovery

Create a ClusterIP service for internal cluster communication

The kubectl create service clusterip command creates a ClusterIP service that exposes pods on a cluster-internal IP address. This service type is only reachable from within the cluster.

For the anihpj Django project, ClusterIP services are used for:

  • Internal communication between Django and PostgreSQL
  • Connecting Django to Redis cache
  • Inter-service communication (web to worker)
  • Backend services not exposed externally

Syntax

# Basic ClusterIP service
kubectl create service clusterip NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT

# With specific cluster IP
kubectl create service clusterip NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT --clusterip=IP

# Headless service (no cluster IP)
kubectl create service clusterip NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT --clusterip=None

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--tcp[]stringPort pairs (port:targetPort)
--clusteripstringClusterIP or "None" for headless
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid port)

Examples

Example 1: PostgreSQL Internal Service

Scenario: Create ClusterIP service for PostgreSQL database access.

Command:

# Create PostgreSQL service
kubectl create service clusterip postgres \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=5432:5432

Output:

service/postgres created

✅ Verification:

# Check service
kubectl get svc postgres -n anihpj-production

# Test connection from Django pod
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  pg_isready -h postgres -p 5432
NAME       TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
postgres   ClusterIP   10.96.50.10   <none>        5432/TCP   5s

postgres:5432 - accepting connections
Example 2: Redis Cache Service

Scenario: Create ClusterIP service for Redis cache access.

Command:

# Create Redis service
kubectl create service clusterip redis \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=6379:6379

Output:

service/redis created

✅ Verification:

# Test Redis connection
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  redis-cli -h redis ping
PONG
Example 3: Headless Service for StatefulSet

Scenario: Create headless service for direct pod DNS access.

Command:

# Create headless service (ClusterIP: None)
kubectl create service clusterip postgres-headless \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=5432:5432 \
  --clusterip=None

Output:

service/postgres-headless created

✅ Verification:

# Headless returns pod IPs directly
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -n anihpj-production -- nslookup postgres-headless
Name:      postgres-headless
Address 1: 10.244.2.5 postgres-0.postgres-headless
Address 2: 10.244.2.6 postgres-1.postgres-headless
Example 4: Multi-Port Service

Scenario: Create service exposing multiple ports.

Command:

# Django with HTTP and metrics ports
kubectl create service clusterip jobpost-internal \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=8000:8000 \
  --tcp=9090:9090

Output:

service/jobpost-internal created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc jobpost-internal -n anihpj-production
NAME               TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)             AGE
jobpost-internal   ClusterIP   10.96.50.20   <none>        8000/TCP,9090/TCP   5s
Example 5: Service with Static ClusterIP

Scenario: Create service with specific cluster IP for legacy configurations.

Command:

# Specify exact cluster IP
kubectl create service clusterip legacy-db \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=3306:3306 \
  --clusterip=10.96.100.100

Output:

service/legacy-db created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc legacy-db -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}'
10.96.100.100
Example 6: Generate YAML for GitOps

Scenario: Generate ClusterIP service manifest for version control.

Command:

# Generate YAML
kubectl create service clusterip jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > k8s/services/jobpost-web.yaml

Output (jobpost-web.yaml):

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    app: jobpost-web
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  ports:
  - name: 80-8000
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  type: ClusterIP

kubectl create service externalname

CREATE Networking DNS Alias External

Create an ExternalName service that maps to a DNS name

The kubectl create service externalname command creates a service that returns a CNAME record with the specified external DNS name. No proxying occurs - it's purely DNS-based.

For the anihpj Django project, ExternalName services are used for:

  • Accessing external managed databases (AWS RDS, Cloud SQL)
  • Connecting to external APIs with internal service names
  • Migration scenarios (gradual shift from external to internal)
  • Cross-namespace service references

Syntax

# Create ExternalName service
kubectl create service externalname NAME --external-name=HOSTNAME

Parameters

ParameterTypeRequiredDescription
--external-namestringYesExternal DNS name
--dry-runstringNo"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid DNS name)

Examples

Example 1: AWS RDS Database Access

Scenario: Create service pointing to AWS RDS PostgreSQL instance.

Command:

# Map internal 'postgres' name to RDS endpoint
kubectl create service externalname postgres \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=anihpj-db.xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com

Output:

service/postgres created

✅ Verification:

# DNS resolves to RDS
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -n anihpj-production -- nslookup postgres
Name:      postgres
Address 1: postgres CNAME anihpj-db.xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com
Example 2: External API Gateway

Scenario: Create alias for external payment API.

Command:

# Alias Stripe API
kubectl create service externalname stripe-api \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=api.stripe.com

Output:

service/stripe-api created

✅ Verification:

# Django can now use 'stripe-api' as hostname
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  curl -I https://stripe-api/v1/charges 2>&1 | head -3
HTTP/2 401 
content-type: application/json
Example 3: Cross-Namespace Service Reference

Scenario: Reference a service in another namespace.

Command:

# Reference shared monitoring service
kubectl create service externalname prometheus \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.local

Output:

service/prometheus created

✅ Verification:

# Access Prometheus from anihpj namespace
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  curl -s http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/status/config | jq .status
"success"
Example 4: Migration Helper (External to Internal)

Scenario: Gradual migration from external DB to in-cluster.

📄 Context - Migration Strategy:

# Phase 1: External RDS
postgres → anihpj-db.rds.amazonaws.com (ExternalName)

# Phase 2: Internal PostgreSQL
postgres → postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local (ClusterIP)

# No application changes needed - same service name!

Command:

# Phase 1: Point to external RDS
kubectl create service externalname postgres \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=anihpj-db.xxxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com

# Phase 2: Delete and create ClusterIP
kubectl delete service postgres -n anihpj-production
kubectl create service clusterip postgres \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=5432:5432

Output:

service/postgres created  # ExternalName
service "postgres" deleted
service/postgres created  # ClusterIP - now internal!
Example 5: Google Cloud SQL Access

Scenario: Connect to Google Cloud SQL instance.

Command:

# Map to Cloud SQL private IP hostname
kubectl create service externalname cloudsql \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=10.0.0.5.bc.googleusercontent.com

Output:

service/cloudsql created
Example 6: Generate YAML

Scenario: Generate ExternalName manifest for GitOps.

Command:

kubectl create service externalname external-db \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --external-name=db.external-provider.com \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    app: external-db
  name: external-db
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  externalName: db.external-provider.com
  selector: {}
  type: ExternalName

kubectl create service loadbalancer

CREATE Networking External Access Cloud Provider

Create a LoadBalancer service for external access via cloud provider

The kubectl create service loadbalancer command creates a LoadBalancer service that provisions an external load balancer through the cloud provider (AWS ELB, GCP Load Balancer, Azure Load Balancer).

For the anihpj Django project, LoadBalancer services are used for:

  • Exposing Django web application to the internet
  • Production external access with automatic load balancing
  • Health check integration with cloud providers
  • SSL termination at load balancer level

Syntax

# Basic LoadBalancer service
kubectl create service loadbalancer NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--tcp[]stringPort pairs (port:targetPort)
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (already exists, cloud provider error)

Examples

Example 1: Basic Web Application LoadBalancer

Scenario: Expose Django application externally.

Command:

# Create LoadBalancer service
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000

Output:

service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Wait for external IP
kubectl get svc jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -w

# Test external access
curl http://<EXTERNAL-IP>/health/
NAME          TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)       AGE
jobpost-web   LoadBalancer   10.96.50.30   <pending>     80:31234/TCP  5s
jobpost-web   LoadBalancer   10.96.50.30   34.123.45.67  80:31234/TCP  45s

{"status": "healthy"}
Example 2: HTTPS LoadBalancer (Port 443)

Scenario: Create LoadBalancer for SSL/TLS traffic.

Command:

# LoadBalancer for HTTPS
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-https \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=443:8000

Output:

service/jobpost-https created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc jobpost-https -n anihpj-production
NAME            TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)         AGE
jobpost-https   LoadBalancer   10.96.50.40   34.123.45.68  443:32456/TCP   30s
Example 3: Multi-Port LoadBalancer

Scenario: Expose HTTP and HTTPS on same LoadBalancer.

Command:

# Multi-port LoadBalancer
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-multi \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --tcp=443:8443

Output:

service/jobpost-multi created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc jobpost-multi -n anihpj-production
NAME            TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)                     AGE
jobpost-multi   LoadBalancer   10.96.50.50   34.123.45.69  80:30123/TCP,443:30456/TCP  30s
Example 4: AWS NLB Annotations

Scenario: Create LoadBalancer with AWS-specific annotations.

📄 Context - AWS NLB Configuration:

# To use NLB instead of Classic LB, apply annotations after creation:
# service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb
# service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal: "false"
# service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-cross-zone-load-balancing-enabled: "true"

Command:

# Create and annotate for AWS NLB
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-nlb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubectl annotate -f - --local -o yaml \
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type=nlb \
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-cross-zone-load-balancing-enabled=true | \
  kubectl apply -f -

Output:

service/jobpost-nlb created
Example 5: Internal LoadBalancer

Scenario: Create internal-only LoadBalancer (private subnet).

Command:

# Create internal LoadBalancer (AWS)
kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-internal-lb \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubectl annotate -f - --local -o yaml \
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal="true" | \
  kubectl apply -f -

Output:

service/jobpost-internal-lb created

✅ Verification:

# Internal IP only (10.x.x.x)
kubectl get svc jobpost-internal-lb -n anihpj-production
NAME                  TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)       AGE
jobpost-internal-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.50.60   10.0.1.100    80:30789/TCP  30s
Example 6: Generate YAML for GitOps

Scenario: Generate LoadBalancer manifest with annotations.

Command:

kubectl create service loadbalancer jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    app: jobpost-web
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  ports:
  - name: 80-8000
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  type: LoadBalancer

kubectl create service nodeport

CREATE Networking Node Access Development

Create a NodePort service for accessing pods via node IP and port

The kubectl create service nodeport command creates a NodePort service that exposes pods on a static port on each node's IP. This allows external access without requiring a cloud provider load balancer.

For the anihpj Django project, NodePort services are used for:

  • Development/testing access without cloud load balancer
  • On-premises Kubernetes clusters
  • Quick debugging and troubleshooting
  • Integration with external load balancers (HAProxy, F5)

Syntax

# Basic NodePort service (auto-assigned port)
kubectl create service nodeport NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT

# With specific node port
kubectl create service nodeport NAME --tcp=PORT:TARGET_PORT --node-port=NODE_PORT

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--tcp[]stringPort pairs (port:targetPort)
--node-portintSpecific node port (30000-32767)
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (port already in use, out of range)

Examples

Example 1: Basic NodePort Service

Scenario: Create NodePort service with auto-assigned port.

Command:

# Create NodePort (auto-assigned port)
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=80:8000

Output:

service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Get node port and node IP
kubectl get svc jobpost-web -n anihpj-dev
kubectl get nodes -o wide
NAME          TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)       AGE
jobpost-web   NodePort   10.96.50.20   <none>        80:31234/TCP  5s

NAME    STATUS   EXTERNAL-IP    OS-IMAGE
node-1  Ready    192.168.1.10   Ubuntu 22.04
node-2  Ready    192.168.1.11   Ubuntu 22.04

# Access: http://192.168.1.10:31234 or http://192.168.1.11:31234
Example 2: Specific NodePort Number

Scenario: Create service with specific node port for firewall rules.

Command:

# Specify exact node port
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --node-port=30080

Output:

service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc jobpost-web -n anihpj-dev -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[0].nodePort}'
30080
Example 3: Development Access with Minikube

Scenario: Access Django app in Minikube via NodePort.

Command:

# Create NodePort service
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=80:8000

# Get Minikube URL
minikube service jobpost-web -n anihpj-dev --url

Output:

service/jobpost-web created
http://192.168.49.2:31234

✅ Verification:

# Open in browser
minikube service jobpost-web -n anihpj-dev
Example 4: Multi-Port NodePort Service

Scenario: Expose web and metrics endpoints.

Command:

# Multi-port NodePort
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-multi \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --tcp=9090:9090

Output:

service/jobpost-multi created

✅ Verification:

kubectl get svc jobpost-multi -n anihpj-dev
NAME            TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)                        AGE
jobpost-multi   NodePort   10.96.50.30   <none>        80:30123/TCP,9090:30456/TCP   5s
Example 5: On-Premises with External LB

Scenario: NodePort backing an external HAProxy load balancer.

📄 Context - HAProxy Configuration:

# /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
frontend http_front
    bind *:80
    default_backend django_backend

backend django_backend
    balance roundrobin
    server node1 192.168.1.10:30080 check
    server node2 192.168.1.11:30080 check
    server node3 192.168.1.12:30080 check

Command:

# Create NodePort with specific port for HAProxy
kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --node-port=30080

Output:

service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Test through HAProxy
curl http://haproxy-vip/health/
{"status": "healthy"}
Example 6: Generate YAML

Scenario: Generate NodePort manifest for version control.

Command:

kubectl create service nodeport jobpost-web \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --node-port=30080 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    app: jobpost-web
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  ports:
  - name: 80-8000
    nodePort: 30080
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost-web
  type: NodePort

kubectl create serviceaccount

CREATE Identity Security RBAC

Create a service account with the specified name

The kubectl create serviceaccount command creates a ServiceAccount resource that provides an identity for processes running in pods. ServiceAccounts are used for authentication and authorization within the cluster.

For the anihpj Django project, service accounts are used for:

  • Django pods accessing secrets and configmaps
  • CI/CD pipelines deploying to the cluster
  • Celery workers accessing job queues
  • Monitoring tools scraping metrics
  • Database migration jobs
  • Backup and restore operations

Syntax

# Create a service account
kubectl create serviceaccount NAME [--namespace=NAMESPACE]

# Short alias
kubectl create sa NAME

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
NAMEstringName of the service account
-n, --namespacestringNamespace for the service account
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
-o, --outputstringOutput format (yaml, json, etc.)

ServiceAccount Properties

PropertyDescription
automountServiceAccountTokenAuto-mount API token into pods
secretsList of secrets for image pulls (deprecated K8s 1.24+)
imagePullSecretsSecrets for pulling private images

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0ServiceAccount created successfully
1Error (already exists, invalid name)

📁 Context: ServiceAccount Architecture for anihpj

Kubernetes 1.24+ Change: Service accounts no longer auto-create secrets. Use TokenRequest API or kubectl create token for short-lived tokens.
ServiceAccount Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ServiceAccount Architecture                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  ServiceAccount                    Authentication Flow                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐          ┌────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ jobpost-app         │          │                                    │    │
│  │ namespace: prod     │          │  Pod        →  API Server  →  RBAC │    │
│  │                     │──────────│  (SA token)    (AuthN)       (AuthZ)│    │
│  │ imagePullSecrets:   │          │                                    │    │
│  │ - ghcr-registry     │          └────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│  └─────────────────────┘                                                    │
│           │                                                                  │
│           │ bound via RoleBinding                                           │
│           ▼                                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────┐                   │
│  │ Role                │          │ Pod Spec            │                   │
│  │ - get configmaps    │          │                     │                   │
│  │ - get secrets       │          │ serviceAccountName: │                   │
│  │ - watch endpoints   │          │   jobpost-app       │                   │
│  └─────────────────────┘          │                     │                   │
│                                    │ Mounted at:         │                   │
│                                    │ /var/run/secrets/   │                   │
│                                    │   kubernetes.io/    │                   │
│                                    │   serviceaccount/   │                   │
│                                    └─────────────────────┘                   │
│                                                                              │
│  ServiceAccounts in anihpj:                                                  │
│  ├─ jobpost-app        (Django pods, minimal permissions)                   │
│  ├─ github-actions     (CI/CD, deploy permissions)                          │
│  ├─ celery-worker      (Celery pods, queue access)                          │
│  ├─ db-migration       (Migration jobs, elevated DB access)                 │
│  └─ prometheus         (Monitoring, read-only cluster access)               │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Create Application ServiceAccount

Scenario: Create a service account for Django application pods with minimal permissions.

📄 Context - Application Identity Requirements:

# ServiceAccount for Django application pods
#
# Purpose: Identity for jobpost-web pods
# Permissions needed:
# - Read configmaps (django-config)
# - Read secrets (database-credentials, api-keys)
# - No write permissions
# - No cluster-wide access
#
# Security: Principle of least privilege

Command:

# Create service account for application
kubectl create serviceaccount jobpost-app \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

Output:

serviceaccount/jobpost-app created

✅ Verification:

# Check service account
kubectl get serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production

# View service account details
kubectl describe serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production

# Get YAML for documentation
kubectl get serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production -o yaml
NAME          SECRETS   AGE
jobpost-app   0         1m

Name:                jobpost-app
Namespace:           anihpj-production
Labels:              
Annotations:         
Image pull secrets:  
Mountable secrets:   
Tokens:              

apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: jobpost-app
  namespace: anihpj-production
Example 2: ServiceAccount with ImagePullSecrets

Scenario: Create a service account configured to pull images from a private registry.

📄 Context - Private Registry Access:

# ServiceAccount with imagePullSecrets
#
# Purpose: Allow pods to pull from private registry
# Registry: ghcr.io/company/anihpj
#
# Steps:
# 1. Create docker-registry secret
# 2. Create serviceaccount
# 3. Patch serviceaccount with imagePullSecrets
#
# This removes need to specify imagePullSecrets in every deployment

Command:

# First ensure registry secret exists
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-registry \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --docker-server=ghcr.io \
  --docker-username=github-user \
  --docker-password=ghp_xxxx \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Create service account
kubectl create serviceaccount jobpost-app \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Patch with imagePullSecrets
kubectl patch serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production \
  -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "ghcr-registry"}]}'

Output:

secret/ghcr-registry created
serviceaccount/jobpost-app created
serviceaccount/jobpost-app patched

📄 Context - Deployment Using ServiceAccount:

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: jobpost-app  # Use SA with imagePullSecrets
      # No need to specify imagePullSecrets here!
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest  # Will use SA's imagePullSecrets

✅ Verification:

# Check imagePullSecrets attached
kubectl get serviceaccount jobpost-app -n anihpj-production -o yaml

# Test image pull works
kubectl run test-pull --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest \
  --serviceaccount=jobpost-app \
  -n anihpj-production -- echo "Image pulled successfully"
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: jobpost-app
  namespace: anihpj-production
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-registry

Image pulled successfully
pod "test-pull" deleted
Example 3: Create CI/CD ServiceAccount

Scenario: Create a service account for GitHub Actions with deployment permissions.

📄 Context - CI/CD Identity Setup:

# CI/CD ServiceAccount for GitHub Actions
#
# Purpose: Identity for deployment pipelines
# Authentication: OIDC federation or kubeconfig
#
# Permissions needed:
# - Create/Update deployments
# - Create/Update services
# - Create/Update configmaps
# - Read secrets (for validation)
# - NO delete permissions (safety)

Command:

# Create CI/CD service account
kubectl create serviceaccount github-actions \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

# Create deployer role
kubectl create role deployer \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch \
  --resource=deployments,services,configmaps,ingresses

# Bind role to service account
kubectl create rolebinding github-actions-deployer \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --role=deployer \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj-production:github-actions

Output:

serviceaccount/github-actions created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/deployer created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/github-actions-deployer created

📄 Context - GitHub Actions OIDC Configuration:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Kubernetes
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    
    - name: Configure AWS credentials (for EKS)
      uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
      with:
        role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456:role/github-actions
        aws-region: us-east-1
    
    - name: Update kubeconfig
      run: aws eks update-kubeconfig --name anihpj-cluster
    
    - name: Deploy
      run: |
        kubectl set image deployment/jobpost-web \
          django=ghcr.io/company/anihpj:${{ github.sha }} \
          -n anihpj-production

✅ Verification:

# Test CI/CD permissions
kubectl auth can-i update deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions \
  -n anihpj-production

kubectl auth can-i delete deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions \
  -n anihpj-production

# List all permissions
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions \
  -n anihpj-production | grep -E "^(deploy|service|config|ingress)"
yes
no

deployments.apps                     []      []      [get list watch create update patch]
services                             []      []      [get list watch create update patch]
configmaps                           []      []      [get list watch create update patch]
ingresses.networking.k8s.io          []      []      [get list watch create update patch]
Example 4: ServiceAccount for Database Migrations

Scenario: Create a service account for database migration jobs with elevated permissions.

📄 Context - Migration Job Requirements:

# ServiceAccount for database migration jobs
#
# Purpose: Run Django migrations with database access
# Job type: One-time or pre-deployment hook
#
# Permissions needed:
# - Read database credentials secret
# - Create/Read jobs (for status checks)
# - Write to migration configmap (track state)
#
# Security: Short-lived, scoped to migration resources

Command:

# Create migration service account
kubectl create serviceaccount db-migration \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

# Create migration role with specific secret access
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: db-migration
  namespace: anihpj-production
rules:
# Access database credentials
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  resourceNames: ["database-credentials"]
  verbs: ["get"]
# Track migration state
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  resourceNames: ["migration-state"]
  verbs: ["get", "create", "update", "patch"]
# Check job status
- apiGroups: ["batch"]
  resources: ["jobs"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF

# Bind role
kubectl create rolebinding db-migration-binding \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --role=db-migration \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj-production:db-migration

Output:

serviceaccount/db-migration created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/db-migration created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/db-migration-binding created

📄 Context - Migration Job Using ServiceAccount:

# k8s/jobs/migration-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: django-migrate
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: db-migration  # Use migration SA
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: migrate
        image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--noinput"]
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: database-credentials

✅ Verification:

# Test migration SA permissions
kubectl auth can-i get secrets/database-credentials \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:db-migration \
  -n anihpj-production

kubectl auth can-i get secrets/api-keys \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:db-migration \
  -n anihpj-production

kubectl auth can-i create pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:db-migration \
  -n anihpj-production
yes
no
no
Example 5: ServiceAccount with Token Auto-Mount Disabled

Scenario: Create a secure service account that doesn't auto-mount tokens (defense in depth).

📄 Context - Secure ServiceAccount Pattern:

# Secure ServiceAccount configuration
#
# Best practice: Disable auto-mount when not needed
#
# Reasons to disable:
# - Pod doesn't need Kubernetes API access
# - Reduces attack surface
# - Prevents token theft if pod compromised
#
# Use for:
# - Stateless web applications
# - Batch processing without API calls

Command:

# Create service account with disabled auto-mount
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web-secure
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
automountServiceAccountToken: false  # Security hardening
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-registry
EOF

Output:

serviceaccount/jobpost-web-secure created

📄 Context - Deployment with Secure ServiceAccount:

# k8s/deployment-secure.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: jobpost-web-secure
      automountServiceAccountToken: false  # Also disable at pod level
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/company/anihpj:latest
        # No /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ mounted
        securityContext:
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          runAsNonRoot: true

✅ Verification:

# Check automount setting
kubectl get serviceaccount jobpost-web-secure -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.automountServiceAccountToken}'

# Verify no token mounted in pod
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ 2>&1 || echo "No token mounted (expected)"

# Verify pod can't access API
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  wget -qO- --timeout=2 https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/ 2>&1 || echo "API access denied (expected)"
false

ls: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/: No such file or directory
No token mounted (expected)

wget: error getting response: Connection refused
API access denied (expected)
Example 6: Complete ServiceAccount Setup for anihpj

Scenario: Create comprehensive service account configuration for all anihpj workloads.

📄 Context - Complete ServiceAccount Manifest:

# k8s/rbac/serviceaccounts.yaml
# Complete ServiceAccount configuration for anihpj
---
# Django application pods
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: jobpost-app
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: app
automountServiceAccountToken: false  # Doesn't need API access
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-registry
---
# Celery worker pods
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: celery-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: worker
automountServiceAccountToken: false
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-registry
---
# CI/CD deployment
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: github-actions
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: ci-cd
automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Needs API access for deployments
---
# Database migrations
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: db-migration
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: migration
automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Needs to read secrets
imagePullSecrets:
- name: ghcr-registry
---
# Prometheus monitoring
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: prometheus
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: monitoring
automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Needs API for service discovery

Command:

# Apply all service accounts
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac/serviceaccounts.yaml

# Or create individually
for sa in jobpost-app celery-worker github-actions db-migration prometheus; do
  kubectl create serviceaccount $sa -n anihpj-production --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
    kubectl apply -f -
done

# Patch with imagePullSecrets where needed
for sa in jobpost-app celery-worker db-migration; do
  kubectl patch serviceaccount $sa -n anihpj-production \
    -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "ghcr-registry"}]}'
done

Output:

serviceaccount/jobpost-app created
serviceaccount/celery-worker created
serviceaccount/github-actions created
serviceaccount/db-migration created
serviceaccount/prometheus created

✅ Verification:

# List all service accounts
kubectl get serviceaccounts -n anihpj-production -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj

# Check configuration summary
kubectl get serviceaccounts -n anihpj-production -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj \
  -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
AUTOMOUNT:.automountServiceAccountToken,\
IMAGE_PULL_SECRETS:.imagePullSecrets[*].name

# Verify workloads use correct service accounts
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production \
  -o custom-columns=POD:.metadata.name,SA:.spec.serviceAccountName
NAME             SECRETS   AGE
celery-worker    0         1m
db-migration     0         1m
github-actions   0         1m
jobpost-app      0         1m
prometheus       0         1m

NAME             AUTOMOUNT   IMAGE_PULL_SECRETS
celery-worker    false       ghcr-registry
db-migration     true        ghcr-registry
github-actions   true        
jobpost-app      false       ghcr-registry
prometheus       true        

POD                              SA
jobpost-web-abc123               jobpost-app
jobpost-web-def456               jobpost-app
celery-worker-ghi789             celery-worker
prometheus-jkl012                prometheus

kubectl create token

CREATE Authentication Security TokenRequest API

Request a service account token

The kubectl create token command generates short-lived authentication tokens for service accounts using the TokenRequest API. This is the recommended way to obtain tokens in Kubernetes 1.24+ where auto-generated secrets are deprecated.

For the anihpj Django project, tokens are used for:

  • CI/CD pipeline authentication to deploy
  • External tools accessing the cluster API
  • Testing RBAC permissions
  • Debugging service account access
  • Generating kubeconfig for external access
  • Workload identity federation
Kubernetes 1.24+ Best Practice: TokenRequest API tokens are time-bound and audience-bound, making them more secure than legacy secrets. Default expiration is 1 hour.

Syntax

# Request token with default expiration (1 hour)
kubectl create token SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME

# Request token with custom duration
kubectl create token SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME --duration=24h

# Request token for specific audience
kubectl create token SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME --audience=https://example.com

# Request token bound to specific pod/secret
kubectl create token SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME --bound-object-ref=pod/mypod

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--durationdurationToken validity duration (default: 1h, max: cluster configured)
--audience[]stringIntended recipients of the token (aud claim)
--bound-object-refstringObject reference to bind token to (kind/name)
-n, --namespacestringNamespace of the service account

Token Properties

PropertyDescription
expExpiration time (Unix timestamp)
audIntended audience(s)
subSubject (service account reference)
issIssuer (API server URL)

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Token created and printed to stdout
1Error (SA not found, invalid duration)

📁 Context: Token Management for anihpj

Token Request Flow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        TokenRequest API Flow                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  kubectl/client                  API Server                   Token         │
│  ┌───────────┐                  ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────┐  │
│  │ create    │    POST          │              │   Returns   │ JWT      │  │
│  │ token     │─────────────────│► TokenRequest │────────────│► Token   │  │
│  │ github-   │  /serviceaccounts│   API        │             │          │  │
│  │ actions   │  /github-actions │              │             │ exp: 1h  │  │
│  └───────────┘  /token          └──────────────┘             │ aud: k8s │  │
│                                        │                      └──────────┘  │
│                                        │                                     │
│                                        ▼                                     │
│                               ┌──────────────┐                              │
│                               │ ServiceAccount│                              │
│                               │ github-actions│                              │
│                               │ + RBAC Roles │                              │
│                               └──────────────┘                              │
│                                                                              │
│  Token Lifecycle:                                                           │
│  ├─ Created: Immediately generated                                          │
│  ├─ Validity: 1h default (configurable)                                     │
│  ├─ Renewal: Request new token before expiry                                │
│  └─ Revoked: When SA deleted or bound object deleted                        │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Generate Token for CI/CD

Scenario: Generate a short-lived token for GitHub Actions to deploy to the cluster.

📄 Context - CI/CD Token Requirements:

# Token for CI/CD deployment
#
# Purpose: Authenticate GitHub Actions for deployment
# Duration: 1 hour (sufficient for deployment)
# Permissions: Via RoleBinding to deployer role
#
# Security considerations:
# - Short-lived (reduces window if leaked)
# - Scoped to specific namespace
# - No cluster-admin access

Command:

# Generate token for CI/CD service account
kubectl create token github-actions \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --duration=1h

Output:

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InRva2VuLXNpZ25pbiJ9.eyJhdWQiOlsiaHR0cHM6Ly...
(JWT token - truncated)

✅ Verification:

# Decode token to view claims (using jq)
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token github-actions -n anihpj-production)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq .

# Test token authentication
kubectl --token=$TOKEN get deployments -n anihpj-production

# Verify token works for deployment
kubectl --token=$TOKEN set image deployment/jobpost-web \
  django=ghcr.io/company/anihpj:test \
  -n anihpj-production --dry-run=client
{
  "aud": ["https://kubernetes.default.svc"],
  "exp": 1705315200,
  "iat": 1705311600,
  "iss": "https://kubernetes.default.svc",
  "kubernetes.io": {
    "namespace": "anihpj-production",
    "serviceaccount": {
      "name": "github-actions",
      "uid": "abc123-def456-ghi789"
    }
  },
  "nbf": 1705311600,
  "sub": "system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions"
}

NAME          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           7d

deployment.apps/jobpost-web image updated (dry run)
Example 2: Generate Long-Lived Token for External Tools

Scenario: Create a token with extended duration for external monitoring tools.

📄 Context - Monitoring Integration:

# Long-lived token for Prometheus/Grafana
#
# Purpose: External monitoring tool authentication
# Duration: 7 days (check cluster max)
# Audience: monitoring.company.com
#
# Note: Longer tokens have security tradeoffs
# Consider: External secrets rotation, Vault integration

Command:

# Generate long-lived token for monitoring
kubectl create token prometheus \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --duration=168h \
  --audience=https://monitoring.company.com

Output:

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InByb21ldGhldXMtdG9rZW4ifQ.eyJhdWQiOls...
(JWT token)

📄 Context - Using Token in Prometheus Config:

# prometheus/prometheus.yml
# Prometheus scrape config for Kubernetes
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
- job_name: 'kubernetes-pods'
  kubernetes_sd_configs:
  - role: pod
    api_server: https://kubernetes.company.internal:6443
    authorization:
      type: Bearer
      credentials: |
        eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InByb21ldGhldXMtdG9rZW4ifQ...
    tls_config:
      insecure_skip_verify: false
      ca_file: /etc/prometheus/ca.crt

  relabel_configs:
  - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_namespace]
    regex: anihpj-production
    action: keep

✅ Verification:

# Check token expiration
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token prometheus -n anihpj-production --duration=168h)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq '{exp: .exp, aud: .aud}'

# Convert exp to human readable
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | \
  jq -r '.exp | strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC")'

# Test read-only access
kubectl --token=$TOKEN get pods -n anihpj-production -o wide
{
  "exp": 1705920000,
  "aud": ["https://monitoring.company.com"]
}

2024-01-22 10:00:00 UTC

NAME                           READY   STATUS    IP            NODE
jobpost-web-abc123             1/1     Running   10.244.1.5    node-1
jobpost-web-def456             1/1     Running   10.244.2.8    node-2
Example 3: Create Kubeconfig with Token

Scenario: Generate a kubeconfig file using a service account token for external access.

📄 Context - Kubeconfig for External Users:

# Creating kubeconfig for limited user access
#
# Use case: Give developer read-only access without cloud credentials
# Contains: Cluster info, user token, context
#
# Security: Token-based auth, not certificates

Command:

# Get cluster info
CLUSTER_NAME="anihpj-cluster"
API_SERVER=$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.server}')
CA_DATA=$(kubectl config view --raw --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.certificate-authority-data}')

# Generate token
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token github-actions -n anihpj-production --duration=24h)

# Create kubeconfig
cat <<EOF > kubeconfig-github-actions.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: ${CA_DATA}
    server: ${API_SERVER}
  name: ${CLUSTER_NAME}
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: ${CLUSTER_NAME}
    namespace: anihpj-production
    user: github-actions
  name: ${CLUSTER_NAME}-github-actions
current-context: ${CLUSTER_NAME}-github-actions
users:
- name: github-actions
  user:
    token: ${TOKEN}
EOF

echo "Kubeconfig created: kubeconfig-github-actions.yaml"

Output:

Kubeconfig created: kubeconfig-github-actions.yaml

✅ Verification:

# Test the kubeconfig
export KUBECONFIG=./kubeconfig-github-actions.yaml

# Check current context
kubectl config current-context

# Test access
kubectl get deployments
kubectl get pods

# Verify can't access other namespaces
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
anihpj-cluster-github-actions

NAME          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           7d

NAME               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-xxx    1/1     Running   0          2h

Error from server (Forbidden): pods is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:github-actions" cannot list resource "pods" in API group "" in the namespace "kube-system"
Example 4: Token Bound to Specific Pod

Scenario: Create a token that's automatically revoked when a specific pod is deleted.

📄 Context - Pod-Bound Token Security:

# Pod-bound tokens for enhanced security
#
# Purpose: Token lifecycle tied to pod lifecycle
# Benefit: Token auto-revoked when pod deleted
#
# Use cases:
# - Debug tokens that expire with the debug pod
# - Temporary elevated access for troubleshooting
# - Tokens that must not outlive the workload

Command:

# Get a running pod name
POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost-web \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# Create token bound to that pod
kubectl create token jobpost-app \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --bound-object-ref=pod/$POD_NAME \
  --duration=1h

Output:

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6ImJvdW5kLXRva2VuIn0.eyJhdWQiOlsiaHR0cHM6Ly...
(Pod-bound JWT token)

✅ Verification:

# Check token claims including bound reference
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token jobpost-app -n anihpj-production \
  --bound-object-ref=pod/$POD_NAME)

echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq '.kubernetes.io.pod'

# Test token works while pod exists
kubectl --token=$TOKEN get pods -n anihpj-production

# Delete the pod (token becomes invalid)
kubectl delete pod $POD_NAME -n anihpj-production

# Token no longer works
kubectl --token=$TOKEN get pods -n anihpj-production
{
  "name": "jobpost-web-abc123",
  "uid": "12345-67890-abcdef"
}

NAME               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-xxx    1/1     Running   0          2h

pod "jobpost-web-abc123" deleted

error: You must be logged in to the server (Unauthorized)
Example 5: Token with Multiple Audiences

Scenario: Create a token for federated identity that works with both Kubernetes and external services.

📄 Context - Workload Identity Federation:

# Token for workload identity federation
#
# Use case: AWS IRSA, GCP Workload Identity, Azure AD
# Multiple audiences for cross-service authentication
#
# Token can be used to:
# 1. Authenticate to Kubernetes API
# 2. Exchange for cloud provider credentials
# 3. Authenticate to external services

📄 Context - AWS IRSA Integration:

# k8s/serviceaccount-irsa.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: jobpost-aws
  namespace: anihpj-production
  annotations:
    # AWS IRSA annotation
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/anihpj-s3-access

Command:

# Create token with multiple audiences
kubectl create token jobpost-aws \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --audience=https://kubernetes.default.svc \
  --audience=sts.amazonaws.com \
  --duration=1h

Output:

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6ImlyLXRva2VuIn0.eyJhdWQiOlsiaHR0cHM6Ly9...
(Multi-audience JWT)

✅ Verification:

# Check audiences in token
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token jobpost-aws -n anihpj-production \
  --audience=https://kubernetes.default.svc \
  --audience=sts.amazonaws.com)

echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq '{aud, exp, sub}'

# Test with AWS STS (example)
aws sts assume-role-with-web-identity \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/anihpj-s3-access \
  --role-session-name anihpj-session \
  --web-identity-token $TOKEN
{
  "aud": [
    "https://kubernetes.default.svc",
    "sts.amazonaws.com"
  ],
  "exp": 1705315200,
  "sub": "system:serviceaccount:anihpj-production:jobpost-aws"
}

{
    "Credentials": {
        "AccessKeyId": "ASIA...",
        "SecretAccessKey": "xxx...",
        "SessionToken": "xxx...",
        "Expiration": "2024-01-15T11:00:00Z"
    },
    "AssumedRoleUser": {
        "Arn": "arn:aws:sts::123456789:assumed-role/anihpj-s3-access/anihpj-session"
    }
}
Example 6: Token Rotation Script

Scenario: Implement automated token rotation for external services.

📄 Context - Token Rotation Strategy:

# Automated token rotation for CI/CD and monitoring
#
# Strategy:
# - Generate new tokens before old ones expire
# - Update secrets/configmaps with new tokens
# - No downtime during rotation
#
# Schedule: CronJob runs every 12 hours
# Token duration: 24 hours (50% overlap)

📄 Context - Token Rotation CronJob:

# k8s/cronjobs/token-rotation.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: rotate-monitoring-token
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  schedule: "0 */12 * * *"  # Every 12 hours
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: token-rotator
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: rotator
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/bash
            - -c
            - |
              # Generate new token
              TOKEN=$(kubectl create token prometheus \
                --namespace=anihpj-production \
                --duration=24h)
              
              # Update secret with new token
              kubectl create secret generic prometheus-token \
                --from-literal=token=$TOKEN \
                --namespace=anihpj-production \
                --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
              
              echo "Token rotated at $(date)"

Command:

# Manual token rotation script
#!/bin/bash
# rotate-tokens.sh - Rotate service account tokens

set -e

NAMESPACE="anihpj-production"
SERVICES=("prometheus" "grafana" "github-actions")
DURATION="24h"

for SA in "${SERVICES[@]}"; do
  echo "Rotating token for: $SA"
  
  # Generate new token
  TOKEN=$(kubectl create token $SA \
    --namespace=$NAMESPACE \
    --duration=$DURATION)
  
  # Update secret
  kubectl create secret generic ${SA}-token \
    --from-literal=token=$TOKEN \
    --namespace=$NAMESPACE \
    --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
  
  echo "✓ Token rotated for $SA"
done

echo "All tokens rotated at $(date)"

Output:

Rotating token for: prometheus
secret/prometheus-token configured
✓ Token rotated for prometheus
Rotating token for: grafana
secret/grafana-token configured
✓ Token rotated for grafana
Rotating token for: github-actions
secret/github-actions-token configured
✓ Token rotated for github-actions
All tokens rotated at Mon Jan 15 10:00:00 UTC 2024

✅ Verification:

# Check token secrets updated
kubectl get secrets -n anihpj-production -l purpose=token \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Verify token expiration times
for secret in prometheus-token grafana-token github-actions-token; do
  TOKEN=$(kubectl get secret $secret -n anihpj-production \
    -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d)
  EXP=$(echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.exp')
  echo "$secret expires: $(date -d @$EXP)"
done

# Test rotated tokens work
for secret in prometheus-token; do
  TOKEN=$(kubectl get secret $secret -n anihpj-production \
    -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d)
  kubectl --token=$TOKEN get pods -n anihpj-production --no-headers | wc -l
done
NAME                    AGE
github-actions-token    2024-01-15T10:00:00Z
grafana-token           2024-01-15T10:00:00Z
prometheus-token        2024-01-15T10:00:00Z

prometheus-token expires: Tue Jan 16 10:00:00 UTC 2024
grafana-token expires: Tue Jan 16 10:00:00 UTC 2024
github-actions-token expires: Tue Jan 16 10:00:00 UTC 2024

3

kubectl get

GET Resource Listing Query Output Formats

Display one or many resources

The kubectl get command is the most commonly used kubectl command. It retrieves and displays information about Kubernetes resources in various output formats including table, JSON, YAML, and custom columns.

For the anihpj Django project, this command is essential for:

  • Monitoring pod health and status for jobpost deployments
  • Viewing service endpoints and load balancer IPs
  • Checking deployment rollout status across environments
  • Debugging configuration issues with configmaps and secrets
  • Auditing RBAC policies and service accounts

Syntax

# Get resources of a specific type
kubectl get RESOURCE [NAME] [flags]

# Get resources from a specific namespace
kubectl get RESOURCE -n NAMESPACE

# Get resources with specific output format
kubectl get RESOURCE -o [json|yaml|wide|name|custom-columns=...]

# Get resources matching label selector
kubectl get RESOURCE -l KEY=VALUE

# Get resources with field selector
kubectl get RESOURCE --field-selector=KEY=VALUE

# Get all resources of multiple types
kubectl get RESOURCE1,RESOURCE2,RESOURCE3

# Watch resources for changes
kubectl get RESOURCE -w

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-A, --all-namespacesboolList resources across all namespaces
-n, --namespacestringNamespace scope for the request
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|wide|name|custom-columns|jsonpath
-l, --selectorstringLabel selector (equality or set-based)
--field-selectorstringField selector to filter on
-w, --watchboolWatch for changes after listing
--watch-onlyboolWatch for changes without initial listing
--show-labelsboolShow all labels as the last column
--show-kindboolShow resource kind in NAME column
--sort-bystringSort list by specified field (e.g., .metadata.creationTimestamp)
--no-headersboolDon't print headers when using default output
--chunk-sizeintReturn large lists in chunks (default 500)
-f, --filenamestring[]Filename to get resource from
--ignore-not-foundboolExit code 0 if resource not found

Common Resource Types

ResourceShort NameDescription
podspoRunning containers (Django app instances)
deploymentsdeployDeployment controllers
servicessvcNetwork services and load balancers
configmapscmConfiguration data
secrets-Sensitive configuration
nodesnoCluster nodes
namespacesnsVirtual clusters
ingressingHTTP routing rules
persistentvolumeclaimspvcStorage claims
eventsevCluster events

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Resources retrieved successfully (or none found with --ignore-not-found)
1Error occurred or resource not found

📁 Context: anihpj Django Project Kubernetes Infrastructure

Production Environment: The following manifests represent the deployed anihpj jobpost application infrastructure that we'll query with kubectl get.
Complete Infrastructure Overview (k8s/production/)
