# k8s/production/namespace.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    environment: production
    team: backend
---
# k8s/production/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
    version: v2.1.0
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: web
        version: v2.1.0
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: jobpost-sa
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
---
# k8s/production/worker-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: worker
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "info"]
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
---
# k8s/production/service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
---
# k8s/production/configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "jobpost.anihpj.com,api.anihpj.com"
  DB_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DB_NAME: "jobpost_db"
  DB_HOST: "postgres-service"
  REDIS_URL: "redis://redis-service:6379/0"
---
# k8s/production/postgres.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-service
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15-alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: "jobpost_db"
        - name: POSTGRES_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-secrets
              key: username
        volumeMounts:
        - name: postgres-data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: postgres-data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 20Gi

Examples

Example 1: Get All Pods in a Namespace with Wide Output

Scenario: DevOps engineer needs to check the status of all running jobpost pods, including which nodes they're scheduled on and their IP addresses for network debugging.

📄 Context - Production Pod Distribution:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Production Cluster - Node Distribution                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Node: node-1 (10.0.1.10)          Node: node-2 (10.0.1.11)                     │
│  ├── jobpost-web-abc123            ├── jobpost-web-def456                       │
│  ├── jobpost-worker-xyz789         ├── postgres-0                               │
│  └── redis-master-0                └── jobpost-worker-uvw012                    │
│                                                                                  │
│  Node: node-3 (10.0.1.12)                                                        │
│  ├── jobpost-web-ghi789                                                          │
│  └── nginx-ingress-controller                                                    │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Command:

# Get all pods in production namespace with wide output
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o wide

Output:

NAME                              READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE     IP           NODE      NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12      1/1     Running   0          4h      10.244.1.5   node-1    <none>           <none>
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45      1/1     Running   0          4h      10.244.2.8   node-2    <none>           <none>
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78      1/1     Running   0          4h      10.244.3.3   node-3    <none>           <none>
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89   1/1     Running   0          4h      10.244.1.6   node-1    <none>           <none>
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01   1/1     Running   0          4h      10.244.2.9   node-2    <none>           <none>
postgres-0                        1/1     Running   0          7d      10.244.2.4   node-2    <none>           <none>
redis-master-0                    1/1     Running   0          7d      10.244.1.3   node-1    <none>           <none>

✅ Verification:

# Verify pod count matches expected replicas
kubectl get deployment -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,REPLICAS:.spec.replicas,READY:.status.readyReplicas

# Check pods are distributed across nodes
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.spec.nodeName}{"\n"}{end}' | sort | uniq -c
NAME              REPLICAS   READY
jobpost-web       3          3
jobpost-worker    2          2

      3 node-1
      3 node-2
      1 node-3
Example 2: Get Resources with Label Selectors

Scenario: Filter and view only the web tier components of the jobpost application to troubleshoot user-reported latency issues.

📄 Context - Label Strategy (docs/labeling-convention.md):

# anihpj Kubernetes Labeling Convention
# 
# Standard Labels:
# ================
# app: jobpost              # Application name
# tier: web|worker|db|cache # Component tier
# version: v2.1.0           # Semantic version
# environment: production   # Environment (dev/staging/production)
# team: backend             # Owning team
# managed-by: helm          # Deployment tool
#
# Example Label Selectors:
# ========================
# All web components:           app=jobpost,tier=web
# All production resources:     environment=production
# All backend team resources:   team=backend
# Specific version:             app=jobpost,version=v2.1.0
# Multiple tiers:               app=jobpost,tier in (web,worker)

Command:

# Get all resources with label app=jobpost and tier=web
kubectl get pods,svc,deployments -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web

# Using set-based selector for multiple tiers
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l 'app=jobpost,tier in (web,worker)'

Output:

NAME                                   READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12       1/1     Running   0          4h
pod/jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45       1/1     Running   0          4h
pod/jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78       1/1     Running   0          4h

NAME                      TYPE           CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP     PORT(S)        AGE
service/jobpost-service   LoadBalancer   10.96.100.50    34.123.45.67    80:31234/TCP   7d

NAME                          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           7d

# Set-based selector output:
NAME                                   READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12           1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45           1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78           1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89        1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01        1/1     Running   0          4h

✅ Verification:

# Show all labels to verify correct filtering
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost --show-labels

# Count resources by tier
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.labels.tier}{"\n"}{end}' | sort | uniq -c
NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   LABELS
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12       1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,pod-template-hash=6d4f8b7c9d,tier=web,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45       1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,pod-template-hash=6d4f8b7c9d,tier=web,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78       1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,pod-template-hash=6d4f8b7c9d,tier=web,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89    1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,pod-template-hash=7f8a9b6c5d,tier=worker,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01    1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,pod-template-hash=7f8a9b6c5d,tier=worker,version=v2.1.0

      3 web
      2 worker
Example 3: Get Resources with Custom Columns

Scenario: Create a custom report showing pod resource usage limits and requests for capacity planning of the jobpost application.

📄 Context - Resource Planning Spreadsheet:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Resource Planning - Q1 2026                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Component          Instances  CPU Request  CPU Limit  Mem Request  Mem Limit   │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│  jobpost-web        3          250m         500m       256Mi        512Mi       │
│  jobpost-worker     2          200m         400m       256Mi        512Mi       │
│  postgres           1          500m         1000m      512Mi        1Gi         │
│  redis              1          100m         200m       128Mi        256Mi       │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│  TOTAL REQUESTED:   7 pods     1.55 cores              1.41Gi                   │
│  TOTAL LIMITS:                 2.9 cores               2.79Gi                   │
│                                                                                  │
│  Cluster Capacity:  3 nodes x 4 cores = 12 cores, 3 nodes x 8Gi = 24Gi         │
│  Utilization:       Request: 13% CPU, 6% Memory | Limit: 24% CPU, 12% Memory   │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Command:

# Get pods with custom columns showing resource requests and limits
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns='\
POD:metadata.name,\
CONTAINER:spec.containers[0].name,\
CPU_REQ:spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu,\
CPU_LIM:spec.containers[0].resources.limits.cpu,\
MEM_REQ:spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory,\
MEM_LIM:spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory,\
STATUS:status.phase'

Output:

POD                               CONTAINER   CPU_REQ   CPU_LIM   MEM_REQ   MEM_LIM   STATUS
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12      django      250m      500m      256Mi     512Mi     Running
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45      django      250m      500m      256Mi     512Mi     Running
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78      django      250m      500m      256Mi     512Mi     Running
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89   celery      200m      400m      256Mi     512Mi     Running
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01   celery      200m      400m      256Mi     512Mi     Running
postgres-0                        postgres    500m      1000m     512Mi     1Gi       Running
redis-master-0                    redis       100m      200m      128Mi     256Mi     Running

✅ Verification:

# Calculate total resource requests
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o json | jq '
  [.items[].spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu // "0m" | 
   gsub("m"; "") | tonumber] | add | "\(.)m total CPU requested"'

# Save custom columns format for reuse
cat <<'EOF' > ~/.kube/custom-columns/resources.txt
POD:.metadata.name
CPU_REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu
MEM_REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory
CPU_LIM:.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.cpu
MEM_LIM:.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory
EOF

kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -o custom-columns-file=~/.kube/custom-columns/resources.txt
"1550m total CPU requested"

POD                               CPU_REQ   MEM_REQ   CPU_LIM   MEM_LIM
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12      250m      256Mi     500m      512Mi
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45      250m      256Mi     500m      512Mi
...
Example 4: Get Resources with JSONPath for Scripting

Scenario: Extract specific data from Kubernetes resources for use in shell scripts and automation pipelines that monitor the jobpost application.

📄 Context - Monitoring Script (scripts/health-check.sh):

#!/bin/bash
# health-check.sh - Monitor anihpj jobpost application health
# 
# This script is run every 5 minutes by a CronJob to:
# 1. Extract pod IPs for direct health checks
# 2. Verify service endpoints are registered
# 3. Check configmap values are correct
# 4. Alert if any pods are not ready
#
# Required JSONPath queries:
# - Pod IPs: .items[*].status.podIP
# - Container images: .items[*].spec.containers[*].image
# - Ready conditions: .items[*].status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status

NAMESPACE="anihpj-production"
SLACK_WEBHOOK="${SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL}"

echo "=== anihpj Health Check $(date) ==="

Command:

# Get all pod IPs for direct health checks
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.podIP}'

# Get pod names and their ready status
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get container images for version tracking
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get service ClusterIP and endpoints
kubectl get svc jobpost-service -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='ClusterIP: {.spec.clusterIP}, ExternalIP: {.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}'

Output:

# Pod IPs:
10.244.1.5 10.244.2.8 10.244.3.3

# Pod ready status:
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12	True
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45	True
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78	True
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89	True
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01	True
postgres-0	True
redis-master-0	True

# Container images:
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0

# Service info:
ClusterIP: 10.96.100.50, ExternalIP: 34.123.45.67

✅ Verification:

# Health check each pod IP
for IP in $(kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.podIP}'); do
  echo -n "Checking $IP: "
  kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
    curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://$IP:8000/health/
  echo ""
done
Checking 10.244.1.5: 200
Checking 10.244.2.8: 200
Checking 10.244.3.3: 200
Example 5: Get Resources Across All Namespaces

Scenario: Platform engineer needs to audit all jobpost-related resources across development, staging, and production namespaces to ensure consistency.

📄 Context - Multi-Environment Architecture:

# anihpj Multi-Environment Namespace Strategy
# 
# Namespaces:
# ├── anihpj-dev          # Development environment
# │   ├── jobpost-web (1 replica)
# │   ├── postgres-dev
# │   └── redis-dev
# ├── anihpj-staging      # Staging environment  
# │   ├── jobpost-web (2 replicas)
# │   ├── postgres-staging
# │   └── redis-staging
# └── anihpj-production   # Production environment
#     ├── jobpost-web (3 replicas)
#     ├── jobpost-worker (2 replicas)
#     ├── postgres (HA)
#     └── redis (HA)
#
# All environments share:
# - Same container images (different tags)
# - Same ConfigMap structure
# - Same Secret structure
# - Same RBAC policies

Command:

# Get all pods across all namespaces with app=jobpost
kubectl get pods -A -l app=jobpost --sort-by=.metadata.namespace

# Get deployments across all namespaces
kubectl get deployments -A -l app=jobpost \
  -o custom-columns='NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,REPLICAS:.spec.replicas,READY:.status.readyReplicas,IMAGE:.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image'

# Get all services for jobpost across namespaces
kubectl get svc -A -l app=jobpost

Output:

# Pods across all namespaces:
NAMESPACE            NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
anihpj-dev           jobpost-web-5b7c9d8f6e-aaa11       1/1     Running   0          2d
anihpj-staging       jobpost-web-6c8d0e9f7g-bbb22       1/1     Running   0          1d
anihpj-staging       jobpost-web-6c8d0e9f7g-ccc33       1/1     Running   0          1d
anihpj-production    jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12       1/1     Running   0          4h
anihpj-production    jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45       1/1     Running   0          4h
anihpj-production    jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78       1/1     Running   0          4h
anihpj-production    jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-xyz89    1/1     Running   0          4h
anihpj-production    jobpost-worker-7f8a9b6c5d-uvw01    1/1     Running   0          4h

# Deployments summary:
NAMESPACE            NAME             REPLICAS   READY   IMAGE
anihpj-dev           jobpost-web      1          1       anihpj/jobpost:dev-latest
anihpj-staging       jobpost-web      2          2       anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0-rc1
anihpj-production    jobpost-web      3          3       anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0

# Services:
NAMESPACE            NAME              TYPE           CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP     PORT(S)        AGE
anihpj-dev           jobpost-service   ClusterIP      10.96.50.10     <none>          80/TCP         30d
anihpj-staging       jobpost-service   LoadBalancer   10.96.75.20     35.200.10.20    80:30080/TCP   14d
anihpj-production    jobpost-service   LoadBalancer   10.96.100.50    34.123.45.67    80:31234/TCP   7d

✅ Verification:

# Compare image versions across environments
kubectl get deployments -A -l app=jobpost \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}: {.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}{"\n"}{end}'

# Verify all pods are healthy
kubectl get pods -A -l app=jobpost \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}/{.metadata.name}: {.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}' | grep -v Running
anihpj-dev: anihpj/jobpost:dev-latest
anihpj-staging: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0-rc1

# (empty output means all pods are Running)
Example 6: Watch Resources for Real-time Monitoring

Scenario: Monitor deployment rollout in real-time during a production release of the jobpost application to catch any issues immediately.

📄 Context - Deployment Runbook (runbooks/production-deploy.md):

# anihpj Production Deployment Runbook

## Pre-deployment Checklist
- [ ] Staging tests passed (CI/CD pipeline)
- [ ] Database migrations reviewed
- [ ] Rollback plan documented
- [ ] On-call engineer notified

## Deployment Monitoring Commands

During deployment, open multiple terminal windows:

### Terminal 1: Watch pods
```bash
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost -w
```

### Terminal 2: Watch events
```bash
kubectl get events -n anihpj-production -w --field-selector=involvedObject.kind=Pod
```

### Terminal 3: Watch deployment status
```bash
kubectl rollout status deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production
```

## Rollback Command (if needed)
```bash
kubectl rollout undo deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production
```

Command:

# Watch pods during deployment (continues showing updates)
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web -w

# Watch only changes (no initial list)
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost --watch-only

# Watch events related to pods
kubectl get events -n anihpj-production -w \
  --field-selector=involvedObject.kind=Pod,reason!=Pulled

Output:

# Initial state + updates as deployment progresses:
NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12     1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45     1/1     Running   0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78     1/1     Running   0          4h

# New deployment starts (v2.2.0 rollout):
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     0/1     Pending   0          0s
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     0/1     Pending   0          0s
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     0/1     ContainerCreating   0          0s
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     0/1     Running   0          2s
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     1/1     Running   0          12s
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12     1/1     Terminating   0          4h
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02     0/1     Pending   0          0s
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02     0/1     ContainerCreating   0          0s
...

# Events during rollout:
LAST SEEN   TYPE      REASON      OBJECT                            MESSAGE
0s          Normal    Scheduled   pod/jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01  Successfully assigned anihpj-production/jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01 to node-2
0s          Normal    Started     pod/jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01  Started container django
0s          Normal    Killing     pod/jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12  Stopping container django

✅ Verification:

# Check rollout completed successfully
kubectl rollout status deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Verify new image is deployed
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'

# Check all new pods are ready
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}{"\n"}{end}'
deployment "jobpost-web" successfully rolled out

anihpj/jobpost:v2.2.0

jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01: True
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02: True
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new03: True

kubectl run

CREATE Pod Creation Quick Start Debugging

Run a particular image on the cluster

The kubectl run command creates and runs a particular image in a pod. This is the simplest way to create a pod for testing, debugging, or running one-off tasks. Unlike deployments, pods created with kubectl run are not managed by a controller and won't be recreated if they fail.

For the anihpj Django project, kubectl run is commonly used for:

  • Running one-off Django management commands (migrate, collectstatic)
  • Database debugging and maintenance tasks
  • Testing network connectivity between services
  • Running interactive Python/Django shells for debugging
  • Quick tests with the jobpost container image

Syntax

# Create and run a pod
kubectl run NAME --image=IMAGE [flags]

# Run pod with specific command
kubectl run NAME --image=IMAGE -- COMMAND [args]

# Run interactive pod with TTY
kubectl run NAME --image=IMAGE -it --rm -- COMMAND

# Run pod and immediately attach
kubectl run NAME --image=IMAGE --attach

# Create pod from generator (dry-run)
kubectl run NAME --image=IMAGE --dry-run=client -o yaml

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--imagestringContainer image to run (required)
-i, --stdinboolKeep stdin open on the container
-t, --ttyboolAllocate a TTY for the container
--rmboolDelete pod after it exits (with --attach or -it)
--attachboolAttach to the pod after creation
--restartstringRestart policy: Always, OnFailure, Never (default "Always")
--commandboolUse command instead of args for container
--envstring[]Environment variables to set (KEY=VALUE)
--portintPort that the container exposes
--labels, -lstringLabels to apply to the pod (comma separated)
--serviceaccountstringService account to use for the pod
--requestsstringResource requests (cpu=100m,memory=256Mi)
--limitsstringResource limits (cpu=200m,memory=512Mi)
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
-o, --outputstringOutput format for dry-run
-n, --namespacestringNamespace to create the pod in
--overridesstringJSON override for generated pod spec
--image-pull-policystringImage pull policy: Always, IfNotPresent, Never

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Pod created/ran successfully
1Error occurred (image pull failed, resource limits exceeded)
Container exit codeWhen using --attach or -it, returns container's exit code

📁 Context: Django Management Tasks for anihpj

Common Use Cases: The kubectl run command is frequently used for running Django management commands and debugging tasks in the anihpj environment.
Django Management Commands Overview
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Django Management Tasks                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  One-off Tasks (kubectl run --rm):                                               │
│  ├── python manage.py migrate          # Database migrations                     │
│  ├── python manage.py collectstatic    # Collect static files                   │
│  ├── python manage.py createsuperuser  # Create admin user                      │
│  ├── python manage.py shell            # Interactive Django shell               │
│  ├── python manage.py dbshell          # Database CLI                           │
│  └── python manage.py clearsessions    # Clear expired sessions                 │
│                                                                                  │
│  Debugging Tasks:                                                                │
│  ├── Check database connectivity                                                │
│  ├── Test Redis connection                                                      │
│  ├── Verify environment variables                                               │
│  ├── Run model queries                                                          │
│  └── Debug Celery tasks                                                         │
│                                                                                  │
│  Models in jobpost app:                                                          │
│  Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost                                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Run Database Migrations

Scenario: After updating the JobPost model with new fields, run Django migrations in the production cluster before deploying the new application version.

📄 Context - Migration File (jobpost/migrations/0005_jobpost_salary_range.py):

# jobpost/migrations/0005_jobpost_salary_range.py
# Generated by Django 5.2.5 on 2026-03-01

from django.db import migrations, models

class Migration(migrations.Migration):

    dependencies = [
        ('jobpost', '0004_jobpost_remote_option'),
    ]

    operations = [
        migrations.AddField(
            model_name='jobpost',
            name='salary_min',
            field=models.DecimalField(
                decimal_places=2, 
                max_digits=10, 
                null=True, 
                blank=True,
                help_text='Minimum salary for the position'
            ),
        ),
        migrations.AddField(
            model_name='jobpost',
            name='salary_max',
            field=models.DecimalField(
                decimal_places=2, 
                max_digits=10, 
                null=True,
                blank=True, 
                help_text='Maximum salary for the position'
            ),
        ),
        migrations.AddField(
            model_name='jobpost',
            name='salary_currency',
            field=models.CharField(
                max_length=3, 
                default='USD',
                help_text='ISO 4217 currency code'
            ),
        ),
    ]

Command:

# Run migrations using the jobpost image with required env vars
kubectl run django-migrate \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.2.0 \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=postgres://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db" \
  --env="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" \
  --serviceaccount=jobpost-sa \
  -- python manage.py migrate --noinput

Output:

If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
Operations to perform:
  Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
Running migrations:
  Applying jobpost.0005_jobpost_salary_range... OK
pod "django-migrate" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify migration was applied
kubectl run django-showmigrations \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.2.0 \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=postgres://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db" \
  --env="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" \
  -- python manage.py showmigrations jobpost
jobpost
 [X] 0001_initial
 [X] 0002_add_skills
 [X] 0003_add_location
 [X] 0004_jobpost_remote_option
 [X] 0005_jobpost_salary_range
pod "django-showmigrations" deleted
Example 2: Interactive Django Shell for Debugging

Scenario: Debug a production issue where certain JobPost entries are not displaying correctly. Use Django shell to query the database directly.

📄 Context - Support Ticket #4521:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SUPPORT TICKET #4521                                                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Subject: Some job posts not appearing in search results                         │
│  Priority: High                                                                  │
│  Reporter: hiring-manager@company.com                                           │
│                                                                                  │
│  Description:                                                                    │
│  Several job posts created in the last 24 hours are not appearing in search     │
│  results. The posts exist when accessed directly by URL but are missing from    │
│  the listings page.                                                              │
│                                                                                  │
│  Affected Job Post IDs: 1542, 1543, 1545                                        │
│                                                                                  │
│  Investigation Required:                                                         │
│  - Check is_published flag on affected posts                                    │
│  - Verify author_id references are valid                                        │
│  - Check if skills relationships are intact                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Command:

# Start interactive Django shell
kubectl run django-shell \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0 \
  -it --rm \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=postgres://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db" \
  --env="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" \
  -- python manage.py shell

Output (Interactive Session):

Python 3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2024, 08:25:07) [GCC 12.2.0]
Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information
IPython 8.12.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help.

In [1]: from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Skills

In [2]: # Check the problematic job posts
   ...: problem_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(id__in=[1542, 1543, 1545])
   ...: for post in problem_posts:
   ...:     print(f"ID: {post.id}, Title: {post.title}")
   ...:     print(f"  is_published: {post.is_published}")
   ...:     print(f"  author: {post.author_id}")
   ...:     print(f"  skills: {list(post.skills.values_list('name', flat=True))}")

ID: 1542, Title: Senior Django Developer
  is_published: False  # <-- Found the issue!
  author: 12
  skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'PostgreSQL']

ID: 1543, Title: Backend Engineer
  is_published: False
  author: 12
  skills: ['Python', 'FastAPI', 'Docker']

ID: 1545, Title: Full Stack Developer
  is_published: False
  author: 15
  skills: ['Python', 'React', 'Kubernetes']

In [3]: # Fix: Publish the posts
   ...: problem_posts.update(is_published=True)
Out[3]: 3

In [4]: # Verify fix
   ...: JobPost.objects.filter(id__in=[1542, 1543, 1545]).values('id', 'is_published')
Out[4]: <QuerySet [{'id': 1542, 'is_published': True}, {'id': 1543, 'is_published': True}, {'id': 1545, 'is_published': True}]>

In [5]: exit()
pod "django-shell" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Quick verification that posts are now visible
kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
  python manage.py shell -c "from jobpost.models import JobPost; print(JobPost.objects.filter(is_published=True).count())"
1547
Example 3: Test Network Connectivity

Scenario: After deploying a new PostgreSQL service, verify that pods can connect to the database before updating the Django application.

📄 Context - New Database Service (k8s/production/postgres-service.yaml):

# k8s/production/postgres-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: postgres
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - name: postgres
    port: 5432
    targetPort: 5432
---
# DNS Resolution:
# postgres-service.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local:5432
#
# Connection string:
# postgresql://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db

Command:

# Test DNS resolution and TCP connectivity
kubectl run network-test \
  --image=busybox:1.36 \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  -- sh -c "
    echo '=== DNS Resolution Test ==='
    nslookup postgres-service
    
    echo ''
    echo '=== TCP Connection Test ==='
    nc -zv postgres-service 5432
    
    echo ''
    echo '=== Service Discovery ==='
    cat /etc/resolv.conf
  "

Output:

=== DNS Resolution Test ===
Server:		10.96.0.10
Address:	10.96.0.10:53

Name:	postgres-service.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Address: 10.96.200.15

=== TCP Connection Test ===
postgres-service (10.96.200.15:5432) open

=== Service Discovery ===
nameserver 10.96.0.10
search anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
options ndots:5
pod "network-test" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Test actual database connection with psql
kubectl run postgres-test \
  --image=postgres:15-alpine \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="PGPASSWORD=secretpass" \
  -- psql -h postgres-service -U jobpost_user -d jobpost_db -c "\dt"
                   List of relations
 Schema |            Name            | Type  |    Owner     
--------+----------------------------+-------+--------------
 public | auth_group                 | table | jobpost_user
 public | auth_user                  | table | jobpost_user
 public | django_migrations          | table | jobpost_user
 public | django_session             | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_author             | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_jobpost            | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_jobpost_skills     | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_location           | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_skills             | table | jobpost_user
 public | jobpost_user               | table | jobpost_user
(10 rows)

pod "postgres-test" deleted
Example 4: Run One-off Data Export Job

Scenario: HR department needs an export of all active job posts for quarterly reporting. Run a one-off script to generate and email the report.

📄 Context - Export Script (scripts/export_jobposts.py):

# scripts/export_jobposts.py
# Embedded in container at /app/scripts/export_jobposts.py

import csv
import io
from datetime import datetime
from django.core.mail import EmailMessage
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Location

def export_active_jobposts():
    """Export all active job posts to CSV and email to HR."""
    
    # Query active job posts with related data
    posts = JobPost.objects.filter(
        is_published=True
    ).select_related(
        'author', 'location'
    ).prefetch_related('skills')
    
    # Create CSV in memory
    output = io.StringIO()
    writer = csv.writer(output)
    
    # Header row
    writer.writerow([
        'ID', 'Title', 'Author', 'Location', 'Skills',
        'Salary Min', 'Salary Max', 'Remote', 'Created', 'Views'
    ])
    
    # Data rows
    for post in posts:
        writer.writerow([
            post.id,
            post.title,
            post.author.name if post.author else 'N/A',
            str(post.location) if post.location else 'N/A',
            ', '.join(post.skills.values_list('name', flat=True)),
            post.salary_min or 'N/A',
            post.salary_max or 'N/A',
            'Yes' if post.remote_option else 'No',
            post.created_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d'),
            post.view_count
        ])
    
    # Email report
    email = EmailMessage(
        subject=f'JobPost Export - {datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")}',
        body=f'Active job posts export attached. Total: {posts.count()} posts.',
        to=['hr@anihpj.com', 'reports@anihpj.com']
    )
    email.attach('jobposts_export.csv', output.getvalue(), 'text/csv')
    email.send()
    
    print(f"Exported {posts.count()} job posts")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    import django
    django.setup()
    export_active_jobposts()

Command:

# Run the export script as a one-off job
kubectl run jobpost-export \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0 \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=postgres://jobpost_user:secretpass@postgres-service:5432/jobpost_db" \
  --env="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" \
  --env="EMAIL_HOST=smtp.sendgrid.net" \
  --env="EMAIL_HOST_USER=apikey" \
  --env="EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD=SG.xxxx" \
  --labels="purpose=data-export,team=hr" \
  -- python scripts/export_jobposts.py

Output:

Exported 1547 job posts
pod "jobpost-export" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Check if pod completed successfully
kubectl get events -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector=involvedObject.name=jobpost-export \
  --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Alternative: Run and keep logs
kubectl run jobpost-export-v2 \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0 \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=..." \
  -- python scripts/export_jobposts.py

# Then check logs
kubectl logs -n anihpj-production jobpost-export-v2
LAST SEEN   TYPE     REASON      OBJECT                MESSAGE
2m          Normal   Scheduled   pod/jobpost-export    Successfully assigned
2m          Normal   Pulled      pod/jobpost-export    Container image already present
2m          Normal   Created     pod/jobpost-export    Created container jobpost-export
2m          Normal   Started     pod/jobpost-export    Started container jobpost-export
1m          Normal   Completed   pod/jobpost-export    Container completed
Example 5: Generate YAML for Pod Templates

Scenario: Create a pod template for running Django management commands that can be saved and reused, with proper resource limits and security context.

📄 Context - Pod Template Requirements:

# Pod template requirements for Django management tasks:
#
# Security:
#   - Run as non-root user (1000)
#   - Read-only root filesystem where possible
#   - Use dedicated service account
#
# Resources:
#   - CPU: 100m request, 500m limit
#   - Memory: 256Mi request, 512Mi limit
#
# Environment:
#   - DATABASE_URL from secret
#   - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
#   - Disable DEBUG mode

Command:

# Generate pod YAML with dry-run
kubectl run django-task \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0 \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --serviceaccount=jobpost-sa \
  --requests='cpu=100m,memory=256Mi' \
  --limits='cpu=500m,memory=512Mi' \
  --labels='app=jobpost,purpose=management-task' \
  --env="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  -- python manage.py migrate

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: null
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    purpose: management-task
    run: django-task
  name: django-task
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  containers:
  - args:
    - python
    - manage.py
    - migrate
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: anihpj.settings
    image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
    name: django-task
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: 500m
        memory: 512Mi
      requests:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 256Mi
  dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
  restartPolicy: Never
  serviceAccountName: jobpost-sa
status: {}

✅ Verification:

# Save to file and enhance with overrides
kubectl run django-task \
  --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0 \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --serviceaccount=jobpost-sa \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  --overrides='{
    "spec": {
      "securityContext": {
        "runAsNonRoot": true,
        "runAsUser": 1000
      },
      "containers": [{
        "name": "django-task",
        "envFrom": [
          {"secretRef": {"name": "django-secrets"}},
          {"configMapRef": {"name": "django-config"}}
        ]
      }]
    }
  }' \
  -- python manage.py migrate > k8s/templates/django-task-pod.yaml

# Verify the generated template
cat k8s/templates/django-task-pod.yaml
Example 6: Run Redis CLI for Cache Debugging

Scenario: Debug caching issues in the jobpost application by connecting to Redis and inspecting cached data for job listings.

📄 Context - Django Cache Configuration (anihpj/settings.py):

# anihpj/settings.py - Cache configuration

CACHES = {
    'default': {
        'BACKEND': 'django_redis.cache.RedisCache',
        'LOCATION': 'redis://redis-service:6379/0',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'CLIENT_CLASS': 'django_redis.client.DefaultClient',
        },
        'KEY_PREFIX': 'jobpost',
        'TIMEOUT': 300,  # 5 minutes default
    }
}

# Cache keys used by jobpost app:
# - jobpost:listing:page:{page_num} - Paginated listings
# - jobpost:detail:{post_id} - Individual post details
# - jobpost:skills:all - All skills for filter dropdown
# - jobpost:locations:active - Active locations
# - jobpost:stats:total - Total active posts count
# - jobpost:search:{query_hash} - Search results

Command:

# Run interactive Redis CLI
kubectl run redis-cli \
  --image=redis:7-alpine \
  -it --rm \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  -- redis-cli -h redis-service

Output (Interactive Session):

redis-service:6379> # Check all jobpost cache keys
redis-service:6379> KEYS jobpost:*
1) "jobpost:listing:page:1"
2) "jobpost:listing:page:2"
3) "jobpost:skills:all"
4) "jobpost:locations:active"
5) "jobpost:stats:total"
6) "jobpost:detail:1542"
7) "jobpost:detail:1543"
8) "jobpost:search:a1b2c3d4"

redis-service:6379> # Check cache stats
redis-service:6379> INFO stats
# Stats
total_connections_received:1547
total_commands_processed:89432
keyspace_hits:67891
keyspace_misses:21541
hit_rate:75.92%

redis-service:6379> # Check TTL on a listing page
redis-service:6379> TTL jobpost:listing:page:1
(integer) 187

redis-service:6379> # Get cached skills (returns serialized Python data)
redis-service:6379> GET jobpost:skills:all
"\x80\x05\x95...[serialized data]..."

redis-service:6379> # Clear specific cache if stale
redis-service:6379> DEL jobpost:listing:page:1
(integer) 1

redis-service:6379> # Clear all jobpost caches (careful!)
redis-service:6379> KEYS jobpost:* | xargs redis-cli DEL
redis-service:6379> exit
pod "redis-cli" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify cache is rebuilding after clear
kubectl run redis-check \
  --image=redis:7-alpine \
  --rm -it \
  --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  -- redis-cli -h redis-service KEYS "jobpost:*" | wc -l

# Test Django cache from application
kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
  python manage.py shell -c "
from django.core.cache import cache
print('Cache backend:', cache.__class__.__name__)
print('Keys after rebuild:', len(cache.keys('*')))
"
3

Cache backend: RedisCache
Keys after rebuild: 5

kubectl expose

CREATE Service Networking Load Balancing

Expose a resource as a new Kubernetes Service

The kubectl expose command creates a Service to expose pods, deployments, ReplicaSets, or other resources to network traffic. Services provide stable network endpoints, load balancing, and service discovery for your applications.

For the anihpj Django project, kubectl expose is used to:

  • Create ClusterIP services for internal communication between Django and PostgreSQL
  • Expose Django web pods via LoadBalancer for external access
  • Create NodePort services for development and testing environments
  • Set up headless services for StatefulSets like database clusters
  • Configure named ports for health checks and metrics endpoints

Syntax

# Expose a deployment
kubectl expose deployment NAME --port=PORT [flags]

# Expose a pod
kubectl expose pod NAME --port=PORT --target-port=TARGET [flags]

# Expose with specific service type
kubectl expose deployment NAME --type=LoadBalancer --port=80 --target-port=8000

# Expose with named port
kubectl expose deployment NAME --port=80 --target-port=http --name=SERVICE_NAME

# Create service from YAML file
kubectl expose -f resource.yaml --port=PORT

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--portintPort the service should serve on
--target-portstringPort on the container to direct traffic to (number or name)
--typestringService type: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName
--namestringName for the service (defaults to resource name)
--protocolstringProtocol: TCP, UDP, SCTP (default "TCP")
--selectorstringLabel selector to use (overrides resource labels)
--external-ipstringExternal IP address to set for the service
--cluster-ipstringClusterIP to assign (or "None" for headless)
--load-balancer-ipstringIP to assign to the load balancer
--session-affinitystringSession affinity: None, ClientIP
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
-o, --outputstringOutput format: json|yaml|name
-n, --namespacestringNamespace for the service
--overridesstringJSON override for generated service spec

Service Types

TypeDescriptionUse Case
ClusterIPInternal cluster IP only (default)Internal services (Django ↔ PostgreSQL)
NodePortExpose on each node's IP at a static portDevelopment, testing without cloud LB
LoadBalancerExternal load balancer (cloud provider)Production external access
ExternalNameMaps service to external DNS nameExternal database, third-party APIs

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Service created successfully
1Error (resource not found, service exists, invalid selector)

📁 Context: Service Architecture for anihpj

Networking Overview: The anihpj jobpost application uses multiple service types to handle internal and external traffic.
Service Architecture Diagram
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Production Service Architecture                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│   EXTERNAL TRAFFIC                                                               │
│         │                                                                        │
│         ▼                                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────┐                                                           │
│   │  LoadBalancer   │  jobpost-service (34.123.45.67:80)                        │
│   │  Service        │  Type: LoadBalancer                                        │
│   └────────┬────────┘                                                           │
│            │                                                                     │
│            ▼                                                                     │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                                   │
│   │         jobpost-web pods (x3)           │                                   │
│   │     app=jobpost, tier=web               │                                   │
│   │     Port: 8000 (Django/Gunicorn)        │                                   │
│   └────────┬────────────────────────────────┘                                   │
│            │                                                                     │
│   ─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────── INTERNAL NETWORK ────────  │
│            │                                                                     │
│     ┌──────┴──────┐          ┌──────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐          │
│     │             │          │              │         │              │          │
│     ▼             ▼          ▼              │         ▼              │          │
│ ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐      │    ┌─────────┐         │          │
│ │postgres │  │ redis   │  │ celery  │      │    │ flower  │         │          │
│ │-service │  │-service │  │-worker  │      │    │-service │         │          │
│ │ :5432   │  │ :6379   │  │         │      │    │ :5555   │         │          │
│ │ClusterIP│  │ClusterIP│  │         │      │    │NodePort │         │          │
│ └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘      │    └─────────┘         │          │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Expose Deployment as LoadBalancer Service

Scenario: After deploying the jobpost-web deployment, expose it to the internet via a cloud LoadBalancer service for production traffic.

📄 Context - Deployment Configuration (k8s/production/deployment.yaml):

# k8s/production/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
    version: v2.1.0
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: web
        version: v2.1.0
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: http
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5

Command:

# Expose deployment as LoadBalancer service
kubectl expose deployment jobpost-web \
  --type=LoadBalancer \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8000 \
  --name=jobpost-service \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

Output:

service/jobpost-service exposed

✅ Verification:

# Check service details (wait for external IP)
kubectl get svc jobpost-service -n anihpj-production -w

# Once external IP is assigned, test connectivity
kubectl get svc jobpost-service -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}'

# Test the endpoint
EXTERNAL_IP=$(kubectl get svc jobpost-service -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}')
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://$EXTERNAL_IP/health/
NAME              TYPE           CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
jobpost-service   LoadBalancer   10.96.100.50   <pending>     80:31234/TCP   5s
jobpost-service   LoadBalancer   10.96.100.50   34.123.45.67  80:31234/TCP   45s

34.123.45.67

200
Example 2: Create ClusterIP Service for Database

Scenario: Create an internal ClusterIP service for the PostgreSQL StatefulSet so Django pods can connect using a stable DNS name.

📄 Context - PostgreSQL StatefulSet (k8s/production/postgres-statefulset.yaml):

# k8s/production/postgres-statefulset.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: postgres
    tier: database
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
        tier: database
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15-alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
          name: postgres
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: "jobpost_db"
        - name: POSTGRES_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-secrets
              key: username
        - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-secrets
              key: password
        volumeMounts:
        - name: postgres-data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: postgres-data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 20Gi

Command:

# Expose PostgreSQL StatefulSet as ClusterIP service
kubectl expose statefulset postgres \
  --type=ClusterIP \
  --port=5432 \
  --target-port=5432 \
  --name=postgres-service \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

# Alternative: expose pod directly (for single replica)
kubectl expose pod postgres-0 \
  --type=ClusterIP \
  --port=5432 \
  --target-port=postgres \
  --name=postgres-service \
  --namespace=anihpj-production

Output:

service/postgres-service exposed

✅ Verification:

# Verify service created with correct selector
kubectl describe svc postgres-service -n anihpj-production

# Test database connectivity from Django pod
kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
  python -c "
import psycopg2
conn = psycopg2.connect(
    host='postgres-service',
    port=5432,
    dbname='jobpost_db',
    user='jobpost_user',
    password='secretpass'
)
print('Connected to:', conn.get_dsn_parameters()['host'])
conn.close()
"
Name:              postgres-service
Namespace:         anihpj-production
Labels:            app=postgres
                   tier=database
Selector:          app=postgres
Type:              ClusterIP
IP:                10.96.200.15
Port:              <unset>  5432/TCP
TargetPort:        5432/TCP
Endpoints:         10.244.2.4:5432
Session Affinity:  None
Events:            <none>

Connected to: postgres-service
Example 3: Create NodePort Service for Development

Scenario: In the development environment without a cloud load balancer, expose the jobpost application via NodePort to access it from a local machine.

📄 Context - Development Environment (k8s/dev/README.md):

# anihpj Development Environment

## Local Kubernetes Setup (Minikube/Kind)

The development environment runs on a local Kubernetes cluster
without cloud load balancer support. Use NodePort services to
access the application.

### Access Methods:
1. **NodePort Service**: Access via node IP and assigned port
2. **Port Forward**: `kubectl port-forward` for debugging
3. **Minikube Service**: `minikube service` command

### Port Allocation:
- jobpost-web: NodePort 30080
- postgres: ClusterIP only (internal)
- redis: ClusterIP only (internal)
- flower (Celery monitor): NodePort 30555

### Quick Access:
```bash
# Get Minikube IP
minikube ip
# Access: http://<minikube-ip>:30080
```

Command:

# Expose as NodePort with specific port number
kubectl expose deployment jobpost-web \
  --type=NodePort \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8000 \
  --name=jobpost-nodeport \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8000,"nodePort":30080}]}}'

# Simpler version (auto-assigned NodePort)
kubectl expose deployment jobpost-web \
  --type=NodePort \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8000 \
  --name=jobpost-nodeport \
  --namespace=anihpj-dev

Output:

service/jobpost-nodeport exposed

✅ Verification:

# Get the assigned NodePort
kubectl get svc jobpost-nodeport -n anihpj-dev \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[0].nodePort}'

# Get node IP (for Minikube)
minikube ip

# Or get any node IP
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# Test connectivity
NODE_IP=$(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}')
NODE_PORT=$(kubectl get svc jobpost-nodeport -n anihpj-dev -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[0].nodePort}')
curl -s http://$NODE_IP:$NODE_PORT/health/
30080

192.168.49.2

{"status": "healthy", "database": "connected", "cache": "connected"}
Example 4: Create Headless Service for StatefulSet

Scenario: Create a headless service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet to enable direct pod DNS resolution for primary/replica database setup.

📄 Context - Headless Service for Database HA:

# Headless Service DNS Resolution Pattern:
#
# Service Name: postgres-headless
# StatefulSet Name: postgres
# Namespace: anihpj-production
#
# DNS Records created:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
#   → Returns all pod IPs (no load balancing)
#
# postgres-0.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
#   → Direct access to postgres-0 pod
#
# postgres-1.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
#   → Direct access to postgres-1 pod (if replica exists)
#
# Use cases:
# - Primary database: postgres-0.postgres-headless
# - Read replicas: postgres-1.postgres-headless
# - Automatic discovery: postgres-headless (returns all IPs)

Command:

# Create headless service (ClusterIP: None)
kubectl expose statefulset postgres \
  --type=ClusterIP \
  --port=5432 \
  --target-port=5432 \
  --name=postgres-headless \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --cluster-ip=None

Output:

service/postgres-headless exposed

✅ Verification:

# Verify headless service (no ClusterIP)
kubectl get svc postgres-headless -n anihpj-production

# Test DNS resolution returns pod IPs directly
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production -- \
  nslookup postgres-headless

# Test direct pod DNS
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --namespace=anihpj-production -- \
  nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless
NAME                TYPE        CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
postgres-headless   ClusterIP   None         <none>        5432/TCP   5s

Server:		10.96.0.10
Address:	10.96.0.10:53

Name:	postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Address: 10.244.2.4

Server:		10.96.0.10
Address:	10.96.0.10:53

Name:	postgres-0.postgres-headless.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Address: 10.244.2.4
Example 5: Expose with Session Affinity

Scenario: Create a service with ClientIP session affinity to ensure users maintain connections to the same Django pod during file uploads and form submissions.

📄 Context - Session Affinity Requirements:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Session Affinity Use Case - JobPost File Uploads              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Problem:                                                                        │
│  ─────────                                                                       │
│  Job post creation involves multi-step form with file uploads:                  │
│  1. Step 1: Basic info (title, description)                                     │
│  2. Step 2: Upload company logo                                                 │
│  3. Step 3: Upload job description PDF                                          │
│  4. Step 4: Review and submit                                                   │
│                                                                                  │
│  Without session affinity, requests may route to different pods,                │
│  causing uploaded files to be "lost" between steps.                             │
│                                                                                  │
│  Solution:                                                                       │
│  ─────────                                                                       │
│  Use ClientIP session affinity to ensure all requests from the same            │
│  client IP address are routed to the same pod until session expires.           │
│                                                                                  │
│  Alternative approaches:                                                         │
│  - Store temp files in shared storage (S3, NFS)                                │
│  - Use Redis for session storage                                                │
│  - Implement resumable uploads                                                  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Command:

# Expose with ClientIP session affinity
kubectl expose deployment jobpost-web \
  --type=LoadBalancer \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8000 \
  --name=jobpost-sticky \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --session-affinity=ClientIP \
  --overrides='{
    "spec": {
      "sessionAffinityConfig": {
        "clientIP": {
          "timeoutSeconds": 3600
        }
      }
    }
  }'

Output:

service/jobpost-sticky exposed

✅ Verification:

# Verify session affinity is configured
kubectl get svc jobpost-sticky -n anihpj-production -o yaml | grep -A5 sessionAffinity

# Test that multiple requests hit same pod
for i in {1..5}; do
  kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
    curl -s http://jobpost-sticky/api/pod-info/ 2>/dev/null || echo "Request $i"
done
sessionAffinity: ClientIP
sessionAffinityConfig:
  clientIP:
    timeoutSeconds: 3600

# All requests from same client hit same pod:
{"pod_name": "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12", "pod_ip": "10.244.1.5"}
{"pod_name": "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12", "pod_ip": "10.244.1.5"}
{"pod_name": "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12", "pod_ip": "10.244.1.5"}
{"pod_name": "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12", "pod_ip": "10.244.1.5"}
{"pod_name": "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12", "pod_ip": "10.244.1.5"}
Example 6: Generate Service YAML for GitOps

Scenario: Generate service YAML manifests using kubectl expose dry-run for version-controlled GitOps deployment pipeline.

📄 Context - GitOps Repository Structure:

# anihpj GitOps Repository Structure (Flux/ArgoCD)
#
# infrastructure/
# ├── base/
# │   ├── namespace.yaml
# │   ├── deployment.yaml
# │   └── service.yaml           ← Generated with kubectl expose
# ├── overlays/
# │   ├── development/
# │   │   ├── kustomization.yaml
# │   │   └── service-patch.yaml  # NodePort override
# │   ├── staging/
# │   │   ├── kustomization.yaml
# │   │   └── service-patch.yaml  # LoadBalancer with staging IP
# │   └── production/
# │       ├── kustomization.yaml
# │       └── service-patch.yaml  # LoadBalancer with prod IP
# └── kustomization.yaml
#
# Workflow:
# 1. Generate base service YAML with kubectl expose --dry-run
# 2. Commit to git repository
# 3. ArgoCD/Flux syncs and applies to cluster

Command:

# Generate base service YAML
kubectl expose deployment jobpost-web \
  --type=ClusterIP \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8000 \
  --name=jobpost-service \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --labels='app=jobpost,tier=web' \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > k8s/base/service.yaml

# View generated YAML
cat k8s/base/service.yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: null
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  name: jobpost-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  ports:
  - port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  type: ClusterIP
status:
  loadBalancer: {}

✅ Verification:

# Create the production overlay patch
cat <<'EOF' > k8s/overlays/production/service-patch.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  loadBalancerIP: 34.123.45.67  # Reserved static IP
EOF

# Verify kustomize build produces correct output
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/

# Apply via GitOps (commit and push)
git add k8s/
git commit -m "Add jobpost service manifests"
git push origin main

# ArgoCD/Flux will sync automatically
# Kustomize build output (production):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  name: jobpost-service
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  loadBalancerIP: 34.123.45.67
  ports:
  - port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
  type: LoadBalancer

kubectl delete

DELETE Resource Removal Cleanup Termination

Delete resources by file names, stdin, resources and names, or by resources and label selector

The kubectl delete command removes Kubernetes resources from the cluster. Resources can be specified by type and name, by file, or by label selector. Deletion can be immediate or graceful (allowing time for cleanup).

For the anihpj Django project, kubectl delete is used for:

  • Removing failed or stuck pods during deployments
  • Cleaning up test resources after development
  • Removing old deployments and services during migrations
  • Deleting expired jobs and completed pods
  • Tearing down entire namespaces for environment cleanup
  • Removing deprecated configmaps and secrets

Syntax

# Delete by type and name
kubectl delete RESOURCE NAME [flags]

# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f FILENAME

# Delete by label selector
kubectl delete RESOURCE -l KEY=VALUE

# Delete all resources of a type
kubectl delete RESOURCE --all

# Delete with grace period
kubectl delete pod NAME --grace-period=SECONDS

# Force delete (skip graceful termination)
kubectl delete pod NAME --force --grace-period=0

# Delete multiple resource types
kubectl delete pods,services,deployments -l app=jobpost

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filenamestring[]File(s) containing resource to delete
-k, --kustomizestringProcess kustomization directory
-l, --selectorstringLabel selector to filter resources
--allboolDelete all resources of the specified type
-A, --all-namespacesboolDelete across all namespaces
--grace-periodintSeconds to wait before force deletion (-1 = default)
--forceboolImmediate deletion without graceful termination
--cascadestringCascade deletion: background, orphan, foreground
--waitboolWait for resources to be gone (default true)
--timeoutdurationTime to wait before giving up (0 = infinite)
--ignore-not-foundboolDon't error if resource doesn't exist
--nowboolImmediate shutdown (grace period = 1)
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
--field-selectorstringFilter by field (e.g., status.phase=Failed)
-n, --namespacestringNamespace of the resource

Cascade Modes

ModeDescriptionUse Case
backgroundDelete owner, garbage collect dependents in backgroundDefault behavior, delete deployment and pods
foregroundDelete dependents first, then ownerEnsure all pods deleted before deployment
orphanDelete owner only, leave dependents runningKeep pods running after deleting deployment

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Resource(s) deleted successfully (or not found with --ignore-not-found)
1Error occurred (permission denied, timeout, resource not found)

📁 Context: Resource Cleanup Scenarios for anihpj

Cleanup Operations: Various scenarios requiring resource deletion in the anihpj jobpost application lifecycle.
Resource Lifecycle Overview
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj Resource Cleanup Scenarios                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Daily Operations:                                                               │
│  ├── Delete completed/failed migration jobs                                     │
│  ├── Remove stuck pods (ImagePullBackOff, CrashLoopBackOff)                    │
│  └── Clean up debug pods after troubleshooting                                  │
│                                                                                  │
│  Release Management:                                                             │
│  ├── Delete old ReplicaSets after deployment                                    │
│  ├── Remove canary deployments after testing                                    │
│  └── Clean up feature branch namespaces                                         │
│                                                                                  │
│  Environment Teardown:                                                           │
│  ├── Delete entire staging namespace between tests                              │
│  ├── Remove development resources end of sprint                                 │
│  └── Decommission deprecated services                                           │
│                                                                                  │
│  Emergency Operations:                                                           │
│  ├── Force delete stuck terminating pods                                        │
│  ├── Immediate rollback by deleting faulty deployment                          │
│  └── Remove compromised secrets                                                  │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Examples

Example 1: Delete Pods by Label Selector

Scenario: After a failed deployment, remove all pods with the canary label to rollback to the stable version of the jobpost application.

📄 Context - Canary Deployment (k8s/canary/deployment.yaml):

# k8s/canary/deployment.yaml - Failed canary release
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web-canary
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    tier: web
    track: canary
    version: v2.2.0-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      tier: web
      track: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        tier: web
        track: canary
        version: v2.2.0-canary
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.2.0-canary
        # This version has a bug causing 500 errors
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

# Canary traffic split via service mesh or ingress:
# - 90% traffic → track: stable (jobpost-web)
# - 10% traffic → track: canary (jobpost-web-canary)
#
# Monitoring detected:
# - Error rate: 15% (threshold: 1%)
# - Response time: 2.5s (threshold: 500ms)
# - Decision: Rollback canary immediately

Command:

# Delete all canary-track pods and deployments
kubectl delete pods,deployments -n anihpj-production -l track=canary

# Alternative: Delete only the deployment (pods cascade automatically)
kubectl delete deployment jobpost-web-canary -n anihpj-production

Output:

pod "jobpost-web-canary-7f9a8b6c5d-xyz12" deleted
deployment.apps "jobpost-web-canary" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify no canary resources remain
kubectl get all -n anihpj-production -l track=canary

# Confirm only stable pods are serving traffic
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web --show-labels

# Check error rate returned to normal
kubectl exec -n anihpj-production deployment/jobpost-web -- \
  curl -s http://localhost:8000/health/ | jq '.status'
No resources found in anihpj-production namespace.

NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   LABELS
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12     1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,tier=web,track=stable,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45     1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,tier=web,track=stable,version=v2.1.0
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78     1/1     Running   0          4h    app=jobpost,tier=web,track=stable,version=v2.1.0

"healthy"
Example 2: Delete Resources from Manifest Files

Scenario: Remove an entire feature environment that was deployed from manifest files after feature testing is complete.

📄 Context - Feature Branch Deployment (k8s/features/FEAT-1234/):

# k8s/features/FEAT-1234/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: anihpj-feat-1234

resources:
- namespace.yaml
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml
- configmap.yaml
- ingress.yaml

commonLabels:
  feature-branch: FEAT-1234
  team: backend
  
namePrefix: feat-1234-

---
# k8s/features/FEAT-1234/namespace.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-feat-1234
  labels:
    purpose: feature-testing
    feature: FEAT-1234
    auto-cleanup: "true"
    created-by: ci-cd
  annotations:
    janitor/ttl: "7d"  # Auto-delete after 7 days

Command:

# Delete all resources defined in kustomization
kubectl delete -k k8s/features/FEAT-1234/

# Or delete using the namespace (removes everything in it)
kubectl delete namespace anihpj-feat-1234

# Dry-run first to see what will be deleted
kubectl delete -k k8s/features/FEAT-1234/ --dry-run=client

Output:

# Dry-run output:
namespace "anihpj-feat-1234" deleted (dry run)
configmap "feat-1234-django-config" deleted (dry run)
service "feat-1234-jobpost-service" deleted (dry run)
deployment.apps "feat-1234-jobpost-web" deleted (dry run)
ingress.networking.k8s.io "feat-1234-jobpost-ingress" deleted (dry run)

# Actual deletion:
namespace "anihpj-feat-1234" deleted
configmap "feat-1234-django-config" deleted
service "feat-1234-jobpost-service" deleted
deployment.apps "feat-1234-jobpost-web" deleted
ingress.networking.k8s.io "feat-1234-jobpost-ingress" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify namespace is deleted
kubectl get namespace anihpj-feat-1234

# List all feature namespaces remaining
kubectl get namespaces -l purpose=feature-testing

# Check no orphaned resources
kubectl get all -A -l feature-branch=FEAT-1234
Error from server (NotFound): namespaces "anihpj-feat-1234" not found

NAME                   STATUS   AGE
anihpj-feat-1289       Active   3d
anihpj-feat-1301       Active   1d

No resources found
Example 3: Force Delete Stuck Pods

Scenario: A pod is stuck in "Terminating" state for over 10 minutes due to a finalizer or node issue. Force delete it to allow replacement pod to be scheduled.

📄 Context - Stuck Pod Investigation:

# Investigation commands that revealed stuck pod:

$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production
NAME                             READY   STATUS        RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-abc12     1/1     Running       0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-def45     1/1     Running       0          4h
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78     0/1     Terminating   0          4h   # STUCK!
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     1/1     Running       0          10m
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02     1/1     Running       0          10m

$ kubectl describe pod jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78 -n anihpj-production
# Shows: Node affinity conflict, node down, or finalizer blocking

# Possible causes:
# 1. Node is unreachable/down
# 2. Finalizer preventing deletion
# 3. PVC still bound
# 4. PreStop hook hanging

Command:

# Force delete the stuck pod
kubectl delete pod jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78 \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  --force \
  --grace-period=0

# Alternative: Remove finalizers first (if that's the issue)
kubectl patch pod jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78 \
  --namespace=anihpj-production \
  -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}' \
  --type=merge

Output:

Warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely.
pod "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d-ghi78" force deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify pod is gone
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost,tier=web

# Check deployment has correct replica count
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Check for any remaining terminating pods
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production --field-selector=status.phase=Running
NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     1/1     Running   0          12m
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02     1/1     Running   0          12m
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new03     1/1     Running   0          2m  # New pod scheduled

NAME          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           7d

NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new01     1/1     Running   0          12m
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new02     1/1     Running   0          12m
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e-new03     1/1     Running   0          2m
Example 4: Delete Failed Jobs and Completed Pods

Scenario: Clean up completed migration jobs and failed cron job pods that have accumulated over time to free up cluster resources.

📄 Context - Job Cleanup Policy:

# anihpj Job Cleanup Strategy
#
# Problem:
# ────────
# Kubernetes retains completed/failed Jobs and their pods by default.
# Over time, these accumulate and consume etcd storage.
#
# Current state (before cleanup):
# ────────────────────────────────
# - 47 completed migration jobs
# - 12 failed backup jobs  
# - 89 completed cronjob pods
# - Total: ~150 stale resources
#
# Cleanup targets:
# ────────────────
# 1. Jobs with status.succeeded > 0 (completed successfully)
# 2. Jobs with status.failed > 0 (failed, already alerted)
# 3. Pods in phase Succeeded or Failed
#
# Future: Use TTL controller
# ──────────────────────────
# spec:
#   ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600  # Auto-delete after 1 hour

Command:

# Delete completed jobs
kubectl delete jobs -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector=status.successful=1

# Delete failed jobs (after reviewing logs)
kubectl delete jobs -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector=status.failed=1

# Delete pods in Succeeded state
kubectl delete pods -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector=status.phase=Succeeded

# Delete pods in Failed state
kubectl delete pods -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector=status.phase=Failed
