# Combined command for all completed/failed pods
kubectl delete pods -n anihpj-production \
  --field-selector='status.phase!=Running,status.phase!=Pending'

Output:

job.batch "django-migrate-20260301-001" deleted
job.batch "django-migrate-20260228-001" deleted
job.batch "django-collectstatic-20260301" deleted
... (44 more jobs)

job.batch "backup-20260228-failed" deleted
... (11 more failed jobs)

pod "django-migrate-20260301-001-abc12" deleted
pod "cronjob-cleanup-28475693-xyz89" deleted
... (86 more pods)

✅ Verification:

# Count remaining jobs
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-production --no-headers | wc -l

# Count remaining pods by phase
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}' | sort | uniq -c

# Check etcd usage improved (if you have metrics)
kubectl top node
5  # Only active/recent jobs remain

      7 Running
      0 Succeeded
      0 Failed
      1 Pending  # New job just started
Example 5: Delete Namespace with All Resources

Scenario: Decommission the old staging environment after migrating to a new cluster. Delete the entire namespace which removes all resources within it.

📄 Context - Environment Decommission Checklist:

# anihpj Staging Environment Decommission

## Pre-deletion Checklist
- [x] New staging environment verified and operational
- [x] All CI/CD pipelines updated to new environment
- [x] DNS records updated to new staging URLs
- [x] Team notified of decommission date
- [x] Final backup of any persistent data completed
- [x] Secrets exported to vault (if needed elsewhere)

## Resources to be Deleted
Namespace: anihpj-staging-old

Contents:
- 2 Deployments (jobpost-web, jobpost-worker)
- 3 Services (jobpost-service, postgres-service, redis-service)
- 4 ConfigMaps
- 3 Secrets
- 1 PVC (postgres-data)
- 2 ServiceAccounts
- Various RBAC resources

## Deletion Command
```bash
kubectl delete namespace anihpj-staging-old --wait=true --timeout=5m
```

## Post-deletion Verification
- [ ] Namespace removed from cluster
- [ ] Load balancer IPs released
- [ ] PVs reclaimed or deleted
- [ ] No orphaned resources in other namespaces

Command:

# First, inventory what will be deleted
kubectl get all,configmaps,secrets,pvc,serviceaccounts -n anihpj-staging-old

# Delete the namespace (cascades to all resources)
kubectl delete namespace anihpj-staging-old --wait=true --timeout=5m

# If namespace gets stuck, check for finalizers
kubectl get namespace anihpj-staging-old -o json | jq '.spec.finalizers'

Output:

# Inventory:
NAME                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/jobpost-web-5f8a9b7c6d-old01      1/1     Running   0          30d
pod/jobpost-web-5f8a9b7c6d-old02      1/1     Running   0          30d
pod/jobpost-worker-6g9b0c8d7e-old01   1/1     Running   0          30d

NAME                       TYPE           CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP    PORT(S)
service/jobpost-service    LoadBalancer   10.96.50.100   35.200.50.50   80:30080/TCP
service/postgres-service   ClusterIP      10.96.50.101   <none>         5432/TCP
service/redis-service      ClusterIP      10.96.50.102   <none>         6379/TCP

... (more resources)

# Namespace deletion:
namespace "anihpj-staging-old" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify namespace is gone
kubectl get namespace anihpj-staging-old

# Check no resources with old staging labels exist
kubectl get all -A -l environment=staging-old

# Verify PVs are released/deleted
kubectl get pv | grep staging-old

# Check load balancer IP is released (cloud provider specific)
gcloud compute forwarding-rules list --filter="description:anihpj-staging-old"
Error from server (NotFound): namespaces "anihpj-staging-old" not found

No resources found

No resources found

Listed 0 items.
Example 6: Selective Deletion with Dry-Run Preview

Scenario: Safely clean up old ReplicaSets from previous deployments while keeping the current ones, using dry-run to preview before actual deletion.

📄 Context - ReplicaSet Cleanup Strategy:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ReplicaSet Cleanup - jobpost-web                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  Current State:                                                                  │
│  ═════════════                                                                   │
│  Each deployment update creates a new ReplicaSet.                               │
│  By default, Kubernetes keeps 10 old ReplicaSets for rollback.                  │
│                                                                                  │
│  ReplicaSet                          Replicas   Revision   Age                  │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   │
│  jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e (current)    3          10         2h    ← KEEP         │
│  jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d              0          9          1d    ← KEEP recent  │
│  jobpost-web-5c3e7b6a8f              0          8          3d    ← DELETE       │
│  jobpost-web-4b2d6a5e9g              0          7          5d    ← DELETE       │
│  jobpost-web-3a1c5f4d8h              0          6          7d    ← DELETE       │
│  jobpost-web-2z0b4e3c7i              0          5          10d   ← DELETE       │
│  jobpost-web-1y9a3d2b6j              0          4          14d   ← DELETE       │
│                                                                                  │
│  Cleanup Strategy:                                                               │
│  ─────────────────                                                               │
│  1. Keep current (replicas > 0)                                                 │
│  2. Keep 2 most recent for quick rollback                                       │
│  3. Delete ReplicaSets older than 3 days with 0 replicas                        │
│                                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Command:

# Step 1: List all ReplicaSets sorted by creation time
kubectl get replicasets -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost \
  --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp \
  -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,REPLICAS:.spec.replicas,CREATED:.metadata.creationTimestamp'

# Step 2: Dry-run deletion of old ReplicaSets (0 replicas)
kubectl delete replicasets -n anihpj-production \
  -l app=jobpost \
  --field-selector=spec.replicas=0 \
  --dry-run=client

# Step 3: Actually delete (after reviewing dry-run output)
kubectl delete replicasets -n anihpj-production \
  -l app=jobpost \
  --field-selector=spec.replicas=0

# Alternative: Delete specific old ones by name
kubectl delete replicasets -n anihpj-production \
  jobpost-web-5c3e7b6a8f \
  jobpost-web-4b2d6a5e9g \
  jobpost-web-3a1c5f4d8h \
  --dry-run=client

Output:

# Step 1 - Current ReplicaSets:
NAME                        REPLICAS   CREATED
jobpost-web-1y9a3d2b6j      0          2026-02-16T10:00:00Z
jobpost-web-2z0b4e3c7i      0          2026-02-20T10:00:00Z
jobpost-web-3a1c5f4d8h      0          2026-02-23T10:00:00Z
jobpost-web-4b2d6a5e9g      0          2026-02-25T10:00:00Z
jobpost-web-5c3e7b6a8f      0          2026-02-27T10:00:00Z
jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d      0          2026-03-01T08:00:00Z
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e      3          2026-03-02T06:00:00Z  ← Current

# Step 2 - Dry run output:
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-1y9a3d2b6j" deleted (dry run)
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-2z0b4e3c7i" deleted (dry run)
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-3a1c5f4d8h" deleted (dry run)
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-4b2d6a5e9g" deleted (dry run)
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-5c3e7b6a8f" deleted (dry run)
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d" deleted (dry run)

# Step 3 - Actual deletion:
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-1y9a3d2b6j" deleted
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-2z0b4e3c7i" deleted
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-3a1c5f4d8h" deleted
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-4b2d6a5e9g" deleted
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-5c3e7b6a8f" deleted
replicaset.apps "jobpost-web-6d4f8b7c9d" deleted

✅ Verification:

# Verify only current ReplicaSet remains
kubectl get replicasets -n anihpj-production -l app=jobpost

# Confirm deployment still works
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

# Rollback history (note: some revisions no longer available)
kubectl rollout history deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production
NAME                        DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
jobpost-web-7e5g9c8d0e      3         3         3       2h

NAME          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           7d

deployment.apps/jobpost-web
REVISION   CHANGE-CAUSE
10         kubectl set image deployment/jobpost-web django=anihpj/jobpost:v2.2.0

📱 APP MANAGEMENT

Commands for managing application lifecycle, configuration, and updates in your Kubernetes cluster. Includes rolling updates, scaling, and configuration management for the anihpj Django project.

kubectl apply

APPLY Declarative GitOps Configuration

Apply a configuration to a resource by file name or stdin

The kubectl apply command applies a configuration to resources from a file or stdin. It creates resources if they don't exist and updates them if they do, following declarative configuration principles.

For the anihpj Django project, kubectl apply is used for:

  • Deploying Django application manifests
  • Managing ConfigMaps and Secrets
  • Updating infrastructure declaratively
  • GitOps workflows with CI/CD pipelines
  • Kustomize and Helm chart deployments

Syntax

# Apply from file
kubectl apply -f FILENAME

# Apply from directory
kubectl apply -f DIRECTORY/

# Apply from URL
kubectl apply -f URL

# Apply with kustomize
kubectl apply -k DIRECTORY/

# Apply with dry-run
kubectl apply -f FILENAME --dry-run=client

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filename[]stringFiles or directories containing configuration
-k, --kustomizestringKustomize directory path
-R, --recursiveboolProcess directory recursively
--pruneboolDelete resources not in manifest
--selectorstringLabel selector for pruning
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"
--forceboolForce apply (delete and recreate)
--server-sideboolUse server-side apply
--field-managerstringField manager name for server-side apply

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Configuration applied successfully
1Error (invalid YAML, validation failure)

Examples

Example 1: Deploy Complete Django Application Stack

Scenario: Deploy the anihpj Django application with all dependencies from a single manifest file.

📄 Context - k8s/deployment.yaml:

# k8s/deployment.yaml - Complete Django Stack
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    environment: production
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com,www.anihpj.example.com"
  DEBUG: "False"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres"
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-secrets
  namespace: anihpj-production
type: Opaque
stringData:
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-production-key-change-me-xyz123"
  DATABASE_USER: "anihpj_user"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "secure-db-password-456"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    component: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  selector:
    app: jobpost
    component: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  type: ClusterIP

Command:

# Apply the complete stack
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production created
configmap/django-config created
secret/django-secrets created
deployment.apps/jobpost-web created
service/jobpost-web created

✅ Verification:

# Check all resources
kubectl get all -n anihpj-production
NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12   1/1     Running   0          30s
pod/jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-def34   1/1     Running   0          30s
pod/jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-ghi56   1/1     Running   0          30s

NAME                  TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
service/jobpost-web   ClusterIP   10.96.50.10   <none>        80/TCP    30s

NAME                          READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/jobpost-web   3/3     3            3           30s
Example 2: Apply Directory with PostgreSQL and Redis

Scenario: Deploy all manifests from a directory structure for Django with database and cache.

📄 Context - Directory Structure:

k8s/
├── base/
│   ├── namespace.yaml
│   ├── postgres.yaml
│   ├── redis.yaml
│   └── django.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── development/
    └── production/

📄 Context - k8s/base/postgres.yaml:

# k8s/base/postgres.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: standard
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  serviceName: postgres
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15-alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: "anihpj_db"
        - name: POSTGRES_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: DATABASE_USER
        - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: DATABASE_PASSWORD
        volumeMounts:
        - name: postgres-data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
      volumes:
      - name: postgres-data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: postgres-pvc
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    targetPort: 5432
  clusterIP: None

📄 Context - k8s/base/redis.yaml:

# k8s/base/redis.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: redis
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: redis
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: redis
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: redis
        image: redis:7-alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "64Mi"
            cpu: "50m"
          limits:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "100m"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: redis
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  selector:
    app: redis
  ports:
  - port: 6379
    targetPort: 6379

Command:

# Apply all manifests in directory
kubectl apply -f k8s/base/

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production configured
persistentvolumeclaim/postgres-pvc created
statefulset.apps/postgres created
service/postgres created
deployment.apps/redis created
service/redis created
deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured
service/jobpost-web configured

✅ Verification:

# Verify database connectivity
kubectl exec deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production -- \
  python manage.py check --database default
System check identified no issues (0 silenced).
Example 3: Dry-Run Validation Before Production Deploy

Scenario: Validate manifest changes before applying to production cluster.

📄 Context - k8s/production/ingress.yaml:

# k8s/production/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: jobpost-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - anihpj.example.com
    - www.anihpj.example.com
    secretName: anihpj-tls
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 80
      - path: /static
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: static-files
            port:
              number: 80
  - host: www.anihpj.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 80

Command:

# Client-side dry-run (validates YAML syntax)
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/ingress.yaml --dry-run=client

# Server-side dry-run (validates against API server)
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/ingress.yaml --dry-run=server

Output:

# Client-side
ingress.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-ingress created (dry run)

# Server-side
ingress.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-ingress created (server dry run)

✅ Verification:

# Compare with existing - show what would change
kubectl diff -f k8s/production/ingress.yaml
Example 4: Server-Side Apply for Multi-Team Management

Scenario: Use server-side apply with field managers when multiple teams manage the same resources.

📄 Context - CI/CD Pipeline (GitOps):

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Kubernetes
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'k8s/**'
      - 'src/**'

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v4
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      
      - name: Deploy with Server-Side Apply
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/production/ \
            --server-side \
            --field-manager=github-actions \
            --force-conflicts

📄 Context - k8s/production/hpa.yaml (managed by platform team):

# k8s/production/hpa.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: jobpost-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: jobpost-web
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80

Command:

# App team applies deployment
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/deployment.yaml \
  --server-side \
  --field-manager=app-team

# Platform team applies HPA
kubectl apply -f k8s/production/hpa.yaml \
  --server-side \
  --field-manager=platform-team

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web serverside-applied
horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-hpa serverside-applied

✅ Verification:

# Check field managers
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.managedFields[*].manager}'
app-team platform-team
Example 5: Apply with Pruning for GitOps Sync

Scenario: Sync cluster state with Git repository, removing resources that no longer exist in manifests.

📄 Context - ArgoCD-style GitOps Sync:

# k8s/production/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: anihpj-production

commonLabels:
  app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: kubectl-apply
  app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj

resources:
  - namespace.yaml
  - configmap.yaml
  - secrets.yaml
  - deployment.yaml
  - service.yaml
  - ingress.yaml
  - hpa.yaml

images:
  - name: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost
    newTag: v1.3.0

Command:

# Apply with pruning - removes resources not in manifests
kubectl apply -k k8s/production/ \
  --prune \
  --selector=app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj \
  --all

Output:

namespace/anihpj-production configured
configmap/django-config configured
secret/django-secrets configured
deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured
service/jobpost-web configured
ingress.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-ingress configured
horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-hpa configured
configmap/old-config pruned
deployment.apps/old-worker pruned

✅ Verification:

# Verify only expected resources remain
kubectl get all,configmap,secret,ingress,hpa \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=anihpj
Example 6: Force Apply After Manual Changes

Scenario: Force apply when someone made manual changes that conflict with declarative config.

📄 Context - Resolving Configuration Drift:

# Someone manually scaled the deployment
kubectl scale deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production --replicas=5

# Now the manifest says replicas: 3 but cluster has 5
# This creates a conflict with declarative management

📄 Context - k8s/deployment.yaml (source of truth):

# k8s/deployment.yaml - Always 3 replicas in Git
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3  # <-- Source of truth
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    # ... container spec

Command:

# Force apply to override manual changes
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml --force --grace-period=0

# Or use server-side apply with force-conflicts
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml \
  --server-side \
  --force-conflicts

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured

✅ Verification:

# Verify replicas back to 3
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'
3

kubectl apply edit-last-applied

EDIT Configuration History

Edit the last-applied-configuration annotation of resources

The kubectl apply edit-last-applied command opens an editor to modify the last-applied-configuration annotation. This annotation stores the configuration that was last applied to the resource.

For the anihpj Django project, this command is used for:

  • Fixing corrupted last-applied annotations
  • Resolving three-way merge conflicts
  • Updating metadata for proper diff calculations

Syntax

# Edit last-applied for a resource
kubectl apply edit-last-applied TYPE NAME

# Edit from file reference
kubectl apply edit-last-applied -f FILENAME

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filename[]stringResource file reference
--windows-line-endingsboolUse Windows line endings

Examples

Example 1: Edit Deployment Last-Applied Config

Scenario: Fix the last-applied annotation for a deployment that was modified manually.

📄 Context - Current Deployment State:

# Check current last-applied annotation
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.kubectl\.kubernetes\.io/last-applied-configuration}' | jq .
{
  "apiVersion": "apps/v1",
  "kind": "Deployment",
  "metadata": {
    "name": "jobpost-web",
    "namespace": "anihpj-production"
  },
  "spec": {
    "replicas": 2,
    "template": {
      "spec": {
        "containers": [{
          "image": "ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0"
        }]
      }
    }
  }
}

Command:

# Edit last-applied (opens $EDITOR)
kubectl apply edit-last-applied deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

Output (in editor, update values):

# Edit this YAML, then save and close
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3  # <-- Update to match current desired state
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0  # <-- Update version

✅ Verification:

kubectl apply edit-last-applied deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production
deployment.apps/jobpost-web edited
Example 2: Fix ConfigMap Annotation

Scenario: Update ConfigMap last-applied after direct edits.

📄 Context - ConfigMap with stale annotation:

# The ConfigMap was edited directly but annotation is old
# This causes issues with kubectl apply diff calculations

Command:

kubectl apply edit-last-applied configmap/django-config -n anihpj-production

Output:

configmap/django-config edited
Example 3: Edit Using File Reference

Scenario: Use file reference to identify the resource.

📄 Context - k8s/service.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000

Command:

kubectl apply edit-last-applied -f k8s/service.yaml

Output:

service/jobpost-web edited
Example 4: Resolve Three-Way Merge Issue

Scenario: Fix three-way merge conflicts by correcting last-applied annotation.

📄 Context - Merge Conflict Scenario:

# Three-way merge uses:
# 1. Original (last-applied annotation)
# 2. Current (live cluster state)
# 3. Modified (new manifest being applied)

# If last-applied is wrong, merge calculations fail

Command:

# Edit to match what Git says should be the "original"
KUBE_EDITOR="code --wait" kubectl apply edit-last-applied \
  deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web edited
Example 5: Edit Secret Annotation

Scenario: Update last-applied for a Secret that was modified.

📄 Context - Secret Management:

# Secret was updated via kubectl edit, annotation is stale
kubectl get secret django-secrets -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq .

Command:

# Edit last-applied annotation
kubectl apply edit-last-applied secret/django-secrets -n anihpj-production

Output:

secret/django-secrets edited

kubectl apply set-last-applied

SET Configuration Annotation

Set the last-applied-configuration annotation on a live resource

The kubectl apply set-last-applied command sets the kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration annotation on a live resource to match a file. This is useful for adopting resources into declarative management.

Syntax

# Set last-applied from file
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f FILENAME

# Create annotation if missing
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f FILENAME --create-annotation

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filename[]stringConfiguration file
--create-annotationboolCreate annotation if missing
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Examples

Example 1: Adopt Imperatively Created Resources

Scenario: Add last-applied annotation to resources created with kubectl create.

📄 Context - Adopting existing resources:

# Resource was created imperatively
kubectl create deployment jobpost-worker \
  --image=ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0 \
  -n anihpj-production

# It has no last-applied annotation
kubectl get deployment jobpost-worker -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
# Returns: null or empty

📄 Context - k8s/worker-deployment.yaml (new declarative config):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    component: worker
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "info"]
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets

Command:

# Set last-applied annotation
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f k8s/worker-deployment.yaml \
  --create-annotation

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-worker configured

✅ Verification:

# Verify annotation exists
kubectl get deployment jobpost-worker -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.kubectl\.kubernetes\.io/last-applied-configuration}' | jq -r '.spec.replicas'
2
Example 2: Adopt Helm-Managed Resources

Scenario: Migrate from Helm to pure kubectl apply management.

📄 Context - Helm to kubectl Migration:

# Previously deployed with Helm
helm install anihpj ./helm/anihpj

# Now want to manage with kubectl apply
# Export current config and set annotation

📄 Context - Exported k8s/service.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost

Command:

# Set last-applied from exported manifest
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f k8s/service.yaml --create-annotation

Output:

service/jobpost-web configured
Example 3: Batch Set for Multiple Resources

Scenario: Set last-applied for all resources in a directory.

📄 Context - Directory with exported manifests:

k8s/adopted/
├── deployment.yaml
├── service.yaml
├── configmap.yaml
└── secret.yaml

Command:

# Set last-applied for all files in directory
for f in k8s/adopted/*.yaml; do
  kubectl apply set-last-applied -f "$f" --create-annotation
done

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured
service/jobpost-web configured
configmap/django-config configured
secret/django-secrets configured
Example 4: Dry-Run Before Setting

Scenario: Preview what would be set before actually modifying.

📄 Context - k8s/deployment.yaml:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3

Command:

# Dry-run first
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f k8s/deployment.yaml \
  --create-annotation \
  --dry-run=client

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured (dry run)
Example 5: Reset After Breaking Change

Scenario: Reset last-applied after a bad apply corrupted the annotation.

📄 Context - Known good configuration:

# Get known good config from Git
git show main:k8s/deployment.yaml > /tmp/good-deployment.yaml

Command:

# Reset last-applied to known good state
kubectl apply set-last-applied -f /tmp/good-deployment.yaml

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web configured

kubectl apply view-last-applied

VIEW Configuration History

View the last-applied-configuration annotation of resources

The kubectl apply view-last-applied command displays the configuration that was last applied to a resource. This is stored in the kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration annotation.

Syntax

# View last-applied for a resource
kubectl apply view-last-applied TYPE NAME

# View from file reference
kubectl apply view-last-applied -f FILENAME

# Output as YAML or JSON
kubectl apply view-last-applied TYPE NAME -o yaml

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filename[]stringResource file reference
-o, --outputstringOutput format (yaml, json)

Examples

Example 1: View Deployment Last-Applied

Scenario: Check what configuration was last applied to the deployment.

📄 Context - Debugging configuration drift:

# Production deployment seems different from Git
# Check what was actually applied last

Command:

kubectl apply view-last-applied deployment/jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production

Output:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: jobpost
    component: web
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        name: django
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
Example 2: Compare with Git Version

Scenario: Compare last-applied with current Git manifest.

📄 Context - CI/CD drift detection:

# Check if cluster matches Git

Command:

# View and compare
kubectl apply view-last-applied deployment/jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production > /tmp/cluster-config.yaml

diff k8s/deployment.yaml /tmp/cluster-config.yaml

Output:

5c5
<   replicas: 5
---
>   replicas: 3
Example 3: View ConfigMap Configuration

Scenario: Check what Django settings were last applied.

📄 Context - Debugging Django configuration:

# App is behaving unexpectedly
# Check what config was applied

Command:

kubectl apply view-last-applied configmap/django-config \
  -n anihpj-production -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: anihpj.settings
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: anihpj.example.com
  DEBUG: "False"
  DATABASE_HOST: postgres
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
Example 4: Export for Backup

Scenario: Export last-applied configurations for disaster recovery.

📄 Context - Backup procedure:

# Create backup of all applied configs
mkdir -p backups/$(date +%Y%m%d)

Command:

# Export all deployments' last-applied
for deploy in $(kubectl get deployments -n anihpj-production -o name); do
  name=$(basename $deploy)
  kubectl apply view-last-applied $deploy -n anihpj-production \
    > backups/$(date +%Y%m%d)/${name}.yaml 2>/dev/null || true
done

Output:

backups/20260302/
├── jobpost-web.yaml
├── jobpost-worker.yaml
└── redis.yaml
Example 5: View Using File Reference

Scenario: Use file to identify which resource to view.

📄 Context - k8s/service.yaml:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production

Command:

kubectl apply view-last-applied -f k8s/service.yaml

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: jobpost
  type: ClusterIP

kubectl annotate

UPDATE Metadata Annotations Configuration

Update the annotations on a resource

The kubectl annotate command updates annotations on Kubernetes resources. Annotations are used to attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata to objects.

For the anihpj Django project, annotations are used for:

  • Ingress configurations (SSL, proxy settings)
  • Deployment tracking (Git commit, CI run ID)
  • Load balancer configurations
  • Monitoring and alerting metadata
  • Custom controller configurations

Syntax

# Add or update annotation
kubectl annotate TYPE NAME KEY=VALUE

# Remove annotation (use minus sign)
kubectl annotate TYPE NAME KEY-

# Overwrite existing annotation
kubectl annotate TYPE NAME KEY=VALUE --overwrite

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--overwriteboolAllow overwriting existing annotations
--allboolSelect all resources in namespace
-l, --selectorstringLabel selector to filter resources
--resource-versionstringRequired resource version for update
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Annotation updated successfully
1Error (resource not found, conflict)

Examples

Example 1: Add Deployment Tracking Annotations

Scenario: Track Git commit and CI pipeline info on deployments for audit trail.

📄 Context - CI/CD Pipeline (.github/workflows/deploy.yaml):

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Production
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/production/
          
          # Add tracking annotations
          kubectl annotate deployment/jobpost-web \
            -n anihpj-production \
            kubernetes.io/change-cause="Deploy ${{ github.sha }}" \
            app.anihpj.com/git-commit="${{ github.sha }}" \
            app.anihpj.com/git-branch="${{ github.ref_name }}" \
            app.anihpj.com/ci-run="${{ github.run_id }}" \
            app.anihpj.com/deployed-by="${{ github.actor }}" \
            app.anihpj.com/deployed-at="$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
            --overwrite

Command:

# Add deployment tracking annotations
kubectl annotate deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Deploy commit abc123" \
  app.anihpj.com/git-commit="abc123def456" \
  app.anihpj.com/git-branch="main" \
  app.anihpj.com/ci-run="12345678" \
  app.anihpj.com/deployed-by="github-actions" \
  app.anihpj.com/deployed-at="2026-03-02T10:30:00Z" \
  --overwrite

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web annotated

✅ Verification:

# View annotations
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq .
{
  "kubernetes.io/change-cause": "Deploy commit abc123",
  "app.anihpj.com/git-commit": "abc123def456",
  "app.anihpj.com/git-branch": "main",
  "app.anihpj.com/ci-run": "12345678",
  "app.anihpj.com/deployed-by": "github-actions",
  "app.anihpj.com/deployed-at": "2026-03-02T10:30:00Z"
}
Example 2: Configure NGINX Ingress Annotations

Scenario: Add NGINX ingress annotations for Django app routing.

📄 Context - k8s/ingress.yaml (base):

# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: jobpost-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: jobpost-web
            port:
              number: 80

Command:

# Add NGINX-specific annotations
kubectl annotate ingress/jobpost-ingress -n anihpj-production \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect="true" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size="50m" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-connect-timeout="60" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout="60" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-send-timeout="60" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-cors="true" \
  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/cors-allow-origin="https://anihpj.example.com"

Output:

ingress.networking.k8s.io/jobpost-ingress annotated

✅ Verification:

# Test upload with large file
curl -X POST -F "file=@large-resume.pdf" \
  https://anihpj.example.com/api/upload/
Example 3: Configure AWS Load Balancer Annotations

Scenario: Add AWS ALB annotations for production service.

📄 Context - Service with LoadBalancer type:

# Need to configure AWS ALB for the service
# Annotations control ALB behavior

Command:

# Configure AWS Application Load Balancer
kubectl annotate service/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type="nlb" \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-cross-zone-load-balancing-enabled="true" \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-cert="arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789:certificate/abc-123" \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-ports="443" \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol="http" \
  service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-healthcheck-path="/health/"

Output:

service/jobpost-web annotated

✅ Verification:

# Check AWS console or describe service
kubectl describe service/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production | grep -A5 "Annotations"
Example 4: Add Prometheus Monitoring Annotations

Scenario: Configure Prometheus scraping for Django metrics.

📄 Context - Django with prometheus-client:

# anihpj/urls.py
from django_prometheus import exports

urlpatterns = [
    # ... other urls
    path('metrics/', exports.ExportToDjangoView, name='prometheus-metrics'),
]

Command:

# Add Prometheus scrape annotations to pods via deployment
kubectl annotate deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  prometheus.io/scrape="true" \
  prometheus.io/port="8000" \
  prometheus.io/path="/metrics/" \
  --overwrite

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web annotated

✅ Verification:

# Check Prometheus targets
curl -s http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/targets | \
  jq '.data.activeTargets[] | select(.labels.job=="kubernetes-pods")'
Example 5: Remove Annotation

Scenario: Remove an annotation that's no longer needed.

📄 Context - Cleaning up deprecated annotations:

# Old annotation causing issues
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.deprecated\.io/old-setting}'

Command:

# Remove annotation using minus sign suffix
kubectl annotate deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  deprecated.io/old-setting-

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web annotated

✅ Verification:

# Confirm annotation removed
kubectl get deployment jobpost-web -n anihpj-production \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.deprecated\.io/old-setting}'
# Returns empty
Example 6: Batch Annotate Multiple Resources

Scenario: Add cost tracking annotations to all deployments.

📄 Context - Cost allocation tracking:

# Finance requires cost center tracking on all resources
# Add annotations for cost allocation

Command:

# Annotate all deployments in namespace
kubectl annotate deployments --all -n anihpj-production \
  cost.anihpj.com/cost-center="engineering" \
  cost.anihpj.com/team="backend" \
  cost.anihpj.com/project="jobpost"

Output:

deployment.apps/jobpost-web annotated
deployment.apps/jobpost-worker annotated
deployment.apps/redis annotated

kubectl autoscale

CREATE Scaling HPA Auto-scaling

Auto-scale a deployment, replica set, stateful set, or replication controller

The kubectl autoscale command creates a HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) that automatically scales the number of pods based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics.

For the anihpj Django project, autoscaling is used for:

  • Handling traffic spikes during job posting peaks
  • Cost optimization by scaling down during low traffic
  • Ensuring availability during high-demand periods
  • Automated capacity management

Syntax

# Autoscale a deployment
kubectl autoscale deployment NAME --min=MIN --max=MAX --cpu-percent=PERCENT

# Autoscale a statefulset
kubectl autoscale statefulset NAME --min=MIN --max=MAX

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--minintMinimum number of pods (default 1)
--maxintMaximum number of pods (required)
--cpu-percentintTarget CPU utilization (default 80)
--namestringName for the HPA resource
--dry-runstring"none", "server", or "client"

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0HPA created successfully
1Error (invalid target, HPA exists)

Examples

Example 1: Basic CPU-Based Autoscaling

Scenario: Create HPA for Django web deployment with CPU-based scaling.

📄 Context - k8s/deployment.yaml (requires resource requests):

# k8s/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "250m"      # Required for HPA!
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"

Command:

# Create HPA with CPU-based autoscaling
kubectl autoscale deployment jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production \
  --min=3 \
  --max=10 \
  --cpu-percent=70

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-web autoscaled

✅ Verification:

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa jobpost-web -n anihpj-production
NAME          REFERENCE                TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
jobpost-web   Deployment/jobpost-web   25%/70%   3         10        3          30s
Example 2: Autoscale Celery Worker

Scenario: Scale Celery workers based on CPU usage for background job processing.

📄 Context - k8s/worker-deployment.yaml:

# k8s/worker-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-worker
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "info", "-c", "4"]
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1Gi"
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-config
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets

Command:

# Autoscale workers with higher threshold
kubectl autoscale deployment jobpost-worker \
  -n anihpj-production \
  --min=2 \
  --max=20 \
  --cpu-percent=60

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-worker autoscaled

✅ Verification:

# Monitor scaling during job processing
kubectl get hpa jobpost-worker -n anihpj-production -w
NAME             REFERENCE                   TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
jobpost-worker   Deployment/jobpost-worker   45%/60%   2         20        2          1m
jobpost-worker   Deployment/jobpost-worker   75%/60%   2         20        4          2m
jobpost-worker   Deployment/jobpost-worker   58%/60%   2         20        4          3m
Example 3: Autoscale with Custom Name

Scenario: Create HPA with custom name for better organization.

📄 Context - Multiple environments:

# Different HPA configurations per environment
# Production: aggressive scaling
# Staging: conservative scaling

Command:

# Create HPA with custom name
kubectl autoscale deployment jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production \
  --name=jobpost-web-production-hpa \
  --min=5 \
  --max=50 \
  --cpu-percent=65

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-web-production-hpa autoscaled
Example 4: Generate HPA YAML for GitOps

Scenario: Generate HPA manifest for version control.

📄 Context - GitOps workflow:

# Generate HPA manifest to commit to Git
# for declarative management

Command:

# Generate YAML
kubectl autoscale deployment jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-production \
  --min=3 \
  --max=10 \
  --cpu-percent=70 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: autoscaling/v1
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  maxReplicas: 10
  minReplicas: 3
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: jobpost-web
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
Example 5: Autoscale StatefulSet

Scenario: Autoscale a PostgreSQL read replica StatefulSet.

📄 Context - k8s/postgres-replicas.yaml:

# k8s/postgres-replicas.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres-replica
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-replica
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
      role: replica
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
        role: replica
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15-alpine
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"

Command:

# Autoscale read replicas
kubectl autoscale statefulset postgres-replica \
  -n anihpj-production \
  --min=2 \
  --max=5 \
  --cpu-percent=75

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/postgres-replica autoscaled
Example 6: Development Environment Minimal Scaling

Scenario: Configure minimal autoscaling for development cluster.

📄 Context - Development cost optimization:

# Development environment
# Scale down aggressively to save costs
# But still test autoscaling behavior

Command:

# Minimal scaling for dev
kubectl autoscale deployment jobpost-web \
  -n anihpj-dev \
  --min=1 \
  --max=3 \
  --cpu-percent=80

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/jobpost-web autoscaled

✅ Verification:

# Watch scaling behavior
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-dev -w

kubectl debug

CREATE Debugging Troubleshooting Ephemeral Containers

Create debugging sessions for troubleshooting workloads and nodes

The kubectl debug command creates debugging sessions using ephemeral containers or pod copies. It allows you to attach debugging tools to running pods without modifying the original container.

For the anihpj Django project, debugging is used for:

  • Troubleshooting Django application issues in production
  • Inspecting distroless container file systems
  • Network debugging between services
  • Node-level troubleshooting
  • Analyzing crashed or CrashLoopBackOff pods

Syntax

# Debug with ephemeral container
kubectl debug POD_NAME -it --image=IMAGE

# Debug by copying pod
kubectl debug POD_NAME -it --copy-to=NEW_POD --image=IMAGE

# Debug node
kubectl debug node/NODE_NAME -it --image=IMAGE

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
--imagestringDebug container image
--copy-tostringCreate copy of pod with new name
--containerstringTarget container to debug
--targetstringTarget container for process sharing
--share-processesboolShare process namespace
-itboolInteractive terminal

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0Debug session completed
1Error (pod not found, image pull failed)

Examples

Example 1: Debug Django Pod with Python Shell

Scenario: Add ephemeral debug container to running Django pod for live debugging.

📄 Context - Production pod structure:

# Current production pod (distroless image)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
    # Distroless - no shell, no debugging tools

Command:

# Add ephemeral debug container with Python
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12 \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --image=python:3.11-slim \
  --target=django

Output:

Targeting container "django". If you don't see processes from this container it may be because the container runtime doesn't support this feature.
Defaulting debug container name to debugger-abc12.
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
root@jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12:/#

✅ Debugging Session:

# Inside debug container - Django debugging
root@jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12:/# pip install django ipython
root@jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12:/# python
>>> import os
>>> os.environ.get('DATABASE_HOST')
'postgres'
>>> # Can access same environment
Example 2: Debug CrashLoopBackOff Pod

Scenario: Debug a pod that keeps crashing before you can exec into it.

📄 Context - Crashing Django pod:

# Pod status showing crash loop
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-production
NAME                           READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE
jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-crash   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          3m

Command:

# Create copy of crashing pod with different command
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-crash \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --copy-to=jobpost-debug \
  --container=django \
  -- sh -c "sleep infinity"

Output:

pod/jobpost-debug created
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
/ #

✅ Debugging Session:

# Investigate crash cause
/ # cat /app/logs/django.log
ERROR 2026-03-02 ImportError: No module named 'missing_package'

/ # pip list | grep -i django
Django==5.2
Example 3: Network Debugging Between Services

Scenario: Debug network connectivity issues between Django and PostgreSQL.

📄 Context - Django can't connect to database:

# Django logs show connection refused
kubectl logs deployment/jobpost-web -n anihpj-production | tail -5
django.db.utils.OperationalError: could not connect to server: Connection refused

Command:

# Debug with network tools
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12 \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --image=nicolaka/netshoot \
  --target=django

Output:

netshoot:~#

✅ Network Debugging:

# Test DNS resolution
netshoot:~# nslookup postgres
Server:    10.96.0.10
Address 1: 10.96.0.10 kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

Name:      postgres
Address 1: 10.96.45.20 postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local

# Test TCP connectivity
netshoot:~# nc -zv postgres 5432
postgres (10.96.45.20:5432) open

# DNS works, port open - issue might be authentication
Example 4: Debug Node Issues

Scenario: Debug node-level issues affecting Django pod scheduling.

📄 Context - Node showing NotReady:

# Node status
kubectl get nodes
NAME      STATUS     ROLES    AGE   VERSION
node-1    Ready      worker   30d   v1.29.0
node-2    NotReady   worker   30d   v1.29.0  # Problem node

Command:

# Debug the node
kubectl debug node/node-2 \
  -it \
  --image=ubuntu:22.04

Output:

Creating debugging pod node-debugger-node-2-abc12 with container debugger on node node-2.
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
root@node-2:/#

✅ Node Debugging:

# Check node filesystem (host root mounted at /host)
root@node-2:/# chroot /host
root@node-2:/# systemctl status kubelet
● kubelet.service - kubelet: The Kubernetes Node Agent
   Active: active (running)

root@node-2:/# df -h
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1       100G   95G    5G  95% /  # Disk full!

root@node-2:/# journalctl -u kubelet | tail -20
# Check kubelet logs
Example 5: Debug with Shared Process Namespace

Scenario: Debug Django processes by sharing the process namespace.

📄 Context - Need to see Django processes:

# Want to inspect gunicorn workers and memory usage
# Need to see processes from the target container

Command:

# Debug with process sharing
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12 \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --image=busybox:1.36 \
  --share-processes \
  --target=django

Output:

Targeting container "django".
/ #

✅ Process Debugging:

# See all processes including target container
/ # ps aux
PID   USER     TIME  COMMAND
    1 root      0:00 /pause
    7 root      0:05 gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
   25 root      0:02 gunicorn: worker [anihpj.wsgi:application]
   26 root      0:02 gunicorn: worker [anihpj.wsgi:application]
   45 root      0:00 sh
   52 root      0:00 ps aux

# Check memory of specific process
/ # cat /proc/25/status | grep -i vmrss
VmRSS:    125432 kB
Example 6: Debug with Custom Profile

Scenario: Use a custom debug profile for security-restricted environments.

📄 Context - Security-restricted cluster:

# Cluster has PodSecurityPolicies
# Need appropriate security context for debug pod

Command:

# Debug with baseline profile
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12 \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --image=python:3.11-slim \
  --profile=baseline \
  --copy-to=jobpost-debug-secure

# Or with restricted profile (most secure)
kubectl debug jobpost-web-7d9f8c6b5d-abc12 \
  -n anihpj-production \
  -it \
  --image=python:3.11-slim \
  --profile=restricted \
  --copy-to=jobpost-debug-restricted

Output:

pod/jobpost-debug-secure created
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
$

kubectl diff

COMPARE Validation Preview GitOps

Diff the live version against a would-be applied version

The kubectl diff command shows the differences between the current state of resources in the cluster and the state that would result from applying a configuration file.

For the anihpj Django project, diff is used for:

  • Pre-deployment validation in CI/CD pipelines
  • Detecting configuration drift
  • Code review for infrastructure changes
  • Safe preview before applying changes

Syntax

# Diff a file
kubectl diff -f FILENAME

# Diff a directory
kubectl diff -f DIRECTORY/

# Diff with kustomize
kubectl diff -k DIRECTORY/

Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
-f, --filename[]stringFiles or directories to diff
-k, --kustomizestringKustomize directory
-R, --recursiveboolProcess directories recursively
--server-sideboolUse server-side diff
--field-managerstringField manager name

Return Value

Exit CodeDescription
0No differences found
1Differences found (or error)

Examples

Example 1: Pre-Deployment Review

Scenario: Review changes before deploying updated Django configuration.

📄 Context - k8s/deployment.yaml (updated):

# k8s/deployment.yaml - Updated version
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 5  # Changed from 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.3.0  # Updated version
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"  # Increased from 256Mi
            cpu: "500m"      # Increased from 250m

Command:

# Preview changes before apply
kubectl diff -f k8s/deployment.yaml

Output:

diff -u -N /tmp/LIVE-123/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-web /tmp/MERGED-123/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-web
--- /tmp/LIVE-123/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-web
+++ /tmp/MERGED-123/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-web
@@ -6,7 +6,7 @@
   namespace: anihpj-production
 spec:
-  replicas: 3
+  replicas: 5
   selector:
     matchLabels:
       app: jobpost
@@ -18,11 +18,11 @@
       containers:
       - name: django
-        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.2.0
+        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.3.0
         resources:
           requests:
-            memory: "256Mi"
-            cpu: "250m"
+            memory: "512Mi"
+            cpu: "500m"

✅ Then apply if satisfied:

kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
Example 2: CI/CD Pipeline Drift Detection

Scenario: Detect if cluster state has drifted from Git repository.

📄 Context - .github/workflows/drift-check.yaml:

# .github/workflows/drift-check.yaml
name: Drift Detection
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 */6 * * *'  # Every 6 hours
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  check-drift:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v4
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      
      - name: Check for drift
        run: |
          if kubectl diff -f k8s/production/ > /dev/null 2>&1; then
            echo "✅ No drift detected"
          else
            echo "⚠️ Configuration drift detected!"
            kubectl diff -f k8s/production/
            exit 1
          fi

Command:

# Check entire directory for drift
kubectl diff -f k8s/production/ -R

Output (drift detected):

diff -u -N /tmp/LIVE-456/v1.ConfigMap.anihpj-production.django-config ...
--- /tmp/LIVE-456/v1.ConfigMap.anihpj-production.django-config
+++ /tmp/MERGED-456/v1.ConfigMap.anihpj-production.django-config
@@ -8,4 +8,4 @@
 data:
   DEBUG: "False"
-  LOG_LEVEL: "WARNING"  # Someone changed this manually!
+  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
Example 3: Kustomize Diff

Scenario: Diff kustomize overlay before deployment.

📄 Context - k8s/overlays/production/kustomization.yaml:

# k8s/overlays/production/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: anihpj-production

resources:
  - ../../base

images:
  - name: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost
    newTag: v1.3.0

replicas:
  - name: jobpost-web
    count: 5

patches:
  - path: production-resources.yaml

📄 Context - k8s/overlays/production/production-resources.yaml:

# Production resource patch
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-web
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"

Command:

# Diff kustomize overlay
kubectl diff -k k8s/overlays/production/

Output:

diff -u -N /tmp/LIVE-789/apps.v1.Deployment...
@@ -21,10 +21,10 @@
         resources:
           requests:
-            memory: "512Mi"
-            cpu: "500m"
+            memory: "1Gi"
+            cpu: "1000m"
           limits:
-            memory: "1Gi"
-            cpu: "1000m"
+            memory: "2Gi"
+            cpu: "2000m"
Example 4: Diff New Resources

Scenario: Preview what new resources will be created.

📄 Context - k8s/new-worker.yaml (new file):

# k8s/new-worker.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: jobpost-scheduler
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost
      component: scheduler
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost
        component: scheduler
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: scheduler
        image: ghcr.io/anihpj/jobpost:v1.3.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "beat", "-l", "info"]

Command:

# Diff shows entire resource as addition
kubectl diff -f k8s/new-worker.yaml

Output:

diff -u -N /tmp/LIVE-abc/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-scheduler /tmp/MERGED-abc/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-scheduler
--- /tmp/LIVE-abc/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-scheduler
+++ /tmp/MERGED-abc/apps.v1.Deployment.anihpj-production.jobpost-scheduler
@@ -0,0 +1,25 @@
+apiVersion: apps/v1
+kind: Deployment
+metadata:
+  name: jobpost-scheduler
+  namespace: anihpj-production
+... (entire resource shown as addition)
Example 5: Server-Side Diff

Scenario: Use server-side diff for more accurate comparison.

📄 Context - Server-side apply workflow:

# When using server-side apply, use server-side diff
# This respects field managers and gives accurate results

Command:

# Server-side diff with field manager
kubectl diff -f k8s/deployment.yaml \
  --server-side \
  --field-manager=github-actions

Output:

diff -u -N ...
# Shows only fields managed by 'github-actions'
Example 6: Diff with Exit Code for Scripts

Scenario: Use diff exit code in deployment scripts.

📄 Context - deploy.sh script:

#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh - Safe deployment script

set -e

echo "🔍 Checking for changes..."
if kubectl diff -f k8s/production/ > /tmp/diff-output.txt 2>&1; then
    echo "✅ No changes to apply"
    exit 0
fi

echo "📋 Changes detected:"
cat /tmp/diff-output.txt

read -p "Apply these changes? (y/N) " confirm
if [[ "$confirm" =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    kubectl apply -f k8s/production/
    echo "✅ Changes applied"
else
    echo "❌ Deployment cancelled"
    exit 1
fi

Command:

./deploy.sh

Output:

🔍 Checking for changes...
📋 Changes detected:
diff -u -N ...
-  replicas: 3
+  replicas: 5

Apply these changes? (y/N) y
✅ Changes applied

kubectl edit

INTERACTIVE EDIT MODIFY

Edit resources directly on the server using your preferred editor

The kubectl edit command opens a resource in your default editor, allowing you to modify it directly. When you save and close the editor, kubectl applies the changes immediately to the cluster. This is useful for quick fixes and debugging but should be used sparingly in production - prefer declarative configuration management with version-controlled manifests.

Syntax

kubectl edit (RESOURCE/NAME | -f FILENAME) [options]

Parameters

  • RESOURCE/NAME: Type and name of resource to edit (e.g., deployment/myapp)
  • -f, --filename: Filename, directory, or URL to identify the resource
  • -o, --output: Output format for the edited resource (yaml or json)
  • --save-config: Save current configuration to annotation
  • --windows-line-endings: Use Windows line-endings (CRLF) for editing

Return Value

Opens the resource in an editor; upon save, applies changes and returns a confirmation message. If validation fails, the editor reopens with error annotations.

Examples

Example 1: Edit Deployment Replicas and Resource Limits

Quickly adjust a deployment's replica count and resource allocation for a traffic spike. Useful for emergency scaling before proper HPA is configured.

Context - Current Deployment (production/django-api.yaml):

# Current production deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: django-api
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.3.1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "config.settings.production"
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: database-url

Command:

# Set your preferred editor (optional - defaults to vi/vim)
export KUBE_EDITOR="nano"

# Edit the deployment
kubectl edit deployment/django-api -n production

Editor Opens With Resource - Make These Changes:

# The editor shows the full resource; modify these sections:
spec:
  replicas: 5  # Changed from 3 to 5 for traffic spike
  # ... (selector section stays the same)
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"   # Increased from 256Mi
            cpu: "500m"       # Increased from 250m
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"     # Increased from 512Mi
            cpu: "1000m"      # Increased from 500m

Output (after saving and closing editor):

deployment.apps/django-api edited

Verify Changes:

kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o wide
# NAME         READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE   CONTAINERS   IMAGES
# django-api   5/5     5            5           24d   django       myregistry/django-api:v2.3.1
Example 2: Edit ConfigMap for Application Settings

Update application configuration stored in ConfigMap without redeploying. Pods may need restart to pick up changes depending on how they mount the ConfigMap.

Context - ConfigMap (configs/django-settings-configmap.yaml):

# Django application settings ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-settings
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: django-api
    config-type: application
data:
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "api.example.com,www.example.com"
  LOG_LEVEL: "WARNING"
  CACHE_TIMEOUT: "300"
  RATE_LIMIT_REQUESTS: "100"
  RATE_LIMIT_PERIOD: "60"
  FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD: "false"
  MAINTENANCE_MODE: "false"

Context - Deployment Using ConfigMap:

# How the deployment references this ConfigMap
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-settings

Command:

# Edit ConfigMap to enable feature flag and increase rate limits
kubectl edit configmap/django-settings -n production

Changes Made in Editor:

data:
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "api.example.com,www.example.com,staging.example.com"  # Added staging
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"          # Changed from WARNING for debugging
  CACHE_TIMEOUT: "300"
  RATE_LIMIT_REQUESTS: "200" # Increased from 100
  RATE_LIMIT_PERIOD: "60"
  FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD: "true"   # Enabled feature flag
  MAINTENANCE_MODE: "false"

Output:

configmap/django-settings edited

Restart Pods to Pick Up Changes:

# If using envFrom, pods need restart for env var changes
kubectl rollout restart deployment/django-api -n production

# Verify new config is applied
kubectl exec -it deployment/django-api -n production -- printenv | grep -E "LOG_LEVEL|FEATURE_NEW"
# LOG_LEVEL=INFO
# FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD=true
Example 3: Edit Secret with Base64 Decoding

Update secrets stored in Kubernetes. Note that secret values are base64-encoded in the editor. Use caution when modifying secrets directly.

Context - Secret (secrets/api-credentials.yaml):

# API credentials secret (values shown decoded for reference)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-credentials
  namespace: production
type: Opaque
stringData:  # Using stringData for readability; stored as base64 data
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
  STRIPE_API_KEY: "sk_live_oldkey123456789"
  SENDGRID_API_KEY: "SG.oldapikey.1234567890"

Command:

# Edit secret - values will appear base64-encoded
kubectl edit secret/api-credentials -n production

Editor Shows Base64-Encoded Values:

# Values are base64 encoded in the editor
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-credentials
  namespace: production
type: Opaque
data:
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: QUtJQUlPU0ZPRE5ON0VYQU1QTEU=
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: d0phbHJYVXRuRkVNSS9LN01ERU5HL2JQeFJmaUNZRVhBTVBMRUtFWQ==
  STRIPE_API_KEY: c2tfbGl2ZV9vbGRrZXkxMjM0NTY3ODk=
  SENDGRID_API_KEY: U0cub2xkYXBpa2V5LjEyMzQ1Njc4OTA=

To Update - First Encode New Value:

# Encode new Stripe key before editing
echo -n "sk_live_newkey987654321" | base64
# c2tfbGl2ZV9uZXdrZXk5ODc2NTQzMjE=

# Then replace the old base64 value in the editor with this new one

Output:

secret/api-credentials edited

Verify (carefully - don't log secrets in production):

# In development only - verify the change
kubectl get secret api-credentials -n production -o jsonpath='{.data.STRIPE_API_KEY}' | base64 -d
# sk_live_newkey987654321
Example 4: Edit Service to Change Type and Ports

Modify a service configuration to change service type or update port mappings. Useful for debugging or temporary external access.

Context - Service (services/django-api-service.yaml):

# Current ClusterIP service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: django-api
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: django-api
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP

Context - Need to Debug from Local Machine:

# Scenario: Need temporary direct access to service for debugging
# Options:
# 1. kubectl port-forward (preferred for temporary access)
# 2. Change service to NodePort/LoadBalancer (edit approach)
# 3. Create separate debug service

# For quick debugging session, editing to NodePort:

Command:

# Edit service to change type
kubectl edit service/django-api -n production

Changes in Editor:

spec:
  type: NodePort  # Changed from ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: django-api
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP
    nodePort: 30080  # Added specific NodePort

Output:

service/django-api edited

Access and Revert:

# Access via NodePort
curl http://<node-ip>:30080/health/

# After debugging, revert to ClusterIP
kubectl edit service/django-api -n production
# Change type back to ClusterIP and remove nodePort line
Example 5: Edit with JSON Output Format

Use JSON format instead of YAML when editing. Some prefer JSON for its strict syntax, and it's useful when integrating with JSON-based tools.

Context - HPA Resource (k8s/hpa-django.yaml):

# Current HPA configuration
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: django-api-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: django-api
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70

Command:

# Edit using JSON format
kubectl edit hpa/django-api-hpa -n production -o json

Editor Shows JSON Format:

{
    "apiVersion": "autoscaling/v2",
    "kind": "HorizontalPodAutoscaler",
    "metadata": {
        "name": "django-api-hpa",
        "namespace": "production"
    },
    "spec": {
        "scaleTargetRef": {
            "apiVersion": "apps/v1",
            "kind": "Deployment",
            "name": "django-api"
        },
        "minReplicas": 2,
        "maxReplicas": 10,
        "metrics": [
            {
                "type": "Resource",
                "resource": {
                    "name": "cpu",
                    "target": {
                        "type": "Utilization",
                        "averageUtilization": 70
                    }
                }
            }
        ]
    }
}

Make Changes in JSON:

{
    "spec": {
        "minReplicas": 3,
        "maxReplicas": 20,
        "metrics": [
            {
                "type": "Resource",
                "resource": {
                    "name": "cpu",
                    "target": {
                        "type": "Utilization",
                        "averageUtilization": 60
                    }
                }
            },
            {
                "type": "Resource",
                "resource": {
                    "name": "memory",
                    "target": {
                        "type": "Utilization",
                        "averageUtilization": 80
                    }
                }
            }
        ]
    }
}

Output:

horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/django-api-hpa edited
Example 6: Edit with Record Flag for Audit Trail

Use --record flag to save the edit command in the resource's annotations. Useful for tracking changes in deployment history.

Context - CI/CD Change Tracking Requirements:

# Change management policy - all changes must be trackable
# infra/policies/change-management.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: change-policy
data:
  requirements: |
    1. All production changes must be recorded
    2. Change author must be identifiable
    3. Rollback must be possible within 15 minutes
    4. Change reason must be documented

Command:

# Edit with record flag (deprecated but still functional in some versions)
# Note: --record is deprecated, use annotations instead for new clusters
kubectl edit deployment/django-api -n production --save-config

# Better approach: Add annotation before/after edit
kubectl annotate deployment/django-api -n production \
    kubernetes.io/change-cause="Emergency scaling: JIRA-1234 traffic spike"

View Change History:

kubectl rollout history deployment/django-api -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api 
REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
1         Initial deployment via CI/CD
2         Image update to v2.3.0
3         Emergency scaling: JIRA-1234 traffic spike

Rollback if Needed:

# Rollback to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production

# Or to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production --to-revision=2

kubectl kustomize

BUILD KUSTOMIZE GENERATE

Build a kustomization target from a directory or URL

The kubectl kustomize command processes a kustomization.yaml file and outputs the resulting Kubernetes manifests. Kustomize allows you to customize raw YAML files without templates using overlays, patches, and generators. It's built into kubectl and enables environment-specific configurations while maintaining a single source of truth.

Syntax

kubectl kustomize DIR [flags]

Parameters

  • DIR: Directory containing kustomization.yaml, or a git URL
  • --enable-alpha-plugins: Enable alpha plugins
  • --enable-helm: Enable Helm chart inflation
  • -o, --output: Output destination (file or stdout)
  • --as-current-user: Use current user for exec plugins

Return Value

Outputs fully rendered Kubernetes manifests to stdout (or specified file). The output can be piped directly to kubectl apply.

Examples

Example 1: Build and Apply Django Production Stack

Use Kustomize overlays to manage different environments (dev, staging, production) from a single base configuration. This is the recommended approach for GitOps workflows.

Context - Directory Structure:

k8s/
├── base/
│   ├── kustomization.yaml
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── configmap.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── development/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   └── replica-patch.yaml
    ├── staging/
    │   ├── kustomization.yaml
    │   └── resource-patch.yaml
    └── production/
        ├── kustomization.yaml
        ├── replica-patch.yaml
        ├── resource-patch.yaml
        └── hpa.yaml

Context - Base Configuration (k8s/base/kustomization.yaml):

# k8s/base/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

commonLabels:
  app: django-api
  managed-by: kustomize

resources:
  - deployment.yaml
  - service.yaml
  - configmap.yaml

configMapGenerator:
  - name: django-settings
    literals:
      - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.base
      - LOG_LEVEL=INFO

Context - Base Deployment (k8s/base/deployment.yaml):

# k8s/base/deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: django-api:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-settings
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "100m"
          limits:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "200m"

Context - Production Overlay (k8s/overlays/production/kustomization.yaml):

# k8s/overlays/production/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: production

namePrefix: prod-

resources:
  - ../../base
  - hpa.yaml

patchesStrategicMerge:
  - replica-patch.yaml
  - resource-patch.yaml

configMapGenerator:
  - name: django-settings
    behavior: merge
    literals:
      - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.production
      - LOG_LEVEL=WARNING
      - DEBUG=false

images:
  - name: django-api
    newName: myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api
    newTag: v2.3.1

Context - Production Patches:

# k8s/overlays/production/replica-patch.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  replicas: 5

---
# k8s/overlays/production/resource-patch.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"

Command - Build and Review:

# Build and output to stdout for review
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/

Output (Rendered Manifests):

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  labels:
    app: django-api
    managed-by: kustomize
  name: prod-django-settings-8g9f2k
  namespace: production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: config.settings.production
  LOG_LEVEL: WARNING
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: django-api
    managed-by: kustomize
  name: prod-django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.3.1
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
...

Apply to Cluster:

# Pipe directly to apply
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ | kubectl apply -f -

# Or use built-in apply with kustomize flag
kubectl apply -k k8s/overlays/production/
Example 2: Add Common Labels and Annotations Across Resources

Use Kustomize to automatically add labels and annotations to all resources, useful for cost tracking, team ownership, and resource organization.

Context - Kustomization with Labels (k8s/team-backend/kustomization.yaml):
