# k8s/team-backend/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  - django-api/
  - celery-worker/
  - redis/

# Labels added to all resources
commonLabels:
  team: backend
  cost-center: engineering
  environment: production

# Annotations added to all resources  
commonAnnotations:
  contact: "backend-team@company.com"
  documentation: "https://wiki.company.com/backend"
  oncall-schedule: "https://pagerduty.com/schedules/backend"

Context - Sample Resource (k8s/team-backend/django-api/deployment.yaml):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: django-api:v2.0.0

Command:

kubectl kustomize k8s/team-backend/

Output - All Resources Get Labels/Annotations:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  annotations:
    contact: backend-team@company.com
    documentation: https://wiki.company.com/backend
    oncall-schedule: https://pagerduty.com/schedules/backend
  labels:
    cost-center: engineering
    environment: production
    team: backend
  name: django-api
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      cost-center: engineering
      environment: production
      team: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        cost-center: engineering
        environment: production
        team: backend
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: django-api:v2.0.0
        name: django

Query Resources by Label:

# Find all resources owned by backend team
kubectl get all -l team=backend

# Find cost allocation
kubectl get pods -l cost-center=engineering --all-namespaces
Example 3: Secret Generation with Kustomize

Generate Kubernetes Secrets from files or literals with automatic base64 encoding. Kustomize adds a hash suffix for immutable configuration management.

Context - Directory Structure:

k8s/secrets/
├── kustomization.yaml
├── .env.production        # Environment variables
├── db-credentials.txt     # Database credentials
└── ssl/
    ├── tls.crt
    └── tls.key

Context - Environment File (.env.production):

# k8s/secrets/.env.production
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@db.example.com:5432/django
REDIS_URL=redis://redis.example.com:6379/0
SECRET_KEY=your-super-secret-key-here
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY

Context - Kustomization (k8s/secrets/kustomization.yaml):

# k8s/secrets/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

namespace: production

secretGenerator:
  # From environment file
  - name: django-env-secrets
    envs:
      - .env.production
    
  # From literal values
  - name: api-tokens
    literals:
      - STRIPE_API_KEY=sk_live_123456789
      - SENDGRID_API_KEY=SG.abcdefg
    
  # From files (TLS certificates)
  - name: django-tls
    type: kubernetes.io/tls
    files:
      - tls.crt=ssl/tls.crt
      - tls.key=ssl/tls.key

generatorOptions:
  disableNameSuffixHash: false  # Default: adds hash for immutable config

Command:

# Build secrets
kubectl kustomize k8s/secrets/

Output:

apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-env-secrets-7g9mk5c2f4
  namespace: production
type: Opaque
data:
  DATABASE_URL: cG9zdGdyZXM6Ly91c2VyOnBhc3NAZGIuZXhhbXBsZS5jb206NTQzMi9kamFuZ28=
  REDIS_URL: cmVkaXM6Ly9yZWRpcy5leGFtcGxlLmNvbTo2Mzc5LzA=
  SECRET_KEY: eW91ci1zdXBlci1zZWNyZXQta2V5LWhlcmU=
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: QUtJQUlPU0ZPRE5ON0VYQU1QTEU=
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: d0phbHJYVXRuRkVNSS9LN01ERU5HL2JQeFJmaUNZRVhBTVBMRUtFWQ==
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-tls-f9gh8k2m
  namespace: production
type: kubernetes.io/tls
data:
  tls.crt: <base64-encoded-certificate>
  tls.key: <base64-encoded-key>
...

Note - Hash Suffix Behavior:

# The hash (7g9mk5c2f4) changes when secret content changes
# This triggers a rolling update when referenced in deployments
# To disable, set disableNameSuffixHash: true in generatorOptions
Example 4: JSON Patches for Complex Modifications

Use JSON 6902 patches for precise modifications that strategic merge patches can't handle, like modifying array elements or adding items at specific positions.

Context - Kustomization (k8s/patches/kustomization.yaml):

# k8s/patches/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  - ../base

patches:
  # JSON 6902 patch for array manipulation
  - target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: django-api
    path: add-sidecar-patch.yaml
    
  # Inline patch for quick modifications
  - target:
      kind: Service
      name: django-api
    patch: |-
      - op: add
        path: /spec/ports/-
        value:
          name: metrics
          port: 9090
          targetPort: 9090

Context - Sidecar Patch (k8s/patches/add-sidecar-patch.yaml):

# k8s/patches/add-sidecar-patch.yaml
# JSON 6902 patch to add a log shipper sidecar
- op: add
  path: /spec/template/spec/containers/-
  value:
    name: fluentbit
    image: fluent/fluent-bit:2.1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/django
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "50m"

- op: add
  path: /spec/template/spec/volumes/-
  value:
    name: logs
    emptyDir: {}

# Add volume mount to main container
- op: add
  path: /spec/template/spec/containers/0/volumeMounts
  value:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/django

Command:

kubectl kustomize k8s/patches/

Output:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: django-api:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /var/log/django
      - name: fluentbit
        image: fluent/fluent-bit:2.1
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /var/log/django
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "64Mi"
            cpu: "50m"
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        emptyDir: {}
Example 5: Build from Remote Git Repository

Kustomize can build directly from Git URLs, enabling shared base configurations across teams without local copies.

Context - Reference a Remote Base:

# k8s/overlays/my-service/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

# Reference shared templates from GitHub
resources:
  - github.com/company/k8s-templates//base/web-service?ref=v2.1.0

# Apply local customizations
namePrefix: django-
namespace: production

patchesStrategicMerge:
  - custom-settings.yaml

images:
  - name: web-service
    newName: myregistry/django-api
    newTag: v2.3.1

Command - Build from Remote:

# Build from GitHub URL directly
kubectl kustomize "github.com/company/k8s-templates//base/web-service?ref=v2.1.0"

# Build local overlay that references remote
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/my-service/

CI/CD Integration (.github/workflows/deploy.yaml):

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Kubernetes

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
      
      - name: Validate kustomization
        run: |
          kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ > /dev/null
          echo "✅ Kustomization valid"
      
      - name: Preview changes
        run: |
          kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ | \
            kubectl diff -f - || true
      
      - name: Deploy
        run: |
          kubectl apply -k k8s/overlays/production/
      
      - name: Verify
        run: |
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production
Example 6: Output to File for GitOps Review

Save rendered manifests to a file for review, audit, or use with other tools like conftest for policy validation.

Command:

# Output to file
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ -o rendered-manifests.yaml

# Using shell redirect (equivalent)
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ > rendered-manifests.yaml

Context - Policy Validation with Conftest:

# policy/deployment.rego
package main

deny[msg] {
    input.kind == "Deployment"
    not input.spec.template.spec.containers[_].resources.limits
    msg = sprintf("Deployment %s must have resource limits", [input.metadata.name])
}

deny[msg] {
    input.kind == "Deployment"
    input.spec.replicas < 2
    msg = sprintf("Deployment %s must have at least 2 replicas for HA", [input.metadata.name])
}

Validate with Conftest:

# Build and validate
kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ > rendered.yaml
conftest test rendered.yaml -p policy/

Output:

PASS - rendered.yaml - main - data.main.deny
2 tests, 2 passed, 0 warnings, 0 failures

Complete CI Pipeline:

# .github/workflows/validate.yaml
name: Validate Manifests

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build manifests
        run: kubectl kustomize k8s/overlays/production/ > rendered.yaml
      
      - name: Lint with kubeval
        run: kubeval rendered.yaml --strict
      
      - name: Policy check with conftest
        run: conftest test rendered.yaml -p policy/
      
      - name: Security scan with kubesec
        run: kubesec scan rendered.yaml
      
      - name: Upload rendered manifests
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: rendered-manifests
          path: rendered.yaml

kubectl label

UPDATE LABELS MODIFY

Add, update, or remove labels on Kubernetes resources

The kubectl label command manages labels on resources. Labels are key-value pairs used for organizing resources, selecting pods with services, scheduling with node selectors, and implementing cost allocation. Unlike annotations, labels are meant for identifying and grouping resources.

Syntax

kubectl label [--overwrite] (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) KEY=VALUE [KEY=VALUE...]
kubectl label [--overwrite] (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) KEY- # Remove label

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name (e.g., pod/nginx, deployment/api)
  • KEY=VALUE: Label key-value pair to add or update
  • KEY-: Remove label with the minus suffix
  • --overwrite: Allow overwriting existing label values
  • --all: Select all resources of the specified type
  • -l, --selector: Filter resources by existing labels
  • --resource-version: Specify resource version for conflict resolution

Return Value

Returns confirmation of label changes. Shows "labeled" for each affected resource, or error if label already exists without --overwrite.

Examples

Example 1: Label Resources for Cost Allocation

Add cost center and team labels to track cloud spending by department. Essential for FinOps and chargeback reporting.

Context - Cost Tracking Requirements:

# finops/labeling-policy.yaml
# Company labeling standard for cost allocation
required_labels:
  - cost-center       # Finance department code
  - team              # Owning team
  - environment       # dev, staging, production
  - product           # Product or service name
  
optional_labels:
  - component         # Component within product
  - version           # Application version
  - managed-by        # Tool managing the resource

Context - Current Deployment (without labels):

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api

Commands - Add Cost Labels:

# Add cost allocation labels to deployment
kubectl label deployment/django-api -n production \
    cost-center=CC-1234 \
    team=backend \
    product=marketplace \
    environment=production

# Label the service as well
kubectl label service/django-api -n production \
    cost-center=CC-1234 \
    team=backend \
    product=marketplace \
    environment=production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api labeled
service/django-api labeled

Verify Labels:

kubectl get deployment django-api -n production --show-labels

Output:

NAME         READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE   LABELS
django-api   3/3     3            3           24d   app=django-api,cost-center=CC-1234,environment=production,product=marketplace,team=backend

Query by Cost Center:

# Find all resources for cost reporting
kubectl get all -l cost-center=CC-1234 --all-namespaces
Example 2: Label Nodes for Workload Placement

Add labels to nodes for scheduling specific workloads on appropriate hardware (GPU nodes, high-memory nodes, SSD nodes).

Context - Node Types in Cluster:

# Cluster node inventory
node-pool-1 (3 nodes):  Standard compute, 8 CPU, 32GB RAM
node-pool-2 (2 nodes):  High-memory, 8 CPU, 128GB RAM  
node-pool-3 (2 nodes):  GPU nodes, NVIDIA T4, 16GB VRAM
node-pool-4 (4 nodes):  SSD storage, NVMe drives

Commands - Label Nodes:

# Label high-memory nodes
kubectl label nodes node-pool-2-{1,2} \
    node-type=high-memory \
    workload-class=memory-intensive

# Label GPU nodes
kubectl label nodes node-pool-3-{1,2} \
    node-type=gpu \
    gpu-type=nvidia-t4 \
    workload-class=ml-training

# Label SSD nodes
kubectl label nodes node-pool-4-{1,2,3,4} \
    node-type=ssd \
    storage-class=nvme \
    workload-class=database

Output:

node/node-pool-2-1 labeled
node/node-pool-2-2 labeled
node/node-pool-3-1 labeled
node/node-pool-3-2 labeled
node/node-pool-4-1 labeled
node/node-pool-4-2 labeled
node/node-pool-4-3 labeled
node/node-pool-4-4 labeled

Context - Deployment Using Node Selector:

# k8s/deployments/celery-worker.yaml
# Schedule Celery workers on high-memory nodes
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: celery-worker
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 4
  template:
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        node-type: high-memory
        workload-class: memory-intensive
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: django-api:v2.3.1
        command: ["celery", "-A", "config", "worker", "-l", "INFO"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"

Verify Scheduling:

# Check which nodes have the label
kubectl get nodes -l node-type=high-memory

# Verify pods scheduled on correct nodes
kubectl get pods -n production -o wide | grep celery
Example 3: Batch Label Multiple Resources

Label all resources matching a selector. Useful for retroactively applying labels to existing resources.

Context - Migration to New Labeling Standard:

# Scenario: Need to add 'managed-by=kubectl' label to all
# deployments in production namespace that don't have it yet

Commands:

# Label all deployments in namespace
kubectl label deployments --all -n production managed-by=kubectl

# Label all pods with specific app label
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api -n production version=v2.3.1

# Label multiple resource types at once
kubectl label deployment,service,configmap -l app=django-api -n production \
    release=stable \
    tier=backend

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api labeled
deployment.apps/celery-worker labeled
deployment.apps/celery-beat labeled
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 labeled
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34 labeled
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56 labeled
deployment.apps/django-api labeled
service/django-api labeled
configmap/django-settings labeled

Dry Run Before Applying:

# Preview what would be labeled
kubectl label deployments --all -n production new-label=value --dry-run=client

# With output to see full changes
kubectl label deployments --all -n production new-label=value \
    --dry-run=client -o yaml | grep -A5 labels:
Example 4: Remove Labels from Resources

Remove labels by specifying the key with a minus sign suffix. Useful when deprecating label standards or fixing incorrect labels.

Context - Label Cleanup:

# Scenario: Deprecating old labeling scheme
# Old labels to remove: 'dept', 'owner', 'legacy-app'
# New labels to use: 'team', 'cost-center', 'product'

Commands:

# Remove single label
kubectl label deployment/django-api -n production legacy-app-

# Remove multiple labels at once
kubectl label deployment/django-api -n production \
    dept- \
    owner- \
    old-version-

# Remove label from all deployments
kubectl label deployments --all -n production legacy-label-

# Remove label from all pods with selector
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api -n production temporary-debug-

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api unlabeled
deployment.apps/django-api unlabeled
deployment.apps/django-api unlabeled
deployment.apps/celery-worker unlabeled
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 unlabeled
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34 unlabeled

Verify Removal:

# Confirm label is gone
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels}'
# Output should not contain removed labels
Example 5: Overwrite Existing Labels

Use --overwrite flag to change existing label values. Without this flag, attempting to change an existing label fails.

Context - Environment Promotion:

# Deployment currently labeled as staging, promoting to production
metadata:
  name: django-api
  labels:
    app: django-api
    environment: staging  # Need to change to production
    version: v2.3.0       # Need to update to v2.3.1

Without --overwrite (fails):

kubectl label deployment/django-api -n production environment=production

Error:

error: 'environment' already has a value (staging), and --overwrite is false

With --overwrite (succeeds):

# Update environment label
kubectl label deployment/django-api -n production \
    environment=production \
    version=v2.3.1 \
    --overwrite

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api labeled

Automation Script for Promotions:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/promote-to-production.sh

DEPLOYMENT=$1
VERSION=$2

echo "Promoting $DEPLOYMENT to production with version $VERSION"

kubectl label deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n production \
    environment=production \
    version=$VERSION \
    promoted-at=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
    --overwrite

kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n production
Example 6: Label Pods for Service Routing

Modify pod labels to add or remove them from service load balancers. Useful for blue-green deployments and canary releases.

Context - Blue-Green Deployment Setup:

# k8s/services/django-api-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: django-api
    active: "true"  # Only route to pods with active=true
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000

Context - Two Deployment Versions:

# Blue deployment (current production)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api-blue
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
        version: v2.3.0
        active: "true"  # Receiving traffic
---
# Green deployment (new version)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api-green
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
        version: v2.4.0
        active: "false"  # Not receiving traffic yet

Commands - Switch Traffic to Green:

# Step 1: Label green pods as active
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api,version=v2.4.0 -n production \
    active=true --overwrite

# Step 2: Remove active label from blue pods
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api,version=v2.3.0 -n production \
    active=false --overwrite

# Or atomic switch using deployment labels:
kubectl label deployment/django-api-green -n production active=true --overwrite
kubectl label deployment/django-api-blue -n production active=false --overwrite

Output:

pod/django-api-green-5f8d9c7b2-abc12 labeled
pod/django-api-green-5f8d9c7b2-def34 labeled
pod/django-api-green-5f8d9c7b2-ghi56 labeled
pod/django-api-blue-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 labeled
pod/django-api-blue-7d9f8b6c4-def34 labeled
pod/django-api-blue-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56 labeled

Verify Traffic Routing:

# Check which pods are receiving traffic
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=django-api,active=true

# Check service endpoints
kubectl get endpoints django-api -n production

Rollback if Issues:

# Quick rollback to blue
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api,version=v2.3.0 -n production active=true --overwrite
kubectl label pods -l app=django-api,version=v2.4.0 -n production active=false --overwrite

kubectl patch

UPDATE PATCH MODIFY

Update fields of a resource using strategic merge, JSON merge, or JSON patch

The kubectl patch command updates specific fields of a resource without replacing the entire object. It supports three patch types: strategic merge patch (default, smart merging of arrays), JSON merge patch (RFC 7386), and JSON patch (RFC 6902, array of operations). Essential for automation scripts and CI/CD pipelines where you need predictable, targeted updates.

Syntax

kubectl patch (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) --patch PATCH [options]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name to patch
  • -p, --patch: The patch content (JSON or YAML)
  • --patch-file: Read patch from file
  • --type: Patch type: strategic, merge, or json (default: strategic)
  • --dry-run: Preview without applying (client or server)
  • -o, --output: Output format for the patched object

Return Value

Returns the patched resource and confirmation message. With -o flag, outputs the full patched resource in specified format.

Examples

Example 1: Update Image with Strategic Merge Patch

The most common use case: updating container images for deployments. Strategic merge patch intelligently merges arrays by name.

Context - Current Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.3.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80

Command - Update Django Image:

# Strategic merge patch - only updates the django container
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=strategic -p '
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
'

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api patched

Verify - Both Containers Preserved:

kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}'
# myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0 nginx:1.24

CI/CD Script (automated image updates):

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/update-image.sh
DEPLOYMENT=$1
CONTAINER=$2
IMAGE=$3
NAMESPACE=${4:-production}

kubectl patch deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --type=strategic -p "
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: $CONTAINER
        image: $IMAGE
"

kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE
Example 2: JSON Patch for Precise Array Modifications

Use JSON patch (RFC 6902) when you need precise control over array operations - add, remove, replace at specific indices.

Context - Add Environment Variable:

# Current container spec
containers:
- name: django
  image: django-api:v2.3.0
  env:
  - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
    value: config.settings.production
  - name: DATABASE_URL
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: db-secret
        key: url

Command - Add New Env Variable:

# JSON patch to add env variable at end of array
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=json -p '[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/env/-",
    "value": {
      "name": "FEATURE_FLAG_NEW_UI",
      "value": "true"
    }
  }
]'

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api patched

Multiple Operations in One Patch:

# Add env var, update resource limits, add label
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=json -p '[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/env/-",
    "value": {"name": "LOG_LEVEL", "value": "DEBUG"}
  },
  {
    "op": "replace",
    "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/limits/memory",
    "value": "1Gi"
  },
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/metadata/labels/patched-by",
    "value": "ci-pipeline"
  }
]'

Remove Item at Specific Index:

# Remove second environment variable (index 1)
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=json -p '[
  {"op": "remove", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/env/1"}
]'
Example 3: Patch from File for Complex Changes

Store patches in files for version control and reusability. Essential for GitOps workflows.

Context - Patch File (k8s/patches/enable-debug.yaml):

# k8s/patches/enable-debug.yaml
# Enable debug mode for troubleshooting
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        debug-enabled: "true"
        debug-timestamp: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        env:
        - name: DEBUG
          value: "true"
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          value: "DEBUG"
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"  # Increase for profiling

Context - Production Scaling Patch (k8s/patches/scale-production.yaml):

# k8s/patches/scale-production.yaml
# High-traffic production configuration
spec:
  replicas: 10
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        readinessProbe:
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 10

Command - Apply Patch from File:

# Apply debug patch
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --patch-file k8s/patches/enable-debug.yaml

# Apply production scaling patch
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --patch-file k8s/patches/scale-production.yaml

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api patched

CI/CD Integration:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
jobs:
  deploy:
    steps:
      - name: Apply environment-specific patches
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production \
              --patch-file k8s/patches/scale-production.yaml
          else
            kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n staging \
              --patch-file k8s/patches/scale-staging.yaml
          fi
Example 4: Patch Service Selector for Traffic Switching

Update service selectors to switch traffic between deployment versions. Core technique for blue-green deployments.

Context - Service and Deployments:

# k8s/services/django-api-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: django-api
    version: v2.3.0  # Currently pointing to blue
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000

---
# Blue deployment (v2.3.0)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api-blue
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
        version: v2.3.0
---
# Green deployment (v2.4.0)  
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api-green
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
        version: v2.4.0

Command - Switch to Green:

# Patch service selector to point to green
kubectl patch service/django-api -n production --type=strategic -p '
spec:
  selector:
    version: v2.4.0
'

Output:

service/django-api patched

Automation Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/blue-green-switch.sh
SERVICE=$1
TARGET_VERSION=$2
NAMESPACE=${3:-production}

echo "Switching $SERVICE to version $TARGET_VERSION"

# Verify target deployment exists and is ready
kubectl rollout status deployment/${SERVICE}-${TARGET_VERSION} -n $NAMESPACE

# Switch traffic
kubectl patch service/$SERVICE -n $NAMESPACE --type=strategic -p "
spec:
  selector:
    version: $TARGET_VERSION
"

# Verify endpoints
echo "Current endpoints:"
kubectl get endpoints $SERVICE -n $NAMESPACE

# Health check
sleep 5
curl -s https://${SERVICE}.example.com/health/ | jq .
Example 5: Patch ConfigMap Using Merge Patch

JSON merge patch (RFC 7386) is simpler than strategic patch but replaces entire arrays. Good for ConfigMaps and simple objects.

Context - ConfigMap:

# Current ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-settings
  namespace: production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  LOG_LEVEL: "WARNING"
  CACHE_TIMEOUT: "300"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "api.example.com"

Command - Merge Patch to Update Values:

# JSON merge patch preserves unspecified fields
kubectl patch configmap/django-settings -n production --type=merge -p '
{
  "data": {
    "LOG_LEVEL": "DEBUG",
    "CACHE_TIMEOUT": "60",
    "NEW_FEATURE_FLAG": "enabled"
  }
}'

Output:

configmap/django-settings patched

Result - Merged Data:

# After patch - all original keys preserved, new key added
data:
  DEBUG: "false"              # Preserved
  LOG_LEVEL: "DEBUG"          # Updated
  CACHE_TIMEOUT: "60"         # Updated
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "api.example.com"  # Preserved
  NEW_FEATURE_FLAG: "enabled" # Added

Delete a Key with Merge Patch:

# Set value to null to remove
kubectl patch configmap/django-settings -n production --type=merge -p '
{
  "data": {
    "NEW_FEATURE_FLAG": null
  }
}'
Example 6: Dry Run and Output Patched Resource

Preview patches before applying them. Essential for validating changes in CI/CD pipelines.

Command - Dry Run Client Side:

# Preview patch without applying
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=strategic \
    --dry-run=client \
    -o yaml \
    -p '
spec:
  replicas: 10
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
'

Output (shows what would be created):

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 10
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
# ... rest of deployment

Server-Side Dry Run (validates against API):

# Server validates the patch would succeed
kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production --type=strategic \
    --dry-run=server \
    -o json \
    -p '{"spec":{"replicas":20}}'

CI/CD Validation Pipeline:

# .github/workflows/validate-patches.yaml
name: Validate Patches

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Validate patches
        run: |
          for patch in k8s/patches/*.yaml; do
            echo "Testing patch: $patch"
            kubectl patch deployment/django-api -n production \
              --patch-file "$patch" \
              --dry-run=server \
              -o yaml > /dev/null
            echo "✅ $patch is valid"
          done

kubectl replace

REPLACE FULL OVERWRITE

Replace a resource by filename or stdin with a completely new version

The kubectl replace command replaces a resource entirely with a new definition. Unlike apply which merges changes, replace overwrites the entire resource. This is useful when you need complete control over the final state, such as when you've edited a resource and want to ensure no stale fields remain. Requires the resource to already exist (use --force to delete and recreate).

Syntax

kubectl replace -f FILENAME [options]

Parameters

  • -f, --filename: Filename, directory, or URL to files with replacement resource
  • --force: Delete existing resource before creating replacement
  • --grace-period: Period in seconds given for graceful deletion (with --force)
  • --cascade: Delete dependents (default: true)
  • --save-config: Save configuration annotation after replace
  • --validate: Schema validation mode (strict, warn, ignore)

Return Value

Returns confirmation of resource replacement. Fails if resource doesn't exist (unless --force is used).

Examples

Example 1: Replace Deployment After Major Changes

When you've made significant changes to a deployment spec and want to ensure the cluster matches exactly, use replace instead of apply.

Context - Updated Deployment (k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml):

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
# Complete rewrite - new container structure
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: django-api
    version: v3.0.0
    environment: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 2
      maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
        version: v3.0.0
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: django-api
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v3.0.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        - containerPort: 9090
          name: metrics
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-settings
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/ready
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/live
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
      - name: prometheus-exporter
        image: prom/statsd-exporter:v0.24.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9090
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "64Mi"
            cpu: "50m"

Command:

# Replace entire deployment
kubectl replace -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api replaced

Verify Complete Replacement:

# Confirm new structure
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o yaml | grep -A20 "containers:"
Example 2: Force Replace Immutable Resource

Some resources have immutable fields that can't be updated. Use --force to delete and recreate the resource.

Context - Job With Updated Configuration:

# k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml
# Jobs have immutable selectors - must force replace
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: django-migration
  namespace: production
spec:
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600
  backoffLimit: 3
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        job: django-migration
        version: v3.0.0
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: migration
        image: myregistry/django-api:v3.0.0
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--no-input"]
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets

Command - Force Replace:

# Delete and recreate the job
kubectl replace --force -f k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml

Output:

job.batch "django-migration" deleted
job.batch/django-migration replaced

With Grace Period:

# Immediate deletion (no grace period)
kubectl replace --force --grace-period=0 -f k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml

# Or with grace period for cleanup
kubectl replace --force --grace-period=30 -f k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml
Example 3: Replace ConfigMap with New Structure

When reorganizing configuration, replace ensures old keys are removed and only new keys exist.

Context - Old ConfigMap Structure:

# Old structure (currently in cluster)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-settings
  namespace: production
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  OLD_SETTING_1: "value1"
  OLD_SETTING_2: "value2"
  DEPRECATED_FEATURE: "enabled"
  LOG_LEVEL: "WARNING"

Context - New ConfigMap (k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml):

# k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml
# Cleaned up - removed deprecated settings
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-settings
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: django-api
    config-version: "2.0"
data:
  DEBUG: "false"
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
  CACHE_BACKEND: "redis"
  CACHE_URL: "redis://redis:6379/0"
  CELERY_BROKER_URL: "redis://redis:6379/1"
  SESSION_ENGINE: "django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache"

Command:

# Replace removes OLD_SETTING_1, OLD_SETTING_2, DEPRECATED_FEATURE
kubectl replace -f k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml

Output:

configmap/django-settings replaced

Compare with Apply Behavior:

# With apply - old keys would be preserved
kubectl apply -f k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml
# Result: OLD_SETTING_1, OLD_SETTING_2 still exist

# With replace - only new keys exist
kubectl replace -f k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml
# Result: Clean config with only specified keys
Example 4: Replace from kubectl get Output

Export a resource, modify it, and replace. Useful for quick modifications without maintaining manifest files.

Commands - Edit and Replace Pipeline:

# Export current resource
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o yaml > /tmp/django-api.yaml

# Edit the file
# (Make your changes in /tmp/django-api.yaml)

# Replace with modified version
kubectl replace -f /tmp/django-api.yaml

One-liner with sed:

# Quick image update using pipe
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o yaml | \
    sed 's|image: myregistry/django-api:v2.3.0|image: myregistry/django-api:v3.0.0|' | \
    kubectl replace -f -

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api replaced

With jq for Complex Modifications:

# Update multiple fields with jq
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o json | \
    jq '.spec.replicas = 10 | 
        .spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory = "2Gi"' | \
    kubectl replace -f -
Example 5: Replace Service with Type Change

Changing service type (e.g., ClusterIP to LoadBalancer) may require replace due to field changes.

Context - New Service Configuration:

# k8s/services/django-api-service.yaml
# Changing from ClusterIP to LoadBalancer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol: "tcp"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer  # Changed from ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: django-api
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  - name: https
    port: 443
    targetPort: 8000

Command:

# Replace service configuration
kubectl replace -f k8s/services/django-api-service.yaml

Output:

service/django-api replaced

Verify LoadBalancer Creation:

# Check for external IP/hostname
kubectl get service django-api -n production -w

# Output after a few minutes:
# NAME         TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP                           PORT(S)
# django-api   LoadBalancer   10.0.45.123   abc123.us-west-2.elb.amazonaws.com   80:31234/TCP,443:31235/TCP
Example 6: Replace with Validation

Use strict validation to catch errors before replacing production resources.

Command - Strict Validation:

# Validate strictly against API schema
kubectl replace -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml --validate=strict

Error Example (invalid field):

error: error validating "k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml": error validating data: 
ValidationError(Deployment.spec): unknown field "invalidField" in io.k8s.api.apps.v1.DeploymentSpec

Dry Run Before Replace:

# Server-side validation without applying
kubectl replace -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml --dry-run=server --validate=strict

CI/CD Pipeline with Validation:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy

jobs:
  deploy:
    steps:
      - name: Validate manifests
        run: |
          kubectl replace -f k8s/ --dry-run=server --validate=strict -R
      
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: |
          # Use replace for clean deployments
          kubectl replace -f k8s/configmaps/ -R
          kubectl replace -f k8s/services/ -R
          
          # Use apply for deployments (better rolling update support)
          kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/ -R
          
      - name: Verify rollout
        run: |
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

kubectl rollout

MANAGE ROLLOUT CONTROL

Manage the rollout of deployments, daemonsets, and statefulsets

The kubectl rollout command provides a suite of subcommands for managing resource rollouts. It enables you to monitor deployment progress, pause and resume rollouts, view revision history, and rollback to previous versions. Essential for production deployment workflows and incident response.

Subcommands

  • history: View rollout history
  • pause: Pause a rollout
  • restart: Restart a resource (rolling restart)
  • resume: Resume a paused rollout
  • status: Show the status of the rollout
  • undo: Undo a previous rollout

Supported Resources

  • Deployment: Most common, full rollout control
  • DaemonSet: Rolling updates to all nodes
  • StatefulSet: Ordered, graceful updates

kubectl rollout status

MONITOR STATUS WATCH

Show the status of the rollout and wait for completion

The kubectl rollout status command displays the current status of a rollout and optionally watches until completion. It's essential in CI/CD pipelines to verify deployments succeed before proceeding. Returns exit code 0 on success, non-zero on failure.

Syntax

kubectl rollout status (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME) [flags]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name (e.g., deployment django-api)
  • -w, --watch: Watch status until complete (default: true)
  • --timeout: Timeout for watching (e.g., 5m, 300s)
  • --revision: Pin to a specific revision

Return Value

Exit code 0 if rollout completes successfully, non-zero on timeout or failure. Outputs progress messages during rollout.

Examples

Example 1: Monitor Deployment Rollout in CI/CD

Wait for deployment to complete in a CI/CD pipeline. Fails the pipeline if rollout doesn't succeed.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Production

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
      
      - name: Deploy
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
      
      - name: Wait for rollout
        run: |
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api \
            -n production \
            --timeout=5m
      
      - name: Notify success
        if: success()
        run: echo "✅ Deployment successful"
      
      - name: Rollback on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "❌ Deployment failed, rolling back"
          kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production

Command:

kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

Output (during rollout):

Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 1 of 5 updated replicas are available...
Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 2 of 5 updated replicas are available...
Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 3 of 5 updated replicas are available...
Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 4 of 5 updated replicas are available...
deployment "django-api" successfully rolled out
Example 2: Check Status with Timeout

Set a timeout to fail fast if rollout takes too long. Important for preventing stuck pipelines.

Command:

# 5-minute timeout
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=5m

# 2-minute timeout for faster feedback
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=2m

Output (timeout exceeded):

Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 2 of 5 updated replicas are available...
error: timed out waiting for the condition

Exit Code Check:

# Check exit code
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=2m
echo "Exit code: $?"
# Exit code: 1 (on timeout/failure)
# Exit code: 0 (on success)
Example 3: Monitor Multiple Deployments

Use a script to monitor multiple deployments in parallel or sequence.

Context - Multi-service Deployment Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy-stack.sh

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
TIMEOUT=${2:-5m}

DEPLOYMENTS=(
    "django-api"
    "celery-worker"
    "celery-beat"
    "nginx-ingress"
)

echo "🚀 Deploying to $NAMESPACE..."

# Apply all manifests
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/ -n $NAMESPACE

# Monitor each rollout
failed=0
for deployment in "${DEPLOYMENTS[@]}"; do
    echo "⏳ Waiting for $deployment..."
    if kubectl rollout status deployment/$deployment -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=$TIMEOUT; then
        echo "✅ $deployment rolled out successfully"
    else
        echo "❌ $deployment failed to roll out"
        failed=$((failed + 1))
    fi
done

if [ $failed -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "⚠️  $failed deployment(s) failed"
    exit 1
else
    echo "🎉 All deployments successful"
    exit 0
fi

kubectl rollout history

VIEW HISTORY LIST

View the rollout history of a resource

The kubectl rollout history command shows the revision history of a deployment, daemonset, or statefulset. Each revision includes the change cause annotation if set. Use --revision to see details of a specific revision. Essential for understanding deployment history and planning rollbacks.

Syntax

kubectl rollout history (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME) [flags]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name
  • --revision: See details of a specific revision

Examples

Example 1: View Deployment History with Change Causes

See all revisions and what triggered each change. Requires kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation.

Context - Deployment with Change Tracking:

# Adding change cause during deployment
# Method 1: In manifest
metadata:
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v2.3.0 - Added Redis caching"

# Method 2: Via kubectl annotate after apply
kubectl annotate deployment/django-api -n production \
    kubernetes.io/change-cause="v2.3.0 - Added Redis caching"

Command:

kubectl rollout history deployment/django-api -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api 
REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
1         Initial deployment - v2.0.0
2         v2.1.0 - Bug fix for authentication
3         v2.2.0 - Performance improvements
4         v2.3.0 - Added Redis caching
5         v2.3.1 - Hotfix for cache invalidation
Example 2: View Specific Revision Details

Examine the exact configuration of a specific revision before rolling back.

Command:

# View revision 3 details
kubectl rollout history deployment/django-api -n production --revision=3

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api with revision #3
Pod Template:
  Labels:       app=django-api
                pod-template-hash=7d9f8b6c4
  Annotations:  kubernetes.io/change-cause: v2.2.0 - Performance improvements
  Containers:
   django:
    Image:      myregistry/django-api:v2.2.0
    Port:       8000/TCP
    Host Port:  0/TCP
    Limits:
      cpu:     500m
      memory:  512Mi
    Requests:
      cpu:     250m
      memory:  256Mi
    Environment Variables from:
      django-settings  ConfigMap  Optional: false
      django-secrets   Secret     Optional: false

Compare Revisions Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/compare-revisions.sh
DEPLOYMENT=$1
REV1=$2
REV2=$3
NAMESPACE=${4:-production}

echo "Comparing revision $REV1 vs $REV2 for $DEPLOYMENT"
echo "=============================================="

echo "--- Revision $REV1 ---"
kubectl rollout history deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --revision=$REV1

echo ""
echo "--- Revision $REV2 ---"
kubectl rollout history deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --revision=$REV2

kubectl rollout undo

ROLLBACK UNDO REVERT

Roll back a deployment to a previous revision

The kubectl rollout undo command reverts a deployment to a previous revision. By default, it rolls back to the previous revision, but you can specify a target revision with --to-revision. Critical for incident response when a deployment causes problems.

Syntax

kubectl rollout undo (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME) [flags]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name
  • --to-revision: Roll back to a specific revision (0 = previous)
  • --dry-run: Preview without applying

Examples

Example 1: Emergency Rollback in Production

Quickly revert a problematic deployment. This is your primary incident response tool.

Context - Incident Runbook:

# Incident Response: Bad Deployment Rollback

## Symptoms
- 5xx errors spiking
- Health checks failing
- User complaints

## Quick Actions
1. Identify affected deployment
2. Rollback immediately
3. Verify rollback success
4. Investigate root cause

Commands:

# Step 1: Quick rollback to previous version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production

# Step 2: Verify rollback started
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

# Step 3: Confirm pods are healthy
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=django-api

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api rolled back

Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 2 of 5 updated replicas are available...
deployment "django-api" successfully rolled out

NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    1/1     Running   0          45s
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    1/1     Running   0          42s
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    1/1     Running   0          38s
Example 2: Rollback to Specific Revision

When the previous version also had issues, roll back to a known good revision.

Commands:

# First, view available revisions
kubectl rollout history deployment/django-api -n production
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         v2.0.0 - Initial production
# 2         v2.1.0 - (had bugs)
# 3         v2.2.0 - (current, broken)

# Roll back to revision 1 (skip broken revision 2)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production --to-revision=1

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api rolled back

Verify Correct Version:

# Confirm image version
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# myregistry/django-api:v2.0.0
Example 3: Dry Run Rollback

Preview what would happen before executing. Good for understanding the diff.

Command:

# Preview rollback without executing
kubectl rollout undo deployment/django-api -n production --dry-run=client -o yaml

CI/CD Auto-Rollback Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy-with-rollback.sh

DEPLOYMENT=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}
TIMEOUT=${3:-5m}

# Store current revision for potential rollback
CURRENT_REV=$(kubectl rollout history deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE | \
    tail -2 | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')

echo "📦 Current revision: $CURRENT_REV"
echo "🚀 Deploying..."

# Apply new version
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/${DEPLOYMENT}.yaml

# Wait for rollout
if kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=$TIMEOUT; then
    echo "✅ Deployment successful"
    
    # Run smoke tests
    if ./scripts/smoke-test.sh; then
        echo "✅ Smoke tests passed"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "❌ Smoke tests failed, rolling back"
        kubectl rollout undo deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --to-revision=$CURRENT_REV
        exit 1
    fi
else
    echo "❌ Rollout failed, rolling back"
    kubectl rollout undo deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --to-revision=$CURRENT_REV
    exit 1
fi

kubectl rollout restart

RESTART ROLLING REFRESH

Restart a resource by triggering a rolling restart of all pods

The kubectl rollout restart command triggers a rolling restart of all pods in a deployment without changing the image or configuration. Useful when pods need to pick up ConfigMap/Secret changes, clear memory leaks, or recover from unhealthy states.

Syntax

kubectl rollout restart (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME) [flags]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter resources

Examples

Example 1: Restart After ConfigMap Update

Force pods to reload configuration after updating a ConfigMap. Required when using envFrom.

Context - ConfigMap Used as Environment Variables:

# Deployment using envFrom
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: django-settings  # Changes need pod restart

Commands:

# Step 1: Update ConfigMap
kubectl apply -f k8s/configmaps/django-settings.yaml

# Step 2: Restart deployment to pick up changes
kubectl rollout restart deployment/django-api -n production

# Step 3: Wait for restart to complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api restarted

Waiting for deployment "django-api" rollout to finish: 2 of 5 updated replicas are available...
deployment "django-api" successfully rolled out

Verify New Config:

# Check environment in new pod
kubectl exec -it deployment/django-api -n production -- printenv | grep LOG_LEVEL
Example 2: Restart Multiple Deployments

Restart all deployments in a namespace or matching a selector.

Commands:

# Restart all deployments in namespace
kubectl rollout restart deployment -n production

# Restart deployments matching label selector
kubectl rollout restart deployment -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=django-stack -n production

# Restart specific multiple deployments
kubectl rollout restart deployment/django-api deployment/celery-worker -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api restarted
deployment.apps/celery-worker restarted
deployment.apps/celery-beat restarted
Example 3: Scheduled Restarts for Memory Management

Set up periodic restarts to address memory leaks in legacy applications.

Context - CronJob for Scheduled Restart:

# k8s/cronjobs/scheduled-restart.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: django-api-restart
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 4 * * *"  # Daily at 4 AM
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: deployment-restarter
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: kubectl
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              kubectl rollout restart deployment/django-api -n production
              kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=5m

Required RBAC:

# k8s/rbac/deployment-restarter.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: deployment-restarter
  namespace: production
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deployment-restarter
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: deployment-restarter
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: deployment-restarter
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: deployment-restarter
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

kubectl rollout pause

PAUSE ROLLOUT HOLD

Pause a rollout to make multiple changes before resuming

The kubectl rollout pause command pauses an ongoing rollout. This allows you to make multiple changes to a deployment without triggering a new rollout for each change. Once all changes are made, use resume to continue the rollout with all changes applied together.

Syntax

kubectl rollout pause (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME)

Examples

Example 1: Batch Multiple Changes

Make several modifications without triggering multiple rollouts.

Commands:

# Step 1: Pause the deployment
kubectl rollout pause deployment/django-api -n production

# Step 2: Make multiple changes
kubectl set image deployment/django-api django=myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0 -n production
kubectl set resources deployment/django-api -c django --limits=memory=1Gi,cpu=1000m -n production
kubectl set env deployment/django-api -c django FEATURE_FLAG=enabled -n production

# Step 3: Resume to apply all changes in one rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/django-api -n production

# Step 4: Monitor combined rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api paused
deployment.apps/django-api image updated
deployment.apps/django-api resource requirements updated
deployment.apps/django-api env updated
deployment.apps/django-api resumed
deployment "django-api" successfully rolled out
Example 2: Canary Deployment with Pause

Pause mid-rollout to monitor a small percentage of traffic before continuing.

Context - Deployment with Slow Rollout:

# Deployment configured for slow rollout
spec:
  replicas: 10
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1       # Only 1 new pod at a time
      maxUnavailable: 0 # Never remove old pods first

Canary Workflow:

# Step 1: Start rollout
kubectl set image deployment/django-api django=myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0 -n production

# Step 2: Wait for first pod to update
sleep 30

# Step 3: Pause to let canary receive traffic
kubectl rollout pause deployment/django-api -n production
echo "⏸️  Canary deployed - monitoring for 10 minutes..."

# Step 4: Monitor canary metrics (check error rates, latency)
sleep 600

# Step 5: If healthy, resume full rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/django-api -n production
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production

kubectl rollout resume

RESUME ROLLOUT CONTINUE

Resume a paused rollout

The kubectl rollout resume command resumes a previously paused rollout. All changes made while paused are applied in a single rollout operation.

Syntax

kubectl rollout resume (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME)

Examples

Example 1: Resume After Canary Validation

Continue rollout after manual validation of canary pods.

Automated Canary Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/canary-deploy.sh

DEPLOYMENT=$1
IMAGE=$2
NAMESPACE=${3:-production}
CANARY_WAIT=${4:-300}  # 5 minutes default

echo "🚀 Starting canary deployment of $DEPLOYMENT"

# Update image (starts rollout)
kubectl set image deployment/$DEPLOYMENT django=$IMAGE -n $NAMESPACE

# Wait for first new pod
echo "⏳ Waiting for canary pod..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=60s || true

# Pause to test canary
kubectl rollout pause deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE
echo "⏸️  Canary running - monitoring for $CANARY_WAIT seconds..."

# Check error rate during canary period
sleep $CANARY_WAIT
ERROR_RATE=$(curl -s "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query?query=rate(http_errors_total[5m])" | jq '.data.result[0].value[1]')

if (( $(echo "$ERROR_RATE < 0.01" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "✅ Canary healthy (error rate: $ERROR_RATE)"
    kubectl rollout resume deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE
    kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE
    echo "🎉 Full rollout complete"
else
    echo "❌ Canary unhealthy (error rate: $ERROR_RATE)"
    kubectl rollout undo deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE
    echo "⏮️  Rolled back to previous version"
    exit 1
fi

kubectl scale

UPDATE SCALE RESIZE

Set a new size for a deployment, replica set, or stateful set

The kubectl scale command manually adjusts the number of replicas for a workload resource. Use it for immediate scaling needs, emergency capacity adjustments, or scaling to zero for maintenance. For automatic scaling based on metrics, use kubectl autoscale to create an HPA instead.

Syntax

kubectl scale [--resource-version=version] [--current-replicas=count] --replicas=COUNT (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME)

Parameters

  • --replicas: The new desired number of replicas (required)
  • --current-replicas: Fail if current replica count doesn't match (optimistic locking)
  • --resource-version: Require specific resource version (for race prevention)
  • --timeout: Time to wait for scaling to complete
  • --all: Scale all resources of the specified type
  • -l, --selector: Filter resources by label selector

Return Value

Returns confirmation with old and new replica counts. Does not wait for pods to be ready unless combined with rollout status.

Examples

Example 1: Emergency Scale-Up During Traffic Spike

Quickly add capacity when monitoring shows increased load. Immediate response before autoscaling kicks in.

Context - Monitoring Alert:

# Alert from Prometheus/Alertmanager
- alert: HighCPUUsage
  expr: avg(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{pod=~"django-api.*"}[5m])) > 0.8
  for: 2m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Django API CPU usage above 80%"
    runbook: "Scale deployment to handle increased load"

Commands:

# Check current scale
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production
# NAME         READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# django-api   3/3     3            3           24d

# Scale up to handle traffic
kubectl scale deployment/django-api --replicas=10 -n production

# Verify pods are starting
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=django-api -w

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api scaled

NAME                          READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    1/1     Running             0          24d
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    1/1     Running             0          24d
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    1/1     Running             0          24d
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-jkl78    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          5s
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-mno90    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          5s
...

Wait for All Pods Ready:

# Wait for scale operation to complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production
# deployment "django-api" successfully rolled out

# Verify final state
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production
# NAME         READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# django-api   10/10   10           10          24d
Example 2: Scale to Zero for Maintenance

Temporarily stop all pods for database migration or critical maintenance. Cost savings technique for non-production environments.

Context - Maintenance Window Procedure:

# Maintenance Runbook: Database Schema Migration

## Pre-Migration
1. Notify users of maintenance window
2. Scale down application pods to prevent DB access
3. Take database backup

## Migration
4. Run migration scripts
5. Verify data integrity

## Post-Migration
6. Scale up application pods
7. Run smoke tests
8. Monitor for errors

Commands:

# Step 1: Record current replica count
ORIGINAL_REPLICAS=$(kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}')
echo "Original replicas: $ORIGINAL_REPLICAS"

# Step 2: Scale to zero
kubectl scale deployment/django-api --replicas=0 -n production
kubectl scale deployment/celery-worker --replicas=0 -n production

# Step 3: Verify no pods running
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=django-api
# No resources found in production namespace.

# Step 4: Perform maintenance...

# Step 5: Scale back up
kubectl scale deployment/django-api --replicas=$ORIGINAL_REPLICAS -n production
kubectl scale deployment/celery-worker --replicas=4 -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api scaled
deployment.apps/celery-worker scaled

Automated Maintenance Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/maintenance-window.sh

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
DEPLOYMENTS=("django-api" "celery-worker" "celery-beat")

# Store original replica counts
declare -A ORIGINAL_REPLICAS
for dep in "${DEPLOYMENTS[@]}"; do
    ORIGINAL_REPLICAS[$dep]=$(kubectl get deployment $dep -n $NAMESPACE -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}')
done

# Scale down
echo "📉 Scaling down for maintenance..."
for dep in "${DEPLOYMENTS[@]}"; do
    kubectl scale deployment/$dep --replicas=0 -n $NAMESPACE
done

# Wait for shutdown
kubectl wait --for=delete pod -l app=django-api -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=60s || true

echo "🔧 Maintenance window started..."
echo "Press any key when maintenance is complete..."
read -n 1

# Scale back up
echo "📈 Scaling back up..."
for dep in "${DEPLOYMENTS[@]}"; do
    kubectl scale deployment/$dep --replicas=${ORIGINAL_REPLICAS[$dep]} -n $NAMESPACE
done

# Verify
for dep in "${DEPLOYMENTS[@]}"; do
    kubectl rollout status deployment/$dep -n $NAMESPACE
done

echo "✅ Maintenance complete"
Example 3: Scale Multiple Resources

Scale multiple deployments at once or using label selectors.

Commands:

# Scale multiple named deployments
kubectl scale deployment/django-api deployment/celery-worker --replicas=5 -n production

# Scale all deployments matching label
kubectl scale deployment -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=django-stack --replicas=3 -n production

# Scale all deployments in namespace (dangerous!)
kubectl scale deployment --all --replicas=2 -n development

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api scaled
deployment.apps/celery-worker scaled
Example 4: Conditional Scaling with Current Replicas

Use --current-replicas to prevent race conditions. Only scale if the current count matches expected value.

Context - Prevent Conflicting Scale Operations:

# Scenario: Two operators scaling simultaneously could cause issues
# --current-replicas ensures we know the starting state

Commands:

# Only scale if currently at 3 replicas
kubectl scale deployment/django-api --current-replicas=3 --replicas=5 -n production

Output (success):

deployment.apps/django-api scaled

Output (replica mismatch):

error: Expected replicas to be 3, but the current replica count is 5

Script with Retry Logic:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/safe-scale.sh

DEPLOYMENT=$1
TARGET_REPLICAS=$2
NAMESPACE=${3:-production}
MAX_RETRIES=3

for i in $(seq 1 $MAX_RETRIES); do
    CURRENT=$(kubectl get deployment $DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}')
    
    if kubectl scale deployment/$DEPLOYMENT --current-replicas=$CURRENT --replicas=$TARGET_REPLICAS -n $NAMESPACE 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "✅ Scaled from $CURRENT to $TARGET_REPLICAS"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "⚠️  Retry $i: replica count changed during operation"
        sleep 1
    fi
done

echo "❌ Failed to scale after $MAX_RETRIES retries"
exit 1
Example 5: Scale StatefulSet with Ordered Scaling

StatefulSets scale pods in order (0, 1, 2...). Important for databases and distributed systems.

Context - PostgreSQL StatefulSet:

# k8s/statefulsets/postgresql.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgresql
  namespace: production
spec:
  serviceName: postgresql
  replicas: 3  # Primary + 2 replicas
  podManagementPolicy: OrderedReady  # Default - scale in order
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgresql
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgresql
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432

Commands:

# Scale PostgreSQL StatefulSet
kubectl scale statefulset/postgresql --replicas=5 -n production

# Watch ordered creation
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=postgresql -w

Output (pods created in order):

NAME           READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
postgresql-0   1/1     Running             0          5d
postgresql-1   1/1     Running             0          5d
postgresql-2   1/1     Running             0          5d
postgresql-3   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          5s
# (waits for postgresql-3 to be ready before creating postgresql-4)
postgresql-3   1/1     Running             0          30s
postgresql-4   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          5s
postgresql-4   1/1     Running             0          30s

Scale Down (reverse order):

# Scale down - pods removed in reverse order (4, 3, 2...)
kubectl scale statefulset/postgresql --replicas=3 -n production

# postgresql-4 terminated first, then postgresql-3
Example 6: Scheduled Scaling for Cost Optimization

Scale down during off-hours to reduce cloud costs. Common pattern for non-production environments.

Context - CronJobs for Scheduled Scaling:

# k8s/cronjobs/scale-down-night.yaml
# Scale down at 8 PM
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: scale-down-development
  namespace: development
spec:
  schedule: "0 20 * * 1-5"  # 8 PM Mon-Fri
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: scaler
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: kubectl
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              echo "🌙 Scaling down for night..."
              kubectl scale deployment --all --replicas=0 -n development

---
# k8s/cronjobs/scale-up-morning.yaml
# Scale up at 8 AM
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: scale-up-development
  namespace: development
spec:
  schedule: "0 8 * * 1-5"  # 8 AM Mon-Fri
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: scaler
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: kubectl
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              echo "☀️ Scaling up for workday..."
              kubectl scale deployment/django-api --replicas=2 -n development
              kubectl scale deployment/celery-worker --replicas=1 -n development

Verify with Manual Run:

# Trigger job manually to test
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/scale-down-development test-scale-down -n development

# Check job output
kubectl logs job/test-scale-down -n development

kubectl set

UPDATE RESOURCES CONFIGURE

Set specific features on objects - images, environment variables, resources, etc.

The kubectl set command provides targeted updates to specific aspects of resources without needing to edit entire manifests. It's ideal for CI/CD pipelines where you need to update individual fields like container images or environment variables imperative.

Subcommands

  • env: Update environment variables
  • image: Update container images
  • resources: Update resource requests/limits
  • selector: Update selector on a resource
  • serviceaccount: Update ServiceAccount on a pod template
  • subject: Update subjects in RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding

kubectl set image

UPDATE IMAGE DEPLOY

Update container image of a pod template

The kubectl set image command updates the container image for deployments, daemonsets, statefulsets, and other workloads. This is the most common way to deploy new application versions in CI/CD pipelines. Triggers a rolling update when applied to deployments.

Syntax

kubectl set image (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE [CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE...]

Parameters

  • TYPE NAME: Resource type and name (e.g., deployment/django-api)
  • CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE: Container name and new image
  • --all: Update all resources matching type
  • -l, --selector: Filter by label selector
  • --record: Record command in resource annotation (deprecated)

Examples

Example 1: Deploy New Version via CI/CD

The standard deployment pattern in most CI/CD pipelines. Update image and wait for rollout.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Build and Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: myregistry.azurecr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: django-api

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          push: true
          tags: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
  
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
      
      - name: Update image
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
            django=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }} \
            -n production
      
      - name: Wait for rollout
        run: |
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=5m

Command:

# Update single container
kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
    django=myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0 \
    -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api image updated
Example 2: Update Multiple Containers

Update multiple containers in a single command. Useful for multi-container pods.

Context - Multi-container Deployment:

# Deployment with Django app and Nginx sidecar
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.3.0
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
      - name: prometheus-exporter
        image: prom/statsd-exporter:v0.23.0

Command:

# Update multiple containers at once
kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
    django=myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0 \
    nginx=nginx:1.25 \
    -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api image updated

Update All Nginx Containers:

# Wildcard to update all containers with matching name
kubectl set image deployment --all nginx=nginx:1.25 -n production
Example 3: Rollback Using set image

Alternative to rollout undo - explicitly set the previous image version.

Command:

# Explicit rollback to known good version
kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
    django=myregistry/django-api:v2.3.0 \
    -n production

# Verify image was rolled back
kubectl get deployment django-api -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'

kubectl set env

UPDATE ENVIRONMENT CONFIGURE

Update environment variables on a pod template

The kubectl set env command adds, updates, or removes environment variables from pod templates. Useful for feature flags, configuration changes, and debugging without modifying manifest files.

Syntax

kubectl set env (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) ([--from=configmap|secret] | KEY=VALUE) [options]

Parameters

  • KEY=VALUE: Environment variable to set
  • KEY-: Remove environment variable
  • --from: Import from ConfigMap or Secret
  • --prefix: Prefix for imported variables
  • -c, --containers: Target specific containers (default: *)
  • --list: List current environment variables

Examples

Example 1: Enable Feature Flags

Toggle feature flags without editing manifests or ConfigMaps. Quick way to enable/disable features.

Commands:

# Enable feature flag
kubectl set env deployment/django-api \
    FEATURE_NEW_CHECKOUT=true \
    FEATURE_DARK_MODE=true \
    -n production

# Disable feature flag
kubectl set env deployment/django-api \
    FEATURE_NEW_CHECKOUT=false \
    -n production

# Remove feature flag entirely
kubectl set env deployment/django-api \
    FEATURE_DARK_MODE- \
    -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api env updated

Verify Environment Variables:

# List current env vars
kubectl set env deployment/django-api --list -n production

Output:

# deployment/django-api, container django
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings.production
DATABASE_URL from secret django-secrets, key database-url
FEATURE_NEW_CHECKOUT=true
Example 2: Import from ConfigMap or Secret

Import all keys from a ConfigMap or Secret as environment variables.

Commands:

# Import all keys from ConfigMap
kubectl set env deployment/django-api --from=configmap/django-settings -n production

# Import with prefix
kubectl set env deployment/django-api --from=configmap/feature-flags --prefix=FF_ -n production

# Import from Secret
kubectl set env deployment/django-api --from=secret/api-credentials -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api env updated
Example 3: Debug Mode Activation

Quick debugging by enabling verbose logging and debug settings.

Commands:

# Enable debug mode
kubectl set env deployment/django-api \
    DEBUG=true \
    LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG \
    SQL_DEBUG=true \
    -c django \
    -n production

# After debugging, restore normal settings
kubectl set env deployment/django-api \
    DEBUG=false \
    LOG_LEVEL=WARNING \
    SQL_DEBUG- \
    -c django \
    -n production

kubectl set resources

UPDATE RESOURCES ALLOCATE

Update resource requests and limits on containers

The kubectl set resources command updates CPU and memory requests and limits for containers. Essential for right-sizing workloads based on observed usage.

Syntax

kubectl set resources (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) --limits=LIMITS --requests=REQUESTS [options]

Parameters

  • --limits: Resource limits (cpu=X,memory=Y)
  • --requests: Resource requests (cpu=X,memory=Y)
  • -c, --containers: Target specific containers

Examples

Example 1: Right-Size Based on Metrics

Adjust resource allocations based on observed usage from monitoring.

Context - Prometheus Metrics Analysis:

# Query current usage (from Prometheus or kubectl top)
kubectl top pods -n production -l app=django-api
# NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    45m          180Mi
# django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    52m          175Mi
# django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    48m          185Mi

# Current limits are 500m CPU, 512Mi memory - underutilized

Command:

# Reduce limits based on actual usage
kubectl set resources deployment/django-api \
    --limits=cpu=200m,memory=256Mi \
    --requests=cpu=100m,memory=128Mi \
    -c django \
    -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api resource requirements updated
Example 2: Emergency Resource Increase

Quick increase during performance issues or OOMKill events.

Context - Investigating OOMKill:

# Check for OOMKill events
kubectl describe pods -l app=django-api -n production | grep -A5 "Last State"
#     Last State:     Terminated
#       Reason:       OOMKilled
#       Exit Code:    137

Command:

# Increase memory limits
kubectl set resources deployment/django-api \
    --limits=memory=1Gi \
    --requests=memory=512Mi \
    -c django \
    -n production

kubectl set serviceaccount

UPDATE IDENTITY CONFIGURE

Update service account of a pod template

The kubectl set serviceaccount command changes the ServiceAccount used by pods. Important for security when you need to grant or revoke permissions without modifying manifests.

Syntax

kubectl set serviceaccount (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) SERVICE_ACCOUNT

Examples

Example 1: Assign Dedicated ServiceAccount

Move from default ServiceAccount to a dedicated one with specific RBAC permissions.

Context - Create ServiceAccount with Permissions:

# k8s/rbac/django-api-sa.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    azure.workload.identity/client-id: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: django-api-role
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: django-api-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: django-api-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command:

# Apply ServiceAccount and RBAC
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac/django-api-sa.yaml

# Assign to deployment
kubectl set serviceaccount deployment/django-api django-api -n production

Output:

deployment.apps/django-api serviceaccount updated

kubectl set selector

UPDATE SELECTOR MODIFY

Set the selector on a resource (services, deployments)

The kubectl set selector command updates the selector on a service or other resource. Useful for routing traffic to different pod sets.

Syntax

kubectl set selector (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) EXPRESSIONS [--local] [-o OUTPUT]

Examples

Example 1: Blue-Green Traffic Switching

Switch service traffic between blue and green deployments using selector updates.

Commands:

# Switch traffic to green deployment
kubectl set selector service/django-api 'app=django-api,version=green' -n production

# Switch back to blue
kubectl set selector service/django-api 'app=django-api,version=blue' -n production

Output:

service/django-api selector updated

kubectl set subject

UPDATE RBAC AUTHORIZE

Update User, Group, or ServiceAccount in a RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding
