The kubectl set subject command modifies subjects (users, groups, service accounts) in RBAC bindings without recreating them.

Syntax

kubectl set subject (-f FILENAME | TYPE NAME) [--user=username] [--group=groupname] [--serviceaccount=namespace:name]

Examples

Example 1: Add Team Members to RoleBinding

Grant additional users access to namespace resources.

Commands:

# Add user to existing RoleBinding
kubectl set subject rolebinding developers \
    --user=newdeveloper@company.com \
    -n development

# Add a service account
kubectl set subject rolebinding ci-pipeline \
    --serviceaccount=ci-cd:github-actions \
    -n production

# Add a group
kubectl set subject clusterrolebinding cluster-admins \
    --group=platform-team@company.com

Output:

rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/developers subjects updated

kubectl wait

WAIT CONDITION BLOCK

Wait for a specific condition on one or multiple resources

The kubectl wait command blocks until a specified condition is met on resources. Essential for CI/CD pipelines to synchronize deployment steps, wait for jobs to complete, or ensure resources are ready before proceeding. Returns exit code 0 on success, non-zero on timeout or failure.

Syntax

kubectl wait (TYPE NAME | TYPE/NAME | -f FILENAME | -l selector) --for=CONDITION [options]

Parameters

  • --for: The condition to wait for (condition=Ready, delete, jsonpath=...)
  • --timeout: How long to wait (default: 30s, use 0 for infinite)
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter resources
  • --all: Wait on all resources of the type
  • -A, --all-namespaces: Search across all namespaces

Condition Types

  • condition=Ready: Pod is ready
  • condition=Available: Deployment is available
  • condition=Complete: Job completed successfully
  • condition=Established: CRD is established
  • delete: Resource is fully deleted
  • jsonpath='{.status.field}'=value: Custom field condition

Return Value

Exit code 0 when condition is met, 1 on timeout or error. Outputs the resource name when condition is satisfied.

Examples

Example 1: Wait for Pod Ready in CI/CD

Ensure pods are ready before running tests or proceeding to next deployment step.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy-and-test.yaml
name: Deploy and Test

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Deploy application
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/
      
      - name: Wait for pods to be ready
        run: |
          kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready \
            pod -l app=django-api \
            -n production \
            --timeout=300s
      
      - name: Run integration tests
        run: |
          kubectl exec deployment/django-api -n production -- \
            python manage.py test --tag=integration
      
      - name: Verify deployment
        run: |
          curl -f https://api.example.com/health/ || exit 1

Command:

# Wait for all pods with label to be ready
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready \
    pod -l app=django-api \
    -n production \
    --timeout=5m

Output:

pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 condition met
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34 condition met
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56 condition met
Example 2: Wait for Job Completion

Block until a job finishes, such as database migrations before starting application.

Context - Migration Job:

# k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: django-migration
  namespace: production
spec:
  backoffLimit: 3
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: migration
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--no-input"]
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: django-secrets

Deployment Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy-with-migration.sh

echo "🗄️  Running database migration..."

# Create migration job
kubectl apply -f k8s/jobs/db-migration.yaml

# Wait for migration to complete
if kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete \
    job/django-migration \
    -n production \
    --timeout=10m; then
    echo "✅ Migration successful"
    
    # Deploy application
    kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
    kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production
else
    echo "❌ Migration failed"
    kubectl logs job/django-migration -n production
    exit 1
fi

# Clean up job
kubectl delete job/django-migration -n production

Command:

# Wait for job to complete
kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete \
    job/django-migration \
    -n production \
    --timeout=10m

Output:

job.batch/django-migration condition met
Example 3: Wait for Resource Deletion

Ensure resources are fully deleted before recreating them or cleaning up.

Context - Force Recreation Workflow:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/recreate-deployment.sh

DEPLOYMENT=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}

echo "🗑️  Deleting old deployment..."
kubectl delete deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE

# Wait for complete deletion
echo "⏳ Waiting for deletion to complete..."
kubectl wait --for=delete deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=2m

echo "🚀 Creating new deployment..."
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/${DEPLOYMENT}.yaml

kubectl rollout status deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE

Command:

# Wait for pod deletion
kubectl wait --for=delete \
    pod -l app=django-api \
    -n production \
    --timeout=60s

Output:

pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 condition met
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34 condition met
Example 4: Wait with JSONPath for Custom Conditions

Wait for specific field values when standard conditions aren't sufficient.

Command - Wait for Specific Replica Count:

# Wait for deployment to have 5 ready replicas
kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}'=5 \
    deployment/django-api \
    -n production \
    --timeout=5m

Wait for Specific Phase:

# Wait for pod to be in Running phase
kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.phase}'=Running \
    pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 \
    -n production \
    --timeout=2m

Wait for Service External IP:

# Wait for LoadBalancer to get external IP (complex jsonpath)
kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}' \
    service/django-api \
    -n production \
    --timeout=5m
Example 5: Wait for CRD Before Applying Custom Resources

Ensure CRD is established before creating instances of that type.

Context - Installing Cert-Manager:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/install-cert-manager.sh

echo "📦 Installing Cert-Manager CRDs..."
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.13.0/cert-manager.crds.yaml

# Wait for CRDs to be established
echo "⏳ Waiting for CRDs to be ready..."
kubectl wait --for=condition=Established \
    crd/certificates.cert-manager.io \
    crd/issuers.cert-manager.io \
    crd/clusterissuers.cert-manager.io \
    --timeout=60s

echo "📦 Installing Cert-Manager..."
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.13.0/cert-manager.yaml

# Wait for cert-manager pods
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready \
    pod -l app.kubernetes.io/instance=cert-manager \
    -n cert-manager \
    --timeout=5m

echo "🔐 Creating ClusterIssuer..."
kubectl apply -f k8s/cert-manager/cluster-issuer.yaml

Command:

# Wait for multiple CRDs
kubectl wait --for=condition=Established \
    crd/certificates.cert-manager.io \
    crd/issuers.cert-manager.io \
    --timeout=60s

Output:

customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/certificates.cert-manager.io condition met
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/issuers.cert-manager.io condition met
Example 6: Complete CI/CD Pipeline with Wait

Full example showing wait commands integrated into a complete deployment pipeline.

Context - Complete Pipeline Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/full-deploy.sh
set -e  # Exit on any error

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
VERSION=${2:-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)}

echo "🚀 Starting deployment of version $VERSION to $NAMESPACE"

# Step 1: Run migrations
echo "📊 Step 1: Database migration"
envsubst < k8s/jobs/migration.yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete \
    job/django-migration-$VERSION \
    -n $NAMESPACE \
    --timeout=10m
echo "✅ Migration complete"

# Step 2: Update config
echo "⚙️  Step 2: Update configuration"
kubectl apply -f k8s/configmaps/
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready \
    pod -l app=django-api \
    -n $NAMESPACE \
    --timeout=30s || true  # Pods may not exist yet

# Step 3: Deploy application
echo "🐳 Step 3: Deploy application"
kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
    django=myregistry/django-api:$VERSION \
    -n $NAMESPACE
kubectl wait --for=condition=Available \
    deployment/django-api \
    -n $NAMESPACE \
    --timeout=5m
echo "✅ Application deployed"

# Step 4: Wait for all pods ready
echo "🏥 Step 4: Health check"
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready \
    pod -l app=django-api \
    -n $NAMESPACE \
    --timeout=2m

# Step 5: Run smoke tests
echo "🧪 Step 5: Smoke tests"
PODS=$(kubectl get pods -l app=django-api -n $NAMESPACE -o name | head -1)
kubectl exec $PODS -n $NAMESPACE -- python manage.py check --deploy

# Step 6: Cleanup
echo "🧹 Step 6: Cleanup"
kubectl delete job/django-migration-$VERSION -n $NAMESPACE --ignore-not-found

echo "🎉 Deployment complete!"
echo "Version $VERSION is now live in $NAMESPACE"

GitHub Actions Integration:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run full deployment
        env:
          VERSION: ${{ github.sha }}
        run: |
          chmod +x scripts/full-deploy.sh
          ./scripts/full-deploy.sh production $VERSION

🔍 WORKING WITH APPS

Commands for inspecting and debugging running applications. Includes log viewing, executing commands in containers, and monitoring resource usage for Django deployments.

kubectl attach

ATTACH PROCESS INTERACTIVE

Attach to a running container to interact with its primary process

The kubectl attach command attaches to an existing process running inside a container. Unlike exec which starts a new process, attach connects to the main container process (PID 1). Useful for interactive debugging, sending input to applications expecting stdin, or monitoring real-time output from console applications.

Syntax

kubectl attach POD -c CONTAINER [options]

Parameters

  • POD: Name of the pod to attach to
  • -c, --container: Container name (required if pod has multiple containers)
  • -i, --stdin: Pass stdin to the container
  • -t, --tty: Stdin is a TTY
  • -q, --quiet: Only print output from the remote session
  • --pod-running-timeout: Time to wait for pod to be running (default 1m0s)

Return Value

Returns the output stream from the container's main process. Exit code reflects the attachment status, not the container process.

Examples

Example 1: Attach to Django Development Server

Connect to a running Django development server to see real-time request logs and interact with debug output.

Context - Django Development Deployment:

# k8s/dev/django-dev-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-dev
  namespace: development
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-dev
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-dev
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:dev
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
        stdin: true      # Enable stdin for attach
        tty: true        # Allocate TTY for colored output
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DEBUG
          value: "True"
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: code
          mountPath: /app
      volumes:
      - name: code
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: django-code-pvc

Command:

# Attach to Django dev server to see request logs
kubectl attach django-dev-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c django -it -n development

Output:

Watching for file changes with StatReloader
Performing system checks...

System check identified no issues (0 silenced).
March 02, 2026 - 10:30:15
Django version 4.2, using settings 'anihpj.settings'
Starting development server at http://0.0.0.0:8000/
Quit the server with CONTROL-C.

[02/Mar/2026 10:31:22] "GET /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1" 200 4523
[02/Mar/2026 10:31:25] "POST /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1" 201 892
Example 2: Interactive Python REPL Container

Attach to a Python REPL container for interactive debugging and testing.

Context - Python Debug Pod:

# k8s/debug/python-repl.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: python-debug
  namespace: development
  labels:
    purpose: debugging
spec:
  containers:
  - name: python
    image: python:3.11
    command: ["python"]   # Start interactive Python
    stdin: true
    tty: true
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: django-secrets
          key: DATABASE_URL

Command:

# Attach to Python REPL
kubectl attach python-debug -c python -it -n development

Output:

Python 3.11.4 (main, Jun  9 2024, 07:20:50) [GCC 12.2.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import os
>>> os.environ.get('DATABASE_URL')
'postgresql://django:secret@postgres:5432/anihpj'
>>> # Test database connection
>>> import psycopg2
>>> conn = psycopg2.connect(os.environ['DATABASE_URL'])
>>> conn.status
1
Example 3: Monitor Celery Worker Output

Attach to a Celery worker to monitor task processing in real-time.

Context - Celery Worker Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/celery-worker.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: celery-worker
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: celery-worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-l", "INFO"]
        stdin: true
        tty: true
        env:
        - name: CELERY_BROKER_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: celery-secrets
              key: BROKER_URL
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"

Command:

# Attach to celery worker to monitor tasks
kubectl attach celery-worker-6b8f9c7d5-xyz89 -c celery -it -n production

Output:

 -------------- celery@celery-worker-6b8f9c7d5-xyz89 v5.3.0 (emerald-rush)
--- ***** ----- 
-- ******* ---- Linux-5.10.0-x86_64-with-glibc2.31 2026-03-02 10:45:00
- *** --- * --- 
- ** ---------- [config]
- ** ---------- .> app:         anihpj:0x7f8b2c1d4a50
- ** ---------- .> transport:   redis://redis:6379/0
- ** ---------- .> results:     redis://redis:6379/1
- *** --- * --- .> concurrency: 4 (prefork)
-- ******* ---- .> task events: OFF
--- ***** ----- 
 -------------- [queues]
                .> celery           exchange=celery(direct) key=celery

[tasks]
  . jobpost.tasks.send_job_notification
  . jobpost.tasks.process_job_application
  . jobpost.tasks.generate_report

[2026-03-02 10:45:32,123: INFO/MainProcess] celery@celery-worker ready.
[2026-03-02 10:45:45,456: INFO/ForkPoolWorker-2] Task jobpost.tasks.send_job_notification[abc123] received
[2026-03-02 10:45:45,789: INFO/ForkPoolWorker-2] Task jobpost.tasks.send_job_notification[abc123] succeeded in 0.333s
Example 4: Attach to Database CLI Container

Attach to a database client container for interactive SQL queries.

Context - PostgreSQL Client Pod:

# k8s/debug/psql-client.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: psql-client
  namespace: production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: psql
    image: postgres:15
    command: ["psql"]
    args: ["-h", "postgres-service", "-U", "django", "-d", "anihpj"]
    stdin: true
    tty: true
    env:
    - name: PGPASSWORD
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: postgres-secrets
          key: password

Command:

# Attach to PostgreSQL client
kubectl attach psql-client -c psql -it -n production

Output:

psql (15.4)
Type "help" for help.

anihpj=> \dt
              List of relations
 Schema |       Name        | Type  | Owner  
--------+-------------------+-------+--------
 public | auth_group        | table | django
 public | auth_user         | table | django
 public | jobpost_jobpost   | table | django
 public | jobpost_skill     | table | django
 public | jobpost_location  | table | django
(5 rows)

anihpj=> SELECT COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_jobpost WHERE is_active = true;
 count 
-------
   247
(1 row)
Example 5: Attach to Multi-Container Pod

Attach to a specific container in a pod with sidecars.

Context - Django with Nginx Sidecar:

# k8s/deployments/django-with-nginx.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        command: ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
        stdin: true
        tty: true
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        volumeMounts:
        - name: nginx-config
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
      - name: log-shipper
        image: fluent/fluent-bit:2.1
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /var/log/app
      volumes:
      - name: nginx-config
        configMap:
          name: nginx-config
      - name: logs
        emptyDir: {}

Command - Attach to Django:

# Must specify container with -c when pod has multiple containers
kubectl attach django-web-5c8f7b9d4-abc12 -c django -it -n production

Output:

[2026-03-02 10:50:00 +0000] [1] [INFO] Starting gunicorn 21.2.0
[2026-03-02 10:50:00 +0000] [1] [INFO] Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000 (1)
[2026-03-02 10:50:00 +0000] [1] [INFO] Using worker: sync
[2026-03-02 10:50:00 +0000] [8] [INFO] Booting worker with pid: 8
[2026-03-02 10:50:15 +0000] [8] [DEBUG] GET /api/jobs/

Verification - List Containers:

# Check available containers before attaching
kubectl get pod django-web-5c8f7b9d4-abc12 -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# django nginx log-shipper
Example 6: Script for Automated Attach with Timeout

Attach with timeout for CI/CD log capture scenarios.

Context - Log Capture Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/capture-startup-logs.sh
# Capture container startup logs for debugging

POD=$1
CONTAINER=${2:-django}
NAMESPACE=${3:-production}
TIMEOUT=${4:-30}

echo "📋 Capturing startup logs from $POD/$CONTAINER..."
echo "   Timeout: ${TIMEOUT}s"

# Attach with timeout to capture startup output
timeout $TIMEOUT kubectl attach $POD -c $CONTAINER -n $NAMESPACE 2>&1 | tee /tmp/startup-logs.txt

echo ""
echo "✅ Logs captured to /tmp/startup-logs.txt"
echo "   Lines captured: $(wc -l < /tmp/startup-logs.txt)"

Command:

# Run capture script
./scripts/capture-startup-logs.sh django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 django production 30

Output:

📋 Capturing startup logs from django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12/django...
   Timeout: 30s
[2026-03-02 10:55:00 +0000] [1] [INFO] Starting gunicorn 21.2.0
[2026-03-02 10:55:00 +0000] [1] [INFO] Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000 (1)
[2026-03-02 10:55:01 +0000] [8] [INFO] Booting worker with pid: 8

✅ Logs captured to /tmp/startup-logs.txt
   Lines captured: 4

kubectl auth

AUTH RBAC INSPECT

Inspect authorization and authentication configuration

The kubectl auth command provides tools for inspecting authorization. Check if actions are allowed, reconcile RBAC rules, and verify your identity. Essential for troubleshooting permission issues, auditing access controls, and implementing least-privilege security in Django deployments.

Subcommands Overview

  • can-i: Check if an action is allowed
  • reconcile: Reconcile RBAC rules from a file
  • whoami: Show current user identity

kubectl auth can-i

CHECK PERMISSION VERIFY

Check whether an action is allowed in the cluster

The kubectl auth can-i command checks if the current user (or specified user/serviceaccount) is authorized to perform a specific action on a resource. Returns "yes" or "no" with exit codes. Critical for debugging RBAC issues and validating security policies.

Syntax

kubectl auth can-i VERB [TYPE | TYPE/NAME | NONRESOURCEURL] [options]

Parameters

  • VERB: Action to check (get, list, create, delete, update, patch, watch)
  • TYPE: Resource type (pods, deployments, secrets, etc.)
  • --as: Username to impersonate for the check
  • --as-group: Group to impersonate
  • --list: List all allowed actions
  • -A, --all-namespaces: Check across all namespaces
  • --subresource: SubResource such as pod/logs or deployment/scale

Return Value

"yes" (exit 0) if allowed, "no" (exit 1) if denied. With --list, shows all allowed actions.

Examples

Example 1: Check Developer Permissions

Verify what a developer can do in the production namespace before granting additional access.

Context - RBAC Configuration:

# k8s/rbac/developer-role.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "services", "configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# Note: No create/delete/update permissions
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: developer-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: john@company.com
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command - Check Permissions:

# Check if developer can read pods
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n production --as=john@company.com

# Check if developer can delete pods (should be denied)
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n production --as=john@company.com

# Check if developer can create deployments
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n production --as=john@company.com

Output:

yes
no
no
Example 2: Audit ServiceAccount Permissions

Verify that CI/CD service accounts have only necessary permissions.

Context - CI/CD ServiceAccount:

# k8s/rbac/cicd-serviceaccount.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: github-actions
  namespace: production
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deployer
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  verbs: ["get"]  # Only read, not create/modify
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: deployer-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: github-actions
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: deployer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command - Security Audit:

# Check deployment permissions (should be yes)
kubectl auth can-i update deployments -n production \
    --as=system:serviceaccount:production:github-actions

# Check secret creation (should be no - security)
kubectl auth can-i create secrets -n production \
    --as=system:serviceaccount:production:github-actions

# Check delete permissions (should be no)
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments -n production \
    --as=system:serviceaccount:production:github-actions

Output:

yes
no
no
Example 3: List All Allowed Actions

Get a complete list of what the current user can do in a namespace.

Command:

# List all permissions in production namespace
kubectl auth can-i --list -n production

Output:

Resources                                       Non-Resource URLs   Resource Names   Verbs
pods                                            []                  []               [get list watch]
pods/log                                        []                  []               [get list watch]
services                                        []                  []               [get list watch]
deployments.apps                                []                  []               [get list watch update patch]
configmaps                                      []                  []               [get list watch]
secrets                                         []                  []               [get]
selfsubjectaccessreviews.authorization.k8s.io   []                  []               [create]
selfsubjectrulesreviews.authorization.k8s.io    []                  []               [create]
Example 4: Pre-Deployment Permission Validation

CI/CD pipeline step to verify permissions before attempting deployment.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy to Production

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      
      - name: Validate permissions
        run: |
          echo "🔐 Validating deployment permissions..."
          
          # Required permissions
          REQUIRED_PERMS=(
            "get deployments"
            "update deployments"
            "get pods"
            "get secrets"
          )
          
          for perm in "${REQUIRED_PERMS[@]}"; do
            verb=$(echo $perm | cut -d' ' -f1)
            resource=$(echo $perm | cut -d' ' -f2)
            
            if kubectl auth can-i $verb $resource -n production; then
              echo "✅ Can $perm"
            else
              echo "❌ Cannot $perm - deployment will fail"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
          
          echo "✅ All permissions validated"
      
      - name: Deploy
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/ -n production

Command:

# Quick permission validation script
for action in "get pods" "update deployments" "get secrets"; do
    verb=$(echo $action | cut -d' ' -f1)
    resource=$(echo $action | cut -d' ' -f2)
    result=$(kubectl auth can-i $verb $resource -n production)
    echo "$action: $result"
done

Output:

get pods: yes
update deployments: yes
get secrets: yes
Example 5: Check Subresource Permissions

Verify access to subresources like logs, exec, and scale.

Command:

# Check pod log access
kubectl auth can-i get pods --subresource=log -n production

# Check exec into pods
kubectl auth can-i create pods --subresource=exec -n production

# Check deployment scaling
kubectl auth can-i update deployments --subresource=scale -n production

# Check port-forward permission
kubectl auth can-i create pods --subresource=portforward -n production

Output:

yes
yes
yes
yes

kubectl auth reconcile

SYNC RBAC RECONCILE

Reconciles rules for RBAC roles, rolebindings, clusterroles, and clusterrolebindings

The kubectl auth reconcile command applies RBAC resources with special handling for role aggregation. Unlike kubectl apply, it properly handles role additions without removing existing rules, making it safe for incremental RBAC updates in production.

Syntax

kubectl auth reconcile -f FILENAME [options]

Parameters

  • -f, --filename: File containing RBAC resources
  • --remove-extra-permissions: Remove permissions not in manifest
  • --remove-extra-subjects: Remove subjects not in manifest
  • --dry-run: Preview changes without applying

Examples

Example 1: Safe RBAC Updates

Reconcile RBAC rules without accidentally removing existing permissions.

Context - RBAC Manifest:

# k8s/rbac/application-roles.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: django-operator
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "services", "configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "delete"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: ["batch"]
  resources: ["jobs", "cronjobs"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "delete"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: django-operator-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: django-operator
  namespace: production
- kind: User
  name: platform-team@company.com
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: django-operator
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command:

# Reconcile RBAC - adds new rules without removing existing
kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/application-roles.yaml

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/django-operator reconciled
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/django-operator-binding reconciled
Example 2: Dry Run RBAC Changes

Preview RBAC changes before applying them.

Command:

# Preview what would change
kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/application-roles.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml
Example 3: Reconcile with Remove-Extra-Permissions

Remove permissions that are no longer in the manifest.

Command:

# Remove extra permissions not in manifest
kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/application-roles.yaml --remove-extra-permissions

# Remove extra subjects not in manifest
kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/application-roles.yaml --remove-extra-subjects

Output:

role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/django-operator reconciled
removed permission: ["pods/exec"] from role django-operator
Example 4: Reconcile Cluster-Wide RBAC

Update ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings.

Context - Cluster RBAC Manifest:

# k8s/rbac/cluster-roles.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: namespace-viewer
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["namespaces"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-viewer-binding
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: developers
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: namespace-viewer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Command:

kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/cluster-roles.yaml

Output:

clusterrole.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/namespace-viewer reconciled
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/namespace-viewer-binding reconciled
Example 5: CI/CD RBAC Reconciliation

Integrate RBAC reconciliation into deployment pipeline.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy-rbac.yml
name: Deploy RBAC

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'k8s/rbac/**'

jobs:
  deploy-rbac:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    
    - name: Setup kubectl
      uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
    
    - name: Reconcile RBAC
      run: |
        # Dry run first
        kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/ --dry-run=server
        
        # Apply if dry run succeeds
        kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/

Command:

# Reconcile all RBAC files in directory
kubectl auth reconcile -f k8s/rbac/

kubectl auth whoami

IDENTITY USER VERIFY

Display the current user's identity and authentication information

The kubectl auth whoami command shows the current authenticated user, including username, groups, and UID. Essential for troubleshooting authentication issues and verifying which identity is being used across different contexts.

Syntax

kubectl auth whoami [options]

Parameters

  • -o, --output: Output format (yaml, json)

Examples

Example 1: Verify Current Identity

Check which user/identity is currently authenticated to the cluster.

Context - Multi-Cluster Setup:

# ~/.kube/config (partial)
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: production-cluster
    user: admin@company.com
  name: production
- context:
    cluster: staging-cluster
    user: developer@company.com
  name: staging
current-context: production

Command:

# Check current identity
kubectl auth whoami

Output:

ATTRIBUTE         VALUE
Username          admin@company.com
Groups            [system:authenticated system:masters]
Example 2: ServiceAccount Identity in Pod

Verify identity when running inside a Kubernetes pod.

Context - Pod with ServiceAccount:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: django-sa
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0

Command - Inside Pod:

# From inside the pod
kubectl auth whoami -o yaml

Output:

apiVersion: authentication.k8s.io/v1
kind: SelfSubjectReview
status:
  userInfo:
    username: system:serviceaccount:production:django-sa
    uid: 12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012
    groups:
    - system:serviceaccounts
    - system:serviceaccounts:production
    - system:authenticated
Example 3: JSON Output for Scripting

Get identity details in JSON format for automation scripts.

Command:

# Get identity as JSON for scripting
kubectl auth whoami -o json | jq '.status.userInfo.username'

Output:

"admin@company.com"
Example 4: Compare Identity Across Contexts

Verify identity when switching between cluster contexts.

Context - Multi-Cluster Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-identity-all-contexts.sh

echo "=== Identity Check Across All Contexts ==="
for context in $(kubectl config get-contexts -o name); do
    echo ""
    echo "Context: $context"
    kubectl auth whoami --context=$context 2>/dev/null || echo "  Unable to authenticate"
done

Command:

# Check identity in specific context
kubectl auth whoami --context=staging

# Run identity check script
./scripts/check-identity-all-contexts.sh

Output:

=== Identity Check Across All Contexts ===

Context: production
ATTRIBUTE         VALUE
Username          admin@company.com
Groups            [system:authenticated system:masters]

Context: staging
ATTRIBUTE         VALUE
Username          developer@company.com
Groups            [system:authenticated developers]
Example 5: Troubleshoot Authentication Issues

Debug authentication problems by examining the full identity response.

Command:

# Full YAML output for debugging
kubectl auth whoami -o yaml

# Check if using correct kubeconfig
KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/prod-config kubectl auth whoami

Output:

apiVersion: authentication.k8s.io/v1
kind: SelfSubjectReview
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: null
status:
  userInfo:
    extra:
      auth.gke.io/user_type:
      - gcloud
    groups:
    - system:authenticated
    username: admin@company.com

kubectl cp

COPY FILES TRANSFER

Copy files and directories to and from containers

The kubectl cp command copies files and directories between the local filesystem and containers. Uses tar under the hood, requiring tar to be present in the container. Essential for extracting logs, uploading configuration files, or retrieving debug artifacts from running pods.

Syntax

# Copy to pod
kubectl cp /local/path namespace/pod:/container/path

# Copy from pod
kubectl cp namespace/pod:/container/path /local/path

# With container specified
kubectl cp /local/path namespace/pod:/container/path -c container-name

Parameters

  • -c, --container: Container name if pod has multiple containers
  • --no-preserve: Don't preserve ownership and permissions
  • --retries: Number of retries for transient errors (default 0)

Return Value

Exit code 0 on success. Silently succeeds unless errors occur.

Examples

Example 1: Extract Django Logs for Analysis

Copy application logs from a container for local analysis and archival.

Context - Django Deployment with Logging:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        volumeMounts:
        - name: logs
          mountPath: /app/logs
        env:
        - name: LOG_DIR
          value: "/app/logs"
      volumes:
      - name: logs
        emptyDir: {}

Django Logging Config:

# anihpj/settings.py
LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'handlers': {
        'file': {
            'class': 'logging.FileHandler',
            'filename': '/app/logs/django.log',
        },
    },
    'loggers': {
        'django': {
            'handlers': ['file'],
            'level': 'INFO',
        },
    },
}

Command:

# Copy logs from pod to local machine
kubectl cp production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12:/app/logs/django.log \
    ./debug/django-$(date +%Y%m%d).log

Verification:

ls -la ./debug/
# -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 245678 Mar  2 10:30 django-20260302.log

head -20 ./debug/django-20260302.log
Example 2: Upload Configuration Files

Upload configuration or data files to a running container for hot-reload.

Context - Local Config File:

// config/feature-flags.json
{
  "enable_new_job_search": true,
  "enable_ai_matching": false,
  "max_applications_per_day": 10,
  "beta_users": ["user1@test.com", "user2@test.com"]
}

Command:

# Upload feature flags to running container
kubectl cp config/feature-flags.json \
    production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12:/app/config/feature-flags.json

# Verify upload
kubectl exec -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -- \
    cat /app/config/feature-flags.json

Output:

{
  "enable_new_job_search": true,
  "enable_ai_matching": false,
  "max_applications_per_day": 10,
  "beta_users": ["user1@test.com", "user2@test.com"]
}
Example 3: Copy Entire Directory

Copy a complete directory structure from a container for debugging.

Context - Debug Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/extract-debug-bundle.sh

POD=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}
OUTPUT_DIR="./debug-bundle-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"

mkdir -p $OUTPUT_DIR

echo "📦 Extracting debug bundle from $POD..."

# Copy application logs
kubectl cp $NAMESPACE/$POD:/app/logs $OUTPUT_DIR/logs -c django

# Copy temporary files
kubectl cp $NAMESPACE/$POD:/tmp/debug $OUTPUT_DIR/tmp -c django 2>/dev/null || true

# Copy static analysis results
kubectl cp $NAMESPACE/$POD:/app/reports $OUTPUT_DIR/reports -c django 2>/dev/null || true

echo "✅ Debug bundle extracted to $OUTPUT_DIR"
ls -la $OUTPUT_DIR/

Command:

# Copy entire logs directory
kubectl cp production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12:/app/logs ./extracted-logs/

Output:

$ ls -la ./extracted-logs/
total 512
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user   4096 Mar  2 10:35 .
drwxr-xr-x 5 user user   4096 Mar  2 10:35 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 245678 Mar  2 10:30 django.log
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user  45678 Mar  2 10:30 celery.log
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user  12345 Mar  2 10:30 access.log
Example 4: Copy Database Dump

Create and extract a database backup from a PostgreSQL container.

Context - Database Backup Job:

# k8s/jobs/db-backup.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: db-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: backup
        image: postgres:15
        command:
        - /bin/bash
        - -c
        - |
          pg_dump -h postgres-service -U django anihpj > /backup/dump.sql
          echo "Backup completed: $(wc -l < /backup/dump.sql) lines"
        env:
        - name: PGPASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-secrets
              key: password
        volumeMounts:
        - name: backup
          mountPath: /backup
      volumes:
      - name: backup
        emptyDir: {}

Command:

# Run backup job
kubectl apply -f k8s/jobs/db-backup.yaml
kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete job/db-backup -n production --timeout=5m

# Get pod name
BACKUP_POD=$(kubectl get pods -n production -l job-name=db-backup -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# Copy dump file
kubectl cp production/$BACKUP_POD:/backup/dump.sql ./backups/anihpj-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

# Cleanup
kubectl delete job db-backup -n production

Output:

job.batch/db-backup created
job.batch/db-backup condition met
$ ls -la ./backups/
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 5234567 Mar  2 10:40 anihpj-20260302.sql
Example 5: Copy from Multi-Container Pod

Copy files from a specific container in a pod with sidecars.

Context - Pod with Multiple Containers:

# k8s/deployments/django-with-sidecars.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        volumeMounts:
        - name: shared
          mountPath: /app/shared
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
        volumeMounts:
        - name: nginx-logs
          mountPath: /var/log/nginx
      - name: fluentbit
        image: fluent/fluent-bit:2.1
        volumeMounts:
        - name: shared
          mountPath: /shared
      volumes:
      - name: shared
        emptyDir: {}
      - name: nginx-logs
        emptyDir: {}

Command:

# Copy from django container
kubectl cp production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12:/app/shared/data.json ./data.json \
    -c django

# Copy nginx access logs
kubectl cp production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12:/var/log/nginx/access.log ./nginx-access.log \
    -c nginx
Example 6: Bulk File Transfer Script

Automated script for collecting diagnostic files from multiple pods.

Context - Diagnostic Collection Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/collect-diagnostics.sh

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
APP_LABEL=${2:-app=django-api}
OUTPUT_DIR="./diagnostics-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"

mkdir -p $OUTPUT_DIR

echo "🔍 Collecting diagnostics from $NAMESPACE pods with label $APP_LABEL"

# Get all matching pods
PODS=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l $APP_LABEL -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}')

for POD in $PODS; do
    echo "📥 Collecting from $POD..."
    mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR/$POD"
    
    # Copy logs
    kubectl cp $NAMESPACE/$POD:/app/logs "$OUTPUT_DIR/$POD/logs" -c django 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Copy config (non-sensitive)
    kubectl cp $NAMESPACE/$POD:/app/config "$OUTPUT_DIR/$POD/config" -c django 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Get process list
    kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c django -- ps aux > "$OUTPUT_DIR/$POD/processes.txt" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "  ✅ Done"
done

# Create archive
tar -czf "$OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz" "$OUTPUT_DIR"
echo ""
echo "📦 Diagnostics saved to $OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz"
echo "   Size: $(du -h "$OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz" | cut -f1)"

Command:

./scripts/collect-diagnostics.sh production app=django-api

Output:

🔍 Collecting diagnostics from production pods with label app=django-api
📥 Collecting from django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12...
  ✅ Done
📥 Collecting from django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34...
  ✅ Done
📥 Collecting from django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56...
  ✅ Done

📦 Diagnostics saved to diagnostics-20260302-104500.tar.gz
   Size: 2.3M

kubectl describe

DESCRIBE DETAILS INSPECT

Show detailed information about a specific resource or group of resources

The kubectl describe command provides detailed information about Kubernetes resources including events, conditions, and configuration. Unlike kubectl get which shows tabular data, describe gives a human-readable narrative of the resource state. Essential for debugging issues like pod failures, service connectivity, and deployment problems.

Syntax

kubectl describe TYPE [NAME_PREFIX | -l label] [options]

Parameters

  • TYPE: Resource type (pod, deployment, service, node, etc.)
  • NAME: Resource name or prefix
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter resources
  • -A, --all-namespaces: Search across all namespaces
  • --show-events: Show events (default true)

Return Value

Human-readable detailed description including metadata, spec, status, conditions, and events.

Examples

Example 1: Debug Pod Crash with Describe

Diagnose why a Django pod is crashing by examining its events and conditions.

Context - Problematic Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml (with error)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"  # Too low - OOMKilled
            cpu: "500m"
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: DATABASE_URL

Command:

# Describe the crashing pod
kubectl describe pod django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -n production

Output:

Name:             django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12
Namespace:        production
Priority:         0
Service Account:  default
Node:             node-1/10.0.1.5
Start Time:       Mon, 02 Mar 2026 10:00:00 +0000
Labels:           app=django-api
                  pod-template-hash=7d9f8b6c4
Status:           Running
IP:               10.244.1.15
Controlled By:    ReplicaSet/django-api-7d9f8b6c4

Containers:
  django:
    Container ID:   containerd://abc123...
    Image:          myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
    State:          Running
      Started:      Mon, 02 Mar 2026 10:05:00 +0000
    Last State:     Terminated
      Reason:       OOMKilled
      Exit Code:    137
      Started:      Mon, 02 Mar 2026 10:00:00 +0000
      Finished:     Mon, 02 Mar 2026 10:04:55 +0000
    Ready:          True
    Restart Count:  3
    Limits:
      cpu:     500m
      memory:  512Mi
    Requests:
      cpu:     250m
      memory:  256Mi
    Environment:
      DATABASE_URL:  <set to the key 'DATABASE_URL' in secret 'django-secrets'>

Conditions:
  Type              Status
  Initialized       True
  Ready             True
  ContainersReady   True
  PodScheduled      True

Events:
  Type     Reason     Age                  From               Message
  ----     ------     ----                 ----               -------
  Normal   Scheduled  10m                  default-scheduler  Successfully assigned production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 to node-1
  Normal   Pulled     5m (x4 over 10m)     kubelet            Container image "myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0" already present on machine
  Normal   Created    5m (x4 over 10m)     kubelet            Created container django
  Normal   Started    5m (x4 over 10m)     kubelet            Started container django
  Warning  OOMKilled  5m (x3 over 9m)      kubelet            Container django was OOMKilled

Diagnosis: The "OOMKilled" reason shows the container exceeded its 512Mi memory limit. Solution: Increase memory limits.

Example 2: Debug Service Connectivity

Troubleshoot why a service isn't routing traffic to pods.

Context - Service Configuration:

# k8s/services/django-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: django-api   # Must match pod labels
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  type: ClusterIP

Command:

# Describe service to check endpoints
kubectl describe service django-api -n production

Output:

Name:              django-api
Namespace:         production
Labels:            <none>
Annotations:       <none>
Selector:          app=django-api
Type:              ClusterIP
IP Family Policy:  SingleStack
IP Families:       IPv4
IP:                10.96.45.123
IPs:               10.96.45.123
Port:              <unset>  80/TCP
TargetPort:        8000/TCP
Endpoints:         10.244.1.15:8000,10.244.1.16:8000,10.244.2.20:8000
Session Affinity:  None
Events:            <none>

Verification: Endpoints show 3 pod IPs, confirming service is correctly selecting pods.

Example 3: Debug Image Pull Failures

Diagnose ImagePullBackOff errors when deploying new images.

Context - Private Registry Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: registry-credentials  # Missing or incorrect
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0

Command:

kubectl describe pod django-api-7d9f8b6c4-xyz89 -n production

Output (partial):

Name:             django-api-7d9f8b6c4-xyz89
Namespace:        production
Status:           Pending

Containers:
  django:
    Image:          myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0
    State:          Waiting
      Reason:       ImagePullBackOff
    Ready:          False

Events:
  Type     Reason     Age                From               Message
  ----     ------     ----               ----               -------
  Normal   Scheduled  2m                 default-scheduler  Successfully assigned production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-xyz89 to node-1
  Normal   Pulling    90s (x4 over 2m)   kubelet            Pulling image "myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0"
  Warning  Failed     85s (x4 over 2m)   kubelet            Failed to pull image "myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0": unauthorized: authentication required
  Warning  Failed     85s (x4 over 2m)   kubelet            Error: ErrImagePull
  Normal   BackOff    70s (x6 over 2m)   kubelet            Back-off pulling image "myregistry.azurecr.io/django-api:v2.4.0"
  Warning  Failed     70s (x6 over 2m)   kubelet            Error: ImagePullBackOff

Fix:

# Create/fix imagePullSecret
kubectl create secret docker-registry registry-credentials \
    --docker-server=myregistry.azurecr.io \
    --docker-username=$ACR_USERNAME \
    --docker-password=$ACR_PASSWORD \
    -n production

Check deployment conditions and replica status during rollouts.

Command:

Output:

Namespace:              production

Annotations:            deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: 5
                        kubernetes.io/change-cause: Update to v2.4.0
Selector:               app=django-api
Replicas:               3 desired | 3 updated | 3 total | 3 available | 0 unavailable

  Service Account:  django-sa

    Image:        myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0

    Readiness:  http-get http://:8000/ready/ delay=5s timeout=3s period=5s

  Normal  ScalingReplicaSet  4m    deployment-controller  Scaled down replica set django-api-6c8f7b9d4 to 2
  Normal  ScalingReplicaSet  4m    deployment-controller  Scaled up replica set django-api-7d9f8b6c4 to 2

Example 5: Describe Node for Scheduling Issues

Check node capacity and conditions when pods are stuck in Pending.

Command:

kubectl describe node node-1

Output (partial):

Name:               node-1
Roles:              <none>
Labels:             kubernetes.io/hostname=node-1
                    node.kubernetes.io/instance-type=Standard_D4s_v3
                    topology.kubernetes.io/zone=eastus-1
Annotations:        node.alpha.kubernetes.io/ttl: 0

CreationTimestamp:  Mon, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 +0000
Taints:             <none>

Conditions:
  Type             Status  LastHeartbeatTime                 Reason
  ----             ------  -----------------                 ------
  MemoryPressure   False   Mon, 02 Mar 2026 11:00:00 +0000   KubeletHasSufficientMemory
  DiskPressure     False   Mon, 02 Mar 2026 11:00:00 +0000   KubeletHasNoDiskPressure
  PIDPressure      False   Mon, 02 Mar 2026 11:00:00 +0000   KubeletHasSufficientPID

  pods:               110

  cpu:                3920m
  memory:             15928576Ki

  (Total limits may be over 100 percent, i.e., overcommitted.)
  Resource           Requests      Limits
  --------           --------      ------
  cpu                3100m (79%)   5500m (140%)
  memory             12Gi (77%)    18Gi (115%)

Events:              <none>

Diagnosis: Node is overcommitted (140% CPU limits). New pods may be unable to schedule.

Example 6: Describe PersistentVolumeClaim

Debug storage binding issues for stateful applications.

Context - PVC Configuration:

# k8s/storage/postgres-pvc.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-data
  namespace: production
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: managed-premium
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 100Gi

Command:

kubectl describe pvc postgres-data -n production

Output:

Name:          postgres-data
Namespace:     production
StorageClass:  managed-premium
Status:        Bound
Volume:        pvc-12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012
Labels:        <none>
Annotations:   pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed: yes
               pv.kubernetes.io/bound-by-controller: yes
               volume.beta.kubernetes.io/storage-provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com
Finalizers:    [kubernetes.io/pvc-protection]
Capacity:      100Gi
Access Modes:  RWO
VolumeMode:    Filesystem
Used By:       postgres-0

Events:
  Type    Reason                 Age   From                         Message
  ----    ------                 ----  ----                         -------
  Normal  Provisioning           10m   disk.csi.azure.com           External provisioner is provisioning volume
  Normal  ProvisioningSucceeded  9m    disk.csi.azure.com           Successfully provisioned volume pvc-12345678...

kubectl events

EVENTS STREAM MONITOR

List and watch cluster events in real-time

The kubectl events command displays events from the cluster, providing insights into what's happening with resources. Events include scheduling decisions, container starts/stops, errors, and warnings. Essential for monitoring cluster health, debugging issues, and understanding resource lifecycle.

Syntax

kubectl events [--for TYPE/NAME] [--watch] [options]

Parameters

  • --for: Filter events for a specific resource (TYPE/NAME)
  • -w, --watch: Watch for new events in real-time
  • --types: Filter by event type (Normal, Warning)
  • -A, --all-namespaces: Show events from all namespaces
  • --no-headers: Don't print headers

Return Value

List of events with timestamp, type, reason, object, and message. With --watch, streams events continuously.

Examples

Example 1: Monitor Deployment Events

Watch events during a Django deployment to catch issues early.

Context - Deployment Workflow:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy-with-monitoring.sh

echo "🚀 Starting deployment..."

# Start event watcher in background
kubectl events --for=deployment/django-api -n production -w &
EVENTS_PID=$!

# Perform deployment
kubectl set image deployment/django-api \
    django=myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0 \
    -n production

# Wait for rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=5m

# Stop event watcher
kill $EVENTS_PID 2>/dev/null

echo "✅ Deployment complete"

Command:

# Watch events for deployment
kubectl events --for=deployment/django-api -n production -w

Output:

LAST SEEN   TYPE     REASON              OBJECT                         MESSAGE
2m          Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled up replica set django-api-7d9f8b6c4 to 1
90s         Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled down replica set django-api-6c8f7b9d4 to 2
90s         Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled up replica set django-api-7d9f8b6c4 to 2
60s         Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled down replica set django-api-6c8f7b9d4 to 1
60s         Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled up replica set django-api-7d9f8b6c4 to 3
30s         Normal   ScalingReplicaSet   deployment/django-api          Scaled down replica set django-api-6c8f7b9d4 to 0
Example 2: Filter Warning Events

Show only warning events to quickly identify problems.

Command:

# Show only warnings in production namespace
kubectl events --types=Warning -n production

Output:

LAST SEEN   TYPE      REASON           OBJECT                              MESSAGE
5m          Warning   OOMKilled        pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Container django exceeded memory limit
3m          Warning   FailedMount      pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34      MountVolume.SetUp failed for volume "config"
2m          Warning   Unhealthy        pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56      Readiness probe failed: HTTP 503
1m          Warning   BackOff          pod/celery-worker-6b8f9c7d5-xyz89   Back-off restarting failed container
Example 3: Cluster-Wide Event Monitoring

Monitor all events across the cluster for operations visibility.

Context - Monitoring Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/event-monitor.sh
# Real-time cluster event monitoring for on-call

NAMESPACE=${1:---all-namespaces}
FILTER=${2:-}

echo "📡 Monitoring cluster events..."
echo "   Namespace: $NAMESPACE"
echo "   Filter: ${FILTER:-all}"
echo "   Press Ctrl+C to stop"
echo ""

if [ -n "$FILTER" ]; then
    kubectl events $NAMESPACE -w --types=$FILTER
else
    kubectl events $NAMESPACE -w
fi

Command:

# Watch all events cluster-wide
kubectl events -A -w

Output:

NAMESPACE    LAST SEEN   TYPE     REASON     OBJECT                          MESSAGE
production   10s         Normal   Scheduled  pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-new1   Successfully assigned production/django-api...
production   8s          Normal   Pulled     pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-new1   Container image already present on machine
production   7s          Normal   Created    pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-new1   Created container django
production   6s          Normal   Started    pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-new1   Started container django
staging      5s          Normal   Killing    pod/test-runner-abc12           Stopping container test
kube-system  2s          Normal   NodeReady  node/node-3                     Node node-3 status is now: NodeReady
Example 4: Events for Specific Pod

Get all events related to a specific pod for debugging.

Context - Debugging Session:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/debug-pod.sh

POD=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}

echo "🔍 Debugging pod: $POD"
echo ""

echo "=== Pod Status ==="
kubectl get pod $POD -n $NAMESPACE -o wide

echo ""
echo "=== Pod Events ==="
kubectl events --for=pod/$POD -n $NAMESPACE

echo ""
echo "=== Recent Logs ==="
kubectl logs $POD -n $NAMESPACE --tail=20

Command:

# Get events for specific pod
kubectl events --for=pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -n production

Output:

LAST SEEN   TYPE     REASON      OBJECT                              MESSAGE
10m         Normal   Scheduled   pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Successfully assigned production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 to node-1
9m          Normal   Pulled      pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Container image "myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0" already present
9m          Normal   Created     pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Created container django
9m          Normal   Started     pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Started container django
5m          Warning  Unhealthy   pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Readiness probe failed: HTTP probe failed with statuscode: 503
4m          Normal   Pulling     pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12      Pulling image "myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0"
Example 5: Event Alerting Integration

Forward warning events to alerting system for incident response.

Context - Event Forwarder Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/event-alerter.sh
# Forward warning events to Slack/PagerDuty

WEBHOOK_URL=$SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
NAMESPACE=${1:-production}

echo "🚨 Starting event alerter for $NAMESPACE"

kubectl events --types=Warning -n $NAMESPACE -w --no-headers | while read line; do
    TIMESTAMP=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}')
    REASON=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $3}')
    OBJECT=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $4}')
    MESSAGE=$(echo "$line" | cut -d' ' -f5-)
    
    # Send to Slack
    curl -s -X POST $WEBHOOK_URL \
        -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
        -d "{
            \"text\": \"⚠️ K8s Warning Event\",
            \"attachments\": [{
                \"color\": \"warning\",
                \"fields\": [
                    {\"title\": \"Object\", \"value\": \"$OBJECT\", \"short\": true},
                    {\"title\": \"Reason\", \"value\": \"$REASON\", \"short\": true},
                    {\"title\": \"Message\", \"value\": \"$MESSAGE\"}
                ]
            }]
        }"
    
    echo "📤 Alert sent: $REASON on $OBJECT"
done

Command:

# Run event alerter
./scripts/event-alerter.sh production
Example 6: GitHub Actions CI/CD Event Monitoring

Integrate event monitoring into deployment pipelines.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yaml
name: Deploy with Event Monitoring

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      
      - name: Deploy application
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
      
      - name: Monitor deployment events
        run: |
          echo "📊 Monitoring deployment events for 60 seconds..."
          timeout 60 kubectl events --for=deployment/django-api -n production -w || true
      
      - name: Check for warning events
        run: |
          WARNINGS=$(kubectl events --types=Warning -n production --no-headers | wc -l)
          if [ "$WARNINGS" -gt 0 ]; then
            echo "⚠️  Found $WARNINGS warning events:"
            kubectl events --types=Warning -n production
          else
            echo "✅ No warning events found"
          fi
      
      - name: Verify deployment
        run: |
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-api -n production --timeout=5m

kubectl exec

EXEC COMMAND RUN

Execute a command in a container

The kubectl exec command runs a command inside a running container. Unlike attach which connects to the main process, exec starts a new process. Essential for debugging, running migrations, accessing shells, and performing administrative tasks in production Django pods.

Syntax

# Execute single command
kubectl exec POD -- COMMAND [args...]

# Interactive shell
kubectl exec -it POD -- /bin/bash

# Specify container
kubectl exec POD -c CONTAINER -- COMMAND

Parameters

  • POD: Pod name to execute command in
  • -c, --container: Container name (if multiple containers)
  • -i, --stdin: Pass stdin to the container
  • -t, --tty: Stdin is a TTY
  • -q, --quiet: Only print output from the remote command
  • --: Separator between kubectl args and command

Return Value

Returns the output of the executed command. Exit code reflects the command's exit code.

Examples

Example 1: Run Django Migrations

Execute database migrations on a running Django pod.

Context - Django Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        workingDir: /app
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: DATABASE_URL
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"

Command:

# Run migrations on one pod
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py migrate --no-input

Output:

Operations to perform:
  Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
Running migrations:
  Applying jobpost.0015_add_salary_field... OK
  Applying jobpost.0016_add_remote_option... OK

CI/CD Integration:

# .github/workflows/migrate.yaml
name: Run Migrations

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  migrate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Configure kubectl
        uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      
      - name: Run migrations
        run: |
          kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
            python manage.py migrate --no-input
      
      - name: Verify migrations
        run: |
          kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
            python manage.py showmigrations --list
Example 2: Interactive Django Shell

Access Django's interactive shell for debugging and data inspection.

Command:

# Open Django shell
kubectl exec -it -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py shell

Output:

Python 3.11.4 (main, Jun  9 2024, 07:20:50) [GCC 12.2.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> from jobpost.models import JobPost
>>> JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()
247
>>> 
>>> from django.contrib.auth.models import User
>>> User.objects.filter(is_staff=True)
<QuerySet [<User: admin>, <User: moderator>]>
>>>
>>> # Quick data fix
>>> JobPost.objects.filter(company='Acme').update(is_featured=True)
3
Example 3: Debug Container Environment

Inspect container environment, network, and filesystem.

Context - Debugging Checklist Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/debug-container.sh

POD=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}
CONTAINER=${3:-django}

echo "🔍 Debugging container $CONTAINER in pod $POD"
echo ""

echo "=== Environment Variables ==="
kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c $CONTAINER -- env | sort

echo ""
echo "=== Network Configuration ==="
kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c $CONTAINER -- cat /etc/hosts

echo ""
echo "=== DNS Resolution ==="
kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c $CONTAINER -- nslookup postgres-service || true

echo ""
echo "=== Disk Usage ==="
kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c $CONTAINER -- df -h

echo ""
echo "=== Running Processes ==="
kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -c $CONTAINER -- ps aux

Command:

# Check environment variables
kubectl exec -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -- env | grep -E "DATABASE|DJANGO"

Output:

DATABASE_URL=postgresql://django:***@postgres-service:5432/anihpj
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=***
Example 4: Create Django Superuser

Create an admin user in production for emergency access.

Context - Non-Interactive User Creation:

# management/commands/create_superuser_if_missing.py
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from django.contrib.auth.models import User
import os

class Command(BaseCommand):
    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        username = os.environ.get('DJANGO_SUPERUSER_USERNAME', 'admin')
        email = os.environ.get('DJANGO_SUPERUSER_EMAIL', 'admin@example.com')
        password = os.environ.get('DJANGO_SUPERUSER_PASSWORD')
        
        if not User.objects.filter(username=username).exists():
            User.objects.create_superuser(username, email, password)
            self.stdout.write(f'Superuser {username} created')
        else:
            self.stdout.write(f'Superuser {username} already exists')

Command:

# Create superuser with environment variables
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py createsuperuser \
    --username=emergency-admin \
    --email=ops@company.com \
    --no-input

# Or use the custom command
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py create_superuser_if_missing
Example 5: Database Operations

Run database management commands from within the Django container.

Commands:

# Check database connection
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py dbshell <<< "SELECT version();"

# Create database backup
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py dumpdata jobpost --indent 2 > backup.json

# Load fixtures
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python manage.py loaddata initial_data.json

# Run raw SQL check
kubectl exec -n production deployment/django-api -- \
    python -c "
from django.db import connection
cursor = connection.cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM jobpost_jobpost')
print(f'Total jobs: {cursor.fetchone()[0]}')"

Output:

Total jobs: 247
Example 6: Multi-Container Pod Exec

Execute commands in specific containers of multi-container pods.

Context - Pod with Sidecars:

# k8s/deployments/django-full.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
      - name: redis-cache
        image: redis:7
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379

Commands:

# Execute in Django container (default)
kubectl exec -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c django -- \
    python manage.py check

# Check nginx configuration
kubectl exec -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c nginx -- \
    nginx -t

# Clear Redis cache
kubectl exec -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c redis-cache -- \
    redis-cli FLUSHALL

# Interactive shell in specific container
kubectl exec -it -n production django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c nginx -- /bin/sh

Output:

# nginx -t
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful

# redis-cli FLUSHALL
OK

kubectl logs

LOGS OUTPUT STREAM

Print the logs for a container in a pod

The kubectl logs command retrieves container logs from stdout/stderr. Supports following logs in real-time, filtering by time, and accessing previous container logs after restarts. The primary tool for debugging application issues in production Django deployments.

Syntax

# Basic logs
kubectl logs POD [-c CONTAINER]

# Follow logs (like tail -f)
kubectl logs -f POD

# Previous container logs (after crash)
kubectl logs POD --previous

# Logs from all containers matching label
kubectl logs -l app=name --all-containers

Parameters

  • -f, --follow: Stream logs in real-time
  • --previous: Print logs from previous container instance
  • --since: Only return logs newer than duration (e.g., 5m, 1h)
  • --since-time: Only return logs after specific time (RFC3339)
  • --tail: Number of lines to show from end (default -1, all)
  • --timestamps: Include timestamps in output
  • -c, --container: Container name in multi-container pods
  • --all-containers: Get logs from all containers
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter pods

Return Value

Log output from container stdout/stderr. Exit code 0 on success.

Examples

Example 1: Debug Django Application Errors

View and follow Django application logs to debug errors.

Context - Django Logging Configuration:

# anihpj/settings.py
import os

LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'disable_existing_loggers': False,
    'formatters': {
        'verbose': {
            'format': '{levelname} {asctime} {module} {process:d} {thread:d} {message}',
            'style': '{',
        },
        'json': {
            'format': '{"level": "%(levelname)s", "time": "%(asctime)s", "module": "%(module)s", "message": "%(message)s"}',
        },
    },
    'handlers': {
        'console': {
            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
            'formatter': 'json' if os.environ.get('JSON_LOGS') else 'verbose',
        },
    },
    'root': {
        'handlers': ['console'],
        'level': 'INFO',
    },
    'loggers': {
        'django': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': os.environ.get('DJANGO_LOG_LEVEL', 'INFO'),
            'propagate': False,
        },
        'jobpost': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': 'DEBUG',
            'propagate': False,
        },
    },
}

Command:

# Follow Django logs in real-time
kubectl logs -f deployment/django-api -n production

Output:

{"level": "INFO", "time": "2026-03-02 11:30:00", "module": "autoreload", "message": "Watching for file changes"}
{"level": "INFO", "time": "2026-03-02 11:30:15", "module": "basehttp", "message": "\"GET /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1\" 200 4523"}
{"level": "INFO", "time": "2026-03-02 11:30:18", "module": "basehttp", "message": "\"POST /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1\" 201 892"}
{"level": "ERROR", "time": "2026-03-02 11:30:25", "module": "views", "message": "Database connection failed: connection refused"}
{"level": "WARNING", "time": "2026-03-02 11:30:26", "module": "views", "message": "Retrying database connection..."}
Example 2: View Logs After Container Crash

Access logs from previous container instance to diagnose crash causes.

Scenario:

# Pod status shows CrashLoopBackOff
kubectl get pods -n production
# NAME                          READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE
# django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          10m

Command:

# Get logs from the crashed container
kubectl logs django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -n production --previous

Output:

{"level": "INFO", "time": "2026-03-02 11:25:00", "module": "manage", "message": "Starting Django..."}
{"level": "INFO", "time": "2026-03-02 11:25:01", "module": "settings", "message": "Loading settings from anihpj.settings"}
{"level": "ERROR", "time": "2026-03-02 11:25:02", "module": "settings", "message": "Missing required environment variable: SECRET_KEY"}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/app/manage.py", line 22, in <module>
    main()
  File "/app/anihpj/settings.py", line 45, in <module>
    SECRET_KEY = os.environ['SECRET_KEY']
KeyError: 'SECRET_KEY'

Diagnosis: Container crashes because SECRET_KEY environment variable is missing from the Secret.

Example 3: Time-Based Log Filtering

Get logs from a specific time period for incident investigation.

Command:

# Logs from last 30 minutes with timestamps
kubectl logs deployment/django-api -n production \
    --since=30m \
    --timestamps

# Logs since specific time
kubectl logs deployment/django-api -n production \
    --since-time="2026-03-02T11:00:00Z" \
    --timestamps

# Last 100 lines only
kubectl logs deployment/django-api -n production \
    --tail=100

Output:

2026-03-02T11:30:00.123Z {"level": "INFO", "module": "basehttp", "message": "\"GET /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1\" 200"}
2026-03-02T11:30:05.456Z {"level": "INFO", "module": "basehttp", "message": "\"GET /api/jobs/123/ HTTP/1.1\" 200"}
2026-03-02T11:30:10.789Z {"level": "WARNING", "module": "views", "message": "Slow query detected: 2.5s"}
2026-03-02T11:30:15.012Z {"level": "ERROR", "module": "views", "message": "Request timeout"}
Example 4: Aggregate Logs from Multiple Pods

Collect logs from all pods matching a label selector.

Context - Multi-Pod Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
      tier: web

Command:

# Logs from all pods with label
kubectl logs -l app=django-api -n production --all-containers --tail=50

# Follow logs from all pods (useful for debugging)
kubectl logs -f -l app=django-api -n production --max-log-requests=10

Output:

# Logs interleaved from all 5 pods
{"level": "INFO", "pod": "django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12", "message": "Request handled"}
{"level": "INFO", "pod": "django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34", "message": "Request handled"}
{"level": "INFO", "pod": "django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56", "message": "Request handled"}
{"level": "WARNING", "pod": "django-api-7d9f8b6c4-jkl78", "message": "High memory usage"}
Example 5: Log Collection Script for Incidents

Automated script to collect logs for incident response.

Context - Incident Response Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/collect-incident-logs.sh

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
APP_LABEL=${2:-app=django-api}
SINCE=${3:-1h}
OUTPUT_DIR="./incident-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"

mkdir -p $OUTPUT_DIR

echo "🚨 Collecting incident logs"
echo "   Namespace: $NAMESPACE"
echo "   Label: $APP_LABEL"
echo "   Since: $SINCE"
echo ""

# Get all matching pods
PODS=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l $APP_LABEL -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}')

for POD in $PODS; do
    echo "📥 Collecting logs from $POD..."
    
    # Current logs
    kubectl logs $POD -n $NAMESPACE --since=$SINCE --timestamps \
        > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${POD}_current.log" 2>&1
    
    # Previous container logs (if any)
    kubectl logs $POD -n $NAMESPACE --previous --timestamps \
        > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${POD}_previous.log" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Events
    kubectl events --for=pod/$POD -n $NAMESPACE \
        > "$OUTPUT_DIR/${POD}_events.txt" 2>&1
done

# Create archive
tar -czf "$OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz" "$OUTPUT_DIR"

echo ""
echo "✅ Logs collected to $OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz"
echo "   Total size: $(du -h "$OUTPUT_DIR.tar.gz" | cut -f1)"

Command:

./scripts/collect-incident-logs.sh production app=django-api 2h
Example 6: Multi-Container Log Access

Access logs from specific containers in pods with sidecars.

Context - Pod with Multiple Containers:

# k8s/deployments/django-full.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.24
      - name: fluentbit
        image: fluent/fluent-bit:2.1

Commands:

# Django application logs
kubectl logs django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c django -n production -f

# Nginx access logs
kubectl logs django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c nginx -n production --tail=100

# All containers at once
kubectl logs django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -n production --all-containers

# Specific container with timestamps
kubectl logs django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -c django -n production \
    --timestamps \
    --since=10m

Output - Nginx Logs:

10.244.1.1 - - [02/Mar/2026:11:35:00 +0000] "GET /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1" 200 4523 "-" "curl/7.68.0"
10.244.1.1 - - [02/Mar/2026:11:35:05 +0000] "POST /api/jobs/ HTTP/1.1" 201 892 "-" "python-requests/2.28"
10.244.1.2 - - [02/Mar/2026:11:35:10 +0000] "GET /health/ HTTP/1.1" 200 2 "-" "kube-probe/1.28"

kubectl port-forward

FORWARD PORT TUNNEL

Forward local ports to a pod or service

The kubectl port-forward command creates a tunnel from your local machine to a pod or service. Essential for accessing services not exposed externally, debugging internal services, and local development against remote databases. Works without modifying cluster networking.

Syntax

# Forward to pod
kubectl port-forward pod/NAME LOCAL_PORT:REMOTE_PORT

# Forward to service
kubectl port-forward service/NAME LOCAL_PORT:REMOTE_PORT

# Forward to deployment
kubectl port-forward deployment/NAME LOCAL_PORT:REMOTE_PORT

# Forward multiple ports
kubectl port-forward pod/NAME 8080:80 5432:5432

Parameters

  • LOCAL_PORT: Port on local machine to listen on
  • REMOTE_PORT: Port in the pod/service to forward to
  • --address: Addresses to listen on (default: localhost)
  • --pod-running-timeout: Timeout waiting for pod to be running

Return Value

Runs in foreground until terminated. Shows connection info and forwarded addresses.

Examples

Example 1: Access Django Admin Locally

Access the Django admin interface from a production pod for debugging.

Context - Django Deployment:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: ALLOWED_HOSTS
          value: "localhost,127.0.0.1,.company.com"

Command:
