# Forward local 8000 to pod 8000
kubectl port-forward deployment/django-api 8000:8000 -n production

Output:

Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8000 -> 8000
Forwarding from [::1]:8000 -> 8000
Handling connection for 8000

Access in Browser:

http://localhost:8000/admin/
Example 2: Connect to Production Database

Forward PostgreSQL port for local debugging with production data.

Context - PostgreSQL Service:

# k8s/services/postgres-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    targetPort: 5432
  type: ClusterIP  # Not exposed externally

Command:

# Forward PostgreSQL port
kubectl port-forward service/postgres-service 5432:5432 -n production

Local Connection:

# Connect with psql
psql -h localhost -p 5432 -U django -d anihpj

# Or use Django
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://django:password@localhost:5432/anihpj \
    python manage.py shell
Example 3: Access Redis for Cache Debugging

Connect to Redis cache to inspect and clear cached data.

Context - Redis Service:

# k8s/services/redis-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: redis-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: redis
  ports:
  - port: 6379
    targetPort: 6379

Command:

# Forward Redis port
kubectl port-forward service/redis-service 6379:6379 -n production

Debug Session:

# Connect with redis-cli
redis-cli -h localhost -p 6379

# Check cache keys
127.0.0.1:6379> KEYS *session*
1) "django_session:abc123"
2) "django_session:def456"

# Check cache stats
127.0.0.1:6379> INFO stats

# Clear specific cache
127.0.0.1:6379> DEL django_session:abc123
Example 4: Multiple Port Forwarding

Forward multiple ports simultaneously for full-stack debugging.

Context - Development Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/dev-tunnel.sh
# Create tunnels to all production services for local development

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}

echo "🔗 Creating development tunnels to $NAMESPACE..."

# Start port forwards in background
kubectl port-forward service/django-api 8000:8000 -n $NAMESPACE &
PID_DJANGO=$!

kubectl port-forward service/postgres-service 5432:5432 -n $NAMESPACE &
PID_PG=$!

kubectl port-forward service/redis-service 6379:6379 -n $NAMESPACE &
PID_REDIS=$!

echo ""
echo "✅ Tunnels established:"
echo "   Django API: http://localhost:8000"
echo "   PostgreSQL: localhost:5432"
echo "   Redis:      localhost:6379"
echo ""
echo "Press Ctrl+C to close all tunnels"

# Wait and cleanup on exit
trap "kill $PID_DJANGO $PID_PG $PID_REDIS 2>/dev/null" EXIT
wait

Command:

# Forward multiple ports from single pod
kubectl port-forward deployment/django-api 8000:8000 8080:80 -n production
Example 5: Access Monitoring Services

Forward Prometheus, Grafana, and other internal monitoring tools.

Context - Monitoring Stack:

# k8s/monitoring/services.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    app: prometheus
  ports:
  - port: 9090
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: grafana
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    app: grafana
  ports:
  - port: 3000

Command:

# Access Prometheus UI
kubectl port-forward service/prometheus 9090:9090 -n monitoring &

# Access Grafana dashboards
kubectl port-forward service/grafana 3000:3000 -n monitoring &

echo "Prometheus: http://localhost:9090"
echo "Grafana:    http://localhost:3000"
Example 6: Listen on All Interfaces

Allow access from other machines on your network (for team debugging).

Command:

# Listen on all interfaces (accessible from network)
kubectl port-forward deployment/django-api 8000:8000 \
    --address 0.0.0.0 \
    -n production

Output:

Forwarding from 0.0.0.0:8000 -> 8000
# Now accessible at http://YOUR_IP:8000 from other machines

Security Note:

# Prefer specific addresses for security
kubectl port-forward deployment/django-api 8000:8000 \
    --address 192.168.1.100 \
    -n production

kubectl proxy

PROXY API GATEWAY

Run a proxy to the Kubernetes API server

The kubectl proxy command creates an HTTP proxy to the Kubernetes API server. Allows access to the API without authentication tokens (proxy handles auth), access to internal services via API proxy, and direct access to Pod/Service endpoints. Useful for debugging, API exploration, and development.

Syntax

kubectl proxy [--port=PORT] [--address=ADDRESS] [options]

Parameters

  • --port: Port to run proxy on (default 8001)
  • --address: Address to bind to (default: 127.0.0.1)
  • --accept-hosts: Regular expression for hosts proxy accepts
  • --api-prefix: Prefix to serve proxy API under
  • --disable-filter: Disable request filtering (dangerous)

Return Value

Runs in foreground serving proxy requests. Shows listening address on startup.

API Endpoints

  • /api/v1/: Core API resources
  • /apis/apps/v1/: Apps API group
  • /api/v1/namespaces/{ns}/services/{name}/proxy/: Service proxy
  • /api/v1/namespaces/{ns}/pods/{name}/proxy/: Pod proxy

Examples

Example 1: Access Kubernetes Dashboard

Access the Kubernetes dashboard through the API proxy.

Context - Dashboard Installation:

# Install Kubernetes Dashboard
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/dashboard/v2.7.0/aio/deploy/recommended.yaml

Command:

# Start API proxy
kubectl proxy

Output:

Starting to serve on 127.0.0.1:8001

Access Dashboard:

http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/kubernetes-dashboard/services/https:kubernetes-dashboard:/proxy/
Example 2: Explore Kubernetes API

Use proxy to explore and test API endpoints directly.

Command:

# Start proxy in background
kubectl proxy &

# List available API groups
curl http://localhost:8001/apis

# Get pods in production namespace
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/pods

# Get specific deployment
curl http://localhost:8001/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/production/deployments/django-api

# Get pod logs via API
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/pods/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12/log

Output (API groups):

{
  "kind": "APIGroupList",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "groups": [
    {"name": "apps", "versions": [{"groupVersion": "apps/v1", "version": "v1"}]},
    {"name": "batch", "versions": [{"groupVersion": "batch/v1", "version": "v1"}]},
    {"name": "networking.k8s.io", "versions": [{"groupVersion": "networking.k8s.io/v1"}]}
  ]
}
Example 3: Access Service Through API Proxy

Access internal services without port-forward using the API proxy.

Context - Internal Service:

# k8s/services/django-api.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    app: django-api
  ports:
  - port: 8000
    targetPort: 8000

Command:

# Start proxy
kubectl proxy &

# Access service through API proxy
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/services/django-api:8000/proxy/api/jobs/

# Or with service name only (uses first port)
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/services/django-api/proxy/health/

Output:

{
  "status": "healthy",
  "version": "2.4.0",
  "timestamp": "2026-03-02T12:00:00Z"
}
Example 4: API Development and Testing

Use proxy for Kubernetes API development and client testing.

Context - Python API Client Testing:

# scripts/test_k8s_api.py
import requests
import json

BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8001"

def get_pods(namespace="production"):
    """Get all pods in namespace"""
    url = f"{BASE_URL}/api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods"
    response = requests.get(url)
    return response.json()

def get_deployment(name, namespace="production"):
    """Get deployment details"""
    url = f"{BASE_URL}/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name}"
    response = requests.get(url)
    return response.json()

def scale_deployment(name, replicas, namespace="production"):
    """Scale a deployment"""
    url = f"{BASE_URL}/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name}/scale"
    scale = {
        "apiVersion": "autoscaling/v1",
        "kind": "Scale",
        "metadata": {"name": name, "namespace": namespace},
        "spec": {"replicas": replicas}
    }
    response = requests.put(url, json=scale)
    return response.json()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # List pods
    pods = get_pods()
    print(f"Found {len(pods['items'])} pods")
    
    # Get deployment
    deployment = get_deployment("django-api")
    print(f"Deployment replicas: {deployment['spec']['replicas']}")

Command:

# Start proxy then run script
kubectl proxy &
python scripts/test_k8s_api.py
Example 5: Access Pod Directly Through Proxy

Access individual pods through the API proxy endpoint.

Command:

# Start proxy
kubectl proxy &

# Get pod name
POD=$(kubectl get pods -n production -l app=django-api -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# Access pod through proxy
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/pods/$POD/proxy/api/jobs/

# Access specific port
curl http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/production/pods/$POD:8000/proxy/health/
Example 6: Proxy with Custom Settings

Configure proxy for team access and custom ports.

Context - Team Development Setup:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/start-team-proxy.sh

PORT=${1:-8001}
INTERFACE=${2:-0.0.0.0}

echo "🌐 Starting Kubernetes API proxy"
echo "   Port: $PORT"
echo "   Interface: $INTERFACE"
echo ""

kubectl proxy \
    --port=$PORT \
    --address=$INTERFACE \
    --accept-hosts='.*' \
    --accept-paths='^/api/.*,^/apis/.*'

# Note: accept-hosts='.*' allows access from any host
# Use with caution - only in trusted networks

Command:

# Custom port
kubectl proxy --port=9000

# Listen on all interfaces (team access)
kubectl proxy --address=0.0.0.0 --accept-hosts='.*'

# Restrict to specific paths
kubectl proxy --accept-paths='^/api/v1/namespaces/production'

Output:

Starting to serve on 0.0.0.0:9000

kubectl top

TOP METRICS MONITOR

Display resource (CPU/Memory) usage

The kubectl top command displays current resource usage metrics for nodes and pods. Requires the Metrics Server to be installed in the cluster. Essential for monitoring resource consumption, capacity planning, and identifying performance bottlenecks. Use with subcommands: node for cluster nodes or pod for running pods.

Prerequisites

# Install Metrics Server (if not present)
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# Verify installation
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

Subcommands

  • kubectl top node - Display node resource usage
  • kubectl top pod - Display pod resource usage

kubectl top node

TOP NODE METRICS

Display node resource (CPU/Memory) usage

Shows CPU and memory usage for cluster nodes. Displays both actual usage and percentage of allocatable resources. Critical for cluster capacity planning, identifying overloaded nodes, and scheduling decisions.

Syntax

kubectl top node [NAME] [options]

Parameters

  • NAME: Specific node name (optional, shows all if omitted)
  • -l, --selector: Filter by label selector
  • --sort-by: Sort by cpu or memory
  • --no-headers: Omit headers from output

Return Value

Table showing node names, CPU usage (cores and %), and Memory usage (bytes and %).

Examples

Example 1: View All Node Resources

Check resource usage across all cluster nodes.

Command:

kubectl top node

Output:

NAME                    CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
node-pool-a-001         892m         22%    6234Mi          78%
node-pool-a-002         1245m        31%    5891Mi          73%
node-pool-a-003         456m         11%    4123Mi          51%
node-pool-gpu-001       3200m        80%    12456Mi         97%
Example 2: Monitor Specific Node

Check resources on a specific node experiencing issues.

Context - Node Alert Scenario:

# alertmanager/node-alerts.yaml
groups:
- name: node-resources
  rules:
  - alert: NodeHighMemory
    expr: (1 - node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes) > 0.9
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "Node {{ $labels.instance }} high memory usage"

Command:

# Check specific node from alert
kubectl top node node-pool-gpu-001

Output:

NAME                CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
node-pool-gpu-001   3200m        80%    12456Mi         97%
Example 3: Sort by Resource Usage

Find nodes with highest CPU or memory consumption.

Command:

# Sort by CPU usage (highest first)
kubectl top node --sort-by=cpu

# Sort by memory usage
kubectl top node --sort-by=memory

Output (sorted by CPU):

NAME                CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
node-pool-gpu-001   3200m        80%    12456Mi         97%
node-pool-a-002     1245m        31%    5891Mi          73%
node-pool-a-001     892m         22%    6234Mi          78%
node-pool-a-003     456m         11%    4123Mi          51%
Example 4: Filter Nodes by Label

Check resources on specific node pools.

Context - Node Labels:

# Label nodes by pool type
kubectl label nodes node-pool-a-001 node-pool-a-002 pool=general
kubectl label nodes node-pool-gpu-001 pool=gpu

Command:

# Check only GPU nodes
kubectl top node -l pool=gpu

# Check general pool nodes
kubectl top node -l pool=general
Example 5: Cluster Capacity Monitoring Script

Automated script for cluster capacity tracking.

Context - Monitoring Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cluster-capacity-check.sh

echo "=== Cluster Capacity Report ==="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

echo "📊 Node Resource Usage (sorted by memory):"
kubectl top node --sort-by=memory
echo ""

# Check for nodes above thresholds
echo "⚠️  High Usage Nodes (CPU > 80% or Memory > 85%):"
kubectl top node --no-headers | while read name cpu cpupct mem mempct; do
    cpu_val=${cpupct%\%}
    mem_val=${mempct%\%}
    
    if [ "$cpu_val" -gt 80 ] || [ "$mem_val" -gt 85 ]; then
        echo "  - $name: CPU=$cpupct, Memory=$mempct"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "📈 Total Cluster Resources:"
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A 5 "Allocated resources"

Command:

chmod +x scripts/cluster-capacity-check.sh
./scripts/cluster-capacity-check.sh

kubectl top pod

TOP POD METRICS

Display pod resource (CPU/Memory) usage

Shows CPU and memory usage for running pods. Essential for identifying resource-hungry applications, validating resource requests/limits, and troubleshooting performance issues. Can show container-level breakdown for multi-container pods.

Syntax

kubectl top pod [NAME | -l label] [options]

Parameters

  • NAME: Specific pod name (optional)
  • -n, --namespace: Target namespace
  • -A, --all-namespaces: All namespaces
  • -l, --selector: Label selector filter
  • --containers: Show container-level metrics
  • --sort-by: Sort by cpu or memory

Return Value

Table showing pod/container names, CPU usage (millicores), and Memory usage (bytes).

Examples

Example 1: View Pod Resources in Namespace

Check resource usage for Django application pods.

Command:

kubectl top pod -n production

Output:

NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    125m         256Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    118m         248Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    132m         261Mi
celery-worker-5f4d3c2b-jkl78  89m          412Mi
redis-master-0                23m          64Mi
postgres-0                    156m         892Mi
Example 2: Find Resource-Heavy Pods

Identify pods consuming most resources across cluster.

Command:

# Top CPU consumers across all namespaces
kubectl top pod -A --sort-by=cpu | head -20

# Top memory consumers
kubectl top pod -A --sort-by=memory | head -20

Output:

NAMESPACE     NAME                              CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
production    ml-training-job-8x7k2             3850m        14256Mi
production    postgres-0                        456m         2892Mi
production    elasticsearch-data-0              312m         4096Mi
monitoring    prometheus-server-0               245m         1536Mi
production    django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12        125m         256Mi
Example 3: Container-Level Metrics

View per-container resource usage in multi-container pods.

Context - Multi-Container Pod:

# k8s/deployment-with-sidecar.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 256Mi
      - name: nginx-sidecar
        image: nginx:alpine
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 50m
            memory: 64Mi
      - name: log-collector
        image: fluent/fluent-bit
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 25m
            memory: 32Mi

Command:

# Show container-level breakdown
kubectl top pod -n production --containers

Output:

POD                           NAME            CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    django          98m          234Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    nginx-sidecar   12m          45Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    log-collector   8m           28Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    django          102m         241Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    nginx-sidecar   11m          44Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    log-collector   7m           26Mi
Example 4: Monitor Specific Application

Track resources for specific application using labels.

Command:

# All Django API pods
kubectl top pod -n production -l app=django-api

# All Celery workers
kubectl top pod -n production -l component=celery-worker

# Specific pod
kubectl top pod django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 -n production
Example 5: Resource Utilization Report Script

Generate resource utilization report for production namespace.

Context - Report Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/pod-resource-report.sh

NAMESPACE=${1:-production}
THRESHOLD_CPU=${2:-80}
THRESHOLD_MEM=${3:-80}

echo "=== Pod Resource Report: $NAMESPACE ==="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

echo "📊 All Pods (sorted by memory):"
kubectl top pod -n $NAMESPACE --sort-by=memory
echo ""

echo "🔍 Pods with Container Breakdown:"
kubectl top pod -n $NAMESPACE --containers --sort-by=memory
echo ""

# Compare against requests
echo "📈 Resource Utilization vs Requests:"
echo "Pod                          | CPU Used | CPU Req | Memory Used | Memory Req"
echo "--------------------------------------------------------------------"

kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] | 
  .metadata.name as $name |
  .spec.containers[0].resources.requests as $req |
  "\($name)|\($req.cpu // "none")|\($req.memory // "none")"
' | while IFS='|' read name cpu_req mem_req; do
    usage=$(kubectl top pod $name -n $NAMESPACE --no-headers 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$usage" ]; then
        echo "$usage | $cpu_req | $mem_req"
    fi
done

Command:

chmod +x scripts/pod-resource-report.sh
./scripts/pod-resource-report.sh production
Example 6: Continuous Monitoring Watch

Watch pod resources in real-time during load testing.

Command:

# Watch pods refresh every 2 seconds
watch -n 2 'kubectl top pod -n production -l app=django-api --sort-by=cpu'

# Or use kubectl with watch flag (if available)
kubectl top pod -n production -l app=django-api --sort-by=cpu

# Script for continuous monitoring with timestamps
while true; do
    echo "=== $(date) ==="
    kubectl top pod -n production -l app=django-api
    sleep 5
done

Output (during load test):

=== Mon Mar 02 12:00:05 UTC 2026 ===
NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    425m         312Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    418m         308Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    432m         315Mi

=== Mon Mar 02 12:00:10 UTC 2026 ===
NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    512m         345Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-def34    498m         338Mi
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-ghi56    521m         351Mi

🏗️ CLUSTER MANAGEMENT

Commands for managing cluster resources, certificates, and node operations. Essential for maintaining production Kubernetes clusters running Django applications.

kubectl api-versions

API VERSIONS LIST

Print the supported API versions on the server

The kubectl api-versions command lists all API groups and versions available on the Kubernetes cluster. Essential for verifying which API versions your cluster supports, debugging manifest compatibility issues, and ensuring your Django deployment manifests use correct API versions for the target cluster.

Syntax

kubectl api-versions [options]

Parameters

  • --request-timeout: Timeout for API request (default: 0)

Return Value

List of API group/version strings (e.g., apps/v1, batch/v1, v1). One per line, alphabetically sorted.

Examples

Example 1: List All API Versions

Check which API versions are available before deploying Django application.

Context - Deployment Manifest:

# k8s/deployments/django-api.yaml
# Need to verify apiVersion: apps/v1 is supported
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

Command:

# List all supported API versions
kubectl api-versions

Output:

admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
apiregistration.k8s.io/v1
apps/v1
authentication.k8s.io/v1
authorization.k8s.io/v1
autoscaling/v1
autoscaling/v2
batch/v1
certificates.k8s.io/v1
coordination.k8s.io/v1
discovery.k8s.io/v1
events.k8s.io/v1
networking.k8s.io/v1
node.k8s.io/v1
policy/v1
rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
scheduling.k8s.io/v1
storage.k8s.io/v1
v1
Example 2: Verify CronJob API Version

Check if batch/v1 is available for Django scheduled tasks.

Context - CronJob for Django Management Command:

# k8s/jobs/cleanup-cronjob.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1  # Need to verify this is supported
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: django-cleanup
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # Run at 2 AM daily
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: cleanup
            image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
            command: ["python", "manage.py", "clearsessions"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

Command:

# Check if batch API is available
kubectl api-versions | grep batch

Output:

batch/v1
Example 3: Pre-Deployment Validation Script

Validate required API versions before deploying Django stack.

Context - Validation Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/validate-api-versions.sh

REQUIRED_APIS=(
    "apps/v1"           # Deployments, StatefulSets
    "batch/v1"          # CronJobs, Jobs
    "networking.k8s.io/v1"  # Ingress, NetworkPolicy
    "v1"                # Core resources
)

echo "🔍 Checking required API versions..."
AVAILABLE=$(kubectl api-versions)

MISSING=0
for api in "${REQUIRED_APIS[@]}"; do
    if echo "$AVAILABLE" | grep -q "^$api$"; then
        echo "  ✅ $api"
    else
        echo "  ❌ $api - NOT AVAILABLE"
        MISSING=$((MISSING + 1))
    fi
done

if [ $MISSING -gt 0 ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "❌ $MISSING required API(s) missing. Deployment may fail."
    exit 1
else
    echo ""
    echo "✅ All required APIs available. Ready to deploy."
fi

Command:

chmod +x scripts/validate-api-versions.sh
./scripts/validate-api-versions.sh

Output:

🔍 Checking required API versions...
  ✅ apps/v1
  ✅ batch/v1
  ✅ networking.k8s.io/v1
  ✅ v1

✅ All required APIs available. Ready to deploy.
Example 4: Check for Beta/Alpha APIs

Identify unstable APIs in use before upgrading cluster.

Command:

# Find beta versions (may be deprecated)
kubectl api-versions | grep beta

# Find alpha versions (unstable)
kubectl api-versions | grep alpha

# Count stable vs beta APIs
echo "Stable APIs: $(kubectl api-versions | grep -v 'beta\|alpha' | wc -l)"
echo "Beta APIs: $(kubectl api-versions | grep beta | wc -l)"
echo "Alpha APIs: $(kubectl api-versions | grep alpha | wc -l)"

Output:

flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1beta3
Stable APIs: 20
Beta APIs: 1
Alpha APIs: 0
Example 5: Compare API Versions Across Clusters

Ensure staging and production have compatible API versions.

Context - Multi-Cluster Comparison Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/compare-cluster-apis.sh

echo "=== API Version Comparison ==="
echo ""

echo "📊 Staging Cluster APIs:"
kubectl api-versions --context=staging | sort > /tmp/staging-apis.txt
cat /tmp/staging-apis.txt | wc -l
echo ""

echo "📊 Production Cluster APIs:"
kubectl api-versions --context=production | sort > /tmp/prod-apis.txt
cat /tmp/prod-apis.txt | wc -l
echo ""

echo "📋 APIs in Staging but NOT in Production:"
diff /tmp/staging-apis.txt /tmp/prod-apis.txt | grep "^<" | sed 's/< /  /'

echo ""
echo "📋 APIs in Production but NOT in Staging:"
diff /tmp/staging-apis.txt /tmp/prod-apis.txt | grep "^>" | sed 's/> /  /'

Command:

./scripts/compare-cluster-apis.sh

Output:

=== API Version Comparison ===

📊 Staging Cluster APIs:
22

📊 Production Cluster APIs:
20

📋 APIs in Staging but NOT in Production:
  flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1beta3
  resource.k8s.io/v1alpha1

📋 APIs in Production but NOT in Staging:
  (none)

kubectl api-resources

API RESOURCES LIST

Print the supported API resources on the server

The kubectl api-resources command lists all available resource types in the cluster with their API group, namespaced status, kind, and short names. Essential for discovering what resources your cluster supports, finding correct resource names for kubectl commands, and understanding resource API groups for Django deployments.

Syntax

kubectl api-resources [--namespaced=bool] [--api-group=GROUP] [options]

Parameters

  • --namespaced: If true, show only namespaced resources; if false, show only non-namespaced
  • --api-group: Limit to specific API group
  • --verbs: Filter by supported verbs (get, list, create, delete, etc.)
  • -o, --output: Output format (wide, name)
  • --sort-by: Sort by name or kind

Return Value

Table with NAME, SHORTNAMES, APIVERSION, NAMESPACED, KIND columns.

Examples

Example 1: List All Available Resources

Discover all resource types available for Django deployment.

Command:

# List all API resources
kubectl api-resources

Output:

NAME                              SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION                        NAMESPACED   KIND
bindings                                       v1                                true         Binding
configmaps                        cm           v1                                true         ConfigMap
endpoints                         ep           v1                                true         Endpoints
events                            ev           v1                                true         Event
namespaces                        ns           v1                                false        Namespace
nodes                             no           v1                                false        Node
persistentvolumeclaims            pvc          v1                                true         PersistentVolumeClaim
persistentvolumes                 pv           v1                                false        PersistentVolume
pods                              po           v1                                true         Pod
secrets                                        v1                                true         Secret
serviceaccounts                   sa           v1                                true         ServiceAccount
services                          svc          v1                                true         Service
deployments                       deploy       apps/v1                           true         Deployment
replicasets                       rs           apps/v1                           true         ReplicaSet
statefulsets                      sts          apps/v1                           true         StatefulSet
cronjobs                          cj           batch/v1                          true         CronJob
jobs                                           batch/v1                          true         Job
ingresses                         ing          networking.k8s.io/v1              true         Ingress
networkpolicies                   netpol       networking.k8s.io/v1              true         NetworkPolicy
Example 2: Find Short Names for Commands

Discover short names to speed up kubectl commands.

Context - Using Short Names:

# Instead of typing full resource names:
kubectl get deployments -n production
kubectl get persistentvolumeclaims -n production
kubectl get configmaps -n production

# Use short names:
kubectl get deploy -n production
kubectl get pvc -n production
kubectl get cm -n production

Command:

# Find short names for commonly used resources
kubectl api-resources | grep -E 'deploy|pvc|cm|svc|po|ns'

Output:

configmaps                        cm           v1                                true         ConfigMap
namespaces                        ns           v1                                false        Namespace
persistentvolumeclaims            pvc          v1                                true         PersistentVolumeClaim
pods                              po           v1                                true         Pod
services                          svc          v1                                true         Service
deployments                       deploy       apps/v1                           true         Deployment
Example 3: List Namespaced vs Cluster-Wide Resources

Understand which resources are namespace-scoped for Django multi-tenant setup.

Context - Multi-Tenant Architecture:

# Multi-namespace Django setup
# production/ - Live Django application
# staging/ - Testing environment
# Each namespace has its own:
#   - Deployments (namespaced)
#   - ConfigMaps (namespaced)
#   - Secrets (namespaced)
# Shared across all:
#   - PersistentVolumes (cluster-wide)
#   - StorageClasses (cluster-wide)
#   - ClusterRoles (cluster-wide)

Command:

# List cluster-wide (non-namespaced) resources
kubectl api-resources --namespaced=false

# List namespaced resources only
kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true

Output (cluster-wide):

NAME                              SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION                             NAMESPACED   KIND
clusterrolebindings                            rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1           false        ClusterRoleBinding
clusterroles                                   rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1           false        ClusterRole
namespaces                        ns           v1                                     false        Namespace
nodes                             no           v1                                     false        Node
persistentvolumes                 pv           v1                                     false        PersistentVolume
storageclasses                    sc           storage.k8s.io/v1                      false        StorageClass
Example 4: Filter by API Group

List resources for specific API groups when debugging.

Command:

# List all apps group resources (deployments, etc.)
kubectl api-resources --api-group=apps

# List batch group resources (jobs, cronjobs)
kubectl api-resources --api-group=batch

# List networking resources
kubectl api-resources --api-group=networking.k8s.io

# List RBAC resources
kubectl api-resources --api-group=rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Output (apps group):

NAME                  SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION   NAMESPACED   KIND
controllerrevisions                apps/v1      true         ControllerRevision
daemonsets            ds           apps/v1      true         DaemonSet
deployments           deploy       apps/v1      true         Deployment
replicasets           rs           apps/v1      true         ReplicaSet
statefulsets          sts          apps/v1      true         StatefulSet
Example 5: Find Resources Supporting Specific Verbs

Discover which resources support delete operations for cleanup scripts.

Context - Cleanup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cleanup-namespace.sh
# Delete all user-creatable resources in a namespace

NAMESPACE=${1:-staging}

echo "🗑️ Cleaning up namespace: $NAMESPACE"

# Get all deletable namespaced resources
RESOURCES=$(kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true --verbs=delete -o name | tr '\n' ',')

# Delete all resources
kubectl delete $RESOURCES --all -n $NAMESPACE --ignore-not-found

Command:

# Find resources that support delete verb
kubectl api-resources --verbs=delete

# Find resources that support create verb
kubectl api-resources --verbs=create

# Find resources supporting watch (for controllers)
kubectl api-resources --verbs=watch

Output (delete verb):

NAME                 SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION   NAMESPACED   KIND
configmaps           cm           v1           true         ConfigMap
deployments          deploy       apps/v1      true         Deployment
pods                 po           v1           true         Pod
secrets                           v1           true         Secret
services             svc          v1           true         Service

kubectl certificate

CERT CSR MANAGE

Modify certificate resources

The kubectl certificate command manages CertificateSigningRequests (CSRs) in the cluster. Used for approving or denying certificate requests from nodes, users, or applications. Essential for TLS authentication, mTLS between Django services, and managing cluster PKI infrastructure.

Subcommands Overview

  • approve: Approve a certificate signing request
  • deny: Deny a certificate signing request

kubectl certificate approve

APPROVE CSR SIGN

Approve a certificate signing request

The kubectl certificate approve command approves pending CertificateSigningRequests, allowing the cluster CA to sign them. Used when new nodes join the cluster, when creating user certificates, or when setting up mTLS for Django microservices.

Syntax

kubectl certificate approve CSR_NAME [options]

Parameters

  • CSR_NAME: Name of the CertificateSigningRequest to approve
  • --allow-missing-template-keys: Allow missing template keys

Return Value

Confirmation message indicating the CSR was approved.

Examples

Example 1: Approve Node Bootstrap Certificate

Approve kubelet certificate request when new node joins cluster.

Context - Node Join Process:

# New node joins cluster and creates CSR
# kubeadm join generates CSR for kubelet

# List pending CSRs
kubectl get csr
# NAME                                  AGE   SIGNERNAME                                    REQUESTOR           CONDITION
# node-csr-abc123                      10s   kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving                  kubelet-bootstrap   Pending

Command:

# Approve the node certificate request
kubectl certificate approve node-csr-abc123

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/node-csr-abc123 approved
Example 2: Approve User Certificate for Developer

Create and approve certificate for Django developer access.

Context - Generate User CSR:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/create-user-cert.sh

USERNAME="django-developer"
GROUP="developers"

# Generate private key
openssl genrsa -out ${USERNAME}.key 2048

# Generate CSR
openssl req -new -key ${USERNAME}.key \
    -out ${USERNAME}.csr \
    -subj "/CN=${USERNAME}/O=${GROUP}"

# Create Kubernetes CSR
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: ${USERNAME}
spec:
  request: $(cat ${USERNAME}.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n')
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
  usages:
  - client auth
EOF

Command:

# Approve the user certificate
kubectl certificate approve django-developer

# Get signed certificate
kubectl get csr django-developer -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' | base64 -d > django-developer.crt

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/django-developer approved
Example 3: Approve Multiple Pending CSRs

Bulk approve all pending node CSRs during cluster scaling.

Context - Auto-scaling Event:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/approve-pending-csrs.sh
# Run after cluster autoscaler adds new nodes

echo "🔍 Checking for pending CSRs..."

PENDING=$(kubectl get csr -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.status.conditions==null)]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}')

if [ -z "$PENDING" ]; then
    echo "No pending CSRs found."
    exit 0
fi

echo "Found pending CSRs:"
echo "$PENDING"
echo ""

read -p "Approve all? (y/n) " -n 1 -r
echo ""

if [[ $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    for csr in $PENDING; do
        kubectl certificate approve $csr
    done
fi

Command:

# Approve all pending CSRs
kubectl get csr -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl certificate approve {}

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/node-csr-abc123 approved
certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/node-csr-def456 approved
certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/node-csr-ghi789 approved
Example 4: mTLS Certificate for Django Service

Approve certificate for service-to-service authentication.

Context - Service mTLS Setup:

# k8s/csr/django-api-mtls.yaml
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: django-api-mtls
spec:
  request: <base64-encoded-csr>
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving
  usages:
  - digital signature
  - key encipherment
  - server auth
  - client auth

Command:

# Apply and approve service certificate
kubectl apply -f k8s/csr/django-api-mtls.yaml
kubectl certificate approve django-api-mtls

# Verify approval
kubectl get csr django-api-mtls

Output:

NAME              AGE   SIGNERNAME                        REQUESTOR      CONDITION
django-api-mtls   5s    kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving     admin          Approved,Issued
Example 5: CI/CD Certificate Approval Workflow

Automated CSR approval in deployment pipeline.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/approve-certs.yml
name: Approve Certificates

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      csr_name:
        description: 'CSR name to approve'
        required: true

jobs:
  approve:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - name: Setup kubectl
      uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
      
    - name: Configure kubeconfig
      run: |
        echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}" | base64 -d > ~/.kube/config
        
    - name: Verify CSR exists
      run: kubectl get csr ${{ github.event.inputs.csr_name }}
      
    - name: Approve CSR
      run: kubectl certificate approve ${{ github.event.inputs.csr_name }}
      
    - name: Verify approval
      run: |
        kubectl get csr ${{ github.event.inputs.csr_name }} -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].type}'

Command:

# Trigger workflow
gh workflow run approve-certs.yml -f csr_name=django-api-mtls

kubectl certificate deny

DENY CSR REJECT

Deny a certificate signing request

The kubectl certificate deny command denies pending CertificateSigningRequests. Used to reject suspicious or unauthorized certificate requests, preventing untrusted entities from obtaining valid cluster certificates.

Syntax

kubectl certificate deny CSR_NAME [options]

Parameters

  • CSR_NAME: Name of the CertificateSigningRequest to deny

Return Value

Confirmation message indicating the CSR was denied.

Examples

Example 1: Deny Suspicious CSR

Reject certificate request from unknown source.

Context - Security Investigation:

# Inspect suspicious CSR
kubectl get csr suspicious-csr -o yaml

# Check who requested it
kubectl get csr suspicious-csr -o jsonpath='{.spec.username}'
# Output: system:anonymous

# Check what permissions it requests
kubectl get csr suspicious-csr -o jsonpath='{.spec.usages}'
# Output: ["client auth","server auth"]

Command:

# Deny the suspicious request
kubectl certificate deny suspicious-csr

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/suspicious-csr denied
Example 2: Deny Expired or Invalid CSR

Clean up old CSR that should not be approved.

Command:

# Find old pending CSRs (older than 1 hour)
kubectl get csr -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.status.conditions == null) | select(now - (.metadata.creationTimestamp | fromdateiso8601) > 3600) | .metadata.name'

# Deny old CSRs
kubectl certificate deny old-csr-12345

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/old-csr-12345 denied
Example 3: CSR Review and Deny Script

Interactive script for reviewing and denying suspicious CSRs.

Context - Security Review Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/review-csrs.sh

echo "=== Pending Certificate Signing Requests ==="
echo ""

kubectl get csr --field-selector=spec.signerName!=kubernetes.io/legacy-unknown \
    -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,REQUESTOR:.spec.username,USAGES:.spec.usages,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp'

echo ""
echo "Enter CSR name to review (or 'exit'):"

while read -r csr_name; do
    [ "$csr_name" = "exit" ] && break
    
    echo ""
    echo "=== CSR Details: $csr_name ==="
    kubectl get csr $csr_name -o yaml
    
    echo ""
    read -p "Action? (a)pprove / (d)eny / (s)kip: " action
    
    case $action in
        a) kubectl certificate approve $csr_name ;;
        d) kubectl certificate deny $csr_name ;;
        s) echo "Skipped" ;;
    esac
    
    echo ""
    echo "Enter next CSR name (or 'exit'):"
done

Command:

./scripts/review-csrs.sh
Example 4: Deny CSRs from Unauthorized Requestors

Automatically deny CSRs from non-approved sources.

Context - Automated Security Policy:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/enforce-csr-policy.sh

ALLOWED_REQUESTORS=(
    "admin"
    "system:node:*"
    "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:*"
)

echo "🔒 Enforcing CSR policy..."

kubectl get csr -o json | jq -r '
    .items[] | 
    select(.status.conditions == null) | 
    "\(.metadata.name) \(.spec.username)"
' | while read name requestor; do
    ALLOWED=false
    
    for pattern in "${ALLOWED_REQUESTORS[@]}"; do
        if [[ "$requestor" == $pattern ]]; then
            ALLOWED=true
            break
        fi
    done
    
    if [ "$ALLOWED" = false ]; then
        echo "  ❌ Denying $name (requestor: $requestor)"
        kubectl certificate deny $name
    else
        echo "  ✅ Allowing $name (requestor: $requestor)"
    fi
done

Command:

./scripts/enforce-csr-policy.sh
Example 5: Deny and Document for Audit

Deny CSR while logging for security audit.

Command:

# Log denial with reason
CSR_NAME="unauthorized-csr"
REASON="Unauthorized requestor - not in approved list"
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

# Record to audit log
echo "$TIMESTAMP | DENY | $CSR_NAME | $REASON" >> /var/log/k8s-csr-audit.log

# Deny the CSR
kubectl certificate deny $CSR_NAME

# Verify denial
kubectl get csr $CSR_NAME -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].type}'

Output:

certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/unauthorized-csr denied
Denied

kubectl cluster-info

CLUSTER INFO VIEW

Display cluster endpoint information

The kubectl cluster-info command displays the addresses of the control plane and cluster services. Essential for verifying cluster connectivity, troubleshooting networking issues, and documenting cluster endpoints for Django application configuration.

Syntax

kubectl cluster-info [flags]

Parameters

  • --context: Kubeconfig context to use

Subcommands

  • dump - Dump cluster state for debugging

Return Value

Colored output showing Kubernetes control plane and service URLs.

Examples

Example 1: View Cluster Endpoints

Check cluster API server and CoreDNS endpoints.

Command:

# Display cluster info
kubectl cluster-info

Output:

Kubernetes control plane is running at https://192.168.1.100:6443
CoreDNS is running at https://192.168.1.100:6443/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/services/kube-dns:dns/proxy

To further debug and diagnose cluster problems, use 'kubectl cluster-info dump'.
Example 2: Verify Multi-Cluster Connectivity

Check connectivity to different cluster contexts.

Context - Multi-Cluster Setup:

# ~/.kube/config snippet
contexts:
- name: development
  context:
    cluster: dev-cluster
    user: dev-admin
- name: staging
  context:
    cluster: staging-cluster
    user: staging-admin
- name: production
  context:
    cluster: prod-cluster
    user: prod-admin

Command:

# Check each cluster
kubectl cluster-info --context=development
kubectl cluster-info --context=staging
kubectl cluster-info --context=production

Output:

Kubernetes control plane is running at https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://staging.k8s.company.com:6443
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443
Example 3: Health Check Script

Automated cluster health verification for Django CI/CD.

Context - Health Check Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cluster-health-check.sh

CONTEXTS=("development" "staging" "production")

echo "=== Kubernetes Cluster Health Check ==="
echo "Timestamp: $(date)"
echo ""

for ctx in "${CONTEXTS[@]}"; do
    echo "📊 Checking: $ctx"
    
    if kubectl cluster-info --context=$ctx >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        API_URL=$(kubectl cluster-info --context=$ctx 2>/dev/null | grep "control plane" | awk '{print $NF}')
        echo "  ✅ Connected: $API_URL"
    else
        echo "  ❌ Connection failed"
    fi
    echo ""
done

Command:

./scripts/cluster-health-check.sh

Output:

=== Kubernetes Cluster Health Check ===
Timestamp: Mon Mar  2 14:30:00 UTC 2026

📊 Checking: development
  ✅ Connected: https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443

📊 Checking: staging
  ✅ Connected: https://staging.k8s.company.com:6443

📊 Checking: production
  ✅ Connected: https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443
Example 4: Configure Django Settings from Cluster Info

Extract cluster endpoint for Django configuration.

Context - Django Settings Generation:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/generate-django-k8s-config.sh

# Get cluster API URL
API_URL=$(kubectl cluster-info | grep "control plane" | awk '{print $NF}')

# Generate Django settings
cat > django_k8s_settings.py <<EOF
# Auto-generated Kubernetes settings
KUBERNETES_API_URL = "$API_URL"
KUBERNETES_NAMESPACE = "$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.contexts[0].context.namespace}')"
KUBERNETES_CONTEXT = "$(kubectl config current-context)"
EOF

echo "Generated django_k8s_settings.py"
cat django_k8s_settings.py

Command:

./scripts/generate-django-k8s-config.sh
Example 5: GitHub Actions Cluster Verification

Verify cluster connectivity in CI/CD pipeline.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy Django

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    
    - name: Setup kubectl
      uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
      
    - name: Configure kubeconfig
      run: echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}" | base64 -d > ~/.kube/config
      
    - name: Verify cluster connectivity
      run: |
        echo "🔍 Verifying cluster connectivity..."
        kubectl cluster-info
        echo ""
        echo "✅ Cluster is reachable"
        
    - name: Deploy application
      run: kubectl apply -f k8s/

Command:

# In CI/CD, verify before deployment
kubectl cluster-info || exit 1
kubectl apply -f k8s/

kubectl cluster-info dump

DUMP STATE DEBUG

Dump relevant information for debugging and diagnosis

The kubectl cluster-info dump command exports comprehensive cluster state for debugging. Includes logs, events, and resource definitions. Essential for troubleshooting Django deployment issues, collecting support information, and diagnosing cluster problems.

Syntax

kubectl cluster-info dump [--namespaces=NS1,NS2] [--output-directory=DIR] [options]

Parameters

  • --namespaces: Comma-separated list of namespaces to dump (default: all)
  • --all-namespaces: Dump all namespaces
  • --output-directory: Directory to write dump files
  • -o, --output: Output format (json, yaml)
  • --pod-running-timeout: Timeout for pod running check

Return Value

Cluster state dump in JSON/YAML format or written to specified directory.

Examples

Example 1: Dump Production Namespace for Support

Collect Django deployment state for troubleshooting.

Command:

# Dump production namespace to directory
kubectl cluster-info dump \
    --namespaces=production \
    --output-directory=/tmp/cluster-dump-$(date +%Y%m%d)

Output:

Cluster info dumped to /tmp/cluster-dump-20260302

Directory structure:

/tmp/cluster-dump-20260302/
├── nodes.json
├── production/
│   ├── daemonsets.json
│   ├── deployments.json
│   ├── events.json
│   ├── pods.json
│   ├── replicasets.json
│   ├── services.json
│   └── django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12/
│       └── logs.txt
└── kube-system/
    └── ...
Example 2: Quick Dump for Incident Response

Fast cluster state capture during incident.

Context - Incident Response Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/incident-capture.sh

INCIDENT_ID=${1:-$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)}
DUMP_DIR="/var/log/k8s-incidents/$INCIDENT_ID"

echo "🚨 Incident Response - Capturing cluster state"
echo "   Incident ID: $INCIDENT_ID"
echo "   Output: $DUMP_DIR"
echo ""

mkdir -p $DUMP_DIR

# Capture cluster dump
kubectl cluster-info dump \
    --all-namespaces \
    --output-directory=$DUMP_DIR

# Capture additional context
kubectl get events --all-namespaces --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' > $DUMP_DIR/all-events.txt
kubectl top nodes > $DUMP_DIR/node-resources.txt 2>/dev/null
kubectl top pods --all-namespaces > $DUMP_DIR/pod-resources.txt 2>/dev/null

# Create archive
tar -czf ${DUMP_DIR}.tar.gz -C /var/log/k8s-incidents $INCIDENT_ID

echo ""
echo "✅ Capture complete: ${DUMP_DIR}.tar.gz"

Command:

./scripts/incident-capture.sh INC-2026-0302
Example 3: Dump Specific Namespaces

Export only Django-related namespaces.

Command:

# Dump multiple Django namespaces
kubectl cluster-info dump \
    --namespaces=production,staging,monitoring \
    --output-directory=/tmp/django-stack-dump

# List what was captured
find /tmp/django-stack-dump -type f -name "*.json" | head -20

Output:

/tmp/django-stack-dump/production/deployments.json
/tmp/django-stack-dump/production/pods.json
/tmp/django-stack-dump/production/services.json
/tmp/django-stack-dump/staging/deployments.json
/tmp/django-stack-dump/staging/pods.json
/tmp/django-stack-dump/monitoring/deployments.json
Example 4: JSON Output for Analysis

Export cluster state as JSON for automated analysis.

Context - Analysis Script:

# scripts/analyze-dump.py
import json
import os

def analyze_namespace_dump(dump_dir, namespace):
    """Analyze dumped namespace for issues"""
    
    ns_dir = os.path.join(dump_dir, namespace)
    
    # Load pods
    with open(os.path.join(ns_dir, 'pods.json')) as f:
        pods = json.load(f)
    
    issues = []
    for pod in pods['items']:
        name = pod['metadata']['name']
        phase = pod['status']['phase']
        
        if phase not in ['Running', 'Succeeded']:
            issues.append(f"Pod {name}: {phase}")
        
        # Check container status
        for cs in pod['status'].get('containerStatuses', []):
            if cs.get('restartCount', 0) > 5:
                issues.append(f"Pod {name}: High restart count ({cs['restartCount']})")
    
    return issues

# Run analysis
issues = analyze_namespace_dump('/tmp/cluster-dump', 'production')
for issue in issues:
    print(f"  ⚠️ {issue}")

Command:

# Dump and analyze
kubectl cluster-info dump --namespaces=production -o json > /tmp/cluster-dump.json
python scripts/analyze-dump.py
Example 5: Scheduled Cluster State Backup

Regular cluster state backups via CronJob.

Context - Backup CronJob:

# k8s/jobs/cluster-backup-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: cluster-state-backup
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"  # Every 6 hours
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: cluster-admin
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/bash
            - -c
            - |
              TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
              kubectl cluster-info dump \
                --all-namespaces \
                --output-directory=/backups/$TIMESTAMP
              # Keep only last 7 days
              find /backups -type d -mtime +7 -exec rm -rf {} +
            volumeMounts:
            - name: backup-storage
              mountPath: /backups
          volumes:
          - name: backup-storage
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: cluster-backup-pvc
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

Command:

# Apply backup job
kubectl apply -f k8s/jobs/cluster-backup-job.yaml

# Trigger manual backup
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/cluster-state-backup manual-backup-$(date +%s)

kubectl cordon

CORDON NODE UNSCHEDULABLE

Mark node as unschedulable

The kubectl cordon command marks a node as unschedulable, preventing new pods from being scheduled on it. Existing pods continue running. Essential for planned maintenance, node upgrades, and gracefully removing nodes from the cluster without disrupting running Django workloads.

Syntax

kubectl cordon NODE [options]

Parameters

  • NODE: Name of the node to cordon
  • --dry-run: Preview without making changes (client, server, none)
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter nodes

Return Value

Confirmation that node was cordoned. Node status shows SchedulingDisabled.

Examples

Example 1: Cordon Node for Maintenance

Prepare node for scheduled maintenance without disrupting Django pods.

Context - Maintenance Window:

# Node maintenance schedule
# node-pool-a-001: Security patches required
# Maintenance window: 02:00-04:00 UTC

# Check current node status
kubectl get nodes
# NAME              STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# node-pool-a-001   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-002   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-003   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0

Command:

# Cordon the node to prevent new pods
kubectl cordon node-pool-a-001

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 cordoned

Verification:

kubectl get nodes
# NAME              STATUS                     ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# node-pool-a-001   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-002   Ready                      <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-003   Ready                      <none>   90d   v1.28.0
Example 2: Rolling Node Upgrade Script

Automated node upgrade with cordon/drain/uncordon pattern.

Context - Node Upgrade Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/rolling-node-upgrade.sh

NODE=$1

if [ -z "$NODE" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <node-name>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🔧 Starting rolling upgrade for: $NODE"
echo ""

# Step 1: Cordon
echo "Step 1: Cordoning node..."
kubectl cordon $NODE
echo ""

# Step 2: Drain (will be covered next)
echo "Step 2: Draining pods..."
kubectl drain $NODE --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --force
echo ""

# Step 3: Perform upgrade (placeholder)
echo "Step 3: Performing upgrade..."
echo "  (SSH to node and run: apt update && apt upgrade -y)"
read -p "Press enter when upgrade is complete..."
echo ""

# Step 4: Uncordon
echo "Step 4: Uncordoning node..."
kubectl uncordon $NODE
echo ""

echo "✅ Node upgrade complete for: $NODE"
kubectl get node $NODE

Command:

./scripts/rolling-node-upgrade.sh node-pool-a-001
Example 3: Cordon Multiple Nodes by Label

Cordon all nodes in a specific pool for bulk maintenance.

Context - Node Pool Labels:

# Node labels setup
# kubectl label nodes node-pool-a-001 pool=general
# kubectl label nodes node-pool-a-002 pool=general
# kubectl label nodes node-pool-gpu-001 pool=gpu

Command:

# Cordon all nodes in general pool
kubectl cordon -l pool=general

# Verify
kubectl get nodes -l pool=general

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 cordoned
node/node-pool-a-002 cordoned

NAME              STATUS                     ROLES    AGE   VERSION
node-pool-a-001   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   <none>   90d   v1.28.0
node-pool-a-002   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   <none>   90d   v1.28.0
Example 4: Dry Run Before Cordoning

Preview cordon operation before executing.

Command:

# Preview what would happen
kubectl cordon node-pool-a-001 --dry-run=client

# Check what pods are running on the node first
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o wide --field-selector spec.nodeName=node-pool-a-001

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 cordoned (dry run)

NAMESPACE    NAME                          READY   STATUS    NODE
production   django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    1/1     Running   node-pool-a-001
production   celery-worker-5f4d3c2b-xyz    1/1     Running   node-pool-a-001
kube-system  kube-proxy-abc12              1/1     Running   node-pool-a-001
Example 5: Cordon with Monitoring Alert Integration

Cordon node and notify monitoring system.

Context - Maintenance Script with Alerting:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cordon-with-alert.sh

NODE=$1
SLACK_WEBHOOK=$SLACK_MAINTENANCE_WEBHOOK

# Send start notification
curl -X POST -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
    --data "{\"text\": \"🔧 Starting maintenance: Cordoning $NODE\"}" \
    $SLACK_WEBHOOK

# Cordon the node
if kubectl cordon $NODE; then
    STATUS="✅ Successfully cordoned"
else
    STATUS="❌ Failed to cordon"
fi

# Send completion notification
curl -X POST -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
    --data "{\"text\": \"$STATUS $NODE\nPods still running: $(kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=$NODE --no-headers | wc -l)\"}" \
    $SLACK_WEBHOOK

Command:

./scripts/cordon-with-alert.sh node-pool-a-001

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 cordoned

kubectl drain

DRAIN NODE EVICT PODS

Drain node in preparation for maintenance

The kubectl drain command safely evicts all pods from a node and marks it as unschedulable. Essential for graceful node maintenance, ensuring Django applications are rescheduled to other nodes with proper pod disruption budgets honored.

Syntax

kubectl drain NODE [options]

Parameters

  • NODE: Name of the node to drain
  • --delete-emptydir-data: Delete pods using emptyDir volumes
  • --force: Force drain even if pods not managed by controller
  • --grace-period: Seconds to wait for graceful termination (default: -1)
  • --ignore-daemonsets: Ignore DaemonSet pods
  • --pod-selector: Label selector to filter pods
  • --timeout: Time to wait for drain to complete
  • --disable-eviction: Force delete instead of eviction

Return Value

Progress of pod evictions and confirmation when complete.

Examples

Example 1: Basic Drain with Common Flags

Standard drain for node maintenance with DaemonSet handling.

Context - Django Deployment with PodDisruptionBudget:

# k8s/django-pdb.yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: django-api-pdb
  namespace: production
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:latest
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: django-api
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname

Command:

kubectl drain node-pool-a-001 \
    --ignore-daemonsets \
    --delete-emptydir-data

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 cordoned
evicting pod production/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12
evicting pod production/celery-worker-5f4d3c2b-xyz
pod/django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 evicted
pod/celery-worker-5f4d3c2b-xyz evicted
node/node-pool-a-001 drained
Example 2: Force Drain with Timeout

Drain node with stuck pods using force and timeout.

Command:

# Force drain with 5-minute timeout
kubectl drain node-pool-a-001 \
    --ignore-daemonsets \
    --delete-emptydir-data \
    --force \
    --timeout=300s

Handling Stuck Pods:

# If drain is stuck, check what pods remain
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces \
    --field-selector spec.nodeName=node-pool-a-001

# Check for pods with local storage
kubectl get pods -A -o json --field-selector spec.nodeName=node-pool-a-001 | \
    jq '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.emptyDir != null) | .metadata.name'
Example 3: Graceful Drain Script for Production

Complete maintenance script with pre-checks and verification.

Context - Production Drain Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/graceful-drain.sh

set -e

NODE=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}

echo "=========================================="
echo "  Graceful Node Drain: $NODE"
echo "=========================================="

# Pre-flight checks
echo ""
echo "📋 Pre-flight Checks:"

# Check node exists
if ! kubectl get node $NODE > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "❌ Node $NODE not found"
    exit 1
fi

# Count pods on node
POD_COUNT=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE --field-selector spec.nodeName=$NODE --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
echo "  Pods in $NAMESPACE on node: $POD_COUNT"

# Check PDB status
echo ""
echo "📊 PodDisruptionBudget Status:"
kubectl get pdb -n $NAMESPACE

# Check available capacity on other nodes
echo ""
echo "💾 Cluster Capacity:"
kubectl top nodes 2>/dev/null || echo "  (metrics-server not installed)"

# Confirmation
echo ""
read -p "Proceed with drain? (y/n) " -n 1 -r
echo
if [[ ! $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    echo "Aborted."
    exit 1
fi

# Execute drain
echo ""
echo "🔧 Draining node..."
kubectl drain $NODE \
    --ignore-daemonsets \
    --delete-emptydir-data \
    --grace-period=60 \
    --timeout=600s

echo ""
echo "✅ Drain complete!"
kubectl get node $NODE

Command:

./scripts/graceful-drain.sh node-pool-a-001 production
Example 4: Drain Specific Pod Types Only

Selectively drain pods by label selector.

Command:

# Drain only Celery workers from node
kubectl drain node-pool-a-001 \
    --ignore-daemonsets \
    --delete-emptydir-data \
    --pod-selector="app=celery-worker"

# Verify Django API pods still running
kubectl get pods -l app=django-api -o wide | grep node-pool-a-001

Alternative - Manual Pod Eviction:

# Evict specific pods manually without draining entire node
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=celery-worker \
    --field-selector spec.nodeName=node-pool-a-001 \
    -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl delete {} -n production
Example 5: Rolling Maintenance GitHub Action

Automated rolling node maintenance via CI/CD.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/node-maintenance.yaml
name: Rolling Node Maintenance

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      node_pool:
        description: 'Node pool label (pool=general)'
        required: true
        default: 'pool=general'
      namespace:
        description: 'Primary namespace to monitor'
        default: 'production'

jobs:
  rolling-maintenance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Setup kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
        
      - name: Configure kubeconfig
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}" | base64 -d > kubeconfig
          export KUBECONFIG=kubeconfig
          
      - name: Get nodes in pool
        id: nodes
        run: |
          NODES=$(kubectl get nodes -l ${{ github.event.inputs.node_pool }} -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}')
          echo "nodes=$NODES" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          
      - name: Rolling drain and upgrade
        run: |
          for NODE in ${{ steps.nodes.outputs.nodes }}; do
            echo "Processing: $NODE"
            
            # Cordon
            kubectl cordon $NODE
            
            # Wait for PDB compliance
            sleep 30
            
            # Drain
            kubectl drain $NODE \
              --ignore-daemonsets \
              --delete-emptydir-data \
              --timeout=600s
            
            # Verify pods rescheduled
            kubectl get pods -n ${{ github.event.inputs.namespace }} \
              --field-selector spec.nodeName=$NODE --no-headers | wc -l
            
            # Placeholder: Node upgrade would happen here
            echo "Node $NODE drained - ready for maintenance"
            
            # Uncordon after maintenance
            kubectl uncordon $NODE
            
            # Wait for pods to stabilize
            sleep 60
          done
          
      - name: Final verification
        run: |
          kubectl get nodes -l ${{ github.event.inputs.node_pool }}
          kubectl get pods -n ${{ github.event.inputs.namespace }}

Trigger:

# Via GitHub CLI
gh workflow run node-maintenance.yaml \
    -f node_pool="pool=general" \
    -f namespace="production"

kubectl taint

TAINT NODE SCHEDULING

Update the taints on one or more nodes

The kubectl taint command adds, updates, or removes taints from nodes. Taints allow nodes to repel pods unless pods have matching tolerations. Essential for dedicating nodes to specific workloads (GPU, high-memory), isolating environments, and implementing node affinity rules for Django applications.

Syntax

kubectl taint node NODE KEY=VALUE:EFFECT [--overwrite] [options]
kubectl taint node NODE KEY:EFFECT-   # Remove taint

Parameters

  • NODE: Name of the node to taint (or use -l for selector)
  • KEY=VALUE:EFFECT: Taint specification
  • EFFECT: NoSchedule | PreferNoSchedule | NoExecute
  • --overwrite: Overwrite existing taint with same key
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter nodes

Effects

  • NoSchedule: Never schedule pods without toleration
  • PreferNoSchedule: Avoid scheduling but allow if necessary
  • NoExecute: Evict existing pods without toleration

Return Value

Confirmation of taint addition, update, or removal.

Examples

Example 1: Dedicated GPU Node for ML Workloads

Taint GPU nodes so only ML pods with tolerations are scheduled.

Context - Taint GPU Nodes:

# Add taint to GPU nodes
kubectl taint nodes node-gpu-001 gpu=true:NoSchedule
kubectl taint nodes node-gpu-002 gpu=true:NoSchedule

# Verify taints
kubectl describe node node-gpu-001 | grep Taints

Context - ML Training Pod with Toleration:

# k8s/ml-training-job.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: model-training
  namespace: ml-workloads
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - key: "gpu"
        operator: "Equal"
        value: "true"
        effect: "NoSchedule"
      nodeSelector:
        gpu: "true"
      containers:
      - name: training
        image: myregistry/ml-trainer:latest
        resources:
          limits:
            nvidia.com/gpu: 2
        command: ["python", "train.py"]
      restartPolicy: Never

Command:

kubectl apply -f k8s/ml-training-job.yaml

Output:

job.batch/model-training created
Example 2: Environment Isolation with Taints

Dedicate nodes to production workloads.

Context - Taint Production Nodes:

# Taint nodes for production-only workloads
kubectl taint nodes -l pool=production environment=production:NoSchedule

Context - Django Production Deployment:

# k8s/django-production.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - key: "environment"
        operator: "Equal"
        value: "production"
        effect: "NoSchedule"
      nodeSelector:
        pool: production
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.1.0
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings.production"
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"

Verification:

# Staging pods should NOT schedule on production nodes
kubectl get pods -n staging -o wide
# All pods should be on non-production nodes
Example 3: High-Memory Node Dedication

Reserve high-memory nodes for Celery workers with large tasks.

Context - Setup High-Memory Taints:

# Taint high-memory nodes
kubectl taint nodes node-highmem-001 workload=memory-intensive:NoSchedule
kubectl taint nodes node-highmem-002 workload=memory-intensive:NoSchedule

# Label them for node selector
kubectl label nodes node-highmem-001 node-highmem-002 memory=high

Context - Celery Worker Deployment:

# k8s/celery-heavy-worker.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: celery-heavy-worker
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: celery-heavy-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: celery-heavy-worker
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - key: "workload"
        operator: "Equal"
        value: "memory-intensive"
        effect: "NoSchedule"
      nodeSelector:
        memory: high
      containers:
      - name: celery
        image: myregistry/django-api:latest
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "-Q", "heavy", "-c", "2"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
          limits:
            memory: "8Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"
Example 4: NoExecute Taint for Immediate Eviction

Use NoExecute to evict running pods during emergency maintenance.

Command:

# Emergency: evict all non-critical pods from problematic node
kubectl taint nodes node-pool-a-001 maintenance=emergency:NoExecute

# Critical pods can survive with tolerationSeconds
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=node-pool-a-001

Context - Pod with Eviction Grace Period:

# k8s/critical-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: critical-django-job
  namespace: production
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "maintenance"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "emergency"
    effect: "NoExecute"
    tolerationSeconds: 300  # 5 minutes to finish
  containers:
  - name: worker
    image: myregistry/django-api:latest
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "critical_cleanup"]

Remove Taint After Maintenance:

# Remove the emergency taint
kubectl taint nodes node-pool-a-001 maintenance=emergency:NoExecute-

# Verify taint removed
kubectl describe node node-pool-a-001 | grep -A5 Taints
Example 5: Spot/Preemptible Node Taints

Manage spot instances with taints for cost optimization.

Context - Spot Node Configuration:

# Cloud provider typically adds this taint automatically
# Or add manually for spot instances
kubectl taint nodes -l node.kubernetes.io/instance-type=spot \
    cloud.google.com/gke-spot=true:NoSchedule

Context - Batch Job on Spot Instances:

# k8s/batch-job-spot.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-processing-batch
  namespace: batch
spec:
  parallelism: 10
  completions: 100
  template:
    spec:
      tolerations:
      # Tolerate spot instance taint
      - key: "cloud.google.com/gke-spot"
        operator: "Equal"
        value: "true"
        effect: "NoSchedule"
      # Tolerate preemption (node may be reclaimed)
      - key: "cloud.google.com/gke-preemptible"
        operator: "Exists"
        effect: "NoSchedule"
      nodeSelector:
        node.kubernetes.io/instance-type: spot
      containers:
      - name: processor
        image: myregistry/batch-processor:latest
        command: ["python", "process_batch.py"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "500m"
      restartPolicy: OnFailure

List All Taints:

# View all node taints across cluster
kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=\
  NAME:.metadata.name,\
  TAINTS:.spec.taints

kubectl uncordon

UNCORDON NODE SCHEDULABLE

Mark node as schedulable

The kubectl uncordon command marks a previously cordoned node as schedulable again, allowing new pods to be scheduled on it. Used after maintenance or drain operations to return nodes to active service in the cluster.

Syntax

kubectl uncordon NODE [options]

Parameters

  • NODE: Name of the node to uncordon
  • --dry-run: Preview without making changes (client, server, none)
  • -l, --selector: Label selector to filter nodes

Return Value

Confirmation that node is schedulable. Node status removes SchedulingDisabled.

Examples

Example 1: Basic Uncordon After Maintenance

Return node to service after completing maintenance window.

Context - Current State:

# Node was cordoned for maintenance
kubectl get nodes
# NAME              STATUS                     ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# node-pool-a-001   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-002   Ready                      <none>   90d   v1.28.0

Command:

kubectl uncordon node-pool-a-001

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 uncordoned

Verification:

kubectl get nodes
# NAME              STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# node-pool-a-001   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
# node-pool-a-002   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
Example 2: Uncordon All Nodes in Pool

Bulk uncordon after pool-wide maintenance.

Command:

# Uncordon all nodes in general pool
kubectl uncordon -l pool=general

# Verify all nodes are schedulable
kubectl get nodes -l pool=general

Output:

node/node-pool-a-001 uncordoned
node/node-pool-a-002 uncordoned
node/node-pool-a-003 uncordoned

NAME              STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
node-pool-a-001   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
node-pool-a-002   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
node-pool-a-003   Ready    <none>   90d   v1.28.0
Example 3: Post-Maintenance Verification Script

Uncordon with health verification before returning to service.

Context - Safe Uncordon Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/safe-uncordon.sh

NODE=$1

if [ -z "$NODE" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <node-name>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🔍 Pre-uncordon Health Check: $NODE"
echo ""

# Check node is Ready
STATUS=$(kubectl get node $NODE -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}')
if [ "$STATUS" != "True" ]; then
    echo "❌ Node is not Ready. Status: $STATUS"
    exit 1
fi
echo "  ✅ Node status: Ready"

# Check kubelet is healthy
KUBELET=$(kubectl get node $NODE -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].reason}')
echo "  ✅ Kubelet: $KUBELET"

# Check node resources
echo ""
echo "📊 Node Resources:"
kubectl describe node $NODE | grep -A5 "Allocated resources"

# Check disk pressure, memory pressure, PID pressure
PRESSURES=$(kubectl get node $NODE -o jsonpath='{range .status.conditions[*]}{.type}={.status} {end}')
echo ""
echo "  Conditions: $PRESSURES"

# Proceed with uncordon
echo ""
read -p "Proceed with uncordon? (y/n) " -n 1 -r
echo
if [[ $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    kubectl uncordon $NODE
    echo ""
    echo "✅ Node uncordoned successfully"
    
    # Watch for new pod scheduling
    echo ""
    echo "Watching for pods scheduled to $NODE..."
    kubectl get pods -A --watch --field-selector spec.nodeName=$NODE &
    sleep 30
    kill %1 2>/dev/null
fi

Command:

./scripts/safe-uncordon.sh node-pool-a-001
Example 4: Complete Maintenance Workflow

Full cordon → drain → maintenance → uncordon cycle.

Context - Complete Maintenance Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/complete-node-maintenance.sh

NODE=$1
NAMESPACE=${2:-production}

echo "=========================================="
echo "  Complete Node Maintenance: $NODE"
echo "=========================================="

# Phase 1: Cordon
echo ""
echo "Phase 1: Cordon"
kubectl cordon $NODE
echo "  ✅ Node cordoned"

# Phase 2: Drain
echo ""
echo "Phase 2: Drain"
kubectl drain $NODE \
    --ignore-daemonsets \
    --delete-emptydir-data \
    --grace-period=60 \
    --timeout=600s
echo "  ✅ Node drained"

# Phase 3: Maintenance
echo ""
echo "Phase 3: Maintenance"
echo "  Perform your maintenance tasks now..."
echo "  Examples:"
echo "    - SSH to node and apply patches"
echo "    - Reboot node: ssh $NODE 'sudo reboot'"
echo "    - Wait for node to come back online"
echo ""
read -p "Press enter when maintenance is complete..."

# Wait for node to be Ready
echo ""
echo "Waiting for node to be Ready..."
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready node/$NODE --timeout=300s
echo "  ✅ Node is Ready"

# Phase 4: Uncordon
echo ""
echo "Phase 4: Uncordon"
kubectl uncordon $NODE
echo "  ✅ Node uncordoned and schedulable"

# Final verification
echo ""
echo "=========================================="
echo "  Final Status"
echo "=========================================="
kubectl get node $NODE
echo ""
kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -o wide | grep $NODE || echo "No pods scheduled yet"

Command:

./scripts/complete-node-maintenance.sh node-pool-a-001 production
Example 5: Automated Uncordon with Slack Notification

Uncordon with team notification for visibility.

Context - CI/CD Job for Uncordon:

# .github/workflows/uncordon-node.yaml
name: Uncordon Node After Maintenance

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      node_name:
        description: 'Node to uncordon'
        required: true
      verify_health:
        description: 'Verify node health before uncordon'
        type: boolean
        default: true

jobs:
  uncordon:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Setup kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
        
      - name: Configure kubeconfig
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}" | base64 -d > kubeconfig
          export KUBECONFIG=kubeconfig
          
      - name: Health check
        if: ${{ github.event.inputs.verify_health == 'true' }}
        run: |
          export KUBECONFIG=kubeconfig
          
          # Check node is Ready
          STATUS=$(kubectl get node ${{ github.event.inputs.node_name }} \
            -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}')
          
          if [ "$STATUS" != "True" ]; then
            echo "::error::Node is not Ready"
            exit 1
          fi
          
          echo "Node health verified"
          
      - name: Uncordon node
        run: |
          export KUBECONFIG=kubeconfig
          kubectl uncordon ${{ github.event.inputs.node_name }}
          
      - name: Notify Slack
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
        with:
          payload: |
            {
              "text": "✅ Node Uncordoned",
              "blocks": [
                {
                  "type": "section",
                  "text": {
                    "type": "mrkdwn",
                    "text": "*Node Maintenance Complete*\n• Node: `${{ github.event.inputs.node_name }}`\n• Status: Schedulable\n• By: ${{ github.actor }}"
                  }
                }
              ]
            }
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}

Trigger:

# Via GitHub CLI
gh workflow run uncordon-node.yaml \
    -f node_name="node-pool-a-001" \
    -f verify_health="true"

⚙️ KUBECTL SETTINGS & USAGE

Commands for configuring kubectl, managing contexts, and accessing documentation. Essential for multi-cluster management and development workflows.

kubectl alpha

ALPHA EXPERIMENTAL FEATURES

Commands for features in alpha stage

The kubectl alpha command provides access to experimental features that are in alpha testing. These commands may change or be removed in future versions. Useful for testing upcoming kubectl features before they become stable, including advanced debugging capabilities for Django deployments.

Syntax

kubectl alpha SUBCOMMAND [options]

Parameters

  • SUBCOMMAND: The alpha feature to use (varies by kubectl version)

Available Alpha Commands

  • debug: Create debugging sessions (graduated to stable in v1.25+)
  • events: List events (graduated to stable)
  • auth: Inspect authorization (experimental features)

Return Value

Output varies by subcommand. May include warnings about alpha status.

Examples

Example 1: Check Available Alpha Commands

Discover what alpha features are available in your kubectl version.

Context - Development Environment:

# Development team wants to test new kubectl features
# before recommending them for production Django workflows

# Check kubectl version first
