kubectl version --client --short 2>/dev/null || kubectl version --client

Command:

# List available alpha commands
kubectl alpha --help

Output:

These commands correspond to alpha features that are not enabled in Kubernetes clusters by default.

Available Commands:
  debug       Create debugging sessions for troubleshooting workloads and pods

Usage:
  kubectl alpha [flags] [options]

Use "kubectl alpha <command> --help" for more information about a given command.
Example 2: Alpha Debug for Django Pod

Use alpha debug feature to troubleshoot Django application issues.

Context - Django Deployment with Issue:

# k8s/django-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: django-api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: myregistry/django-api:v2.4.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: django-secrets
              key: database-url
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30

Command:

# Create debug session with ephemeral container (alpha in older versions)
kubectl alpha debug django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 \
    -n production \
    --image=busybox \
    --target=django \
    -- sh

Output:

Targeting container "django". If you don't see processes from this container 
it may be because the container runtime doesn't support this feature.
Defaulting debug container name to debugger-abc12.
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
/ #

Verification - Inside Debug Container:

# Check network connectivity
wget -qO- http://localhost:8000/health/

# Check environment variables
env | grep DATABASE

# Check DNS resolution
nslookup postgres-service
Example 3: Alpha Debug with Custom Image

Debug Django pod using Python-enabled debug image.

Context - Custom Debug Dockerfile:

# docker/debug-tools/Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Install debugging tools
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    curl \
    wget \
    netcat-openbsd \
    dnsutils \
    postgresql-client \
    redis-tools \
    vim \
    procps \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python debugging packages
RUN pip install \
    django \
    psycopg2-binary \
    redis \
    requests \
    ipython

# Keep container running
CMD ["tail", "-f", "/dev/null"]

Build and Push:

# Build debug image
docker build -t myregistry/debug-tools:latest -f docker/debug-tools/Dockerfile .
docker push myregistry/debug-tools:latest

Command:

# Use custom debug image with Python
kubectl alpha debug django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12 \
    -n production \
    --image=myregistry/debug-tools:latest \
    --share-processes \
    -- python -c "import django; print(django.VERSION)"
Example 4: Debug Node Issues Affecting Django

Create debugging pod on specific node to investigate node-level issues.

Context - Node Investigation Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/debug-node.sh

NODE=$1

if [ -z "$NODE" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <node-name>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🔍 Creating debug pod on node: $NODE"

# Create debug pod on specific node
kubectl alpha debug node/$NODE \
    --image=ubuntu \
    -- chroot /host bash -c "
        echo '=== Node Info ===' && \
        uname -a && \
        echo '' && \
        echo '=== Disk Usage ===' && \
        df -h && \
        echo '' && \
        echo '=== Memory ===' && \
        free -m && \
        echo '' && \
        echo '=== Running Containers ===' && \
        crictl ps
    "

Command:

# Debug node where Django pods are failing
kubectl alpha debug node/node-pool-a-001 --image=ubuntu -- /bin/bash

Output:

Creating debugging pod node-debugger-node-pool-a-001-abc12 with container debugger on node node-pool-a-001.
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
root@node-pool-a-001:/#
Example 5: Feature Gate Testing Script

Script to test alpha features before enabling in production.

Context - Alpha Feature Testing Workflow:

# .github/workflows/test-alpha-features.yaml
name: Test Alpha Features

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # Weekly on Monday
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  test-alpha:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
        with:
          version: 'latest'
          
      - name: Setup test cluster
        uses: helm/kind-action@v1
        with:
          cluster_name: alpha-test
          
      - name: Deploy test Django app
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/test/django-deployment.yaml
          kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=django-test --timeout=120s
          
      - name: Test alpha debug
        run: |
          POD=$(kubectl get pods -l app=django-test -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
          
          # Test alpha debug feature
          kubectl alpha debug $POD --image=busybox -- echo "Alpha debug works!"
          
      - name: Document results
        run: |
          echo "## Alpha Feature Test Results" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "- kubectl version: $(kubectl version --client -o json | jq -r '.clientVersion.gitVersion')" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "- Alpha debug: ✅ Working" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

Command:

# Trigger alpha feature testing workflow
gh workflow run test-alpha-features.yaml

kubectl completion

COMPLETION SHELL GENERATE

Generate shell completion scripts

The kubectl completion command generates autocompletion scripts for bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell. Enables tab-completion for kubectl commands, resource names, and flags, significantly improving productivity when managing Django Kubernetes deployments.

Syntax

kubectl completion SHELL

Parameters

  • SHELL: Target shell (bash, zsh, fish, powershell)

Supported Shells

  • bash: Bourne Again Shell (Linux default)
  • zsh: Z Shell (macOS default since Catalina)
  • fish: Friendly Interactive Shell
  • powershell: Windows PowerShell / pwsh

Return Value

Shell script code for autocompletion. Must be sourced or saved to appropriate location.

Examples

Example 1: Setup Bash Completion (Linux)

Configure kubectl autocompletion for bash on development machine.

Context - Developer Machine Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-dev-machine.sh
# Run this on new developer machines for Django K8s development

echo "🔧 Setting up Kubernetes development environment"

# Install kubectl (if not present)
if ! command -v kubectl &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing kubectl..."
    curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
    sudo install -o root -g root -m 0755 kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl
    rm kubectl
fi

# Install bash-completion (required for kubectl completion)
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y bash-completion

# Enable kubectl completion globally
kubectl completion bash | sudo tee /etc/bash_completion.d/kubectl > /dev/null

echo "✅ Restart your shell or run: source ~/.bashrc"

Command:

# Generate bash completion script
kubectl completion bash

# Install permanently (requires sudo)
kubectl completion bash | sudo tee /etc/bash_completion.d/kubectl > /dev/null

# Or for current user only
echo 'source <(kubectl completion bash)' >> ~/.bashrc

Verification:

# Reload and test
source ~/.bashrc

# Now tab completion works:
kubectl get pod[TAB]         # completes to 'pods'
kubectl -n prod[TAB]         # shows namespaces starting with 'prod'
kubectl get pods django-[TAB] # shows pods starting with 'django-'
Example 2: Setup Zsh Completion (macOS)

Configure kubectl completion for zsh on macOS development machine.

Context - macOS Developer Setup:

#!/bin/zsh
# scripts/setup-macos-k8s.sh

echo "🍎 Setting up Kubernetes for macOS Django development"

# Install kubectl via Homebrew
brew install kubectl

# Create completions directory if needed
mkdir -p ~/.zsh/completions

# Generate and save completion
kubectl completion zsh > ~/.zsh/completions/_kubectl

# Add to .zshrc if not present
if ! grep -q "fpath+=~/.zsh/completions" ~/.zshrc; then
    cat <<'EOF' >> ~/.zshrc

# Kubectl completion
fpath+=~/.zsh/completions
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit
EOF
fi

echo "✅ Run: source ~/.zshrc"

Command:

# Generate zsh completion
kubectl completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_kubectl"

# Or add to .zshrc for dynamic loading
echo 'source <(kubectl completion zsh)' >> ~/.zshrc

Output (completion script header):

#compdef kubectl

# Copyright 2016 The Kubernetes Authors.
# bash completion for kubectl                             -*- shell-script -*-
...
Example 3: PowerShell Completion (Windows)

Setup kubectl autocompletion for PowerShell on Windows.

Context - Windows PowerShell Profile:

# Create PowerShell profile if it doesn't exist
# $PROFILE path: C:\Users\{username}\Documents\PowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1

if (!(Test-Path -Path $PROFILE)) {
    New-Item -ItemType File -Path $PROFILE -Force
}

# Add kubectl completion to profile
Add-Content -Path $PROFILE -Value @'

# Kubectl completion
kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

# Optional: Create alias
Set-Alias -Name k -Value kubectl
'@

Command:

# Generate PowerShell completion
kubectl completion powershell

# Add to current session
kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

# Make permanent by adding to profile
kubectl completion powershell >> $PROFILE

Verification:

# Test tab completion
kubectl get [TAB]           # Shows resource types
kubectl -n [TAB]            # Shows namespaces
kubectl get pods -o [TAB]   # Shows output formats (json, yaml, wide)
Example 4: Alias with Completion

Create 'k' alias with autocompletion support.

Context - .bashrc Configuration:

# ~/.bashrc - Kubernetes aliases with completion

# Enable kubectl completion
source <(kubectl completion bash)

# Create 'k' alias
alias k=kubectl

# Enable completion for 'k' alias
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Additional useful aliases
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get services'
alias kgd='kubectl get deployments'
alias kns='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'
alias kctx='kubectl config use-context'

# Function to quickly switch namespace
kn() {
    if [ -z "$1" ]; then
        kubectl config view --minify --output 'jsonpath={..namespace}' && echo
    else
        kubectl config set-context --current --namespace="$1"
    fi
}

Command:

# Setup 'k' alias with completion
alias k=kubectl
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

Usage:

# Now these all work with tab completion:
k get po[TAB]              # completes to 'pods'
k -n production get de[TAB] # completes to 'deployments'
k logs django-[TAB]        # shows Django pod names
Example 5: Docker Image with Completion

Create a Docker image with kubectl and completion for CI/CD.

Context - CI/CD Kubectl Dockerfile:

# docker/kubectl-ci/Dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:22.04

# Install dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    curl \
    bash-completion \
    ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install kubectl
RUN curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl" \
    && install -o root -g root -m 0755 kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl \
    && rm kubectl

# Setup bash completion
RUN kubectl completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/kubectl

# Setup bashrc for interactive shells
RUN echo 'source /etc/bash_completion' >> /root/.bashrc \
    && echo 'alias k=kubectl' >> /root/.bashrc \
    && echo 'complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k' >> /root/.bashrc

# Set working directory for Django K8s manifests
WORKDIR /k8s

ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash"]

Build and Use:

# Build the image
docker build -t myregistry/kubectl-ci:latest -f docker/kubectl-ci/Dockerfile .

# Run interactively with kubeconfig mounted
docker run -it \
    -v ~/.kube:/root/.kube:ro \
    -v ./k8s:/k8s:ro \
    myregistry/kubectl-ci:latest

Usage in Container:

# Inside container, completion works
k get pods -n production[TAB]
k apply -f django-[TAB]

kubectl config

CONFIG KUBECONFIG MANAGE

Modify kubeconfig files

The kubectl config command manages kubeconfig files for connecting to multiple Kubernetes clusters. Essential for developers working with development, staging, and production Django environments across different clusters.

Subcommands Overview

  • current-context - Display the current context
  • delete-cluster - Delete a cluster from kubeconfig
  • delete-context - Delete a context from kubeconfig
  • delete-user - Delete a user from kubeconfig
  • get-clusters - Display clusters in kubeconfig
  • get-contexts - Display contexts in kubeconfig
  • get-users - Display users in kubeconfig
  • rename-context - Rename a context
  • set - Set individual values in kubeconfig
  • set-cluster - Set cluster entry in kubeconfig
  • set-context - Set context entry in kubeconfig
  • set-credentials - Set user entry in kubeconfig
  • unset - Unset values in kubeconfig
  • use-context - Set the current context
  • view - Display merged kubeconfig

kubectl config current-context

GET CONTEXT CURRENT

Display the current-context

The kubectl config current-context command displays the name of the currently active context. Useful for verifying which cluster you're connected to before executing commands on Django deployments.

Syntax

kubectl config current-context

Parameters

  • No additional parameters

Return Value

Name of the current context. Empty if no context is set.

Examples

Example 1: Check Current Context Before Deploy

Verify context before deploying Django application.

Context - Multi-Cluster kubeconfig:

# ~/.kube/config
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: production-cluster
clusters:
- name: development-cluster
  cluster:
    server: https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443
    certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1...
- name: staging-cluster
  cluster:
    server: https://staging.k8s.company.com:6443
- name: production-cluster
  cluster:
    server: https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443
contexts:
- name: development-cluster
  context:
    cluster: development-cluster
    namespace: django-dev
    user: dev-admin
- name: staging-cluster
  context:
    cluster: staging-cluster
    namespace: django-staging
    user: staging-admin
- name: production-cluster
  context:
    cluster: production-cluster
    namespace: production
    user: prod-admin
users:
- name: dev-admin
  user:
    token: <dev-token>
- name: staging-admin
  user:
    token: <staging-token>
- name: prod-admin
  user:
    token: <prod-token>

Command:

# Check which cluster we're connected to
kubectl config current-context

Output:

production-cluster
Example 2: Safe Deployment Script

Deployment script that verifies context before applying changes.

Context - Safe Deploy Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/safe-deploy.sh

EXPECTED_CONTEXT=$1
MANIFESTS_DIR=${2:-k8s/production}

if [ -z "$EXPECTED_CONTEXT" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <expected-context> [manifests-dir]"
    exit 1
fi

# Get current context
CURRENT=$(kubectl config current-context)

echo "🔍 Deployment Safety Check"
echo "   Expected context: $EXPECTED_CONTEXT"
echo "   Current context:  $CURRENT"
echo ""

if [ "$CURRENT" != "$EXPECTED_CONTEXT" ]; then
    echo "❌ ERROR: Context mismatch!"
    echo "   You're connected to '$CURRENT' but trying to deploy to '$EXPECTED_CONTEXT'"
    echo ""
    echo "   Switch context with: kubectl config use-context $EXPECTED_CONTEXT"
    exit 1
fi

echo "✅ Context verified. Proceeding with deployment..."
kubectl apply -f $MANIFESTS_DIR/

Command:

# Safe deploy to production
./scripts/safe-deploy.sh production-cluster k8s/production
Example 3: Shell Prompt Integration

Add current context to shell prompt for awareness.

Context - .bashrc with K8s Prompt:

# ~/.bashrc - Kubernetes-aware prompt

# Function to get current k8s context
__kube_ps1() {
    local context=$(kubectl config current-context 2>/dev/null)
    local namespace=$(kubectl config view --minify --output 'jsonpath={..namespace}' 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ -n "$context" ]; then
        # Color code by environment
        local color="\033[0;32m"  # Green default
        
        case "$context" in
            *prod*)    color="\033[0;31m" ;;  # Red for production
            *staging*) color="\033[0;33m" ;;  # Yellow for staging
        esac
        
        echo -e "${color}☸ ${context}/${namespace:-default}\033[0m"
    fi
}

# Update PS1 to include Kubernetes context
PS1='$(__kube_ps1) \w \$ '

Result:

☸ production-cluster/production ~/projects/django-api $ kubectl get pods
NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
django-api-7d9f8b6c4-abc12    1/1     Running   0          2d
Example 4: CI/CD Context Verification

GitHub Actions step to verify context before deployment.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy-production.yaml
name: Deploy to Production

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup kubectl
        uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
        
      - name: Configure kubeconfig
        run: |
          mkdir -p ~/.kube
          echo "${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_PRODUCTION }}" | base64 -d > ~/.kube/config
          
      - name: Verify context
        run: |
          CONTEXT=$(kubectl config current-context)
          echo "Current context: $CONTEXT"
          
          if [[ "$CONTEXT" != *"production"* ]]; then
            echo "::error::Expected production context, got: $CONTEXT"
            exit 1
          fi
          
      - name: Deploy Django
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/production/

Command:

# In CI, verify we're on production
CONTEXT=$(kubectl config current-context)
if [[ "$CONTEXT" != "production-cluster" ]]; then exit 1; fi
Example 5: Context-Aware Alias

Create aliases that show current context for safety.

Context - .bashrc Aliases:

# ~/.bashrc - Safe kubectl aliases

# Show context before dangerous operations
alias kdel='echo "⚠️  Context: $(kubectl config current-context)" && kubectl delete'
alias kapply='echo "⚠️  Context: $(kubectl config current-context)" && kubectl apply'

# Quick context check function
kctx() {
    if [ -z "$1" ]; then
        kubectl config current-context
    else
        kubectl config use-context "$1"
    fi
}

# List all contexts with current highlighted
alias kctxs='kubectl config get-contexts'

Usage:

# Check context quickly
kctx
# Output: production-cluster

# Apply with context warning
kapply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
# Output:
# ⚠️  Context: production-cluster
# deployment.apps/django-api configured

kubectl config delete-cluster

DELETE CLUSTER REMOVE

Delete the specified cluster from kubeconfig

The kubectl config delete-cluster command removes a cluster entry from your kubeconfig file. Used when decommissioning clusters or cleaning up obsolete configurations.

Syntax

kubectl config delete-cluster CLUSTER_NAME

Parameters

  • CLUSTER_NAME: Name of the cluster to delete

Return Value

Confirmation message that cluster was deleted.

Examples

Example 1: Remove Decommissioned Cluster

Clean up kubeconfig after decommissioning old Django cluster.

Context - Cluster Migration:

# Old cluster is being decommissioned after migrating Django to new cluster
# Current kubeconfig has:
# - old-django-cluster (being decommissioned)
# - new-django-cluster (active)
# - staging-cluster
# - production-cluster

kubectl config get-clusters

Command:

# Remove old cluster
kubectl config delete-cluster old-django-cluster

Output:

deleted cluster old-django-cluster from /home/user/.kube/config

Verification:

kubectl config get-clusters
# new-django-cluster
# staging-cluster
# production-cluster
Example 2: Cleanup Script for Old Clusters

Script to remove multiple obsolete clusters.

Context - Cleanup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cleanup-kubeconfig.sh

# Clusters to remove (old/test clusters)
OLD_CLUSTERS=(
    "test-cluster-2024"
    "temp-django-cluster"
    "poc-kubernetes"
)

echo "🧹 Cleaning up old clusters from kubeconfig"
echo ""

for cluster in "${OLD_CLUSTERS[@]}"; do
    if kubectl config get-clusters | grep -q "^$cluster$"; then
        echo "  Removing: $cluster"
        kubectl config delete-cluster "$cluster"
    else
        echo "  Skipped (not found): $cluster"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "✅ Remaining clusters:"
kubectl config get-clusters

Command:

./scripts/cleanup-kubeconfig.sh
Example 3: Delete All Test Clusters

Remove all clusters matching a pattern.

Command:

# Delete all clusters with 'test' in the name
kubectl config get-clusters | grep test | while read cluster; do
    echo "Deleting: $cluster"
    kubectl config delete-cluster "$cluster"
done
Example 4: Safe Delete with Backup

Backup kubeconfig before deleting cluster.

Command:

# Backup before deletion
cp ~/.kube/config ~/.kube/config.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Delete the cluster
kubectl config delete-cluster old-cluster

# Verify backup exists for rollback
ls -la ~/.kube/config.backup.*
Example 5: Delete Cluster and Associated Context

Remove cluster and its associated context together.

Context - Complete Cluster Removal Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/remove-cluster-complete.sh

CLUSTER=$1

if [ -z "$CLUSTER" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <cluster-name>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🗑️  Removing cluster: $CLUSTER"

# Find and remove associated contexts
CONTEXTS=$(kubectl config get-contexts -o name | while read ctx; do
    CTX_CLUSTER=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.contexts[?(@.name=='$ctx')].context.cluster}")
    if [ "$CTX_CLUSTER" == "$CLUSTER" ]; then
        echo "$ctx"
    fi
done)

for ctx in $CONTEXTS; do
    echo "  Removing context: $ctx"
    kubectl config delete-context "$ctx"
done

# Remove the cluster
kubectl config delete-cluster "$CLUSTER"

echo "✅ Complete removal done"

Command:

./scripts/remove-cluster-complete.sh old-django-cluster

kubectl config delete-context

DELETE CONTEXT REMOVE

Delete the specified context from kubeconfig

The kubectl config delete-context command removes a context entry from kubeconfig. Contexts associate clusters, users, and namespaces. Used for cleanup when removing access to environments.

Syntax

kubectl config delete-context CONTEXT_NAME

Parameters

  • CONTEXT_NAME: Name of the context to delete

Return Value

Confirmation message that context was deleted.

Examples

Example 1: Remove Unused Context

Clean up context from previous project.

Context - Current Contexts:

kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME                    CLUSTER              AUTHINFO       NAMESPACE
# *         production              prod-cluster         prod-admin     production
#           staging                 staging-cluster      staging-admin  staging
#           old-project-context     old-cluster          old-user       default

Command:

kubectl config delete-context old-project-context

Output:

deleted context old-project-context from /home/user/.kube/config
Example 2: Remove Temporary Dev Context

Delete temporary context created for testing.

Context - Temporary Context Setup:

# Earlier: Created temp context for testing
kubectl config set-context temp-debug \
    --cluster=production \
    --user=admin \
    --namespace=debug-namespace

# After testing: Remove it
kubectl config delete-context temp-debug

Command:

kubectl config delete-context temp-debug

Output:

deleted context temp-debug from /home/user/.kube/config
Example 3: Delete Current Context (Requires Switch)

Handle deletion of currently active context.

Command:

# Cannot delete current context while active
# First switch to another context
kubectl config use-context staging

# Now delete the old current context
kubectl config delete-context old-production

# Verify current is now staging
kubectl config current-context
Example 4: Bulk Delete Contexts by Pattern

Remove all contexts matching a pattern.

Command:

# Delete all contexts containing 'test' or 'temp'
kubectl config get-contexts -o name | grep -E 'test|temp' | while read ctx; do
    echo "Deleting context: $ctx"
    kubectl config delete-context "$ctx"
done
Example 5: Offboarding Script - Remove User Contexts

Remove contexts associated with a user leaving the team.

Context - Offboarding Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/offboard-user.sh

USER=$1

if [ -z "$USER" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <username>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🔐 Offboarding user: $USER"
echo ""

# Find and remove contexts using this user
kubectl config get-contexts -o name | while read ctx; do
    CTX_USER=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.contexts[?(@.name=='$ctx')].context.user}")
    if [ "$CTX_USER" == "$USER" ]; then
        echo "  Removing context: $ctx (uses user $USER)"
        kubectl config delete-context "$ctx"
    fi
done

# Remove the user
echo "  Removing user: $USER"
kubectl config delete-user "$USER"

echo ""
echo "✅ Offboarding complete"

Command:

./scripts/offboard-user.sh former-developer

kubectl config delete-user

DELETE USER REMOVE

Delete the specified user from kubeconfig

The kubectl config delete-user command removes a user entry from kubeconfig. Users contain authentication credentials. Used during offboarding or credential rotation.

Syntax

kubectl config delete-user USER_NAME

Parameters

  • USER_NAME: Name of the user to delete

Return Value

Confirmation message that user was deleted.

Examples

Example 1: Remove Old Service Account User

Remove deprecated service account credentials.

Context - List Current Users:

kubectl config get-users
# dev-admin
# staging-admin
# prod-admin
# old-ci-sa        <-- This one needs removal

Command:

kubectl config delete-user old-ci-sa

Output:

deleted user old-ci-sa from /home/user/.kube/config
Example 2: Credential Rotation Script

Rotate user credentials by removing old and adding new.

Context - Credential Rotation:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/rotate-credentials.sh

USER=$1
NEW_TOKEN=$2

if [ -z "$USER" ] || [ -z "$NEW_TOKEN" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <user> <new-token>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🔄 Rotating credentials for: $USER"

# Delete old user entry
kubectl config delete-user "$USER"

# Create new user entry with new token
kubectl config set-credentials "$USER" --token="$NEW_TOKEN"

echo "✅ Credentials rotated"

Command:

./scripts/rotate-credentials.sh prod-admin "new-token-abc123"
Example 3: Remove Test Users

Clean up test user entries from kubeconfig.

Command:

# Remove all users with 'test' in name
kubectl config get-users | grep test | while read user; do
    kubectl config delete-user "$user"
done
Example 4: Remove User After Certificate Expiry

Remove user with expired client certificate.

Command:

# Check if user certificate is expired
USER="django-dev"
CERT=$(kubectl config view --raw -o jsonpath='{.users[?(@.name=="'$USER'")].user.client-certificate-data}')

if [ -n "$CERT" ]; then
    EXPIRY=$(echo "$CERT" | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -enddate 2>/dev/null)
    echo "Certificate expiry: $EXPIRY"
fi

# If expired, remove and regenerate
kubectl config delete-user "$USER"
Example 5: Security Audit - Remove Leaked Credentials

Emergency removal of compromised user credentials.

Context - Security Response Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/security-revoke-user.sh
# EMERGENCY: Run when credentials are compromised

USER=$1
AUDIT_LOG=/var/log/k8s-security-audit.log

if [ -z "$USER" ]; then
    echo "SECURITY: Usage: $0 <compromised-user>"
    exit 1
fi

TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

echo "🚨 SECURITY: Revoking credentials for: $USER"
echo "$TIMESTAMP | REVOKE | $USER | Credential compromise suspected" >> $AUDIT_LOG

# Remove from local kubeconfig
kubectl config delete-user "$USER" 2>&1 | tee -a $AUDIT_LOG

# NOTE: Also revoke in cluster (RBAC, service accounts)
echo "⚠️  Remember to also:"
echo "   1. Revoke cluster RBAC bindings"
echo "   2. Delete service account if applicable"
echo "   3. Rotate any shared secrets"

echo ""
echo "✅ Local credentials revoked. Check audit log: $AUDIT_LOG"

Command:

./scripts/security-revoke-user.sh compromised-account

kubectl config get-clusters

GET CLUSTERS LIST

Display clusters defined in the kubeconfig

The kubectl config get-clusters command lists all cluster entries in kubeconfig. Shows available Kubernetes clusters for Django deployments.

Syntax

kubectl config get-clusters

Parameters

  • No additional parameters

Return Value

List of cluster names, one per line.

Examples

Example 1: List Available Clusters

View all clusters configured for Django development.

Context - Multi-Environment Setup:

# ~/.kube/config showing typical Django infrastructure
# - development: For local/dev testing
# - staging: Pre-production testing
# - production: Live Django application
# - dr-cluster: Disaster recovery site

Command:

kubectl config get-clusters

Output:

NAME
development
staging
production
dr-cluster
Example 2: Cluster Inventory Script

Generate cluster inventory for documentation.

Context - Inventory Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/cluster-inventory.sh

echo "# Kubernetes Cluster Inventory"
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

kubectl config get-clusters | tail -n +2 | while read cluster; do
    SERVER=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.clusters[?(@.name=='$cluster')].cluster.server}")
    echo "## $cluster"
    echo "- Server: $SERVER"
    echo ""
done

Command:

./scripts/cluster-inventory.sh > docs/cluster-inventory.md

Output:

# Kubernetes Cluster Inventory
Generated: Mon Mar 2 10:00:00 UTC 2026

## development
- Server: https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443

## production
- Server: https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443
Example 3: Check Cluster Exists

Validate cluster exists before operations.

Command:

# Check if specific cluster is configured
CLUSTER="production"

if kubectl config get-clusters | grep -q "^$CLUSTER$"; then
    echo "✅ Cluster '$CLUSTER' is configured"
else
    echo "❌ Cluster '$CLUSTER' not found in kubeconfig"
    exit 1
fi
Example 4: Count Clusters

Get count of configured clusters.

Command:

# Count clusters (subtract 1 for header)
CLUSTER_COUNT=$(($(kubectl config get-clusters | wc -l) - 1))

echo "Total clusters configured: $CLUSTER_COUNT"

# List with numbers
kubectl config get-clusters | tail -n +2 | nl

Output:

Total clusters configured: 4
     1  development
     2  staging
     3  production
     4  dr-cluster
Example 5: Health Check All Clusters

Verify connectivity to all configured clusters.

Context - Cluster Health Check Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-all-clusters.sh

echo "🔍 Checking connectivity to all clusters..."
echo ""

kubectl config get-clusters | tail -n +2 | while read cluster; do
    # Find a context using this cluster
    CONTEXT=$(kubectl config get-contexts -o name | while read ctx; do
        CTX_CLUSTER=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.contexts[?(@.name=='$ctx')].context.cluster}")
        if [ "$CTX_CLUSTER" == "$cluster" ]; then
            echo "$ctx"
            break
        fi
    done)
    
    if [ -n "$CONTEXT" ]; then
        if kubectl cluster-info --context="$CONTEXT" &>/dev/null; then
            echo "✅ $cluster - Connected via context '$CONTEXT'"
        else
            echo "❌ $cluster - Connection failed"
        fi
    else
        echo "⚠️  $cluster - No context found"
    fi
done

Command:

./scripts/check-all-clusters.sh

Output:

🔍 Checking connectivity to all clusters...

✅ development - Connected via context 'dev'
✅ staging - Connected via context 'staging'
✅ production - Connected via context 'prod'
❌ dr-cluster - Connection failed

kubectl config get-contexts

GET CONTEXTS LIST

Display one or many contexts from kubeconfig

The kubectl config get-contexts command lists all contexts in kubeconfig. Contexts bind clusters, users, and namespaces together. Shows which context is currently active with an asterisk.

Syntax

kubectl config get-contexts [CONTEXT_NAME] [flags]

Parameters

  • -o, --output: Output format (name)
  • --no-headers: Don't print headers

Return Value

Table showing context details with current context marked.

Examples

Example 1: List All Contexts

View all contexts for Django environments.

Command:

kubectl config get-contexts

Output:

CURRENT   NAME         CLUSTER      AUTHINFO     NAMESPACE
          dev          development  dev-admin    django-dev
*         staging      staging      stg-admin    django-staging
          prod         production   prod-admin   django-prod
Example 2: Get Context Names Only

List just context names for scripting.

Command:

kubectl config get-contexts -o name

Output:

dev
staging
prod
Example 3: Interactive Context Switcher

Create menu-based context switcher using fzf.

Context - Shell Function:

# ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

# Interactive context switcher
kctx() {
    local context
    context=$(kubectl config get-contexts -o name | fzf --prompt="Select context: ")
    
    if [ -n "$context" ]; then
        kubectl config use-context "$context"
    fi
}

# Show context details
kctx-info() {
    echo "📋 Available Contexts:"
    echo ""
    kubectl config get-contexts
}

Command:

kctx
Example 4: Get Specific Context Details

View details of a specific context.

Command:

# Get specific context
kubectl config get-contexts staging

Output:

CURRENT   NAME      CLUSTER   AUTHINFO    NAMESPACE
*         staging   staging   stg-admin   django-staging
Example 5: Context Report Generator

Generate context configuration report.

Context - Report Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/context-report.sh

echo "========================================="
echo "     KUBECONFIG CONTEXT REPORT"
echo "========================================="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

CURRENT=$(kubectl config current-context)

kubectl config get-contexts --no-headers | while read line; do
    echo "$line"
done

echo ""
echo "========================================="
echo "Current Context: $CURRENT"
echo "========================================="

Command:

./scripts/context-report.sh

kubectl config get-users

GET USERS LIST

Display users defined in the kubeconfig

The kubectl config get-users command lists all user entries in kubeconfig. Users contain authentication credentials for cluster access.

Syntax

kubectl config get-users

Parameters

  • No additional parameters

Return Value

List of user names, one per line.

Examples

Example 1: List All Users

View users with cluster access credentials.

Command:

kubectl config get-users

Output:

NAME
dev-admin
stg-admin
prod-admin
ci-service-account
Example 2: User Audit Script

Audit users and their authentication methods.

Context - User Audit Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/audit-users.sh

echo "👥 USER AUTHENTICATION AUDIT"
echo "============================"
echo ""

kubectl config get-users | tail -n +2 | while read user; do
    echo "User: $user"
    
    # Check authentication method
    TOKEN=$(kubectl config view --raw -o jsonpath='{.users[?(@.name=="'$user'")].user.token}')
    CERT=$(kubectl config view --raw -o jsonpath='{.users[?(@.name=="'$user'")].user.client-certificate-data}')
    EXEC=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{.users[?(@.name=="'$user'")].user.exec.command}')
    
    if [ -n "$TOKEN" ]; then
        echo "  Auth: Token-based"
    elif [ -n "$CERT" ]; then
        echo "  Auth: Client Certificate"
    elif [ -n "$EXEC" ]; then
        echo "  Auth: Exec plugin ($EXEC)"
    else
        echo "  Auth: Unknown/External"
    fi
    echo ""
done

Command:

./scripts/audit-users.sh
Example 3: Find Orphaned Users

Find users not referenced by any context.

Command:

# Find users without associated contexts
echo "🔍 Finding orphaned users..."

# Get users referenced by contexts
USED_USERS=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{.contexts[*].context.user}' | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u)

# Compare with all users
kubectl config get-users | tail -n +2 | while read user; do
    if ! echo "$USED_USERS" | grep -q "^$user$"; then
        echo "⚠️  Orphaned user: $user"
    fi
done
Example 4: Count Users

Get total count of configured users.

Command:

# Count users (subtract 1 for header)
USER_COUNT=$(($(kubectl config get-users | wc -l) - 1))

echo "Total users configured: $USER_COUNT"

# List with numbers
kubectl config get-users | tail -n +2 | nl

Output:

Total users configured: 4
     1  dev-admin
     2  stg-admin
     3  prod-admin
     4  ci-service-account
Example 5: User Certificate Expiry Check

Check certificate expiry for certificate-based users.

Context - Expiry Check Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-cert-expiry.sh

echo "🔐 Certificate Expiry Check"
echo "==========================="
echo ""

kubectl config get-users | tail -n +2 | while read user; do
    CERT=$(kubectl config view --raw -o jsonpath='{.users[?(@.name=="'$user'")].user.client-certificate-data}')
    
    if [ -n "$CERT" ]; then
        EXPIRY_DATE=$(echo "$CERT" | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -enddate 2>/dev/null | cut -d= -f2)
        echo "User: $user"
        echo "  Expires: $EXPIRY_DATE"
        echo ""
    fi
done

Command:

./scripts/check-cert-expiry.sh

Output:

🔐 Certificate Expiry Check
===========================

User: dev-admin
  Expires: Mar  2 10:00:00 2027 GMT

User: prod-admin
  Expires: Dec 31 23:59:59 2026 GMT

kubectl config rename-context

PUT CONTEXT RENAME

Rename a context in the kubeconfig

The kubectl config rename-context command renames a context entry. Useful for standardizing naming conventions or making context names more descriptive.

Syntax

kubectl config rename-context OLD_NAME NEW_NAME

Parameters

  • OLD_NAME: Current name of the context
  • NEW_NAME: New name for the context

Return Value

Confirmation message showing the renaming operation.

Examples

Example 1: Standardize Context Name

Rename to follow team naming convention.

Command:

# Rename from default cluster name to descriptive name
kubectl config rename-context kubernetes-admin@kubernetes django-production

Output:

Context "kubernetes-admin@kubernetes" renamed to "django-production".
Example 2: Rename All Contexts Script

Batch rename contexts to follow naming convention.

Context - Standardization Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/standardize-contexts.sh

# Define renaming rules
declare -A RENAMES=(
    ["kubernetes-admin@kubernetes"]="prod"
    ["minikube"]="local-dev"
    ["gke_project_zone_cluster"]="gke-prod"
    ["arn:aws:eks:region:account:cluster/name"]="eks-prod"
)

for old_name in "${!RENAMES[@]}"; do
    new_name="${RENAMES[$old_name]}"
    
    if kubectl config get-contexts -o name | grep -q "^$old_name$"; then
        echo "Renaming: $old_name → $new_name"
        kubectl config rename-context "$old_name" "$new_name"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "✅ Context standardization complete"
kubectl config get-contexts

Command:

./scripts/standardize-contexts.sh
Example 3: Add Environment Prefix

Prefix all contexts with environment identifier.

Command:

# Add prefix to all contexts
PREFIX="anihpj-"

kubectl config get-contexts -o name | while read ctx; do
    # Skip if already has prefix
    if [[ ! "$ctx" == $PREFIX* ]]; then
        kubectl config rename-context "$ctx" "${PREFIX}${ctx}"
    fi
done
Example 4: Rename Current Context

Rename the currently active context.

Command:

# Get current context and rename it
CURRENT=$(kubectl config current-context)
NEW_NAME="django-active"

kubectl config rename-context "$CURRENT" "$NEW_NAME"
echo "Current context is now: $(kubectl config current-context)"

Output:

Context "staging" renamed to "django-active".
Current context is now: django-active
Example 5: Team Onboarding Setup

Rename contexts for new team member.

Context - Team Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-team-contexts.sh
# Run after kubeconfig is merged for new team member

echo "🔧 Setting up team-standard context names..."

# Rename to team standards
kubectl config rename-context "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789:cluster/prod" "prod" 2>/dev/null || true
kubectl config rename-context "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789:cluster/staging" "staging" 2>/dev/null || true
kubectl config rename-context "minikube" "local" 2>/dev/null || true

# Set default context
kubectl config use-context local

echo ""
echo "✅ Context setup complete. Available contexts:"
kubectl config get-contexts -o name

Command:

./scripts/setup-team-contexts.sh

kubectl config set

POST PROPERTY SET

Set an individual value in kubeconfig file

The kubectl config set command sets arbitrary values in kubeconfig using dot notation for nested properties. Provides low-level kubeconfig manipulation.

Syntax

kubectl config set PROPERTY_NAME PROPERTY_VALUE

Parameters

  • PROPERTY_NAME: Dot-notation path to property
  • PROPERTY_VALUE: Value to set

Return Value

Confirmation of property being set.

Examples

Example 1: Set Current Context

Set current-context property directly.

Command:

kubectl config set current-context production

Output:

Property "current-context" set.
Example 2: Set Context Namespace

Change default namespace for a context.

Command:

# Set default namespace for context
kubectl config set contexts.production.namespace django-prod

Output:

Property "contexts.production.namespace" set.
Example 3: Set Cluster Server

Update cluster API server URL.

Command:

# Update server URL after IP change
kubectl config set clusters.production.server https://new-api-server.example.com:6443
Example 4: Namespace Switcher Function

Shell function to switch namespaces quickly.

Context - Shell Function:

# ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

# Quick namespace switcher
kns() {
    local namespace=$1
    local context=$(kubectl config current-context)
    
    if [ -z "$namespace" ]; then
        # Show current namespace
        kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.contexts[?(@.name=='$context')].context.namespace}"
        echo ""
    else
        kubectl config set "contexts.$context.namespace" "$namespace"
        echo "Switched to namespace: $namespace"
    fi
}

# Usage: kns django-prod

Command:

kns django-staging
Example 5: Bulk Property Updates

Update multiple properties in script.

Context - Migration Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/migrate-cluster-config.sh
# Update kubeconfig after cluster migration

NEW_SERVER="https://new-k8s-api.company.com:6443"
NEW_CA_FILE="/etc/kubernetes/pki/new-ca.crt"

echo "🔄 Migrating cluster configuration..."

# Update server address
kubectl config set clusters.production.server "$NEW_SERVER"

# Update certificate authority
kubectl config set clusters.production.certificate-authority "$NEW_CA_FILE"

echo "✅ Cluster configuration updated"
echo "Server: $NEW_SERVER"

# Verify connectivity
kubectl cluster-info

Command:

./scripts/migrate-cluster-config.sh

kubectl config set-cluster

POST CLUSTER CREATE/UPDATE

Set a cluster entry in kubeconfig

The kubectl config set-cluster command creates or modifies a cluster entry. Defines the Kubernetes API server endpoint and trust settings.

Syntax

kubectl config set-cluster NAME [--server=server] [--certificate-authority=path] [flags]

Parameters

  • NAME: Name for the cluster entry
  • --server: API server URL
  • --certificate-authority: Path to CA certificate file
  • --embed-certs: Embed certs in kubeconfig (default false)
  • --insecure-skip-tls-verify: Skip TLS verification (not recommended)

Return Value

Confirmation that cluster entry was created/updated.

Examples

Example 1: Add Production Cluster

Configure connection to production Kubernetes cluster.

Command:

kubectl config set-cluster production \
    --server=https://k8s-prod.company.com:6443 \
    --certificate-authority=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt

Output:

Cluster "production" set.
Example 2: Add Cluster with Embedded Certificate

Embed CA certificate for portable kubeconfig.

Command:

# Embed certificate for sharing kubeconfig
kubectl config set-cluster staging \
    --server=https://k8s-staging.company.com:6443 \
    --certificate-authority=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
    --embed-certs=true
Example 3: Multi-Cluster Setup Script

Configure multiple clusters for Django environments.

Context - Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-clusters.sh

# Django environment clusters
declare -A CLUSTERS=(
    ["development"]="https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443"
    ["staging"]="https://staging.k8s.company.com:6443"
    ["production"]="https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443"
)

CA_CERT="/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt"

for cluster in "${!CLUSTERS[@]}"; do
    server="${CLUSTERS[$cluster]}"
    
    echo "🔧 Configuring cluster: $cluster"
    kubectl config set-cluster "$cluster" \
        --server="$server" \
        --certificate-authority="$CA_CERT" \
        --embed-certs=true
done

echo ""
echo "✅ All clusters configured"
kubectl config get-clusters

Command:

./scripts/setup-clusters.sh
Example 4: Add Cluster from Base64 CA

Set cluster using base64-encoded CA from secret.

Context - CI/CD Pipeline:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy to Kubernetes

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Configure kubectl
        run: |
          # Decode CA certificate from secret
          echo "${{ secrets.K8S_CA_CERT }}" | base64 -d > /tmp/ca.crt
          
          # Set cluster
          kubectl config set-cluster production \
            --server=${{ secrets.K8S_SERVER }} \
            --certificate-authority=/tmp/ca.crt \
            --embed-certs=true
          
          # Set credentials and context
          kubectl config set-credentials deployer --token=${{ secrets.K8S_TOKEN }}
          kubectl config set-context production --cluster=production --user=deployer
          kubectl config use-context production
          
      - name: Deploy
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/
Example 5: Update Cluster Server Address

Update existing cluster's API server URL.

Context - After Load Balancer Change:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/update-cluster-server.sh

CLUSTER=$1
NEW_SERVER=$2

if [ -z "$CLUSTER" ] || [ -z "$NEW_SERVER" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <cluster> <new-server-url>"
    exit 1
fi

# Get current server
OLD_SERVER=$(kubectl config view -o jsonpath="{.clusters[?(@.name=='$CLUSTER')].cluster.server}")

echo "Updating cluster: $CLUSTER"
echo "  Old server: $OLD_SERVER"
echo "  New server: $NEW_SERVER"

# Update server (keeps existing CA settings)
kubectl config set-cluster "$CLUSTER" --server="$NEW_SERVER"

# Verify connectivity
echo ""
echo "Testing connectivity..."
kubectl cluster-info

Command:

./scripts/update-cluster-server.sh production https://new-lb.k8s.company.com:6443

kubectl config set-context

POST CONTEXT CREATE/UPDATE

Set a context entry in kubeconfig

The kubectl config set-context command creates or modifies a context entry. Contexts bind a cluster, user, and optional namespace together for convenient switching.

Syntax

kubectl config set-context NAME [--cluster=cluster] [--user=user] [--namespace=namespace]

Parameters

  • NAME: Name for the context
  • --cluster: Cluster name for this context
  • --user: User name for this context
  • --namespace: Default namespace for this context
  • --current: Modify the current context

Return Value

Confirmation that context was created/modified.

Examples

Example 1: Create Production Context

Set up context for Django production deployment.

Command:

kubectl config set-context django-prod \
    --cluster=production \
    --user=prod-admin \
    --namespace=django-prod

Output:

Context "django-prod" created.
Example 2: Set Namespace for Current Context

Change default namespace without switching context.

Command:

# Set namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=django-staging

Output:

Context "staging" modified.
Example 3: Complete Environment Setup

Set up full context configuration for all environments.

Context - Environment Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-contexts.sh
# Set up contexts for Django project environments

# Development context
kubectl config set-context dev \
    --cluster=development \
    --user=dev-admin \
    --namespace=django-dev

# Staging context  
kubectl config set-context staging \
    --cluster=staging \
    --user=stg-admin \
    --namespace=django-staging

# Production context
kubectl config set-context prod \
    --cluster=production \
    --user=prod-admin \
    --namespace=django-prod

# Set default to dev
kubectl config use-context dev

echo "✅ All contexts configured"
kubectl config get-contexts

Command:

./scripts/setup-contexts.sh
Example 4: Create Context for Specific Namespace

Create dedicated contexts for different namespaces.

Command:

# Create namespace-specific contexts sharing same cluster/user
for ns in django-web django-api django-workers; do
    kubectl config set-context "prod-$ns" \
        --cluster=production \
        --user=prod-admin \
        --namespace="$ns"
done

echo "Created namespace contexts:"
kubectl config get-contexts -o name | grep prod-

Output:

Context "prod-django-web" created.
Context "prod-django-api" created.
Context "prod-django-workers" created.
Created namespace contexts:
prod-django-web
prod-django-api
prod-django-workers
Example 5: CI/CD Context Configuration

Configure context in GitHub Actions workflow.

Context - GitHub Actions Workflow:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy Django to Kubernetes

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  K8S_NAMESPACE: django-prod

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup kubectl
        run: |
          # Set cluster (from previous step or secret)
          kubectl config set-cluster production \
            --server=${{ secrets.K8S_SERVER }} \
            --certificate-authority-data=${{ secrets.K8S_CA_DATA }}
          
          # Set user credentials
          kubectl config set-credentials ci-deployer \
            --token=${{ secrets.K8S_TOKEN }}
          
          # Create context binding cluster + user + namespace
          kubectl config set-context deploy-context \
            --cluster=production \
            --user=ci-deployer \
            --namespace=${{ env.K8S_NAMESPACE }}
          
          # Use the context
          kubectl config use-context deploy-context
          
      - name: Deploy
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/
          kubectl rollout status deployment/django-web

kubectl config set-credentials

POST USER CREATE/UPDATE

Set a user entry in kubeconfig

The kubectl config set-credentials command creates or modifies user credentials. Supports tokens, certificates, auth providers, and exec plugins.

Syntax

kubectl config set-credentials NAME [--token=token] [--client-certificate=path] [--client-key=path] [flags]

Parameters

  • NAME: Name for the user entry
  • --token: Bearer token for authentication
  • --client-certificate: Path to client certificate
  • --client-key: Path to client key
  • --embed-certs: Embed certs in kubeconfig
  • --exec-api-version: API version for exec plugin
  • --exec-command: Command for exec-based auth

Return Value

Confirmation that user was created/modified.

Examples

Example 1: Set Token-Based Credentials

Configure service account token for authentication.

Command:

# Get service account token
TOKEN=$(kubectl -n django-prod get secret django-sa-token -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d)

# Set credentials
kubectl config set-credentials django-sa --token="$TOKEN"

Output:

User "django-sa" set.
Example 2: Set Certificate-Based Credentials

Configure client certificate authentication.

Command:

kubectl config set-credentials prod-admin \
    --client-certificate=/etc/kubernetes/pki/admin.crt \
    --client-key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/admin.key \
    --embed-certs=true
Example 3: AWS EKS Configuration

Configure AWS IAM authenticator for EKS.

Context - EKS Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-eks-credentials.sh

CLUSTER_NAME="django-eks-prod"
AWS_REGION="us-east-1"

# Set up exec-based authentication for EKS
kubectl config set-credentials eks-admin \
    --exec-api-version=client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1 \
    --exec-command=aws \
    --exec-arg=eks \
    --exec-arg=get-token \
    --exec-arg=--cluster-name \
    --exec-arg="$CLUSTER_NAME" \
    --exec-arg=--region \
    --exec-arg="$AWS_REGION"

echo "✅ EKS credentials configured"
echo "Make sure AWS CLI is configured with appropriate IAM permissions"

Command:

./scripts/setup-eks-credentials.sh
Example 4: GKE Configuration

Configure gcloud for GKE authentication.

Command:

# GKE uses gcloud for authentication
kubectl config set-credentials gke-admin \
    --exec-api-version=client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1 \
    --exec-command=gcloud \
    --exec-arg=config \
    --exec-arg=config-helper \
    --exec-arg=--format=json
Example 5: Complete Kubeconfig Setup

Full setup of cluster, user, and context.

Context - Complete Setup Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/setup-complete-kubeconfig.sh
# Set up complete kubeconfig for new environment

CLUSTER_NAME=$1
SERVER_URL=$2
CA_CERT=$3
CLIENT_CERT=$4
CLIENT_KEY=$5
NAMESPACE=${6:-default}

if [ $# -lt 5 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <cluster-name> <server-url> <ca-cert> <client-cert> <client-key> [namespace]"
    exit 1
fi

USER_NAME="${CLUSTER_NAME}-admin"
CONTEXT_NAME="${CLUSTER_NAME}"

echo "🔧 Setting up kubeconfig for: $CLUSTER_NAME"

# 1. Set cluster
kubectl config set-cluster "$CLUSTER_NAME" \
    --server="$SERVER_URL" \
    --certificate-authority="$CA_CERT" \
    --embed-certs=true

# 2. Set credentials
kubectl config set-credentials "$USER_NAME" \
    --client-certificate="$CLIENT_CERT" \
    --client-key="$CLIENT_KEY" \
    --embed-certs=true

# 3. Set context
kubectl config set-context "$CONTEXT_NAME" \
    --cluster="$CLUSTER_NAME" \
    --user="$USER_NAME" \
    --namespace="$NAMESPACE"

# 4. Use the new context
kubectl config use-context "$CONTEXT_NAME"

echo ""
echo "✅ Setup complete"
kubectl config get-contexts "$CONTEXT_NAME"
echo ""
kubectl cluster-info

Command:

./scripts/setup-complete-kubeconfig.sh production \
    https://k8s-prod.company.com:6443 \
    /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
    /etc/kubernetes/pki/admin.crt \
    /etc/kubernetes/pki/admin.key \
    django-prod

kubectl config unset

DELETE PROPERTY REMOVE

Unset an individual value in kubeconfig

The kubectl config unset command removes a property from kubeconfig. Uses dot notation for nested properties. Useful for removing sensitive data or cleaning up configuration.

Syntax

kubectl config unset PROPERTY_NAME

Parameters

  • PROPERTY_NAME: Dot-notation path to property

Return Value

Confirmation that property was unset.

Examples

Example 1: Remove Context Namespace

Clear default namespace from context.

Command:

kubectl config unset contexts.production.namespace

Output:

Property "contexts.production.namespace" unset.
Example 2: Remove User Token

Clear credentials from user entry.

Command:

# Remove token (before sharing kubeconfig or for security)
kubectl config unset users.admin.token
Example 3: Clear Current Context

Unset current context to require explicit selection.

Command:

# Force explicit context selection
kubectl config unset current-context

# Now requires --context flag or setting
kubectl get pods  # Error: no context set

kubectl get pods --context=production  # Works
Example 4: Security Cleanup Script

Remove sensitive data before sharing kubeconfig.

Context - Sanitize Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/sanitize-kubeconfig.sh
# Remove sensitive data before sharing

# Create backup first
cp ~/.kube/config ~/.kube/config.backup

echo "🔒 Sanitizing kubeconfig..."

# Get all users and remove tokens/certs
kubectl config get-users | tail -n +2 | while read user; do
    # Remove token
    kubectl config unset "users.$user.token" 2>/dev/null
    # Remove client certificate data
    kubectl config unset "users.$user.client-certificate-data" 2>/dev/null
    # Remove client key data
    kubectl config unset "users.$user.client-key-data" 2>/dev/null
done

echo "✅ Kubeconfig sanitized"
echo "⚠️  Backup saved to ~/.kube/config.backup"

Command:

./scripts/sanitize-kubeconfig.sh
Example 5: Reset Context Configuration

Reset context to defaults by removing customizations.

Command:

#!/bin/bash
# Reset a context to cluster defaults

CONTEXT="staging"

echo "Resetting context: $CONTEXT"

# Remove namespace setting (use cluster default)
kubectl config unset "contexts.$CONTEXT.namespace"

# Verify
echo ""
echo "Context configuration after reset:"
kubectl config get-contexts "$CONTEXT"

kubectl config use-context

PUT CONTEXT SWITCH

Set the current-context in kubeconfig

The kubectl config use-context command sets the current context. All subsequent kubectl commands will use this context's cluster, user, and namespace.

Syntax

kubectl config use-context CONTEXT_NAME

Parameters

  • CONTEXT_NAME: Name of the context to switch to

Return Value

Confirmation of context switch.

Examples

Example 1: Switch to Production

Switch to production context for deployment.

Command:

kubectl config use-context production

Output:

Switched to context "production".
Example 2: Safe Context Switch Function

Shell function with confirmation for production.

Context - Shell Function:

# ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

# Safe context switcher
kuse() {
    local context=$1
    
    if [ -z "$context" ]; then
        echo "Usage: kuse <context>"
        kubectl config get-contexts -o name
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Require confirmation for production
    if [[ "$context" == *"prod"* ]]; then
        echo "⚠️  WARNING: Switching to PRODUCTION context"
        read -p "Are you sure? (yes/no): " confirm
        if [ "$confirm" != "yes" ]; then
            echo "Aborted"
            return 1
        fi
    fi
    
    kubectl config use-context "$context"
    echo ""
    echo "📍 Current context: $(kubectl config current-context)"
    echo "🏷  Namespace: $(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.contexts[0].context.namespace}')"
}

Command:

kuse production
Example 3: Interactive Context Selector

Use fzf for interactive selection.

Command:

# Interactive context switch with fzf
kubectl config use-context $(kubectl config get-contexts -o name | fzf)
Example 4: Deployment Script with Context

Deployment script ensuring correct context.

Context - Deployment Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy.sh

ENV=$1
: ${ENV:=staging}

echo "🚀 Deploying to: $ENV"

# Map environment to context
case $ENV in
    dev|development)
        CONTEXT="dev"
        ;;
    staging|stg)
        CONTEXT="staging"
        ;;
    prod|production)
        CONTEXT="production"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "❌ Unknown environment: $ENV"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

# Switch context
kubectl config use-context "$CONTEXT"

# Verify correct cluster
echo "📍 Deploying to cluster: $(kubectl cluster-info | head -1)"

# Deploy
kubectl apply -f k8s/$ENV/
kubectl rollout status deployment/django-web

Command:

./scripts/deploy.sh production
Example 5: Context Switch with Verification

Switch and verify cluster connectivity.

Command:

#!/bin/bash
# Switch context and verify

CONTEXT=$1

kubectl config use-context "$CONTEXT"

echo ""
echo "Verifying cluster connectivity..."

if kubectl cluster-info &>/dev/null; then
    echo "✅ Connected to cluster"
    kubectl get nodes
else
    echo "❌ Failed to connect to cluster"
    exit 1
fi

kubectl config view

GET CONFIG VIEW

Display merged kubeconfig settings

The kubectl config view command displays kubeconfig contents. Shows clusters, users, and contexts. Use --minify to show only current context settings. Sensitive data is redacted by default.

Syntax

kubectl config view [flags]

Parameters

  • --minify: Show only current context configuration
  • --raw: Show raw byte data (unredacted)
  • --flatten: Flatten config to single file
  • -o, --output: Output format (yaml, json)

Return Value

YAML/JSON formatted kubeconfig content.

Examples

Example 1: View Current Configuration

Display full kubeconfig (sensitive data redacted).

Command:

kubectl config view

Output:

apiVersion: v1
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: DATA+OMITTED
    server: https://k8s-prod.company.com:6443
  name: production
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: production
    namespace: django-prod
    user: prod-admin
  name: production
current-context: production
kind: Config
preferences: {}
users:
- name: prod-admin
  user:
    client-certificate-data: DATA+OMITTED
    client-key-data: DATA+OMITTED
Example 2: View Current Context Only

Show only active context configuration.

Command:

kubectl config view --minify

Output:

apiVersion: v1
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: DATA+OMITTED
    server: https://k8s-prod.company.com:6443
  name: production
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: production
    namespace: django-prod
    user: prod-admin
  name: production
current-context: production
kind: Config
users:
- name: prod-admin
  user:
    client-certificate-data: DATA+OMITTED
Example 3: Extract Specific Values

Use jsonpath to extract specific configuration values.

Command:

# Get current server URL
kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.server}'

# Get current namespace
kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.contexts[0].context.namespace}'

# Get all cluster names
kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{.clusters[*].name}'

Output:

https://k8s-prod.company.com:6443
django-prod
development staging production
Example 4: Export Portable Kubeconfig

Create flattened kubeconfig for sharing.

Command:

# Export current context as standalone kubeconfig
kubectl config view --minify --flatten > kubeconfig-export.yaml

# View JSON format
kubectl config view --minify -o json | jq .
Example 5: Configuration Audit Script

Audit kubeconfig for security review.

Context - Audit Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/audit-kubeconfig.sh

echo "🔍 KUBECONFIG SECURITY AUDIT"
echo "============================"
echo ""

# Current context
echo "📍 Current Context: $(kubectl config current-context)"
echo ""

# List all clusters
echo "🌐 Clusters:"
kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{range .clusters[*]}  - {.name}: {.cluster.server}{"\n"}{end}'
echo ""

# List all contexts  
echo "📋 Contexts:"
kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{range .contexts[*]}  - {.name} (cluster: {.context.cluster}, user: {.context.user}){"\n"}{end}'
echo ""

# Check for insecure settings
echo "⚠️  Security Checks:"

# Check for insecure-skip-tls-verify
if kubectl config view --raw | grep -q "insecure-skip-tls-verify: true"; then
    echo "  ❌ Found insecure-skip-tls-verify: true"
else
    echo "  ✅ No insecure TLS settings"
fi

# Check for embedded credentials
if kubectl config view --raw | grep -q "token:"; then
    echo "  ⚠️  Token credentials found (consider using exec plugin)"
fi

if kubectl config view --raw | grep -q "client-certificate-data:"; then
    echo "  ✅ Certificate-based auth in use"
fi

echo ""
echo "Audit complete."

Command:

./scripts/audit-kubeconfig.sh

Output:

🔍 KUBECONFIG SECURITY AUDIT
============================

📍 Current Context: production

🌐 Clusters:
  - development: https://dev.k8s.company.com:6443
  - production: https://prod.k8s.company.com:6443

📋 Contexts:
  - dev (cluster: development, user: dev-admin)
  - production (cluster: production, user: prod-admin)

⚠️  Security Checks:
  ✅ No insecure TLS settings
  ✅ Certificate-based auth in use

Audit complete.

kubectl explain

GET DOCUMENTATION EXPLAIN

Get documentation for resource fields

The kubectl explain command provides documentation for Kubernetes resource types and their fields. Essential for understanding manifest structure without leaving the terminal.

Syntax

kubectl explain RESOURCE [flags]

Parameters

  • RESOURCE: Resource type (pod, deployment, service, etc.)
  • --recursive: Show all fields recursively
  • --api-version: Specific API version to explain

Return Value

Field documentation with types and descriptions.

Examples

Example 1: Explain Pod Resource

Get overview of Pod resource.

Command:

kubectl explain pod

Output:

KIND:       Pod
VERSION:    v1

DESCRIPTION:
    Pod is a collection of containers that can run on a host. This resource is
    created by clients and scheduled onto hosts.

FIELDS:
  apiVersion    <string>
    APIVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object.

  kind          <string>
    Kind is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents.

  metadata      <ObjectMeta>
    Standard object's metadata.

  spec          <PodSpec>
    Specification of the desired behavior of the pod.

  status        <PodStatus>
    Most recently observed status of the pod. This data may not be up to date.
Example 2: Explain Nested Fields

Drill into specific field documentation.

Command:

# Explain pod.spec.containers
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers

Output:

KIND:       Pod
VERSION:    v1

FIELD: containers <[]Container>

DESCRIPTION:
    List of containers belonging to the pod. Containers cannot currently be
    added or removed. There must be at least one container in a Pod. Cannot be
    updated.

FIELDS:
  args          <[]string>
  command       <[]string>
  env           <[]EnvVar>
  envFrom       <[]EnvFromSource>
  image         <string>
  imagePullPolicy       <string>
  ...
Example 3: Recursive Field Display

Show all fields recursively for manifest writing.

Command:

# Get full structure for Deployment
kubectl explain deployment --recursive | head -50

Output:

KIND:       Deployment
VERSION:    apps/v1

DESCRIPTION:
    Deployment enables declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets.

FIELDS:
  apiVersion    <string>
  kind          <string>
  metadata      <ObjectMeta>
    annotations <map[string]string>
    creationTimestamp   <string>
    deletionGracePeriodSeconds  <integer>
    deletionTimestamp   <string>
    finalizers  <[]string>
    generateName        <string>
    generation  <integer>
    labels      <map[string]string>
    ...
Example 4: Quick Reference Tool

Shell function for quick field lookups.

Context - Shell Function:

# ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

# Quick explain with field path
kex() {
    local resource=$1
    if [ -z "$resource" ]; then
        echo "Usage: kex <resource> [field.path]"
        echo "Examples:"
        echo "  kex pod"
        echo "  kex pod.spec.containers"
        echo "  kex deployment.spec.strategy"
        return 1
    fi
    kubectl explain "$resource"
}

# Recursive explain with grep
kex-find() {
    local resource=$1
    local pattern=$2
    if [ -z "$pattern" ]; then
        kubectl explain "$resource" --recursive
    else
        kubectl explain "$resource" --recursive | grep -i "$pattern"
    fi
}

Command:

# Find all 'volume' related fields in pod
kex-find pod volume
Example 5: Explain for Manifest Generation

Use explain while writing Django deployment manifest.

Context - Understanding Fields for Django:

# When writing Django deployment, check available fields

# Check deployment strategy options
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy

# Check container resource requirements
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources

# Check service types
kubectl explain service.spec.type

# Check ingress rules
kubectl explain ingress.spec.rules

Generated Manifest Using Explain:

# k8s/deployment.yaml - Created using kubectl explain guidance
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: django-web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:                              # kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: django
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/django:latest
        resources:                        # kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"

kubectl options

GET OPTIONS LIST

Print the list of global flags

The kubectl options command lists all global command-line options available across all kubectl commands. Useful for understanding common flags like verbosity, output format, and authentication.

Syntax

kubectl options

Parameters

  • No parameters - displays all global options

Return Value

List of all global flags with descriptions.

Examples

Example 1: View All Global Options

See all available global flags.

Command:

kubectl options

Output:

The following options can be passed to any command:

      --as='':
            Username to impersonate for the operation
      --as-group=[]:
            Group to impersonate for the operation
      --cache-dir='/home/user/.kube/cache':
            Default cache directory
      --certificate-authority='':
            Path to a cert file for the certificate authority
      --client-certificate='':
            Path to a client certificate file for TLS
      --client-key='':
            Path to a client key file for TLS
      --cluster='':
            The name of the kubeconfig cluster to use
      --context='':
            The name of the kubeconfig context to use
      -n, --namespace='':
            If present, the namespace scope for this CLI request
      -v, --v=0:
            Number for the log level verbosity
      ...
Example 2: Find Specific Options

Search for options related to authentication.

Command:

# Find authentication-related options
kubectl options | grep -E "(token|cert|auth|user)"

Output:

      --as='':
            Username to impersonate for the operation
      --certificate-authority='':
            Path to a cert file for the certificate authority
      --client-certificate='':
            Path to a client certificate file for TLS
      --client-key='':
            Path to a client key file for TLS
      --token='':
            Bearer token for authentication to the API server
Example 3: Create Reference Card

Generate quick reference for common options.

Context - Reference Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/kubectl-reference.sh

echo "KUBECTL COMMON OPTIONS REFERENCE"
echo "================================"
echo ""
echo "🔧 Context & Cluster:"
kubectl options | grep -E "^\s+--(context|cluster|namespace|kubeconfig)" | head -8
echo ""
echo "🔐 Authentication:"
kubectl options | grep -E "^\s+--(token|client-cert|client-key|as)" | head -8
echo ""
echo "📝 Output:"
kubectl options | grep -E "^\s+(-o|--output|--v=)" | head -4

Command:

./scripts/kubectl-reference.sh
Example 4: Verbosity Levels

Understanding verbosity options for debugging.

Command:

# Default (no extra output)
kubectl get pods

# Level 4: Debug with curl commands
kubectl get pods -v=4

# Level 6: Show HTTP request/response
kubectl get pods -v=6

# Level 8: Show HTTP body content
kubectl get pods -v=8

# Level 9: Maximum verbosity
kubectl get pods -v=9
Example 5: Using Global Options in Scripts

Script using global options for flexibility.

Context - Flexible Deployment Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/deploy-flexible.sh
# Supports all kubectl global options

# Default values
NAMESPACE="django-prod"
CONTEXT=""
VERBOSITY=0

# Parse arguments
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case $1 in
        -n|--namespace)
            NAMESPACE="$2"
            shift 2
            ;;
        --context)
            CONTEXT="$2"
            shift 2
            ;;
        -v)
            VERBOSITY="$2"
            shift 2
            ;;
        *)
            shift
            ;;
    esac
done

# Build kubectl global args
KUBECTL_ARGS=""
[ -n "$NAMESPACE" ] && KUBECTL_ARGS="$KUBECTL_ARGS -n $NAMESPACE"
[ -n "$CONTEXT" ] && KUBECTL_ARGS="$KUBECTL_ARGS --context=$CONTEXT"
[ "$VERBOSITY" -gt 0 ] && KUBECTL_ARGS="$KUBECTL_ARGS -v=$VERBOSITY"

echo "🚀 Deploying with options: $KUBECTL_ARGS"

kubectl $KUBECTL_ARGS apply -f k8s/
kubectl $KUBECTL_ARGS rollout status deployment/django-web

Command:

./scripts/deploy-flexible.sh --context=production -n django-prod -v 4

kubectl plugin

GET PLUGINS MANAGE

List and manage kubectl plugins

The kubectl plugin command manages kubectl plugins. Plugins extend kubectl functionality. Use kubectl plugin list to see installed plugins.

Syntax

kubectl plugin list

Parameters

  • list: List all available plugins
  • --name-only: Show only plugin names

Return Value

List of discovered plugins with their paths.

Examples

Example 1: List Installed Plugins

View all kubectl plugins in PATH.

Command:

kubectl plugin list

Output:

The following compatible plugins are available:

/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ctx
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ns
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-neat
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-tree
Example 2: Install Plugins with Krew

Use Krew plugin manager to install plugins.

Context - Krew Installation:

# Install Krew (kubectl plugin manager)
(
  set -x; cd "$(mktemp -d)" &&
  OS="$(uname | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')" &&
  ARCH="$(uname -m | sed -e 's/x86_64/amd64/' -e 's/aarch64/arm64/')" &&
  KREW="krew-${OS}_${ARCH}" &&
  curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/krew/releases/latest/download/${KREW}.tar.gz" &&
  tar zxvf "${KREW}.tar.gz" &&
  ./"${KREW}" install krew
)

# Add to PATH
export PATH="${KREW_ROOT:-$HOME/.krew}/bin:$PATH"

# Install useful plugins
kubectl krew install ctx     # Context switcher
kubectl krew install ns      # Namespace switcher
kubectl krew install neat    # Clean up manifest output
kubectl krew install tree    # Show resource hierarchy

Command:

kubectl krew install ctx ns neat
Example 3: Create Custom Plugin

Create a Django-specific kubectl plugin.

Context - Custom Plugin:

#!/bin/bash
# /usr/local/bin/kubectl-django
# Custom plugin for Django deployments

# Make executable: chmod +x /usr/local/bin/kubectl-django

SUBCOMMAND=$1
shift

case $SUBCOMMAND in
    logs)
        # Show Django app logs
        kubectl logs -l app=django -f "$@"
        ;;
    shell)
        # Open Django shell
        POD=$(kubectl get pod -l app=django -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
        kubectl exec -it "$POD" -- python manage.py shell "$@"
        ;;
    migrate)
        # Run migrations
        POD=$(kubectl get pod -l app=django -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
        kubectl exec -it "$POD" -- python manage.py migrate "$@"
        ;;
    status)
        # Show Django deployment status
        echo "📊 Django Deployment Status"
        kubectl get deployments -l app=django
        echo ""
        kubectl get pods -l app=django
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Usage: kubectl django <command>"
        echo ""
        echo "Commands:"
        echo "  logs    - Follow Django application logs"
        echo "  shell   - Open Django interactive shell"
        echo "  migrate - Run database migrations"
        echo "  status  - Show deployment status"
        ;;
esac

Command:

# Install and use
sudo cp kubectl-django /usr/local/bin/
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/kubectl-django

# Now use as kubectl plugin
kubectl django status
kubectl django shell
Example 4: Useful Plugin Commands

Common plugin usage examples.

Command:

# kubectl ctx - Quick context switching
kubectl ctx                  # List contexts
kubectl ctx production       # Switch to production

# kubectl ns - Quick namespace switching
kubectl ns                   # List namespaces
kubectl ns django-prod       # Switch to django-prod

# kubectl neat - Clean up manifest output
kubectl get deployment django-web -o yaml | kubectl neat

# kubectl tree - Show resource hierarchy
kubectl tree deployment django-web
Example 5: Plugin Development Best Practices

Guidelines for creating plugins.

Context - Plugin Template:

#!/bin/bash
# kubectl-<pluginname>
# Plugin naming: kubectl-<name> becomes "kubectl <name>"

set -e

# Parse common kubectl options
KUBECTL_ARGS=""
while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case $1 in
        -n|--namespace)
            KUBECTL_ARGS="$KUBECTL_ARGS -n $2"
            shift 2
            ;;
        --context)
            KUBECTL_ARGS="$KUBECTL_ARGS --context=$2"
            shift 2
            ;;
        -h|--help)
            echo "Usage: kubectl <pluginname> [options]"
            echo ""
            echo "Options:"
            echo "  -n, --namespace   Kubernetes namespace"
            echo "  --context         Kubernetes context"
            echo "  -h, --help        Show this help"
            exit 0
            ;;
        *)
            break
            ;;
    esac
done

# Plugin logic here
echo "Running with kubectl args: $KUBECTL_ARGS"
kubectl $KUBECTL_ARGS get pods

kubectl plugin list

GET PLUGINS LIST

List all kubectl plugins in PATH

The kubectl plugin list command discovers and lists all available kubectl plugins. Plugins are executables named kubectl-<name> found in PATH directories.

Syntax

kubectl plugin list [flags]

Parameters

  • --name-only: Only show plugin names without paths

Return Value

List of discovered plugins with their full paths.

Examples

Example 1: List All Plugins

View all kubectl plugins available on system.

Command:

kubectl plugin list

Output:

The following compatible plugins are available:

/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ctx
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ns
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-neat
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-tree
/home/user/.krew/bin/kubectl-krew
Example 2: Check for Warnings

Identify plugins with issues (naming conflicts, permissions).

Command:

kubectl plugin list 2>&1

Output:

The following compatible plugins are available:

/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ctx
/usr/local/bin/kubectl-ns

The following plugins have warnings:

/usr/local/bin/kubectl-foo
  - warning: kubectl-foo overwrites existing kubectl command: "kubectl foo"
/home/user/bin/kubectl-bar
  - warning: not executable
Example 3: Plugin Inventory Script

Generate plugin inventory for team documentation.

Context - Inventory Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/plugin-inventory.sh

echo "# kubectl Plugin Inventory"
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

echo "## Available Plugins"
kubectl plugin list 2>/dev/null | grep "^/" | while read plugin; do
    name=$(basename "$plugin" | sed 's/kubectl-//')
    echo "- **$name**: \`$plugin\`"
done

echo ""
echo "## Usage"
echo "Run any plugin with: \`kubectl <plugin-name>\`"

Command:

./scripts/plugin-inventory.sh > docs/plugins.md
Example 4: Verify Plugin Installation

Check if specific plugin is installed.

Command:

# Check if ctx plugin is available
if kubectl plugin list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "kubectl-ctx"; then
    echo "✅ kubectl-ctx is installed"
    kubectl ctx --help
else
    echo "❌ kubectl-ctx not found"
    echo "Install with: kubectl krew install ctx"
fi
Example 5: Plugin Health Check

Verify all plugins are working correctly.

Context - Health Check Script:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-plugins.sh

echo "🔍 kubectl Plugin Health Check"
echo "==============================="
echo ""

# Count plugins
TOTAL=$(kubectl plugin list 2>/dev/null | grep "^/" | wc -l)
WARNINGS=$(kubectl plugin list 2>&1 | grep -c "warning:")

echo "Total plugins: $TOTAL"
echo "Warnings: $WARNINGS"
echo ""

kubectl plugin list 2>/dev/null | grep "^/" | while read plugin; do
    name=$(basename "$plugin" | sed 's/kubectl-//')
    
    if [ -x "$plugin" ]; then
        echo "✅ $name"
    else
        echo "❌ $name (not executable)"
    fi
done

if [ "$WARNINGS" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "⚠️  Plugin Warnings:"
    kubectl plugin list 2>&1 | grep -A1 "warning:"
fi

Command:

./scripts/check-plugins.sh

Output:

🔍 kubectl Plugin Health Check
===============================

Total plugins: 4
Warnings: 0

✅ ctx
✅ ns
✅ neat
✅ tree

kubectl version

GET VERSION INFO

Print the client and server version information
